Human beings are secondary causes, acting within the framework God has established. When we do good, it is because of God’s grace working within us (Philippians 2:13). When we sin, the fault lies entirely with us (James 1:13-14).

For the believer, this truth provides immense comfort. We can rest knowing that our lives are not governed by blind fate or random chance, but by a loving and sovereign Father. Even when we make mistakes or face suffering, God is working through those circumstances for our good and His glory (Romans 8:28).

If you find yourself struggling with the weight of your own choices or the brokenness of the world, remember that God’s sovereignty is the only sure foundation for hope. He is holy and just, yet He is also full of mercy. The greatest exercise of a human will is to surrender to the Lordship of Christ.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. Is there anything in particular about your own life or choices that makes it difficult for you to trust in God’s control?

As a practical next step, I encourage you to read through the Gospel of John, paying close attention to how Jesus speaks about both His Father’s sovereign plan and the necessity for people to believe in Him. Consider making it a daily rhythm to pray for God to align your desires with His will, asking Him to give you a heart that loves what He commands.
What was the purpose of the biblical sign gifts?

The primary purpose of the biblical sign gifts, such as speaking in tongues, physical healings, and prophetic revelations, was to authenticate the messengers of God and the divine message they carried during pivotal transitions in redemptive history. These extraordinary displays of the Holy Spirit’s power served as "divine credentials," proving that those speaking were indeed commissioned by God to reveal His truth to the world.

In the New Testament, these signs were specifically linked to the ministry of Jesus and His Apostles as they laid the foundation of the Church. The Bible teaches that God bore witness to the Gospel through signs, wonders, various miracles, and gifts of the Holy Spirit distributed according to His will (Hebrews 2:4). These miracles were not merely for the sake of the supernatural itself, but were pointers to the authority of the Word being preached. For instance, the "signs of a true apostle" included signs, wonders, and miracles which confirmed their unique role in the early Church (2 Corinthians 12:12).

Beyond authentication, these gifts also served to edify and build up the early body of believers during a time when the New Testament scriptures were not yet completed or widely circulated (1 Corinthians 14:12; 1 Corinthians 14:26). They allowed the fledgling Church to receive direct guidance and encouragement as the mystery of Christ was being fully unveiled to both Jews and Gentiles (Ephesians 3:4-6).

Within the Christian faith, there are different perspectives regarding whether these specific sign gifts continue today or if their purpose was fulfilled once the foundation of the Church was laid and the Bible was completed.

*   **Cessationism:** This view holds that the miraculous sign gifts were intended for the apostolic age to authenticate the original messengers and to establish the New Testament canon. Once the Bible was complete and the apostolic foundation was laid, these specific gifts ceased to be a regular part of church life.
*   **Continuationism:** This view maintains that all the gifts of the Spirit mentioned in the New Testament are still available and intended for the Church today. Proponents believe these gifts continue to serve for the edification of believers and as a testimony to the power of God in the present age.

Regardless of one's view on the duration of these gifts, all faithful Christians agree that the Holy Spirit remains active today, convicting the world of sin, regenerating hearts, and empowering believers for holy living.

The ultimate "sign" given to humanity is the person and work of Jesus Christ. Just as the miracles of Jesus were recorded so that you may believe He is the Christ, the Son of God, and that by believing you may have life in His name (John 20:30-31), the greatest miracle is the transformation of a soul from death to life. The signs always pointed to the Savior, who was delivered up for our trespasses and raised for our justification (Romans 4:25).

If you find yourself searching for a sign of God's love, look no further than the cross where Jesus died for sinners. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and the promise of eternal life. What prevents you from resting your soul in the finished work of Christ today?

As a practical next step in your spiritual journey, I encourage you to commit to a systematic reading of the New Testament, perhaps starting with the Gospel of John. Ask the Lord to give you a deeper hunger for His written Word, which is the final and sufficient authority for all of life and godliness (2 Peter 1:3). Seek out a local, Bible-believing church where the Word is faithfully preached and where you can grow in the grace and knowledge of our Lord Jesus Christ.
What is Shintoism?

Shintoism is the indigenous spiritual tradition of Japan, characterized by the worship of *kami*, which are spirits or essences believed to inhabit the natural world, including mountains, rivers, trees, and ancestors. It is not a religion of formal creeds or a single founder; instead, it focuses on ritual practices, traditional festivals, and the maintenance of harmony between humanity, nature, and the spiritual realm.

Central to Shintoism is the concept of ritual purity. Shinto practitioners believe that humans are born fundamentally good but can become "polluted" by physical or spiritual impurities called *kegare*. These are addressed through acts of purification at shrines, often marked by the iconic *torii* gates that signify the entrance into sacred space.

From the perspective of the Holy Scriptures, Shintoism is understood as a form of animism and polytheism. While it rightly perceives a sense of wonder and majesty in the natural world, it attributes divine qualities to the creation rather than to the Creator. The Apostle Paul addresses this inclination in the Book of Romans, noting that people have "exchanged the truth of God for a lie, and worshiped and served the creature rather than the Creator, who is blessed forever" Romans 1:25.

The Bible teaches that there is only one true and living God, who exists eternally in three persons: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. This God is not part of the trees or the wind, but is the transcendent Lord who spoke all things into existence by His power Genesis 1:1; Colossians 1:16. Because God is holy and we have all sinned against Him, we cannot be made pure through outward rituals or physical washings. Our "pollution" is a matter of the heart that separates us from our Maker.

True cleansing and peace are found only in Jesus Christ, the Word made flesh, who came to reveal the Father to us John 1:14; John 14:6. He lived the perfect life we could not live and died a sacrificial death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. His bodily resurrection from the dead confirms His victory over death and His authority as Lord of all. 

While Shintoism seeks harmony with nature, the Gospel offers reconciliation with God. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive total forgiveness and the gift of eternal life. Does the idea of a personal relationship with the Creator of the universe change how you view the beauty of the world around you?
Who is the man of lawlessness in 2 Thessalonians 2:1-12?

In 2 Thessalonians 2:1–12, the man of lawlessness is a figure of ultimate rebellion who opposes God and exalts himself above all that is worshiped. This individual, also called the son of destruction, is characterized by his defiance of divine authority and his eventual destruction by the breath of the Lord Jesus’ mouth and the brightness of His coming as described in 2 Thessalonians 2:3 and 2 Thessalonians 2:8.

While the biblical text provides a clear description of his character and actions, faithful Christians have held different views regarding his specific identity. This is considered a secondary area of eschatology (the study of last things) where believers may disagree while remaining united in the core gospel.

The most common interpretations include the following:

*   The Futurist View: This perspective holds that the man of lawlessness is a single individual, often identified as the Antichrist, who will appear at the very end of the age. He will perform counterfeit signs and wonders to deceive those who are perishing because they refused to love the truth and be saved as noted in 2 Thessalonians 2:9–10.
*   The Historicist View: Many reformers and early Baptists identified the man of lawlessness as the office of the Papacy. They argued that by claiming titles of divine authority and shifting the focus away from the finished work of Christ, this institution fulfilled the description of sitting in the temple of God and proclaiming himself to be God as seen in 2 Thessalonians 2:4.
*   The Preterist View: Some believe this passage refers to a figure contemporary to the first-century church, such as a Roman Emperor like Nero or a leader within the Jewish rebellion who desecrated the temple before its destruction in A.D. 70.

The Apostle Paul’s primary purpose in writing this was not to satisfy curiosity about the future, but to provide pastoral comfort. The Thessalonian believers were shaken and alarmed, fearing they had missed the Day of the Lord. Paul reminded them that God is sovereign over history and that the mystery of lawlessness is already at work, yet it is being restrained until the appointed time as stated in 2 Thessalonians 2:6–7.

A vital theological term to understand here is apostasy, which refers to a falling away or a rebellion against the truth. Paul warns that this man of lawlessness arrives alongside a great apostasy. This serves as a sobering reminder for us to be grounded in the inerrant Word of God so that we are not led astray by the spirit of the age or deceptive signs.

As a practical step, I encourage you to immerse yourself in the study of the Scriptures daily. When we know the truth of God’s Word intimately, we are better equipped to recognize the "lie" that the man of lawlessness promotes. Stand firm in the traditions taught by the apostles and let your heart be established in the hope of Christ’s return.

The warning in this passage is grave: those who do not love the truth will follow the deception of unrighteousness. True life and safety are found only in Jesus Christ. He is the eternal Son of God who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave to defeat death and the powers of darkness. You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for your salvation. What holds you back from placing your full trust in Him today?
What are some commonalities in Eastern religions?

Eastern religions, such as Hinduism, Buddhism, and Taoism, often share several foundational themes that reflect a deep human longing to understand the nature of existence and the means of finding peace. While they possess distinct rituals and philosophies, they frequently converge on the following concepts:

**Cyclical Time and Reincarnation**
Most Eastern traditions view time not as a linear progression toward a final judgment, but as a repeating cycle. This is often expressed through the concept of *Samsara*, the continuous cycle of birth, death, and rebirth. In this view, the soul or the life force is bound to this world through successive lives. This stands in direct contrast to the biblical truth that it is appointed for man to die once, and after that comes judgment (Hebrews 9:27).

**The Law of Karma**
Closely tied to cyclical life is the principle of *Karma*, the moral law of cause and effect. It suggests that every action, good or bad, produces a corresponding result that affects one's current or future existence. While this recognizes a sense of moral order, it places the entire burden of "balancing the scales" upon the individual. There is no concept of a substitute or a Savior in this system; one must work out their own liberation through merit and effort.

**Impersonal Ultimate Reality**
Many Eastern religions move away from the idea of a personal, sovereign Creator who is distinct from His creation. Instead, they often emphasize an impersonal "oneness" or a cosmic force, such as *Brahman* or the *Tao*. This perspective frequently leads to pantheism (the belief that everything is god) or monism (the belief that all is one). Scripture, however, teaches that God is the transcendent and personal Creator who is separate from His handiwork, though He is intimately involved with it (Genesis 1:1; John 1:1–3).

**Salvation Through Self-Realization**
In these systems, the primary human problem is often defined as ignorance rather than sin. Therefore, the "way out" of suffering or the cycle of rebirth is through enlightenment, meditation, or the shedding of desires. The focus is inward, seeking to find divinity or "nothingness" within oneself.

As a Christian, I observe these beliefs with a heart of compassion, recognizing the heavy yoke that a system of self-effort places upon a person. While Eastern religions seek to solve the problem of human suffering through the human will, the Gospel of Jesus Christ reveals that our deepest problem is not ignorance, but a moral separation from a holy God due to sin (Romans 3:23).

The "Wheel of Life" in Eastern thought offers no end to the striving, but the Cross of Jesus Christ offers a finished work. True peace is not found by looking inward to our own strength, but by looking outward to the one who said, "Come to Me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest" (Matthew 11:28).

Jesus Christ is the Vera Deus, vera homo—truly God and truly man—who entered into our linear history to die a substitutionary death for our sins and rose bodily on the third day to defeat death once and for all (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). Salvation is a gift received by grace through faith alone, not a reward for moral merit or meditative mastery (Ephesians 2:8–9).

You can turn to God even now, beloved, repenting of the heavy burden of trying to save yourself, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. In Him, you will find a forgiveness that Karma can never provide and a hope that transcends the cycles of this world. What is it that holds you back from trusting in His finished work today?
What is the bottomless pit (Revelation 9:1-12)?

The bottomless pit, also known as the Abyss, is a literal place of confinement and incarceration for fallen angelic beings and demonic spirits. The Greek word used in the text is *abyssos*, which suggests a depth so profound that it is immeasurable, signifying a realm of darkness and separation from the light of God's favorable presence.

In the context of Revelation 9:1-12, the opening of this pit represents a specific stage of divine judgment. A fallen "star," representing a spiritual being, is given the key to the shaft of the Abyss. Upon opening it, smoke rises as if from a great furnace, out of which emerge demonic forces described as locusts. These are not common insects but supernatural agents of torment that are permitted to strike only those who do not have the seal of God on their foreheads. This reveals a vital truth: even the most terrifying forces of darkness are under the absolute sovereignty and restrictive command of Almighty God.

The reality of the Abyss as a prison is confirmed elsewhere in Scripture. In the Gospels, when Jesus encountered the man possessed by many demons, the spirits begged Him not to command them to go into the Abyss (Luke 8:31). Later in the book of Revelation, we are told that Satan himself will be bound and cast into this bottomless pit for a thousand years to prevent him from deceiving the nations (Revelation 20:1-3). The pit is presided over by a king named Abaddon in Hebrew and Apollyon in Greek, both of which mean "Destroyer" (Revelation 9:11).

This passage serves as a sobering reminder of the gravity of sin and the certainty of God's judgment upon rebellion. However, for those who belong to Christ, there is no cause for terror. The Lord Jesus Christ holds the keys of Death and Hades (Revelation 1:18), and His authority ensures the protection of His people.

If you find yourself unsettled by the reality of such judgment, know that God has provided a way of escape through His Son. Jesus Christ died for our sins and rose bodily from the grave to defeat the powers of death and the devil. You can turn to God today, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for your salvation and peace.

For further study on God's authority over fallen spirits, you may look to 2 Peter 2:4 and Jude 1:6. A helpful next step would be to spend time in prayer, thanking God for His protective hand over your life and asking Him to deepen your trust in His sovereign care.
What is biblical theology?

Biblical theology is the study of the Bible that tracks the unfolding of God’s redemptive plan throughout history, focusing on how the various parts of Scripture relate to the whole narrative centered on Jesus Christ. It treats the Bible not as a collection of isolated moral lessons, but as a single, unified story of God revealing Himself and His purposes to humanity over time (Luke 24:27; Luke 24:44–45).

While systematic theology organizes truth by categories, such as the doctrine of God or the doctrine of man, biblical theology follows the historical timeline of the Bible. It recognizes the principle of progressive revelation, which means that God did not reveal His entire plan at once. Instead, He unfolded His truth gradually, starting with the first promise of a Savior in the garden (Genesis 3:15) and building upon that promise through successive covenants with Noah, Abraham, Israel, and David (Hebrews 1:1–2).

This approach to Scripture emphasizes several key themes:

*   **The Unity of Scripture:** Biblical theology demonstrates that the Old and New Testaments are not two different religions, but one organic story. The Old Testament is the New Testament concealed, and the New Testament is the Old Testament revealed.
*   **Christ-Centeredness:** It seeks to show how every type, shadow, and prophecy finds its "Yes" and "Amen" in Christ (2 Corinthians 1:20; Colossians 2:17). Whether we are reading about the Tabernacle, the sacrificial system, or the Davidic monarchy, biblical theology points us toward the person and work of the Savior.
*   **Redemptive History:** It focuses on the major turning points in history where God acted to save His people, culminating in the incarnation, death, and resurrection of Jesus.

Practically, biblical theology guards the believer against moralism, which is the error of reading the Bible simply to find "heroes" to imitate. When we see the Bible through the lens of biblical theology, we realize that every story is ultimately about the true Hero, Jesus Christ, who succeeded where all others failed.

The heart of this grand narrative is the Gospel. The Bible tells us that because of our sin, we are separated from our holy Creator. Yet, in His great love, God the Father sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live the perfect life we could not live and to die the atoning death we deserved. By His bodily resurrection, He defeated sin and death for all who believe (1 Corinthians 15:3–4; Romans 5:8).

As you read the Word, I encourage you to look for the "scarlet thread" of redemption in every chapter. Ask how each passage reveals the character of God or the need for the Savior. You can turn to God right now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for the forgiveness and new life He offers. What prevents you from trusting in His finished work today?
Can people in heaven look down and see those of us who are still on the earth?

The Bible does not provide a singular, explicit verse that says those in heaven are looking down on us at every moment, but it does offer several glimpses into the reality of those who have passed into the presence of the Lord. Whether they see us or not is a question that faithful Christians have pondered for centuries, and there are two primary ways to understand what Scripture reveals.

One perspective suggests that those in heaven may indeed have some awareness of what is happening on earth. This is often drawn from Hebrews 12:1, which speaks of a "great cloud of witnesses" surrounding us. While some interpreters believe this refers to the Old Testament saints watching us like spectators in a stadium, others argue it means their lives serve as a "witness" or testimony to us of God’s faithfulness. We also see in Revelation 6:9-10 that the martyrs in heaven are aware that their blood has not yet been avenged on the earth, which implies they have some knowledge of the state of human affairs. Additionally, Jesus tells us in Luke 15:7 that there is joy in heaven over one sinner who repents, suggesting that the heavenly host is aware of the spiritual victories occurring in our world.

Another perspective emphasizes the "rest" and the singular focus of those in heaven. In this view, the saints are so captivated by the "beatific vision," the direct sight of the glory of God, that they are not preoccupied with the fleeting troubles of this world. Scripture describes the intermediate state, the time between death and the final resurrection, as a place of conscious joy and relief from earthly toil, as seen in Revelation 14:13. Some find it difficult to reconcile the perfect peace of heaven with the potential sorrow of watching loved ones struggle or suffer on earth, though we must remember that those in heaven see things from God’s perspective and trust His sovereign purposes perfectly.

Ultimately, whether they see us or not, our comfort does not come from their observation of us, but from our shared union with Jesus Christ. If you are in Christ, you are joined to the same Lord whom they are currently beholding face to face. We are part of one family, the "communion of saints," spanning both earth and heaven.

If you find yourself longing for that heavenly reality or grieving a loss, remember that the only way to enter that place of rest is through the door of the Gospel. God is perfectly holy, and our sin separates us from Him, but in His great mercy, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for sin, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, conquering death forever. He is now exalted as Lord over all.

Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God even now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and the promise of eternal life.

A helpful next step would be to spend time reading through the Gospel of John to see the beauty of Christ’s promises, and to find a local, Bible-believing church where you can fellowship with other believers as you walk this earthly journey toward our true home.

Supporting Passages:
Luke 15:7; Luke 16:19-31; Hebrews 12:1; Revelation 6:9-10; Revelation 14:13; Revelation 21:4
Who are the Free Will Baptists, and what do they believe?

Free Will Baptists are a group of evangelical Christians who belong to the Baptist tradition but distinguish themselves primarily through their adherence to Arminian theology. While they share the foundational core doctrines of the historic Christian faith with other orthodox believers, they differ from Reformed or "Particular" Baptists on the mechanics of how God’s grace is applied to the individual and the nature of Christ’s atonement.

The movement in America took root in the eighteenth century through two primary strands. The first was established in the South by Paul Palmer in 1727, and the second in the North by Benjamin Randall in 1780. Both leaders sought to emphasize the biblical teaching that God desires the salvation of all people and has provided a way for all to be saved through the sacrificial work of Jesus Christ.

The theological distinctives of Free Will Baptists are centered on several key pillars:

**General Atonement**
Free Will Baptists believe that Jesus Christ died as a sacrifice for the sins of the entire world, not only for a specific group of elect individuals. They ground this in passages such as 1 John 2:2, which states, "He Himself is the atoning sacrifice for our sins, and not only for ours but also for the sins of the whole world," and John 3:16.

**Free Will and Prevenient Grace**
While they affirm that human nature is corrupted by sin and that no one can seek God on their own power, they believe God extends "prevenient grace" to all people. This grace acts upon the heart and mind to enable a person to freely choose to accept or reject the Gospel. In their view, God’s grace is resistible. They often point to Acts 7:51, where Stephen tells his accusers they are "always resisting the Holy Spirit."

**Conditional Perseverance**
Unlike those who hold to the "perseverance of the saints" (the belief that a true believer can never finally fall away), Free Will Baptists teach that salvation is conditional upon continued faith. They believe it is possible for a genuine believer to abandon their faith and, through apostasy, forfeit their salvation. This concern is often drawn from warnings found in Hebrews 6:4-6 and 2 Peter 2:20-22.

**The Three Ordinances**
Most Free Will Baptist churches recognize three Gospel ordinances:
1. **Believer’s Baptism:** Baptism by immersion for those who have personally confessed faith in Christ.
2. **The Lord’s Supper:** Observed as a memorial of Christ’s death, usually practiced as "Open Communion," where all believers are invited to partake regardless of denominational affiliation.
3. **The Washing of the Saints' Feet:** Many Free Will Baptists practice foot washing as an act of humility and service, following the example and command of Jesus in John 13:14-15, where He said, "If I then, your Lord and Teacher, have washed your feet, you also ought to wash one another’s feet."

It is important to recognize that these differences regarding the extent of the atonement and the nature of perseverance are secondary doctrines. While they create distinct denominational identities, they do not separate Free Will Baptists from the body of Christ. Faithful Christians may disagree on these points while remaining united in the essential Gospel: that we are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

If you are weighing these deep truths of Scripture, I encourage you to look to Christ Himself. Whether one emphasizes God's sovereign choice or man's responsibility to respond, the call of the Gospel remains the same: "Repent and believe the good news" (Mark 1:15). You can turn to God even now, trusting in the finished work of Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. Have you ever placed your full weight of trust in Him for the forgiveness of your sins?
What does it mean that you are the head and not the tail in Deuteronomy 28:13?

In Deuteronomy 28:13, the phrase "the head and not the tail" serves as a vivid metaphor for a position of leadership, dignity, and divine favor. Within the context of the Mosaic Covenant, God promised the nation of Israel that if they diligently obeyed His commandments, they would occupy a place of preeminence among the nations. To be the "head" means to lead, to have influence, and to be at the forefront of God's visible blessing. Conversely, to be the "tail" signifies being subservient, oppressed, or dragged along by the circumstances of the world (Deuteronomy 28:1; Deuteronomy 28:13; Deuteronomy 28:44).

It is vital to understand that this promise was strictly conditional. The text states that these blessings would follow "if you listen to the commandments of the Lord your God" (Deuteronomy 28:13). While some modern teachers mistakenly use this verse as a universal guarantee of individual wealth or worldly promotion, we must handle the Word of Truth with greater precision. Because every human being has failed to keep God’s law perfectly, the "blessings" of Deuteronomy 28 are technically out of reach for us on our own merit. In fact, the same chapter warns that disobedience leads to being the "tail" (Deuteronomy 28:43-44).

The beauty of the Gospel is that Jesus Christ, the only one who perfectly obeyed the Father, became the "Head" of all things (Colossians 1:18). On the cross, He took the "curses" that we deserved for our disobedience so that we might receive the blessings of God by grace through faith (Galatians 3:13-14). Therefore, for the believer, being "the head" is not necessarily about financial status or social rank, but about our secure standing in Christ. In Him, we are no longer slaves to sin or victims of spiritual darkness, but are seated with Him in heavenly places (Ephesians 2:6).

If you find yourself struggling with a sense of failure or feeling like the "tail" in this world, do not look to your own efforts to climb upward. Instead, look to Jesus. God is holy and His wrath against sin is real, and we have all separated ourselves from Him through our transgressions. However, God in His mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for sinners, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord over all. Salvation is found in Him alone, by grace alone, through faith alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting in His perfect obedience today?

As a practical step in your walk with the Lord, I encourage you to read the book of Galatians this week. It will help you see how Christ fulfilled the Law on your behalf, so you can rest in His favor rather than striving to earn it through your own performance. Seek out a local, Bible-believing church where the Gospel is rightly preached, for we are not meant to follow the Head without being connected to His Body, the church (Colossians 1:18; Hebrews 10:24-25).
What is theistic agnosticism / agnostic theism?

Agnostic theism, often called theistic agnosticism, is the philosophical position that encompasses a belief in the existence of a deity while simultaneously maintaining that the nature, attributes, or even the absolute certainty of that deity’s existence are ultimately unknown or unknowable. It is the marriage of a personal conviction that God exists with an intellectual admission that one lacks the evidence or capacity to "know" Him in a definitive or objective sense.

While this perspective may seem like a humble admission of human limitation, it stands in direct contrast to the historic Christian faith, which is built upon the foundation of divine revelation. As believers, we do not worship an "Unknown God" or peer into a dark void hoping for a glimmer of light; rather, we believe that the Creator of the universe has broken the silence and made Himself known to His creatures.

The Bible teaches that God is indeed "incomprehensible" in the sense that no finite human mind can fully exhaust the depths of His infinite being, but He is not "unknowable." The distinction is vital. We see this through several means:

First, God has revealed Himself generally through the created order. The heavens and the earth act as a mirror of His power and divine nature, leaving humanity without an excuse for total ignorance. This is clearly stated in Romans 1:19–20: "Since what may be known about God is plain to them, because God has made it plain to them. For since the creation of the world God’s invisible qualities—his eternal power and divine nature—have been clearly seen, being understood from what has been made, so that people are without excuse."

Second, God has revealed Himself specially and specifically through the Holy Scriptures. The Bible is not a collection of human guesses about the divine, but the inspired, inerrant, and infallible Word of God. It provides us with a certain and authoritative knowledge of who God is, what He requires of us, and His redemptive plan for the world. In 2 Timothy 3:16–17, we are told that "All Scripture is God-breathed and is useful for teaching, rebuking, correcting and training in righteousness, so that the servant of God may be thoroughly equipped for every good work."

Finally, and most gloriously, God has revealed Himself through the Person of Jesus Christ. If you wish to know what God is like, you must look to the Son. Jesus did not come to give us another philosophy; He came to show us the Father. John 1:18 declares, "No one has ever seen God, but the one and only Son, who is himself God and is in closest relationship with the Father, has made him known."

To remain in a state of agnosticism is to miss the beauty of a personal relationship with the living God. Eternal life is not found in an abstract belief in a distant "something," but in a saving knowledge of the Triune God. As Jesus prayed in John 17:3, "Now this is eternal life: that they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ, whom you have sent."

My dear friend, do not be content with an "unknown" God. The weight of our sin and the reality of our separation from a holy God are too great to leave to guesswork. Because of our rebellion, we deserve His just judgment, but in His great mercy, God sent His Son to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for sinners, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. He has victory over sin and death, and He offers forgiveness to all who will turn from their sin and trust in Him alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. I encourage you to begin reading the Gospel of John in the Bible, asking God to reveal the truth of His Son to you as you read. What is it that makes the idea of a "known" God difficult for you to embrace?
The Fruit of the Holy Spirit – What is kindness?

Kindness is a benevolent disposition of the heart that results in tender, helpful action toward others. In the original language of the New Testament, the word *chrestotes* conveys a sense of moral excellence and usefulness, describing a spirit that is mellow and sweet rather than harsh, sharp, or abrasive. It is a supernatural fruit of the Holy Spirit, which means it is not merely a product of human willpower or a naturally pleasant personality, but a work of **sanctification** as the Spirit conforms the believer to the image of Jesus Christ (Galatians 5:22).

Biblical kindness is rooted in the very character of God and is far more profound than the world's version of "niceness." While niceness often seeks to avoid conflict at the expense of truth, true kindness seeks the highest good of the other person, even when that requires speaking difficult words in love. The Scriptures declare that it is the kindness of God that leads a person to repentance (Romans 2:4). We see this divine kindness perfectly displayed in the person and work of Jesus. When we were lost and dead in our trespasses, "the kindness and love of God our Savior toward man appeared" (Titus 3:4). He saved us, not because of any righteous deeds we had performed, but according to His own mercy (Titus 3:5). In Christ, God has demonstrated the "surpassing riches of His grace in His kindness toward us" (Ephesians 2:7).

This is the glorious heart of the Gospel. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived the perfect life of kindness we could never live. He then took our place on the cross, bearing the full weight of God's just wrath against our sin, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, securing victory over death. He offers you full forgiveness and new life as a gift of sovereign grace. You can turn to God today, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you. What is it that holds you back from resting in His kindness and mercy?

As a follower of Christ, your kindness to others is a practical reflection of the mercy you have personally received. It is an acknowledgment of the **Imago Dei**, the image of God, in every human being. You are commanded to "be kind to one another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another, just as God in Christ also forgave you" (Ephesians 4:32). To grow in this grace, I encourage you to begin your day by reflecting on the cross of Christ. A practical next step is to look for one person today who might be overlooked or even antagonistic toward you, and intentionally offer them a tangible act of service or a word of sincere encouragement, doing so as an act of worship to the Lord who has been so kind to you.
How should Christians view the idea of reparations for slavery?

The question of reparations for the historical sin of slavery is a complex issue that touches on the biblical themes of justice, restitution, and individual responsibility. While the Bible provides clear principles regarding these matters, the specific application of reparations in a modern context is considered a tertiary issue. This means that while it is an important conversation for the church and society, faithful Christians who hold to the authority of Scripture may reach different conclusions on how best to pursue biblical justice in this area.

At the heart of this discussion is the recognition that chattel slavery was a grievous sin and a violation of the image of God in human beings (Genesis 1:26; Exodus 21:16). Christians must never minimize the wickedness of an institution that treated people as property, for God is a God of justice who hates oppression (Proverbs 14:31).

There are generally two primary perspectives that Christians hold when approaching the idea of reparations:

One view emphasizes the biblical principle of restitution. In the Law of Moses, God required that those who caused loss to others make restoration (Exodus 22:1). In the New Testament, we see the beautiful example of Zacchaeus, who, upon being transformed by the grace of Christ, vowed to restore fourfold anything he had taken by fraud (Luke 19:8). Proponents of this view argue that because the systemic effects of slavery have caused ongoing economic and social harm to generations of descendants, and because institutions often profited from that labor, there is a moral and corporate obligation to seek ways to "make right" what was made wrong. They may point to instances where God dealt with Israel or other nations for corporate sins committed by their ancestors (2 Samuel 21:1).

A second view emphasizes the biblical principle of individual responsibility. This perspective highlights passages such as Ezekiel 18:20, which states that the son shall not bear the guilt of the father, nor the father bear the guilt of the son. From this vantage point, some Christians argue that restitution is a specific command for an identifiable perpetrator to pay an identifiable victim. They express concern that modern reparations could involve penalizing those who did not personally commit the sin of slavery to pay those who did not personally experience it. They suggest that justice is better served by ensuring equal treatment under the law and practicing radical, neighborly love in the present day (Leviticus 19:18).

The reason these differences exist among believers is often a matter of how one balances corporate versus individual responsibility and how one applies ancient civil laws to a modern, secular state. Regardless of where a Christian lands on the specific policy of reparations, the call for the believer is clear: we are to do justice, love mercy, and walk humbly with God (Micah 6:8).

As an actionable step, you might consider engaging with your local church or community to identify current areas of injustice or need. Seeking to serve those who are marginalized or suffering in your own neighborhood is a tangible way to live out the heart of the Gospel.

Ultimately, while we strive for justice on earth, we must recognize that no amount of silver or gold can truly heal the deep wounds of sin or the stains of the past. True and lasting reconciliation is found only in the person and work of Jesus Christ. Because of our sin, we were all debtors to God, unable to pay a price we owed. In His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus, who lived a perfect life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the debt for all who believe (2 Corinthians 5:21). He rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to find total forgiveness and a peace that surpasses understanding. Have you ever considered how the perfect justice of Christ's cross addresses the deep brokenness of our world?
Who are we to pray to, the Father, the Son, or the Holy Spirit?

We pray to the one Triune God, who has revealed Himself as Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. While we may rightly address any person of the Trinity because each is fully and eternally God, the primary pattern established in Holy Scripture is to pray to the Father, through the mediation of the Son, and by the enabling power of the Holy Spirit.

Jesus Christ, our Great High Priest, instructed His disciples to pray, "Our Father in heaven, hallowed be Your name" (Matthew 6:9). This reflects our adoption as children of God, a privilege secured for us by the Lord Jesus. We are told that "there is one God, and there is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus" (1 Timothy 2:5). Therefore, we approach the throne of grace with confidence because we come in the name and merit of the Son (John 16:23-24).

The Holy Spirit is also vital to our communion with God, serving as our Helper in prayer. He "helps us in our weakness" and "intercedes for us with groanings too deep for words" (Romans 8:26). While the New Testament predominantly shows believers addressing the Father, we also see instances of prayer directed to the Son. For example, as he was being stoned, Stephen cried out, "Lord Jesus, receive my spirit" (Acts 7:59). Because the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit are one in essence and being, to pray to one is to communicate with the Triune Godhead.

If you find yourself struggling to find the right words, remember that prayer is not about the eloquence of your speech but the posture of a heart trusting in the finished work of Christ. I encourage you to set aside time today to speak to your Father in heaven, thanking Him specifically for the gift of His Son and asking the Holy Spirit to guide your heart into all truth.

The door to this intimate fellowship is open to all who turn from their sin and place their full trust in Jesus Christ, who died for our sins and rose again for our justification. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Why does God hate sin?

God’s hatred of sin is the necessary and righteous reaction of His perfect holiness against everything that contradicts His nature. Because God is inherently good, holy, and just, He must oppose that which is evil, unholy, and destructive. His hatred is not a fit of human temper, but a settled, judicial opposition to anything that violates His moral law and defaces His creation.

There are three primary reasons why the Scriptures present God’s posture toward sin as one of holy antagonism:

**First, sin is a direct assault on God’s character.** 
God is the fountain of all holiness, and sin is the antithesis of who He is. The prophet Habakkuk describes God by saying, "Your eyes are too pure to look on evil; You cannot tolerate wrongdoing" (Habakkuk 1:13). Because God is light, He cannot have fellowship with darkness (1 John 1:5). Every act of sin is an act of "lawlessness" (1 John 3:4), a rebellion that attempts to pull the Creator off His throne and install the creature in His place.

**Second, sin is destructive to the humanity God loves.** 
Just as a loving doctor hates the cancer that ravages a patient, or a father hates the addiction that destroys his child, God hates sin because of what it does to us. Sin brings death, corruption, and misery into a world that was created to be "very good." It separates us from the only Source of life and joy. Isaiah 59:2 explains this separation: "But your iniquities have built a barrier between you and your God, and your sins have hidden His face from you, so that He will not hear."

**Third, God is a perfectly just Judge.** 
A judge who does not hate crime and seek to punish it is not a good judge. Because God is perfectly just, He cannot simply overlook "iniquity, rebellion, and sin" (Exodus 34:7). His justice requires that every sin be accounted for, because "the wages of sin is death" (Romans 6:23).

The most profound demonstration of God’s hatred for sin is actually found in the Cross of Jesus Christ. At Calvary, we see that God’s hatred for sin and His love for sinners met in a single moment of history. Because God is holy and just, sin had to be punished; because He is gracious and loving, He provided the Substitute. Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, took on human nature, lived a perfect life of obedience, and died a substitutionary death. On the cross, He bore the wrath of God that we deserved, satisfying the demands of divine justice so that we might be forgiven.

The Gospel is the good news that Christ's historical death, burial, and bodily resurrection on the third day have achieved victory over sin and death. Through His sacrifice, God remains "just and the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus" (Romans 3:26). 

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the full forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What will we look like in Heaven?

The Bible teaches that your future state in the presence of God will not be a shadowy or ghostly existence, but a physical, resurrected life in a body perfectly suited for eternity. We are promised that our lowly bodies will be transformed to be like the glorious body of our Lord Jesus Christ (Philippians 3:20-21).

When we consider what we will look like, we look to the resurrection of Jesus as our pattern. While we do not yet know every detail of our future appearance, the Scripture provides four specific descriptions of the resurrected body in 1 Corinthians 15:42-44.

First, the body is sown in corruption but raised in incorruption. This means your new body will be imperishable, it will never age, never grow weary, and never suffer the decay of disease or death (1 Corinthians 15:42).

Second, it is sown in dishonor but raised in glory. In this life, our bodies are often marked by the scars of sin and the weaknesses of a fallen world, but in the resurrection, they will reflect the radiant beauty and holiness of the Lord (1 Corinthians 15:43).

Third, it is sown in weakness but raised in power. We will no longer be limited by the physical exhaustion or the fragility we experience now, but we will possess a vitality that never fades (1 Corinthians 15:43).

Fourth, it is sown a natural body but raised a spiritual body. This does not mean we will be "spirit" rather than "matter," but rather that our physical bodies will be perfectly directed and empowered by the Holy Spirit, no longer prone to sinful desires or earthly limitations (1 Corinthians 15:44).

Regarding our personal appearance, faithful Christians have often discussed whether we will appear at a certain "ideal" age, but the Bible does not explicitly state this. However, it is clear that there is a continuity of identity. Just as Jesus was recognizable to His disciples after His resurrection (John 20:20), you will still be "you," yet perfected and free from all the effects of the Fall. We shall be like Him, for we shall see Him as He is (1 John 3:2).

This glorious hope is only possible because of the work of Jesus Christ. God is perfectly holy and just, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23), earning only separation and death. In His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who took on human flesh, lived a perfectly sinless life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death and His lordship over all creation. Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works of our own (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for the forgiveness of sins and the promise of this eternal, glorified life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is sola scriptura?

Sola scriptura is the biblical conviction that the Holy Scriptures are the sole infallible rule of faith and practice for the believer and the church. This means that while God has given us teachers, church history, and reason to assist our understanding, the Bible alone stands as the final and supreme authority by which all doctrines, traditions, and human opinions must be measured.

The doctrine of sola scriptura rests on the inspiration and sufficiency of the Word of God. Because the Bible is "God-breathed," it carries the very weight of His divine character and cannot fail. 2 Timothy 3:16-17 states, "All Scripture is God-breathed and is useful for teaching, rebuking, correcting and training in righteousness, so that the servant of God may be thoroughly equipped for every good work." If the Scriptures are able to equip the believer for *every* good work, then nothing else is required as a primary authority for our spiritual life.

This principle guards the church from being led astray by human traditions that might contradict the heart of God. Our Lord Jesus rebuked the religious leaders of His day for this very reason in Matthew 15:6, saying, "Thus you nullify the word of God for the sake of your tradition." To follow sola scriptura is to follow the example of the Bereans in the book of Acts, who were commended because they "examined the Scriptures every day to see if what Paul said was true" (Acts 17:11).

At its heart, this doctrine is meant to lead us to the Person of Jesus Christ. The Bible is not a mere rulebook, but a finished revelation of God’s plan to redeem a fallen world through His Son. John 20:31 tells us, "But these are written that you may believe that Jesus is the Messiah, the Son of God, and that by believing you may have life in his name."

The Gospel revealed in these Scriptures is simple and profound: God is holy and just, yet we have all rebelled against Him through sin. Because of His great love, Jesus Christ—who is truly God and truly man—took on human flesh, lived a perfect life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death. Salvation is a gift of grace alone, received through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and eternal life.

As a practical next step in honoring the authority of the Word, I encourage you to establish a daily rhythm of reading the Bible, perhaps starting with the Gospel of John, and to join a local church that faithfully preaches the Scriptures as the final authority in all matters. What holds you back from trusting in the sufficiency of God's Word today?
What did Jesus mean when He said He would give us “whatever you ask in my name”?

When Jesus promised to give us whatever we ask in His name, He was not issuing a blank check for our personal desires or selfish whims, but was rather inviting us to pray in alignment with His character, His mission, and His authority. To pray in the name of Jesus is to ask for what He would ask for, seeking the advancement of His kingdom and the glory of the Father through the mediation of the Son.

In the biblical context, a person’s name represents their entire being, their reputation, and their legal authority. Just as an ambassador speaks in the name of a king, a believer prays in the name of Jesus by standing on His merits rather than their own. This is the heart of Christ's intercessory work, where He grants us access to the Father’s throne because of His perfect life, sacrificial death, and bodily resurrection. Jesus explains this purpose clearly when He says, "Whatever you ask in My name, I will do it, so that the Father may be glorified in the Son" in John 14:13.

The promise is inseparable from a life of obedience and abiding in Him. When we remain in His Word, our desires begin to mirror His desires. This is why the Apostle John later clarified this teaching by writing, "And this is the confidence that we have before Him: If we ask anything according to His will, He hears us" in 1 John 5:14. To ask in His name is to submit our will to His, trusting that He knows what is best for His glory and our ultimate sanctification.

Practically, this means we should examine our prayers to see if they reflect the heart of Christ. If you are struggling with unanswered prayer, take a moment to bring those requests before the Lord in light of His Word. Ask yourself if the fulfillment of your request would bring honor to the person of Jesus and further His work in the world.

This intimate access to the Father is a gift of grace reserved for those who belong to Christ. Because of our sin, we were once separated from God and under His just judgment, but God, in His great mercy, sent His Son to take the punishment we deserved. Jesus Christ lived perfectly, died for our sins, and rose again on the third day, conquering death so that we might have eternal life. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What is holding you back from fully trusting Him with your life and your prayers today?
What does the Bible say about witchcraft? Should a Christian fear witchcraft?

The Bible teaches that witchcraft is a direct act of rebellion against the sovereign authority of God. It is consistently identified as an abomination because it seeks to bypass the Creator to obtain power, protection, or hidden knowledge through spiritual sources that are not from Him. In the Old Testament, the Lord gave clear commands to His people to remain holy and separate from such practices. Deuteronomy 18:10–12 states, "Let no one be found among you who sacrifices his son or daughter in the fire, who practices divination or sorcery, interprets omens, engages in witchcraft, or casts spells, or who is a medium or spiritist or who consults the dead. Anyone who does these things is detestable to the Lord."

This prohibition is not merely an ancient cultural rule, but a moral boundary rooted in God’s character. To engage in the occult is to commit spiritual adultery, turning away from the living God to seek help from fallen spirits or human effort. The New Testament maintains this stance, listing sorcery among the "works of the flesh" in Galatians 5:19–21 and warning in Revelation 21:8 that those who practice it without repentance will face the second death. Scripture treats these forces as real and dangerous, yet entirely subordinate to the power of the Almighty.

A child of God should not live in fear of witchcraft, curses, or demonic influence. While the reality of spiritual warfare is acknowledged in the Bible, the victory of Jesus Christ is the far greater reality. By His death on the cross and His bodily resurrection, Jesus "disarmed the powers and authorities" and "made a public spectacle of them, triumphing over them by the cross" (Colossians 2:15). If you belong to Christ, you are under His sovereign protection and the indwelling of the Holy Spirit. 1 John 4:4 provides a firm foundation for your peace, saying, "You, dear children, are from God and have overcome them, because the one who is in you is greater than the one who is in the world."

Fear is often the result of looking at the shadows rather than looking at the Savior. There is no spell, no hex, and no dark power that can snatch a believer out of the hand of the Father (John 10:28–29). As a practical step in your discipleship, I encourage you to put on the full armor of God daily by immersing yourself in the Scriptures and remaining active in a local, Bible-believing church (Ephesians 6:10–18). When you feel the creeping cold of fear, turn your heart to God in prayer, acknowledging His absolute lordship over all creation.

If you find yourself troubled by past involvement in these things or a present fear of the dark, remember that the Gospel is the power of God for salvation. We have all rebelled against God in various ways, but Christ took the penalty for our sins upon Himself. His blood cleanses us from all unrighteousness. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to find perfect security and new life. Is there anything in your life today that makes it difficult for you to trust in the sufficiency of Christ’s protection?
Why am I here?

You are here because you were intentionally created by a sovereign and loving God to know Him, to reflect His character, and to find your ultimate satisfaction in Him. Your life is not a cosmic accident or a product of blind chance, but the result of a divine and deliberate purpose.

The Scriptures reveal that God created all things for His own glory (Isaiah 43:7; Revelation 4:11). To glorify God means to recognize His supreme worth and to live in a way that makes His excellence known to the world. The Apostle Paul explains that all things were created through Jesus Christ and for Him (Colossians 1:16). Therefore, you will only find your true identity and reason for being when you are in a right relationship with your Creator.

However, we all experience a deep restlessness because our relationship with God has been fractured by sin. Instead of living for God’s glory, we often seek our own path and look for meaning in things that can never truly satisfy us (Romans 3:23; Jeremiah 2:13). This separation from God is the root of our search for purpose.

The wonderful news of the Gospel is that God did not leave us in our separation. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live a perfect life on our behalf and to die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. His bodily resurrection from the dead on the third day confirms His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Through faith in Christ alone, you are reconciled to God, and your original purpose is restored. You are invited to move from spiritual darkness into the marvelous light of His presence (1 Peter 2:9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to begin reading the Gospel of John. It was written so that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that by believing you may have life in His name (John 20:31). As you read, speak to God directly in prayer, asking Him to reveal His truth to your heart and to guide you into a local church where the Word of God is faithfully preached.
Did Adam and Eve have belly buttons / navels?

Based on the historical account of creation in the Bible, it is most consistent to conclude that Adam and Eve did not have belly buttons. A navel is the physical mark left by the umbilical cord that connects a developing baby to its mother in the womb; however, Adam and Eve were created directly by God and did not have biological mothers.

The Scriptures state as historical fact that "the LORD God formed man from the dust of the ground and breathed into his nostrils the breath of life, and man became a living being" (Genesis 2:7). Soon after, God caused a deep sleep to fall upon Adam and took one of his ribs to fashion the first woman (Genesis 2:21-22). Because they were the immediate handiwork of the Creator rather than the product of natural birth, they would have had no need for an umbilical cord or the resulting scar of a navel.

While this may seem like a minor anatomical detail, it serves as a beautiful reminder of the uniqueness of our first parents and the intentionality of God’s creative power. Everything God made in that first week was "very good" (Genesis 1:31). Although they were created in a state of maturity and perfection, Adam and Eve eventually chose to rebel against God’s clear command, bringing the curse of sin and death upon all humanity (Romans 5:12).

This history is essential because it sets the stage for the Gospel. Because we are all descendants of Adam, we inherit a nature that is separated from God by sin. However, God in His mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ, the "last Adam," to undo the work of the first (1 Corinthians 15:45). Jesus, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death. 

Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works of our own. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As you reflect on the wonder of how God formed the first humans, I encourage you to read through the first three chapters of Genesis this week. It is a vital foundation for understanding who we are and why we need a Savior. You might also consider speaking with a pastor at a local, Bible-believing church to grow in your understanding of these foundational truths.
What is an appropriate level of physical intimacy before marriage?

God created human sexuality as a profound gift to be enjoyed exclusively within the covenant of a marriage between one man and one woman. The biblical standard for physical intimacy before marriage is characterized by the call to flee sexual immorality and to maintain absolute purity in both body and heart. 1 Corinthians 6:18; 1 Thessalonians 4:3–5.

In the eyes of our Holy God, the appropriate level of intimacy is not found by seeing how close one can get to the edge of a cliff without falling, but by honoring Christ through self-control and respect for His design. Scripture teaches that the marriage bed is to be kept undefiled. Hebrews 13:4. Therefore, any physical action that is intended to arouse sexual desire or that mimics the sexual act belongs only within the safety of the marriage bond. 

We must remember that our bodies are not our own, for they were bought with the precious blood of Christ. 1 Corinthians 6:19–20. This is a matter of the heart as much as the hands. Jesus warned that to look at another person with lustful intent is already to have committed adultery in the heart. Matthew 5:28. If an activity stirs up passions that cannot be righteously satisfied until the wedding night, it acts as a snare to your soul and a dishonor to your partner. True love does not seek its own gratification but seeks the holiness and well-being of the other.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to establish clear, non-negotiable boundaries with your partner during a time of prayerful reflection, rather than waiting until you are in a moment of temptation. Avoid isolated or unobserved settings that make it easy to compromise your convictions. Seek the wisdom and accountability of your local church elders or a mature Christian couple who can walk alongside you as you prepare for a God-honoring marriage.

If you find yourself struggling with guilt from the past or the weight of current temptation, there is profound hope and cleansing in the Gospel. We have all fallen short of God’s holy standard, but Jesus Christ lived a perfectly pure life on our behalf and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. He rose bodily from the grave, demonstrating His victory over sin and death. You can turn to Him now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for forgiveness and the grace to live a transformed life. What is holding you back from fully surrendering your desires and your relationship to His Lordship today?
What are the unfruitful works of darkness (Ephesians 5:11)?

The unfruitful works of darkness are those sinful actions, motives, and words that characterize a life lived in rebellion against God and apart from His transforming grace. These works are called "unfruitful" because, while they may offer fleeting pleasure, they produce nothing of lasting value, leading only to spiritual barrenness, shame, and eternal death (Romans 6:21).

In the immediate context of his letter to the Ephesians, the Apostle Paul identifies specific examples of these works. They include sexual immorality, impurity of any kind, and greed (Ephesians 5:3). He also points to sins of the tongue, such as filthiness, foolish talk, and crude joking, which are entirely out of place for those who have been redeemed (Ephesians 5:4). These behaviors belong to the "old man"—the corrupt nature we inherited from Adam—and stand in total opposition to the fruit of the light, which consists of all goodness, righteousness, and truth (Ephesians 5:9).

To take no part in these works means that the believer must not only abstain from committing such sins but must also refuse to tolerate, excuse, or find entertainment in them. We are commanded to "expose" them. This exposure happens primarily through the contrast of a holy life. When a Christian walks in obedience and purity, the light of Christ shining through them reveals the true, ugly nature of the world’s hidden sins.

This call to holiness is grounded in the reality of God’s character and His judgment. Scripture warns that the wrath of God comes upon the sons of disobedience because of these very things (Ephesians 5:6). Because God is perfectly holy and dwells in unapproachable light, no one who persists in the darkness of unrepentant sin can have an inheritance in the kingdom of Christ and God (Ephesians 5:5).

However, the Gospel provides the only way of escape from this darkness. We were all once in darkness, dead in our trespasses and sins (Ephesians 2:1). But God, being rich in mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to take on human nature, live a sinless life, and die a substitutionary death on the cross to satisfy the righteous wrath of God against our sin. By His bodily resurrection on the third day, Jesus Christ triumphed over the power of death and the devil. Salvation is a gift of grace alone, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works of our own (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him and walking in His light today?

As a practical next step in your walk of faith, I encourage you to read through the fifth chapter of Ephesians daily this week. Ask the Holy Spirit to search your heart and reveal any "hidden" works of darkness—perhaps in your private thoughts, your speech, or your media choices—and then take those things to a trusted elder or mature believer in your local church for prayer and accountability (James 5:16).
How can an unmarried person relieve sexual tension in a non-sinful way?

The struggle with sexual desire while unmarried is a profound trial, yet it is one that God, in His providence, uses to refine your faith and cast you more fully upon His grace. To answer your question directly: Holy Scripture does not provide a physical "release valve" for sexual tension outside of the marriage covenant. Instead, the Word of God calls the believer to the exercise of self-control, the mortification of lustful desires, and the redirection of one’s energies toward the glory of God.

The biblical design for sexual intimacy is exclusive to the union of one man and one woman in marriage, as noted in Hebrews 13:4. Outside of this context, any attempt to seek sexual gratification—whether through physical acts or the entertainment of lustful thoughts—strays from God’s holy standard. Our Lord Jesus warned that to look at another with lustful intent is already to have committed adultery in the heart, as recorded in Matthew 5:28. Therefore, the "relief" the world offers through self-gratification is a deceptive path that often fuels the very fire it claims to extinguish, leading one further into a cycle of guilt rather than the peace of a clear conscience.

The path forward is not found in seeking a way to satisfy the tension, but in learning to master the body through the power of the Holy Spirit. Here are several practical and biblical ways to navigate this season:

First, recognize that your body is not your own; it is a temple of the Holy Spirit, bought with the precious blood of Christ, as stated in 1 Corinthians 6:19–20. When tension arises, treat it as a signal to engage in spiritual discipline rather than a demand for physical indulgence.

Second, follow the example of Joseph and "flee" youthful passions, as commanded in 2 Timothy 2:22 and 1 Corinthians 6:18. This means removing yourself from environments, media, or situations that provoke these desires. If you find yourself dwelling on impure thoughts, immediately redirect your mind to things that are true, honorable, and pure, as exhorted in Philippians 4:8.

Third, employ "body discipline." The Apostle Paul spoke of discipline, saying, "I discipline my body and keep it under control," in 1 Corinthians 9:27. Physical exercise, rigorous work, or the pursuit of a demanding hobby can be helpful means of redirecting the physical energy that often manifests as sexual tension.

Fourth, do not walk this path in isolation. The Christian life was never meant to be a solo performance. Seek the fellowship of the local church and find accountability with a mature believer of the same gender. We are told to "bear one another’s burdens, and so fulfill the law of Christ," in Galatians 6:2.

Finally, remember that your ultimate satisfaction is found in Christ alone. Sexual desire is a shadow of a much deeper longing for communion with God. In times of intense struggle, go to Him in prayer, confessing your weakness and asking for the fruit of the Spirit, which includes self-control, as listed in Galatians 5:22–23.

If you have fallen into sin in this area, do not despair or hide from God. The Gospel declares that Christ died for our sins and rose again to give us new life. If we confess our sins, He is faithful and just to forgive us and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness, according to 1 John 1:9. You can turn to Him right now, trusting in His finished work on the cross for your justification and relying on His Spirit for your ongoing sanctification. What is the greatest obstacle you face today in trusting that God’s grace is sufficient for this particular struggle?
What does it mean to be devout?

To be devout is to possess a heart that is sincerely and wholeheartedly surrendered to God, characterized by a deep reverence for His holiness and a consistent commitment to His will. It is not merely the outward performance of religious rituals, but an inward posture of piety where the love of Christ constrains and directs every aspect of a person’s life.

In the Scriptures, we see this illustrated in the life of Simeon, who is described as righteous and devout, waiting for the consolation of Israel, and the Holy Spirit was upon him (Luke 2:25). Similarly, the early church was marked by this quality as they devoted themselves to the apostles' teaching, to fellowship, to the breaking of bread, and to prayer (Acts 2:42). To be devout means that God is the central gravity of your soul, and His Word is the final authority for your conduct.

True devotion is the fruit of progressive sanctification, where the believer, having been justified by grace alone, grows in the knowledge and likeness of Jesus Christ. It involves several key elements:

First, it requires a love for God’s Word. A devout person does not merely read the Bible as a textbook but treasures it as the very breath of God, seeking to hide its truths in their heart so they might not sin against Him (Psalm 119:11).

Second, it involves a life of prayer. This is not a formal or legalistic obligation, but a vital breath of the soul. The devout person recognizes their total dependence on the Father and seeks His face continually, as seen in the command to pray without ceasing (1 Thessalonians 5:17).

Third, it manifests in a pursuit of holiness. Because God is holy, the devout person seeks to be holy in all their conduct, turning away from the world's idols to serve the living and true God (1 Peter 1:15; 1 Thessalonians 1:9).

It is important to remember that being devout is never the cause of our salvation, but the evidence of it. We are not accepted by God because of the intensity of our devotion; we are accepted because of the perfection of Christ’s finished work on the cross. Our devotion is our "reasonable service" in response to the great mercies of God (Romans 12:1).

If you find your heart cold or your devotion waning, I encourage you to begin a daily rhythm of reading the Scriptures and speaking honestly with God in prayer. Seek out a local, faithful church where the Word is preached clearly, for we are not meant to follow Christ in isolation. 

The greatest tragedy is to have a form of godliness but to deny its power. True devotion starts with a right relationship with God through His Son. This begins when a person recognizes their sin and separation from a holy God and turns to Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for sinners, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What is it that holds you back from fully trusting Him today?
Are we supposed to use musical instruments in church?

The use of musical instruments in the church is a matter that touches on how we honor God in our corporate worship. Throughout the history of the Church, believers have sought to worship God in spirit and in truth, as Christ commanded in John 4:23-24. Whether we use instruments is an area where faithful Christians have held different perspectives, and it is helpful to look at what the Word of God reveals to us.

In the Old Testament, musical instruments were not only permitted but commanded for use in the Tabernacle and the Temple. King David, under the inspiration of the Spirit, appointed musicians and singers to praise the Lord continually. The Book of Psalms, which served as the songbook for Israel and the early Church, concludes with a resounding command in Psalm 150:3-5 to praise God with the trumpet, harp, lyre, tambourine, strings, flute, and clashing cymbals.

When we turn to the New Testament, the emphasis shifts to the "fruit of lips" and the internal melody of the heart. The Apostle Paul instructs the church to speak to one another in psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs, singing and making melody in your heart to the Lord (Ephesians 5:19; Colossians 3:16). While the New Testament does not give an explicit command to use or to forbid instruments in the local assembly, we see a glimpse of heavenly worship in the book of Revelation, where the elders and the redeemed are described as holding harps as they sing the song of the Lamb (Revelation 5:8; Revelation 14:2-3).

Because of these different emphases, this is considered a secondary or tertiary issue where God’s people have reached different conclusions based on their understanding of the Regulative Principle of Worship, which seeks to ensure that only what God has commanded is practiced in worship.

*   **The A Cappella Tradition:** Some traditions believe that since the New Testament specifically mentions singing but is silent regarding instruments in the church, worship should consist only of the human voice. They argue this keeps the focus entirely on the inspired Word of God and ensures that nothing distracts from the congregation's participation.
*   **The Instrumental Tradition:** Most Christians believe that musical instruments are a gift of God’s common grace. In this view, instruments serve as a humble servant to the singing, providing a foundation that helps the congregation sing with unity, order, and joyful expression, echoing the commands found in the Psalms.

In a practical sense, the primary instrument in any Christian gathering is the voice of the people of God. Instruments should never overshadow the lyrics or turn worship into a mere performance. Their purpose is to support the clear proclamation of the Gospel through song.

As you reflect on this, I encourage you to prioritize the heart behind the music. Whether a church uses a full orchestra or no instruments at all, the vital question is whether the congregation is singing the truths of Scripture with thankfulness to God. If you have questions about the practice of your own local church, a wonderful next step would be to ask your pastors or elders about the biblical convictions that guide their choice of music.

The ultimate goal of all our singing is to exalt the Lord Jesus Christ. We must remember that we can only approach God in worship because of what Christ has done for us. God is perfectly holy, and we are all separated from Him by our sin and rebellion. However, in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. He lived a perfect life of obedience, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death. He is now exalted as Lord over all. Salvation is a gift of grace alone, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by our own works. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and eternal life. What is currently your greatest hope for being right with God?
What does the Bible say about miscarriage?

The Bible teaches that every life conceived in the womb is a person created by God and known by Him intimately before birth. While the Scriptures do not use the specific modern word "miscarriage," they speak directly to the value of the unborn, the reality of death in a fallen world, and the certain hope of God’s comfort for those who grieve.

The foundation of our understanding is that God is the Author of life. The Psalmist writes, "For You created my inmost being; You knit me together in my mother’s womb," and "Your eyes saw my unformed body; all the days ordained for me were written in Your book before one of them came to be" in Psalm 139:13; Psalm 139:16. Because God is the one who fashions the child, a miscarriage is the loss of a distinct human being who was made in the image of God.
