We must also recognize that we live in a world fractured by the Fall. The entry of sin into human history brought about decay, sickness, and death. The Apostle Paul explains that "the whole creation has been groaning together in the pains of childbirth until the present time" in Romans 8:22. Miscarriage is a painful manifestation of this brokenness. It is vital to understand that a pregnancy loss is generally a result of this fallen condition of the world and not a specific judgment for a particular sin committed by the parents.

In the midst of such deep sorrow, the Bible reveals the character of God as a compassionate Father. "The LORD is near to the brokenhearted; He saves those crushed in spirit" in Psalm 34:18. He is the "God of all comfort, who comforts us in all our troubles" in 2 Corinthians 1:3-4. You are permitted to lament and cry out to Him, for He is not distant from your pain.

Many grieving parents find great hope in the account of King David’s loss of his infant son. David expressed a confident hope in a future reunion, saying, "I will go to him, but he will not return to me" in 2 Samuel 12:23. While the Bible does not provide an exhaustive systematic treatise on the eternal state of those who die in infancy, we rest in the perfect justice and mercy of God. We believe that God, who is rich in mercy, receives these little ones into His presence through the grace of Christ.

The ultimate answer to the sting of death is found in the Gospel. Jesus Christ, the Son of God, became man and suffered the full weight of our brokenness and sin. He died on the cross as a substitute for sinners and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over death. He promises that "He will wipe away every tear from their eyes, and there will be no more death or mourning or crying or pain" in Revelation 21:4.

If you are walking through this dark valley, I encourage you to pour out your heart to God in prayer, for He hears the cries of His children. Lean upon the fellowship of a local church where elders and brothers and sisters in Christ can bear this burden with you. 

The greatest comfort of all is to know the One who has conquered the grave. If you have not yet placed your life in His hands, you can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and the hope of eternal life. What is your greatest struggle in trusting God's goodness during this season of loss?
Is religion the cause of most wars?

Contrary to popular belief, historical data and the Holy Scriptures reveal that religion is not the primary cause of most wars. While critics often claim that faith is the root of global bloodshed, historical surveys, such as the Encyclopedia of Wars, indicate that less than seven percent of recorded conflicts have had a primarily religious motivation. Most wars throughout history have been fueled by territorial expansion, ethnic tensions, political power, or the acquisition of resources.

The Bible provides a deeper, more sobering diagnosis of human conflict. The Apostle James asks, "What causes conflicts and quarrels among you? Don't they come from the passions that war within you? You want something and do not have it; you murder and covet, but you cannot obtain what you want" (James 4:1–2). Here we see that war is not an issue of outward piety, but of inward sin. It is the result of the fall of man and what we understand as total depravity, where every part of human nature is corrupted by a rebellious desire to be our own god.

When people use the name of Christ to justify violence or hatred, they are acting in direct contradiction to His commands and His character. Jesus told Peter to put away his sword, warning that those who live by the sword will die by the sword (Matthew 26:52). He taught that His kingdom is not of this world (John 18:36), and His followers are called to be peacemakers. To use the Gospel as a pretext for war is a grave sin and a violation of the Third Commandment, which forbids taking the name of the Lord in vain (Exodus 20:7).

The greatest war is not between nations, but between a holy God and sinful humanity. Our rebellion and sin separate us from Him, deserving His just judgment. Yet, in His infinite mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to be the mediator. Jesus took on human nature, lived a perfect life of obedience, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. Through His blood, He has made peace between God and man (Colossians 1:20). 

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive true forgiveness and eternal peace. What holds you back from trusting Him today? 

As a practical next step, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John to see the heart of the Prince of Peace. Seek out a local, Bible-preaching church where you can hear the Word of God and find fellowship with those who have been reconciled to God through Christ. For your own study, consider the following passages: Romans 3:23; Romans 5:1; 2 Corinthians 5:18–19; Ephesians 2:14–16.
What does it mean to “in all your ways acknowledge Him” in Proverbs 3:6?

To acknowledge God in all your ways means to recognize His absolute Lordship and sovereign presence in every dimension of your life, making His glory the primary aim of your heart and actions. It is far more than a casual nod toward heaven or an intellectual agreement that God exists. In the original language, the word for acknowledge implies an intimate, experiential knowledge that results in total submission to His will.

This command calls for the end of the "secular versus sacred" divide in the life of a believer. It means that God is not merely the Lord of your Sunday mornings or your private devotions, but the Lord of your business transactions, your parenting, your recreation, and your secret thoughts. When we acknowledge Him, we are practicing the doctrine of Divine Providence, which is the biblical truth that God is purposefully and powerfully sovereign over all His creatures and all their actions.

The context of this passage is vital, as it commands us to trust in the LORD with all our heart and not to lean on our own understanding (Proverbs 3:5). To lean on our own understanding is to act as if we are the masters of our own fate, whereas to acknowledge Him is to seek His counsel and submit to His Word in every decision. It is an unreserved surrender that says, "Not my will, but Yours be done." Whether you are eating or drinking, or whatever you do, you are to do all to the glory of God (1 Corinthians 10:31).

When we live this way, Scripture promises that "He will make your paths straight" (Proverbs 3:6). This does not mean that the Christian life will be free from difficulty, but rather that God will direct your life toward His holy purposes and provide the clarity and strength needed to walk in His ways.

As a practical step of discipleship this week, I encourage you to choose one specific area where you have struggled to include God, perhaps in a difficult relationship or a financial worry. Before you take any action in that area, pause to pray, "Lord, I recognize You are the Sovereign over this situation. I submit my desires to Your Word and ask You to guide my steps for Your glory."

Ultimately, we cannot perfectly acknowledge God in all our ways because of our fallen nature. We all fall short and seek our own paths. This is why we need the Gospel. Jesus Christ, the only one who perfectly acknowledged the Father in every way, lived the life we could not live and died the death we deserved for our rebellion. He rose again to secure our justification and to give us His Holy Spirit, who empowers us to walk in obedience.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. He is the only way to the Father and the only one who can truly make your paths straight (John 14:6; Romans 10:9). What is it that holds you back from trusting Him with every part of your life today?
Does God still give visions to people today? Should believers expect visions to be a part of their Christian experience?

The Living God is sovereign and free to communicate with His creatures however He chooses, yet He has graciously given us a "more sure word" in the Holy Scriptures to guide our feet. While God is certainly capable of granting a vision to a person today, we must distinguish between the extraordinary acts of God’s providence and the ordinary way He has commanded us to seek His will.

The primary and sufficient way God speaks to His people in this age is through the Bible. We are told in Hebrews 1:1–2 that although God spoke long ago to the fathers by the prophets in many portions and in many ways, in these last days He has spoken to us in His Son. Because the canon of Scripture is complete, the Bible serves as our final and absolute authority for faith and practice. Any experience, including a vision, must be tested against the truth of the Word. If a vision contradicts the Bible, it is false; if it merely repeats the Bible, it is unnecessary for doctrine; and if it adds new revelation to the Bible, it violates the sufficiency of God's Word as described in 2 Timothy 3:16–17.

Whether a believer should expect visions is a matter where faithful Christians hold differing perspectives based on their understanding of the transition from the Apostolic age to the present:

*   Many Christians, particularly in the Reformed tradition, hold to a view called cessationism. This view suggests that miraculous sign gifts and direct revelatory visions were specifically given to the Apostles to lay the foundation of the Church and authenticate their message. Now that the foundation is laid and the New Testament is complete, these extraordinary means have ceased, and we are called to walk by faith in the written Word.
*   Other faithful believers, often described as continuationists, believe that God may still grant visions, particularly for the purpose of personal encouragement, specific direction in ministry, or as a means of reaching people in places where the Gospel is not yet known. They maintain, however, that such experiences are subjective and must never be placed on the same level as the objective authority of the Bible.

Regardless of these views, the Christian life is fundamentally a walk of faith and not of sight, as written in 2 Corinthians 5:7. We are not commanded to seek after signs or supernatural spectacles, but to seek Christ through the means of grace He has provided: the preaching of the Word, prayer, the sacraments, and the fellowship of the saints. 

If you or someone you know believes they have experienced a vision, the most faithful next step is to bring that experience to the light of Scripture. Does it exalt the Lord Jesus Christ? Does it align perfectly with the moral and doctrinal teachings of the Bible? I encourage you to sit with the elders of your local church to discern these matters together, for there is safety in the multitude of counselors.

Ultimately, the most important "vision" any human being can have is a spiritual one: seeing the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ. We are all born separated from God by our sin, but God, being rich in mercy, sent His Son to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. Salvation is found by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. I invite you to turn from your sin and trust in Jesus today, for in Him is the fullness of life and the only revelation we need for eternal peace. What is it that might be keeping you from fully resting in the sufficiency of Christ and His Word?
Is physical abuse an acceptable reason for divorce?

If you are currently in a situation where your physical safety or the safety of your children is at risk, please immediately contact local emergency services or a domestic violence hotline. Your life is an invaluable gift from God, and seeking safety is not a lack of faith, but a wise stewardship of the life He has given you.

Physical abuse is a grievous sin that violates the very essence of the marriage covenant. While the Bible specifically identifies sexual immorality in Matthew 19:9 and the desertion of a believer by an unbeliever in 1 Corinthians 7:15 as clear grounds for divorce, physical violence is understood by many faithful pastors and theologians as a form of "constructive desertion." By turning a hand of violence against a spouse, the abuser has functionally abandoned the marriage and their solemn vow to cherish and protect, treating the spouse not as a partner but as an enemy.

Scripture teaches that a husband is to love his wife as Christ loved the church and gave Himself up for her in Ephesians 5:25. Violence is the total antithesis of this sacrificial love. God expresses His hatred for those who love violence in Psalm 11:5 and warns against those who cover their garments with cruelty in Malachi 2:16. In cases of abuse, the first priority is the protection of the oppressed and the establishment of safety through physical separation. 

Theologically, the abuser has broken the "one flesh" union through their assault on the "Imago Dei," the image of God, in their spouse. When a spouse becomes a physical threat, they are no longer acting as a companion but as an oppressor. The church has a duty to protect the victim and exercise church discipline against the abuser. If the abuser refuses to repent and continues their path of violence, they are to be treated as an unbeliever. If they then refuse to live peaceably and maintain the covenant, the principle of desertion found in 1 Corinthians 7:15 may apply.

Your next step should be to reach out to your local church elders or a trusted, mature believer who can help you find a safe place and provide spiritual and practical support. Do not walk this path alone. 

Remember that Jesus Christ is the Good Shepherd who cares deeply for the brokenhearted. He took the violence of our sin upon Himself on the cross so that we might find peace and healing in Him. He rose from the grave to defeat the power of darkness, offering a new life defined by His grace. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and eternal hope. What is the greatest fear you face as you consider taking this step toward safety?

Supporting passages:
Psalm 11:5; Malachi 2:16; Matthew 19:9; 1 Corinthians 7:15; Ephesians 5:25; Hebrews 13:5
Why is “You shall not steal” in the Ten Commandments?

The command "You shall not steal" is found in Exodus 20:15 because God, in His perfect holiness and wisdom, establishes the right of ownership and the necessity of justice among His people. This commandment serves as a boundary that protects the possessions of our neighbors, affirming that what a person has is entrusted to them by God’s own hand. 

At its heart, this law reflects the character of God, who is the righteous Creator and the source of every good gift. When we steal, we are not merely taking an object from a person; we are rebelling against the providence of God. Providence is the theological truth that God sovereignly provides for His children and governs all things according to His will. To steal is to declare that God has been unkind or insufficient in His provision and that we must take matters into our own hands through dishonesty.

The Bible teaches that this commandment extends beyond the simple act of shoplifting or robbery. It touches upon our integrity in all areas of life, including how we handle our time at work, how we pay our taxes, and how we treat the reputations of others (Romans 13:7; Ephesians 4:28). It calls us to a life of contentment and stewardship, recognizing that we are managers of God's resources rather than absolute owners.

As a practical step in following this command, I encourage you to look at your daily dealings, perhaps in your workplace or your finances, and ask the Lord to reveal any area where you might be taking what is not yours, even in small ways. Pray for a heart that finds its satisfaction in Christ rather than in the accumulation of things.

Ultimately, none of us has kept this law perfectly. We have all, at some point, taken what belonged to God or others, whether in action or in the secret desires of our hearts. This is why the Gospel is such glorious news. Jesus Christ lived the only life of perfect honesty and sacrificial giving. He died on a cross to pay the debt for our sins, including our thefts and our greed, and He rose again to give us new life. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive full forgiveness and a heart that delights in His law. What is it that makes it difficult for you to trust God's provision today?
What does it mean that “His mercy endures forever” (Jeremiah 33:11 and Psalm 136)?

To say that "His mercy endures forever" is to declare that God’s covenant-keeping love is an eternal, unchanging reality that can never be exhausted, revoked, or diminished. This phrase translates the Hebrew word *hesed*, which refers to a steadfast kindness, loyalty, and grace that God pledges to His people, rooted entirely in His own character rather than their performance.

In the biblical context, this mercy is not a vague sentiment but a functional, historical force. When Jeremiah speaks these words in Jeremiah 33:11, he is prophesying to a people facing the desolation of exile and the consequences of their own rebellion. Even in the midst of ruins, the prophet reminds them that God's mercy is more permanent than their failure. Similarly, Psalm 136 repeats this refrain twenty six times, tracing God’s mercy through the acts of creation, the deliverance from Egypt, and the sustaining of Israel in the wilderness. It teaches us that every breath we take and every victory we experience is a direct result of this relentless, enduring favor.

Theologically, this mercy is anchored in the immutability of God. Because God does not change, His mercy does not have an expiration date. It is "new every morning," as recorded in Lamentations 3:22–23. This is a vital truth for the believer to grasp because our hearts are often prone to wander and our spirits are often crushed by guilt. To know that His mercy endures forever is to know that there is no pit so deep that His grace is not deeper still. As the apostle John wrote, "For from his fullness we have all received, grace upon grace," in John 1:16.

This eternal mercy finds its ultimate and most glorious expression in the Person and work of Jesus Christ. We are sinners who deserve the righteous judgment of a holy God, yet because His mercy endures forever, He provided a way of escape. Jesus Christ, being truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life and died a substitutionary death on the cross. He bore the full weight of the Father's wrath against sin so that we might receive the full measure of the Father's mercy. His bodily resurrection from the dead is the final, ironclad guarantee that death has been defeated and that His mercy is indeed everlasting.

If you find yourself overwhelmed by the weight of your past or the uncertainty of your future, I encourage you to read Psalm 136 aloud today. After every sentence, remind yourself that this same God is the one who holds your life in His hands. Consider making it a daily rhythm to confess your sins specifically to Him, resting in the promise that He is faithful and just to forgive us, as stated in 1 John 1:9.

The most important question you must face is whether you have personally received this enduring mercy. No amount of good works or religious effort can earn a standing before a holy God. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Is the Church of God in Christ (COGIC) a good biblical church?

The Church of God in Christ (COGIC) is generally recognized as a biblical and orthodox Christian denomination because it holds firmly to the essential foundations of the historic Christian faith. In evaluating any church or denomination, we must look first at their commitment to the core doctrines that define a follower of Christ.

The Church of God in Christ affirms the Trinity, the deity and humanity of Jesus Christ, His virgin birth, His sinless life, His substitutionary atonement on the cross, His bodily resurrection, and His future return to judge the living and the dead. They believe that the Bible is the inspired and infallible Word of God, serving as the final authority for all matters of faith and practice. On these primary truths, they stand on solid biblical ground.

There are, however, secondary doctrines where COGIC holds distinct views that differ from other traditions, such as the Reformed Baptist or Presbyterian perspectives. It is important to distinguish these from the essentials of the faith.

*   **Pneumatology and Spiritual Gifts:** As a Pentecostal denomination, COGIC teaches that the baptism of the Holy Spirit is a distinct experience following conversion, often evidenced by speaking in tongues. While many faithful Christians hold to cessationism, the belief that certain miraculous gifts ended with the apostles, this is a secondary matter. Both views seek to honor the work of the Holy Spirit, even if they interpret His contemporary operations differently. See Acts 2:4; 1 Corinthians 12:4-11.
*   **The Doctrine of Sanctification:** Rooted in the Holiness movement, COGIC places a heavy emphasis on personal holiness and the process of sanctification. They believe in the "cleansing power of the Blood of Jesus" to deliver a believer from the power of sin. While all Christians believe in sanctification, COGIC’s historical roots emphasize a "second work of grace" or a life of "entire holiness" that is very rigorous. See Hebrews 12:14; 1 Peter 1:15-16.
*   **Church Governance:** COGIC follows an episcopal form of government, meaning they are led by bishops and a presiding bishop. This differs from congregational or presbyterian models but is a matter of administrative preference rather than a test of Christian orthodoxy.

When deciding if a specific local COGIC congregation is "good" or biblical, you must look past the denominational label and examine the fruit of that specific church. A biblical church will prioritize the preaching of the Gospel, which is the message that we are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. See Ephesians 2:8-9; Romans 3:24-26.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to visit the local church and speak with the pastor. Ask clearly how they define the Gospel and how they view the sufficiency of Scripture. Compare everything you hear to the Word of God, as the Bereans did. See Acts 17:11.

If you find yourself searching for a spiritual home, remember that the most vital question is not one of denomination, but of your relationship with the Lord Jesus Christ. The Bible teaches that all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God, but salvation is found in Christ Jesus who died for our sins and rose again. See Romans 3:23; Romans 10:9. You can turn to God even now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What is it that prevents you from fully trusting Him today?
What is the voice of one calling in the wilderness (Isaiah 40:3; John 1:23)?

The "voice of one calling in the wilderness" refers to John the Baptist, the divinely appointed herald who prepared the way for the ministry of Jesus Christ. This specific title is rooted in the prophecy of Isaiah 40:3 and is explicitly claimed by John himself in John 1:23 when he was questioned by the religious leaders concerning his identity.

In the ancient world, a herald was sent ahead of a monarch to announce his arrival and ensure that the paths were made smooth and clear for the royal procession. Spiritually, John the Baptist performed this "road work" by preaching a baptism of repentance for the forgiveness of sins, as noted in Matthew 3:3 and Mark 1:2-4. His mission was to prepare the hearts of the people to receive their King, the Messiah, by calling them to recognize their spiritual bankruptcy and their need for a Savior.

John’s role as the forerunner is a beautiful example of humility and prophetic fulfillment. He did not seek glory for himself but pointed everyone toward the Lord Jesus, declaring, "Behold, the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world!" in John 1:29. His life was defined by the principle that Christ must increase, while he must decrease, as seen in John 3:30.

The message of the "voice" is still relevant for us today because it reminds us that the way to Christ is paved with repentance. To truly receive the King, one must acknowledge their rebellion against God and turn toward His mercy. Jesus Christ, being truly God and truly man, came to satisfy the justice of God through His perfect life and His substitutionary death on the cross. His bodily resurrection confirms His victory over death and His authority as Lord of all.

You are invited to respond to this message today by turning from your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone for your salvation. What is it that might be holding you back from fully surrendering to Him? I encourage you to bring these things to God in prayer and to seek fellowship in a local church where the Word of God is faithfully preached. 

Isaiah 40:3; John 1:23; Matthew 3:3; Mark 1:2-4; John 1:29; John 3:30
What is neonomianism?

Neonomianism is the theological error that describes the Gospel as a "new law" (*neo-nomos*) with lower demands than the original Moral Law. It suggests that God, for the sake of Christ, has set aside the requirement of perfect obedience and now accepts our faith and sincere "evangelical obedience" as the legal ground of our justification.

This teaching is a departure from the biblical Gospel because it shifts the foundation of our salvation from the finished work of Jesus Christ to our own performance. If our acceptance before a holy God depends even in the slightest on the quality of our sincere efforts, then grace is no longer grace. The Bible teaches that the Law of God is perfect and its holy demands are never lowered; rather, those demands were perfectly met by Jesus Christ on our behalf through His active obedience and His substitutionary death.

Justification is an act of God’s free grace, where He pardons our sins and accepts us as righteous in His sight, not because of anything wrought in us or done by us, but only for the righteousness of Christ imputed to us and received by faith alone. Romans 3:24-26; Romans 4:4-5; Galatians 2:16; Ephesians 2:8-9.

By labeling the Gospel as a "new law" that we must fulfill to be saved, neonomianism subtly reintroduces a works-based righteousness that can never offer true peace to a burdened conscience. Beloved, if we could be saved by a "new law" of sincere obedience, we would all be lost, for even our best works are stained with sin. Our hope must be built on nothing less than Jesus’ blood and righteousness. 1 Corinthians 1:30; 2 Corinthians 5:21; Titus 3:5-7.

If you find yourself constantly measuring your sincerity or your "progress" to see if God still loves you, you are looking in the wrong direction. I encourage you to look away from yourself and gaze upon Christ. Stop trying to fulfill a "new law" and instead rest entirely in the Savior who fulfilled the whole Law for you. 

You can turn to God even now, repent of your self-reliance, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you. Is there anything in your own heart today that you are still trying to use as a "payment" for God’s favor? What holds you back from resting fully in His finished work?
What does it mean that neither height nor depth separates us from the love of God (Romans 8:39)?

To say that neither height nor depth can separate us from the love of God is to affirm the absolute totality and invincible nature of God's commitment to His people in Christ Jesus. This phrase serves as a "merism," a figure of speech where two opposites are used to encompass everything in between, signifying that there is no location in the entire cosmos, no spiritual realm, and no extreme of human experience that can sever the bond of grace.

In the biblical context, "height" and "depth" often carried astronomical or spiritual connotations, referring to the highest and lowest points of the stars or the domains of celestial powers. By using these terms, the Apostle Paul is declaring that no spiritual principality, no cosmic force, and no distance in space can intervene between the believer and their Savior. This is a profound comfort because it shifts our security away from our own fluctuating feelings and onto the immutable, sovereign character of God. This truth is captured in Romans 8:38-39: "For I am convinced that neither death nor life, neither angels nor principalities, neither the present nor the future, nor any powers, neither height nor depth, nor anything else in all creation, will be able to separate us from the love of God that is in Christ Jesus our Lord."

This doctrine rests upon the perseverance of the saints, which teaches that those whom God has truly called and justified, He will certainly preserve until the end. Because this love is specifically "in Christ Jesus," it is founded upon His finished work and penal substitutionary atonement on the cross rather than our own merit. Whether you find yourself on the mountain peak of spiritual joy or in the deepest valley of sorrow, struggle, or even the shadow of death, the grip of Christ remains firm. As Psalm 139:8 reminds us, "If I ascend to heaven, You are there; if I make my bed in Sheol, You are there."

If you feel distant from God today, take heart in knowing that your feelings do not dictate the reality of His covenant faithfulness. His love is as wide as the universe and as deep as the grave Christ conquered for you. A practical step in walking in this truth is to meditate daily on the objective promises of Scripture rather than the subjective whispers of your own heart.

For those who have not yet entered into this security, this love is not a vague sentiment but a historical reality found in the person of Jesus Christ. We are all separated from God by our sin, but God demonstrated His love for us in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us. He rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is the doctrine of preterition?

Preterition is the sovereign act of God whereby, in His eternal decree of election, He chooses to pass over certain individuals, leaving them in their self-chosen sin and ultimately ordaining them to suffer the just consequences of that sin for the manifestation of His justice. This term comes from a Latin word meaning a "passing over," and it serves to distinguish God’s active work of drawing the elect to Himself from His decision to leave the rest of fallen humanity to their own will and desires.

In the historic Reformed tradition, preterition is understood as one aspect of the broader doctrine of reprobation. It is vital to recognize that God does not infuse evil into the hearts of the non-elect; rather, He finds all of humanity already dead in trespasses and sins as a result of the Fall. While God sovereignly and graciously chooses to rescue some through the gift of faith, He chooses to leave others in their natural state of rebellion. This is beautifully and solemnly illustrated in the image of the potter who has the authority over the clay to make from the same lump one vessel for honorable use and another for common or dishonorable use, as seen in Romans 9:21.

The Scriptures teach that God’s ways are high above our own, and His judgments are unsearchable. We see this reality in Romans 9:18, which says, "Therefore God has mercy on whom He wants to have mercy, and He hardens whom He wants to harden." Further, Jude 1:4 speaks of certain people "who were long ago designated for this condemnation," and 1 Peter 2:8 describes those who stumble because they disobey the word, "as they were destined to do." These passages do not portray a God who is capricious or cruel, but a God who is perfectly holy and whose justice is as much an attribute of His glory as His mercy.

For the believer, this doctrine is not meant to spark cold speculation but to kindle a fire of profound gratitude. If God has chosen to save you, it is not because of any merit found in you, but solely because of His free and sovereign grace. It humbles the soul to realize that we all deserve the "passing over," yet in Christ, God has reached down to snatch us from the fire.

If you find yourself troubled by the thought of being passed over, let that very concern drive you to the feet of Jesus Christ. The promise of the Gospel is not for a secret group of people whose names you must guess; it is for everyone who hears and believes. The Lord Jesus is a perfect Savior who never casts out anyone who comes to Him in faith. 

The weight of eternity is real, for we are all born separated from God by our sin. But God, in His great love, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for sinners, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord of all. You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What is it that holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical step in reflecting on these truths, I encourage you to read through the ninth chapter of Romans and the first chapter of Ephesians this week. Ask the Holy Spirit to give you a heart that rests in the absolute sovereignty of God while rejoicing in the certain hope of His mercy. Seek out a local, Bible-believing church where the Word is faithfully preached, so you may grow in the grace and knowledge of our Lord.
Are there supposed to be any rituals in Christianity?

Christianity is not a religion of empty, repetitive performance, but our Lord Jesus did graciously institute specific outward signs, often called ordinances, for His people to observe. These are not magical rites that earn us favor with God, but are visible representations of the Gospel that point us to the finished work of Christ on the cross. While many religions use rituals to try to reach God, Christian ordinances are given because God has already reached down to us.

The New Testament establishes two primary ordinances for the church: Baptism and the Lord's Supper.

Baptism is the solemn act of being immersed in water in the name of the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit. It serves as a public declaration of a believer's faith and an outward sign of the inward reality of being united with Christ in His death, burial, and resurrection. Jesus commanded this in the Great Commission, saying, "Go therefore and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit" Matthew 28:19. It is an act of identification with the family of God and a testimony that one has been washed clean by the grace of God.

The Lord's Supper, also known as Communion, is a memorial meal where believers eat bread and drink from the cup to remember Christ’s sacrifice. The bread represents His body broken for us, and the cup represents the new covenant in His blood shed for the forgiveness of sins. Jesus instituted this on the night He was betrayed, telling His disciples, "Do this in remembrance of Me" 1 Corinthians 11:24; 1 Corinthians 11:25. This practice allows the church to proclaim the Lord's death until He returns and to enjoy spiritual fellowship with Him and one another.

It is vital to understand that while these ordinances are beautiful and commanded, they do not possess the power to save a soul. We are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by the performance of any ceremony. The Apostle Paul emphasizes that it is "by grace you have been saved through faith, and this is not from yourselves; it is the gift of God, not by works, so that no one can boast" Ephesians 2:8-9. These practices are meant to strengthen our faith and nourish our souls as we look to Jesus, the Author and Perfecter of our faith.

If you are looking for a way to be right with God, I invite you to consider that no ritual can wash away a single sin. Only the blood of Jesus Christ, the Son of God, can do that. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

A helpful next step would be to read the book of Acts in the Bible to see how the early church practiced these things, and to seek out a local, Bible-believing church where you can see these ordinances observed in a community of faith. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is the Presbyterian Church in America (PCA), and what do they believe?

The Presbyterian Church in America (PCA) is a conservative, evangelical Protestant denomination in the Reformed tradition that was formed in 1973. It was established by believers who sought to maintain a steadfast commitment to the full authority of the Bible and the historic Reformed faith, particularly in response to rising theological liberalism within the older Southern Presbyterian denomination.

At the heart of the PCA’s identity is the conviction that the Bible is the inspired, inerrant, and infallible Word of God. This commitment to Scripture governs their understanding of all reality, from the creation of the world to the return of Jesus Christ. As a Reformed body, the PCA holds to the Westminster Confession of Faith along with the Larger and Shorter Catechisms as their secondary standards, believing these documents accurately summarize the teachings of the Bible.

Theologically, the PCA emphasizes the absolute sovereignty of God in all things, especially in the work of salvation. They teach that human beings are fallen in sin and utterly unable to save themselves, making the grace of God the only hope for reconciliation. They proclaim the glorious truth of justification by faith alone, where a sinner is declared righteous before God not based on their own works, but solely through the imputed righteousness of Jesus Christ received by faith. Romans 5:1; Ephesians 2:8-9. Following this, they emphasize progressive sanctification, the ongoing work of the Holy Spirit in the believer’s life to conform them more into the image of Christ. 1 Thessalonians 4:3.

The name "Presbyterian" refers to their form of church government. In the PCA, local churches are governed by "presbyters," or elders, who are elected by the congregation. These include teaching elders (pastors) and ruling elders (lay leaders). This structure is connectional, meaning local churches are not independent but are united in regional "presbyteries" and a national "General Assembly" to ensure accountability, doctrinal purity, and shared mission. 1 Timothy 5:17; Titus 1:5.

Regarding secondary doctrines, such as the sacraments, the PCA practices what is known as covenant theology. This leads them to practice infant baptism (paedobaptism), viewing it as a sign and seal of God’s covenant promises to believers and their children, much like circumcision was in the Old Testament. This differs from the Reformed Baptist view of believer's baptism (credobaptism), which reserves the ordinance for those who have made a personal profession of faith. While this is a significant denominational distinction, it is a matter over which faithful Christians may charitably disagree while remaining united in the essentials of the Gospel. Romans 14:1-5.

In their worship and ministry, the PCA is known for a "plainness" that focuses on the preaching of the Word and the administration of the sacraments—the Lord’s Supper and Baptism. They are deeply committed to the Great Commission, seeking to spread the Gospel both locally and through extensive global missions.

If you are exploring the PCA, it is likely because you desire a church that takes the holiness of God and the authority of His Word seriously. The greatest need for any person, whether in a Presbyterian pew or elsewhere, is to be reconciled to God through His Son. The Bible declares that all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God, but the gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord. Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23. 

You can turn to God even now, repenting of your sins and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died for our sins and rose bodily from the grave to secure victory over death. I encourage you to visit a local gospel-preaching church, speak with the elders there, and sit under the faithful preaching of God's Word. What is it that draws you to seek a deeper understanding of these biblical truths today?
Where is God now? Where is God when it hurts?

God is omnipresent, which means He is present everywhere at all times in the fullness of His being. He is not limited by space or time, as He fills the heavens and the earth with His glory (Jeremiah 23:24). While He is sovereignly enthroned in heaven, He is also intimately near to His creation, sustaining all things by the word of His power (Hebrews 1:3).

When you walk through the dark valley of suffering, God is not a distant spectator. He is near to the brokenhearted and saves those who are crushed in spirit (Psalm 34:18). Scripture reveals that God is our refuge and strength, a very present help in times of trouble (Psalm 46:1). In His divine providence, God is never absent from the trials of His children, though our tears may sometimes cloud our vision of His face.

The greatest proof of God’s nearness in our pain is the Person of Jesus Christ. He is Immanuel, which means "God with us" (Matthew 1:23). He did not remain aloof from our misery but stepped into our world of sin and death. As the Man of Sorrows, He was acquainted with grief and bore our infirmities (Isaiah 53:3; Isaiah 53:4). On the cross, Jesus endured the ultimate abandonment and the righteous wrath of God against sin so that we might never be truly alone. Because of His historical death, burial, and bodily resurrection, we have the certain hope that He has conquered death and will one day wipe every tear from our eyes (Revelation 21:4).

If you are hurting today, I encourage you to pour out your heart to Him in prayer. He invites you to cast all your anxieties on Him because He cares for you (1 Peter 5:7). Do not suffer in isolation, but seek the fellowship and prayers of a faithful local church where the Word of God is honored and where brothers and sisters can bear your burdens with you.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him with your heart and your hurts today?
What is the just war theory?

Just war theory is the historic Christian framework used to determine when it is morally permissible for a nation to go to war and how that war must be conducted to remain within the bounds of justice. This tradition acknowledges that we live in a fallen world where sin abounds, yet it seeks to restrain the horrors of violence by bringing them under the authority of God’s moral law.

The Bible teaches that God has vested the civil magistrate with the "power of the sword" to be a terror to bad conduct and an avenger who carries out God’s wrath on the wrongdoer, as seen in Romans 13:4. Just war theory is the application of this principle on an international scale. It is typically divided into two categories: the justice of going to war (jus ad bellum) and justice in the conduct of war (jus in bello).

The criteria for entering a war justly generally include:
*   Just Cause: The war must be fought to confront a real and certain danger, such as protecting innocent life or restoring justice after a grave public evil.
*   Right Authority: Only legitimate government leaders have the biblical right to declare war, not private individuals or groups.
*   Right Intention: The ultimate goal must be to establish a redirected and lasting peace, rather than seeking territorial gain, national prestige, or cruel revenge.
*   Last Resort: All peaceful avenues, such as diplomacy and economic sanctions, must be exhausted before turning to military force.
*   Proportionality: The potential good to be achieved by the war must outweigh the expected destruction and loss of life.
*   Reasonable Chance of Success: Leaders should not send men to die in a cause that has no hope of achieving its objectives.

Once a conflict has begun, justice in conduct requires:
*   Discrimination: A sharp distinction must be made between combatants and non-combatants. The intentional killing of innocent civilians is a violation of the commandment "You shall not murder," found in Exodus 20:13.
*   Proportionality of Means: The force used should be limited to what is truly necessary to achieve the military objective.

While many Christians throughout history have held to this view, it is a secondary doctrine where faithful believers sometimes disagree. Some, following a tradition often called Christian Pacifism, believe that the call of Jesus to "turn the other cheek" in Matthew 5:39 precludes any participation in lethal force. They emphasize the distinctiveness of the Kingdom of God from the kingdoms of this world. Others hold to "Preventative War" or "Crusade" models, though these are generally less common in orthodox Protestant thought.

The existence of war is a sobering reminder of our rebellion against our Creator. James 4:1 asks, "What causes conflicts and quarrels among you? Don't they come from the settled desires that war within you?" As we look at the brokenness of our nations, we are reminded that true and lasting peace cannot be manufactured by treaties or tanks. It was only through the sacrificial death of Jesus Christ on the cross that the greatest war—our enmity with God—was ended. He suffered the "sword" of divine justice in our place so that we might be reconciled to the Father through faith.

If you find your heart troubled by the violence of this world, remember that Jesus is the Prince of Peace. He died and rose again to conquer sin and death, and He will one day return to judge the living and the dead and make all things new. You can turn to Him today, repent of your sins, and find a peace that surpasses all understanding. What prevents you from trusting in His finished work today?

In the meantime, the Christian's duty is to pray for those in authority, as commanded in 1 Timothy 2:1-2, so that we may lead peaceful and quiet lives in all godliness and dignity. Seeking the counsel of your local church elders can provide further guidance on how to navigate these heavy questions with a clear conscience.
What does it mean that women should have a gentle and quiet spirit (1 Peter 3:4)?

To have a gentle and quiet spirit means to possess an internal composure and a heart at rest that flows from a deep, unwavering trust in the sovereignty of God. This "imperishable beauty" described by the Apostle Peter is not a matter of a woman’s natural temperament or whether she is introverted or extroverted, but rather a spiritual disposition cultivated by the Holy Spirit. 1 Peter 3:4.

The word "gentle" is the same word often translated as "meek." In the biblical sense, meekness is not weakness; it is strength under the authority of God. It is the same quality that the Lord Jesus used to describe His own heart when He said, "I am gentle and humble in heart." Matthew 11:29. A gentle spirit is one that does not demand its own way or react with bitterness when wronged, because it is anchored in the goodness of the Father.

The word "quiet" refers to a tranquility of soul. It describes a life that is not turbulent, agitated, or prone to causing strife. This quietness is the opposite of a spirit that is constantly anxious, loud in its self-assertion, or demanding to be heard above others. It is the portrait of a soul that has learned to be still before the Lord, much like the psalmist who wrote, "I have calmed and quieted my soul, like a weaned child with its mother." Psalm 131:2.

In the context of 1 Peter 3:1–6, this inner beauty is presented as far more valuable and powerful than any outward adornment, such as elaborate hairstyles or jewelry. Peter encourages wives that such a spirit can even be a means of winning over a husband who does not obey the Word, because he sees the "purity and reverence" of her life. 1 Peter 3:1–2. This is not a call to a legalistic silence or to be a "doormat" for mistreatment; rather, it is a call to find one's ultimate security in God so that one's reactions to the world are marked by peace rather than panic.

The truth is that we all, by nature, possess spirits that are restless and prone to prideful self-defense. This gentle and quiet spirit is a fruit of the Spirit that comes through the process of sanctification. It is only possible when a person has been reconciled to God through the Gospel of Jesus Christ. 

The Gospel tells us that though we were rebels and sinners deserving of judgment, God in His great mercy sent His Son. Jesus Christ, truly God and truly man, lived a life of perfect obedience and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, conquering death. We are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. When we trust in Him, He gives us His Spirit to transform our hearts from the inside out.

As a practical step in cultivating this spirit, I encourage you to spend time each day in the Word, focusing on the character of God. When you feel the urge to be anxious or assertive in your own strength, take a moment to pray and hand those cares over to the One who cares for you. 1 Peter 5:7.

You can turn to God even now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. Have you come to a place where you are resting in His finished work for your salvation?
What does it mean to be free from sin?

To be free from sin is to be legally acquitted of its guilt and sovereignly delivered from its mastery through the person and work of the Lord Jesus Christ. This freedom is not a license to do as one pleases, but a liberation to live as one ought, in joyful obedience to the Creator.

Scripture describes this freedom in three distinct but inseparable ways:

**Freedom from the Penalty of Sin**
The most immediate sense of freedom is justification. Because of the fall of Adam, every human being is born in a state of rebellion and stands condemned under the holy law of God. However, for those who trust in Christ, the debt is paid. Jesus Christ took the penalty of our transgressions upon Himself on the cross, satisfying the justice of God through His substitutionary death. As the Apostle Paul writes, "There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus" (Romans 8:1). This is a finished, legal reality; the believer is "justified by faith" and has peace with God (Romans 5:1).

**Freedom from the Power of Sin**
While we are legally free the moment we believe, we are also being practically delivered from sin's dominion. This is known as progressive sanctification. Before we were in Christ, we were "slaves to sin," unable to truly please God because our hearts were at enmity with Him (Romans 8:7). Now, the Holy Spirit dwells within the believer, breaking the chains of our old nature and giving us new desires. While we still encounter the "remnants of indwelling sin," it is no longer our master. The Bible declares, "For sin shall not have dominion over you, for you are not under law but under grace" (Romans 6:14). We are now free to say "no" to ungodliness and "yes" to righteousness.

**Freedom from the Presence of Sin**
Finally, we look forward to glorification. True freedom will reach its zenith when Christ returns or when we go to be with Him. In that state, we will be free from even the very presence and temptation of sin. We will possess hearts that are incapable of wandering from our Savior, and we will see Him as He is (1 John 3:2).

We must be careful not to fall into the error of "sinless perfectionism" in this life. The Apostle John, writing with both truth and love, warns us: "If we say we have no sin, we deceive ourselves, and the truth is not in us" (1 John 1:8). The Christian life is a battle, but it is a battle we fight from a position of victory. When we do stumble, our freedom is found in returning to the fountain of grace: "If we confess our sins, He is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness" (1 John 1:9).

If you are currently weighed down by the burden of your own failures or the crushing weight of guilt, know that your own efforts to "be better" cannot break the chains of sin. Only the Son can set you free. Jesus said, "So if the Son sets you free, you will be free indeed" (John 8:36).

**A Step Toward Freedom**
If you have not yet experienced this freedom, the first step is to stop trying to save yourself through your own works or willpower. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the total forgiveness of your sins and the gift of a new life. If you are already a believer but feel enslaved to a particular struggle, I encourage you to confess that specific sin to a trusted elder or mature brother or sister in your local church. Bringing sin into the light of Christian fellowship is often the very means God uses to break its power. 

What is it that makes the idea of being "free from sin" most compelling to you today? Is it the relief from guilt, or the desire for power over a specific habit?
Is God man-made? Did man create the idea of God?

The Bible teaches that God is not man-made, but rather that He is the eternal, self-existent Creator of all that exists. Before there was a human mind to conceive an idea, God was already present in the fullness of His glory. He is the "Alpha and the Omega, the First and the Last, the Beginning and the End" as recorded in Revelation 22:13.

The scriptures begin with the foundational truth that "In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth" in Genesis 1:1. This means that God is the author of time, space, and matter. He does not depend on us for His existence, a doctrine often called His aseity. While humans certainly have many ideas about God, some of which are mistaken or mere projections of our own desires, the true God exists independently of human imagination. As the Psalmist writes, "Before the mountains were born or You gave birth to the earth and the world, even from everlasting to everlasting, You are God" in Psalm 90:2.

If man had created the idea of God, we likely would have invented a deity who was comfortable with our sins and who functioned merely to serve our interests. Instead, the Bible reveals a God who is perfectly holy, whose "eyes are too pure to look on evil" as seen in Habakkuk 1:13. This holy God is the one who created us in His own image so that we might know Him and enjoy Him forever.

The reason every human culture seems to have a "sense of the divine" is not that we invented the concept, but that God has made His existence plain to us. The Apostle Paul explains that "what may be known about God is plain to them, because God has made it plain to them. For since the creation of the world God’s invisible qualities—his eternal power and divine nature—have been clearly seen, being understood from what has been made, so that people are without excuse" in Romans 1:19–20. We do not create the idea of God; rather, we react to the reality of God that is displayed all around us and written on our hearts.

Because God is our Creator, we are accountable to Him. Sadly, we have all turned away from Him, seeking to be our own gods and following our own ways, which the Bible calls sin. This sin separates us from the source of life. Yet, in His great love, the Creator did not leave us in our rebellion. He stepped into His own creation in the person of Jesus Christ. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over death.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. By grace through faith in Him, you can be reconciled to the one true God who made you. I would encourage you to read the Gospel of John to see the character of this God revealed in the face of Jesus Christ. What is it that makes you wonder if God is merely a human invention?
What is the significance of the statement “God is within her; she will not fall” in Psalm 46:5?

The statement "God is within her; she will not fall" in Psalm 46:5 refers primarily to the city of God, Jerusalem, as the dwelling place of the Most High, signifying that the security of God’s people depends entirely on His presence in their midst. In the immediate historical and poetic context of the Psalm, "she" is the holy habitation of the Almighty, Zion itself, which stands firm even while the nations rage and the kingdoms totter.

The significance of this verse is found in the contrast between the instability of the world and the immutability of God. While the earlier verses of the Psalm describe mountains shaking and waters roaring, the city of God remains tranquil because of the divine presence. The phrase "she will not fall" (or "she shall not be moved") indicates that the city is not sustained by its own fortifications or the bravery of its inhabitants, but by the immanence of Jehovah. Because God is in the midst of her, she partakes in His own stability.

Theologically, this passage carries weight in three specific areas:

First, it highlights the doctrine of God’s presence. Under the Old Covenant, God manifested His presence in the Tabernacle and later the Temple. Today, this promise finds its fulfillment in the Church, which is the "city of the living God" (Hebrews 12:22), and in the individual believer, who is the temple of the Holy Spirit (1 Corinthians 6:19). The security promised is not a promise of an easy life or the absence of conflict, but a guarantee of preservation through the storm.

Second, it emphasizes the timing of God’s help. The verse continues, "God will help her at break of day." This suggests that even after a long night of affliction or siege, God’s intervention is certain and punctual. He is a "very present help in trouble" (Psalm 46:1).

Third, it points to our union with Christ. For the believer, the reality that "God is within her" is only possible because of the mediatorial work of Jesus Christ. It is through His atoning death and victorious resurrection that we are reconciled to a holy God and granted the indwelling of His Spirit. Our "not falling" is a result of the perseverance of the saints, as we are kept by the power of God through faith (1 Peter 1:5).

As an actionable step in your walk with Christ, I encourage you to meditate on this truth when you feel overwhelmed by the "roaring waters" of life’s circumstances. Set aside time this week to pray specifically through Psalm 46, acknowledging your own weakness and consciously placing your trust in the God who is your refuge. Remember that your stability does not come from your own strength, but from the One who has promised never to leave you or forsake you (Hebrews 13:5).

If you find yourself outside of this security, wondering how to know this peace, the Bible is clear that we are all separated from God by our sin. But God, in His great love, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live the perfect life we could not live and to die the death we deserved. He rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for forgiveness and new life. What is it that holds you back from trusting Him today?
What does the Bible say about worship?

The Bible teaches that worship is the active response of a heart that recognizes the supreme worth, holiness, and glory of God. It is the humble surrender of the creature to the Creator, acknowledging that He alone is worthy of all honor, praise, and obedience. True worship is not defined by human preference or cultural trends, but by the character of God and the instructions He has provided in His Word.

The primary object of worship is the Triune God: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. Scripture is clear that we are to worship God alone and flee from all forms of idolatry. God declared in the Law, "You shall have no other gods before Me" (Exodus 20:3). Jesus reinforced this during His temptation, stating, "Worship the Lord your God and serve Him only" (Matthew 4:10). Because God is the sovereign Creator and Sustainer of all things, He is the only one who possesses the inherent right to receive the adoration of His creatures.

Jesus Christ provided the essential foundation for how we are to approach God when He said, "God is spirit, and those who worship Him must worship in spirit and truth" (John 4:24). To worship in spirit means that our worship must come from a heart regenerated by the Holy Spirit, involving our deepest affections and sincerity. To worship in truth means that our worship must be in accordance with the revelation of God found in the Bible. Worship that ignores the truth of Scripture, or that honors God only with the lips while the heart is far from Him, is rejected by God (Matthew 15:8).

The Bible presents worship as both a corporate activity and a total way of life.
* **Corporate Worship:** Christians are commanded to gather regularly for the preaching of the Word, prayer, the singing of psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs, and the administration of the ordinances of baptism and the Lord’s Supper (Acts 2:42; Ephesians 5:19; Hebrews 10:24–25). 
* **Worship as a Lifestyle:** Worship extends beyond the walls of a church building. It involves the daily offering of oneself to God's service. The Apostle Paul wrote, "Therefore I urge you, brothers, in view of God’s mercy, to offer your bodies as living sacrifices, holy and pleasing to God—this is your spiritual act of worship" (Romans 12:1). Whether we eat or drink, or whatever we do, we are called to do it all for the glory of God (1 Corinthians 10:31).

While the world seeks satisfaction in the worship of the self or the creation, true peace is found only in worshipping the Savior. Because of our sin, we were once separated from God and unable to worship Him rightly. However, through the incarnation, perfect life, substitutionary death, and bodily resurrection of Jesus Christ, we are reconciled to God. By grace alone, through faith alone in Christ alone, we are made into a "royal priesthood" to declare the praises of Him who called us out of darkness into His wonderful light (1 Peter 2:9). 

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you, so that your life may become a joyful song of praise to Him. As you consider these truths, what is it that most often distracts your heart from focusing its primary devotion on the Lord?
What is more important, the death of Christ or His resurrection?

To ask whether the death of Christ or His resurrection is more important is much like asking which blade of a pair of scissors is more necessary for the cut. In the sovereign plan of God, these two events are inseparable and equally essential pillars of the Gospel. If you remove either one, the entire bridge of salvation collapses, leaving us without hope.

The death of Jesus Christ is the necessary payment for our sins. On the cross, Jesus acted as our perfect substitute, taking upon Himself the righteous wrath of God that we deserved because of our rebellion. This is the heart of penal substitutionary atonement, where the sinless Lamb of God was sacrificed so that our guilt could be washed away. Scripture teaches us that without the shedding of blood, there is no forgiveness of sins (Hebrews 9:22). It was on the cross that He canceled the record of debt that stood against us (Colossians 2:14) and finished the work of reconciliation.

However, if Christ had remained in the grave, His death would have been a tragedy of a good man, not the triumph of a Savior. The Apostle Paul is very clear on this point, stating that if Christ has not been raised, our preaching is useless and so is our faith (1 Corinthians 15:14). He goes further to say that if Christ is not raised, we are still in our sins (1 Corinthians 15:17). The resurrection is the Father’s divine "Amen" to the Son’s cry of "It is finished." It is the public vindication of Jesus, proving that He is truly the Son of God and that His sacrifice was perfectly sufficient to satisfy divine justice (Romans 1:4; Romans 4:25).

Therefore, the death of Christ provides the merit for our justification, while His resurrection provides the proof of that justification and the power for our new life. Because He died, our debt is paid; because He lives, we are guaranteed eternal life and a future bodily resurrection. We do not choose between the cross and the empty tomb, but rather we find our hope in the complete, finished work of the Savior who was dead and is now alive forevermore.

The message of the Gospel is this: God is holy and we are separated from Him by our sin, but in His great love, He sent His Son to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death in our place. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, conquering sin and death once and for all. Salvation is not earned by our efforts or goodness, but is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Jesus Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is the emerging / emergent church movement?

The Emerging Church movement refers to a diverse trend that began in the late 1990s, primarily in the West, as a reaction to perceived shortcomings in the modern evangelical church. It sought to re-evaluate and "reimagine" Christian practice, worship, and theology to make it more relevant to a "postmodern" culture—a culture that is often skeptical of absolute truth claims, institutional authority, and traditional dogmas.

While the movement was not a single denomination, it was characterized by an emphasis on "conversation" rather than "proclamation." Proponents often focused on community, social justice, and artistic expression, while frequently deconstructing traditional views of the faith. In its more radical "Emergent" forms, the movement began to question the clarity and authority of Scripture, the objective nature of truth, and core doctrines such as the substitutionary atonement of Christ and the reality of eternal judgment.

As believers who love the truth, we must exercise great discernment here. While we should always desire for our churches to be warm, authentic, and engaged with our neighbors, we cannot compromise the "faith that was once for all delivered to the saints" (Jude 1:3). The Bible is not a collection of subjective stories but the inerrant and infallible Word of the living God (2 Timothy 3:16). 

The danger in the movement was often an "epistemological" shift—a change in how we know what is true. By prioritizing human experience or cultural relevance over the plain reading of Scripture, many were led away from the solid rock of Christ. We must remember that while the world changes, Jesus Christ is the same yesterday, today, and forever (Hebrews 13:8). True love for our neighbor does not involve softening the truth of their sin or the exclusivity of Christ, for "your word is truth" (John 17:17).

If you find yourself weary of "religious" routine and longing for something deeper, do not look for a "new" way of being a Christian that discards the old truths. Instead, return to the "ancient paths" (Jeremiah 6:16). Real life is found not in reinventing the Gospel, but in submitting to the Lord Jesus Christ.

The heart of the Gospel is this: God is holy and we are sinners who have fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Out of His great love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9).

I encourage you to spend time in the Gospel of John this week. Ask the Holy Spirit to ground you in the truth of who Jesus is. If you have been wandering in uncertainty, you can turn to Him right now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ for the forgiveness and peace that only He can provide.

What holds you back from resting in the certain truth of God's Word today?
What does the Bible say about underage drinking?

The Bible addresses the principles surrounding underage drinking through the clear commands to obey governing authorities, to honor one's parents, and to maintain a life characterized by self-control and sobriety. While the Scriptures do not mention a specific modern legal age, they provide a firm moral framework that defines such behavior as a matter of obedience to God.

The primary biblical principle regarding underage drinking is submission to the law of the land. In Romans 13:1, the Apostle Paul writes, "Everyone must submit himself to the governing authorities, for there is no authority except that which is from God; the authorities that exist have been established by God." Because the law in many nations prohibits the consumption of alcohol by those under a certain age, to engage in underage drinking is to rebel against the order God has placed over society. This is not merely a legal infraction; it is a matter of Christian integrity and witness. Furthermore, Peter exhorts believers in 1 Peter 2:13 to "submit yourselves for the Lord’s sake to every human institution."

For those who are still under the roof or authority of their parents, underage drinking often involves a violation of the fifth commandment. Exodus 20:12 says, "Honor your father and your mother, so that your days may be long in the land that the LORD your God is giving you." Disobeying a parent's rules or the laws they are bound to uphold shows a heart of rebellion rather than the spirit of Christ, who was submissive to His earthly parents and His Heavenly Father.

Beyond the issue of legality, the Bible is uncompromising in its warnings against the loss of self-control that alcohol can produce. Ephesians 5:18 commands, "Do not get drunk on wine, which leads to debauchery. Instead, be filled with the Spirit." The Christian is called to a high standard of mental and spiritual alertness. In 1 Peter 5:8, we are warned to "be sober-minded and alert" because the devil "prowls around like a roaring lion, seeking someone to devour." Underage drinking often takes place in environments where the temptation to excess is high, and the lack of maturity in the young can lead quickly to the sin of drunkenness, which the Bible names as a work of the flesh in Galatians 5:21.

If you find yourself tempted to participate in underage drinking because of peer pressure or a desire to escape the pressures of life, I encourage you to look at the state of your heart. Often, we seek in a bottle what can only truly be found in the person of Jesus Christ. Sin promises a temporary thrill but pays out in guilt and spiritual death.

The good news of the Gospel is that Jesus Christ lived a life of perfect obedience where we have failed. He was perfectly submissive to authority and perfectly self-controlled. He died on the cross to pay the penalty for our rebellion and rose again to give us a new heart that desires to please God. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

As a practical step, I encourage you to seek out a mature believer or an elder in your local church to talk about the pressures you are facing. Ask God to give you the strength to stand firm in your convictions and to find your satisfaction in His presence rather than in the approval of friends. Have you considered what might be driving the desire to drink before it is legal or wise?
Will there be a great apostasy / falling away during the end times?

Yes, the Scriptures teach that a significant rebellion or falling away, often referred to as the apostasy, will occur before the return of the Lord Jesus Christ. The Apostle Paul spoke clearly about this event to provide clarity and comfort to the church. He wrote, "Let no one deceive you in any way, for it will not come until the rebellion occurs and the man of lawlessness is revealed, the son of destruction" (2 Thessalonians 2:3).

This falling away represents a widespread departure from the core truths of the Gospel. It is a period where many who once associated with the faith will abandon it in favor of worldly lies or the "teachings of demons" (1 Timothy 4:1). It is important to distinguish this from the true children of God losing their salvation. According to the promise of God, those whom He has truly called and justified will be preserved by His power until the end (Romans 8:30; John 10:27-29). The apostasy reveals those who had an outward form of godliness but lacked the internal, regenerating power of the Holy Spirit (2 Timothy 3:5). As Jesus warned, "the love of most will grow cold" because of the multiplication of wickedness (Matthew 24:12).

The exact nature and timing of this apostasy are matters of tertiary doctrine where faithful Christians may disagree. 

*   Some see this as a singular, climactic event that happens just before the visible return of Christ.
*   Others believe it describes a spiritual reality that has been occurring throughout church history but will intensify as the end approaches.

These differences exist because the biblical language regarding the end times often uses prophetic and apocalyptic imagery that allows for varying interpretations of the timeline. 

Regardless of the specific timing, the practical call for every believer is one of vigilance and perseverance. We are called to "contend earnestly for the faith that was once for all entrusted to the saints" (Jude 1:3). To stand firm, you should immerse yourself daily in the Word of God, participate in a faithful local church, and pray for the Spirit to keep your heart tender toward Christ.

The reality of such a falling away reminds us that the only sure foundation is Jesus Christ. If you find your heart growing cold or your mind distracted by the deceptions of this world, I encourage you to return to the simple and pure devotion to Christ. Trust in His finished work on the cross and His bodily resurrection as your only hope. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What is the greatest challenge you face in remaining faithful to the Word of God today?
Should all pronouns referring to God be capitalized?

The practice of capitalizing pronouns referring to God—such as He, Him, or His—is a matter of Christian liberty and stylistic tradition rather than a direct command from Scripture. This is considered a tertiary issue, meaning that while it reflects our heart’s attitude toward the Lord, faithful Christians may hold different convictions without breaking fellowship.

The Bible was originally written in Hebrew, Aramaic, and Greek. The ancient Hebrew scripts did not have capital letters as we understand them today, and the earliest Greek manuscripts of the New Testament were often written entirely in capital letters (uncials) or entirely in lowercase (minuscules). Therefore, the distinction between "he" and "He" is not present in the original inspired texts. Most standard English translations, including the King James Version and the Berean Standard Bible, do not capitalize these pronouns, following the pattern of the original languages.

There are two primary perspectives on this matter within the church:

*   **The Argument for Capitalization:** Many believers choose to capitalize these pronouns as a gesture of deep reverence and to distinguish the Creator from His creatures in written text. This practice can help provide clarity in passages where multiple male figures are mentioned, ensuring the reader knows when the text is referring to God. Proponents often point to the command to honor God’s name, as seen in Exodus 20:7.

*   **The Argument for Standard Case:** Other believers prefer to follow the example of the original manuscripts and the majority of historical English translations. They believe that adding capitalization where the original authors did not might inadvertently add human emphasis to the text. They seek to honor God by letting the clarity of the Word speak for itself without adding typographical traditions.

The heart of the matter is the honor we give to God. We are called to "Sanctify Christ as Lord in your hearts" as stated in 1 Peter 3:15. Whether you choose to capitalize these pronouns or not, the ultimate goal is to treat the character and name of God with the utmost holiness. As the Scripture says in Romans 14:5, "Each one should be fully convinced in his own mind." We must be careful not to judge a brother or sister who follows a different stylistic convention, provided they hold a high view of God's majesty.

While we discuss how to write about God, we must never lose sight of the glorious truth that God has made Himself known to us. He is not a distant "He" in a book, but the living God who stepped into our history. The Word became flesh and dwelt among us, as we read in John 1:14. Because of our sin, we were separated from His holiness, but in His great mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day.

True reverence for God goes beyond the capitalization of a letter; it involves the submission of our entire lives to His Lordship. If you have not yet placed your trust in Him, I invite you to turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and eternal life.

As a practical step, I encourage you to focus your study on the attributes of God in Scripture, such as His holiness and sovereignty, which will naturally produce a spirit of worship in your heart. You might find it helpful to read through the Gospel of John and note how the author emphasizes the deity and glory of Christ. What holds you back from fully resting in His grace today?
What is Kabbalah?

Kabbalah is a traditional system of Jewish mysticism that seeks to explore the relationship between the unchanging, eternal God and the finite, mortal universe. It relies on esoteric interpretations of the Hebrew Scriptures, aiming to uncover hidden meanings and divine secrets that practitioners believe are veiled within the text.
