God created humanity in His own image as male and female, as stated in Genesis 1:27. In marriage, a man and a woman bring different but complementary roles that reflect the wisdom of the Creator. This "one flesh" union is a physical, emotional, and spiritual bonding that is to be kept exclusive and pure.

**A Covenantal Bond**
Marriage is more than a legal agreement; it is a sacred covenant made before God. This bond is intended to be permanent until death, reflecting God’s own faithfulness to His people. As the prophet Malachi notes, God is a witness to the marriage covenant, and He hates the breaking of that bond through treachery as described in Malachi 2:14–16.

**A Reflection of Christ and the Church**
The highest purpose of marriage is to serve as a living parable of the Gospel. The Apostle Paul explains that the relationship between a husband and a wife mirrors the relationship between Jesus Christ and His Church. The husband is called to lead with sacrificial, Christ-like love, while the wife is called to respond with the respect and voluntary submission that the Church offers to Christ as detailed in Ephesians 5:22–33.

**Sanctification and Companionship**
Marriage is a primary means of sanctification, the process by which God makes His children more like Jesus. Through the daily commitment to love, forgive, and serve one another, spouses help each other grow in holiness and provide the companionship that eases the burdens of life in a fallen world.

While marriage is a beautiful gift, it points to a much greater reality. Every earthly marriage is a shadow of the ultimate union between the Lord Jesus and His people. We are all born in sin and separated from God’s holiness, but out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily on the third day to defeat death. He is the true Bridegroom who laid down His life for His bride.

Salvation is found by grace alone, through faith alone, in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and the promise of eternal life.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to read through Ephesians 5 and reflect on how the love of Christ is the foundation for all human relationships. If you are married or seeking marriage, pray that God would strengthen your understanding of His Word and help you to model His faithful love in your home. Are there specific areas of God's design for marriage that you find difficult to understand or apply?
What is the value of group Bible study?

The value of group Bible study lies in the beautiful reality that God has not called His children to walk the path of faith in isolation, but as members of a living Body. When we gather around the Holy Scriptures, we are engaging in a vital means of grace that fosters both individual growth and corporate edification, which is the building up of the church community in strength and love.

One primary benefit of group study is the protection it provides against private, erroneous interpretations. While the Holy Spirit dwells in every believer, our individual perspectives can sometimes be clouded by personal bias or a lack of background knowledge. In a group, as we test our understanding against the shared wisdom of the Body and the historical witness of the faith, we find a safety net that keeps us anchored in sound doctrine. The Bereans were commended because they examined the Scriptures together daily to see if the teachings of Paul were true, demonstrating that communal scrutiny is a hallmark of a healthy spiritual life. Acts 17:11.

Furthermore, group Bible study is a catalyst for progressive sanctification, the ongoing process by which the Spirit makes us more like Jesus Christ. As we discuss the Word, the "iron sharpens iron" principle comes into play, where the insights and even the rebukes of a brother or sister can prune our character and refine our understanding. Proverbs 27:17. This environment allows us to "stir up one another to love and good works," ensuring that our study of the Bible does not remain merely intellectual but becomes practically transformative. Hebrews 10:24-25.

There is also a profound illustrative warmth in seeing how the same passage of Scripture can bring comfort to one person in a season of grief and conviction to another in a season of complacency. This reminds us that the Word of God is "living and active," capable of meeting the diverse needs of the family of God simultaneously. Hebrews 4:12. By hearing how others apply the Word to their lives, we learn to see the sufficiency of Christ in every circumstance. 2 Timothy 3:16-17.

If you are looking to deepen your walk with the Lord, a practical next step would be to join a small group or Bible study within your local church. If one does not exist, consider speaking with your elders about starting a simple gathering focused on a specific book of the Bible, such as the Gospel of John or the Epistle to the Romans. Consistent, prayerful study in the company of other believers will inevitably bear fruit in your life.

Ultimately, all Scripture points us to the person and work of Jesus Christ. If you find yourself searching the Scriptures but have not yet come to know the Author, remember that the Bible’s central message is the Gospel. We are all sinners separated from a holy God, but Jesus Christ, being truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. John 1:1-14; 1 Corinthians 15:3-4. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. John 3:16; Romans 10:9. What prevents you from trusting in His finished work today?
How can I overcome a feeling of spiritual emptiness?

Spiritual emptiness is often a "severe mercy" from God, a holy hunger designed to draw you back to the only Fountain that can truly satisfy the human soul. We were created by God and for God, and as many throughout history have discovered, our hearts remain restless until they find their rest in Him. This sense of a vacuum within is not a flaw in your design, but a signal that you are attempting to find life, purpose, or joy in "broken cisterns that cannot hold water," as described in Jeremiah 2:13.

To overcome this emptiness, you must first recognize that the fundamental cause of spiritual void is our separation from God due to sin. God is perfectly holy and righteous, yet we have all turned our own way, falling short of His glory and leaving us spiritually bankrupt. Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23. Because of this separation, we often try to fill the void with career success, relationships, or even religious activity, but these things are finite and cannot satisfy an eternal soul.

The only remedy is found in Jesus Christ. He is truly God and truly man, the one who came to bridge the chasm between a holy God and sinful humanity. Jesus lived a life of perfect obedience, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and was buried. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, demonstrating His victory over death and His authority as Lord of all. Salvation and true life are found by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. When we trust in Him, we are brought into a "union with Christ," where His life becomes our life, and His Spirit begins the work of "sanctification," which is the process of making us more like Him.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

For those who already follow Christ but feel a season of dryness, the path forward involves returning to the "means of grace." These are the tools God has provided to nourish our souls. 

1. Devote yourself to the Word of God. Do not merely read it for information, but meditate on it to see the beauty of Christ. Begin reading the Gospel of John, focusing on the "I Am" statements of Jesus. John 6:35; John 10:11.
2. Pour out your heart in honest prayer. God already knows your emptiness; tell Him about it. Psalm 62:8. Ask Him to restore the joy of your salvation.
3. Commit to a local, Bible-preaching church. We were never meant to walk the Christian life in isolation. The fellowship of the saints and the preaching of the Word are vital for spiritual vitality. Hebrews 10:24-25.

True fullness is not found in a change of circumstances, but in the presence of a Person. Jesus promised that whoever drinks of the water He gives will never thirst again. John 4:13-14. Seek Him first, and you will find that He is more than enough.
How can I, a Muslim, become assured of paradise?

It is the deepest longing of the human heart to know where we stand with our Creator, yet many find themselves in a state of constant uncertainty, wondering if their good deeds will ever be enough to outweigh their sins. The Bible teaches that God is infinitely holy, and His eyes are too pure to look upon evil (Habakkuk 1:13). Because every one of us has fallen short of His perfect standard (Romans 3:23), no amount of human effort, religious ritual, or personal merit can bridge the gap between us and God. Even our most righteous acts are stained by our sinful nature (Isaiah 64:6). If our entrance into paradise depended on the quality of our own obedience, none of us could ever have true assurance.

However, the heart of the Gospel is that assurance is not found in what we do for God, but in what God has done for us through Jesus Christ. Jesus is the eternal Son of God who took on human flesh, being born of a virgin, to be the perfect Mediator between God and man (1 Timothy 2:5). He lived a life of flawless righteousness that we could never achieve. In an act of staggering love, He offered Himself as a sacrifice on the cross. This is the penal substitutionary atonement, where Jesus took the full weight of the punishment our sins deserved, satisfying the justice of God so that we might receive His mercy (1 Peter 2:24; Romans 3:25-26).

Jesus was buried, and on the third day He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). He is now exalted as Lord over all (Philippians 2:9-11). This means that salvation is a gift of grace, received through faith alone. We are justified, which means being declared legally righteous in God's sight, not because of our works, but because the righteousness of Christ is credited to us when we trust in Him (Ephesians 2:8-9; Romans 4:5).

You can have total assurance of paradise today because this promise rests on the finished work of Christ, which never changes. As the Apostle John wrote, "I have written these things to you who believe in the name of the Son of God, so that you may know that you have eternal life" (1 John 5:13). This is not a "perhaps" or a "hope so," but a "know" based on the faithfulness of God.

To have this assurance, you must turn from your own efforts and any other path, and put your full trust in Jesus Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus who died and rose for you. What is it that holds you back from trusting in His finished work?

As a practical next step, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John in the Bible. It was written specifically so that you might believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that by believing you may have life in His name (John 20:31). I also suggest you speak to God directly in prayer, asking Him to reveal the truth of His Word to your heart, and seek out a local church where the Bible is faithfully preached.
What is the spiritual gift of miracles?

The spiritual gift of miracles, or the "working of miracles," is a supernatural empowerment by the Holy Spirit that enables a believer to serve as an instrument for God’s extraordinary power, performing acts that supersede or suspend the natural laws of the physical world. In the Greek text of the New Testament, this is often referred to as "energemata dynameon," which literally translates to the "workings of powers" (1 Corinthians 12:10). These acts are not merely impressive feats but are divine "signs" intended to point toward a greater spiritual reality.

In the biblical record, the primary purpose of this gift was to authenticate the messenger and the message of God. When the prophets, the Lord Jesus, or the Apostles performed miracles, these acts served as a "seal" of divine approval, proving that they spoke with the authority of the Almighty. We see this clearly when Peter describes Jesus as "a man attested to you by God with miracles, wonders, and signs" (Acts 2:22), and again when the author of Hebrews notes that God bore witness to the Gospel "by signs and wonders and various miracles and by gifts of the Holy Spirit distributed according to His will" (Hebrews 2:4).

Within the Christian tradition, there are two primary perspectives regarding how this gift operates today. It is important to treat these views with charity, as faithful believers often differ on this point of interpretation.

*   **The Cessationist View:** This position, held by many in the Reformed tradition, suggests that the "sign gifts," including miracles, were specifically given for the foundational period of the Church. Their purpose was to authenticate the Apostles and the writing of the New Testament. According to this view, once the foundation was laid and the Scriptures were completed, these specific miraculous gifts ceased to be a regular office or individual endowment within the local church (2 Corinthians 12:12; Ephesians 2:20).

*   **The Continuationist View:** This position holds that all the gifts of the Spirit mentioned in the New Testament remain available to the Church today. Proponents believe the Holy Spirit continues to distribute the gift of miracles as He sees fit for the edification of the body and the advancement of the Gospel in unreached areas (1 Corinthians 12:7-11).

Regardless of which view one holds concerning the "gift" as a personal endowment, all orthodox Christians affirm that God is sovereign and remains a God of miracles. He is not a distant watchmaker but a living Father who hears the prayers of His people and can, at any moment, intervene in the natural order according to His holy will. We must never confuse the "gift of miracles" with the "God of miracles." The greatest miracle of all remains the Resurrection of Jesus Christ, a historical fact that confirms His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4; Romans 1:4).

As you study these things, remember that the pursuit of gifts must never eclipse the pursuit of the Giver. If you find yourself longing to see the power of God, start by looking at the Gospel. It is "the power of God for salvation to everyone who believes" (Romans 1:16). There is no miracle more profound than a dead heart being made alive through the grace of Christ.

If you have never experienced that spiritual rebirth, I invite you to turn to God today. Repent of your sins and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died for our transgressions and rose again for our justification, for He is the only way to the Father (John 14:6). What is it that prevents you from resting in His finished work this very hour?

As a practical step, I encourage you to read through the Gospel of John. Pay close attention to the "signs" Jesus performs and how they reveal His identity as the Son of God. Ask Him in prayer to give you a heart that treasures His truth above all earthly wonders.
What does it mean that God is merciful?

God’s mercy is His divine compassion and pity toward those in distress, specifically His holy restraint in withholding the punishment that our sins justly deserve. To say that God is merciful is to recognize that He does not treat us as our iniquities merit, but instead looks upon our fallen condition with a heart to relieve and pardon. While justice is God giving us what we deserve, and grace is God giving us what we do not deserve, mercy is God *not* giving us the judgment we have rightly earned.

This attribute is not a secondary thought in the mind of God, but is central to His very name. When God revealed His glory to Moses, He proclaimed Himself as the Lord, the Lord God, merciful and gracious, longsuffering, and abounding in goodness and truth. Exodus 34:6; Psalm 103:10-14. Because God is immutable, meaning He does not change, His mercy is a steady and reliable fountain for all who come to Him in their brokenness. It is because of the Lord’s mercies that we are not consumed, for His compassions never fail; they are new every morning. Lamentations 3:22-23.

The ultimate expression of this mercy is seen in the work of Jesus Christ. We were all by nature children of wrath, dead in our trespasses and separated from a holy God. However, God, who is rich in mercy, because of His great love with which He loved us, even when we were dead in trespasses, made us alive together with Christ. Ephesians 2:4-5. On the cross, Jesus underwent the penal substitutionary suffering that was due to us. He satisfied the demands of divine justice so that the floodgates of mercy could be opened to all who believe. Romans 5:8; 1 John 4:10.

Because God has been so merciful to us, we are called to be merciful to others, reflecting His character in our daily lives. Luke 6:36. A practical way to respond to this truth is to examine your own heart for any unforgiveness or harshness you are holding against someone else. Recognizing how much you have been forgiven should lead you to extend that same mercy to those around you.

If you find yourself burdened by the guilt of your past or the weight of your sins, do not run away from God, but run toward Him. His mercy is a vast and welcoming ocean for the repentant soul. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive full forgiveness and new life. What is it that keeps you from resting in His mercy today?
Who is the head of the church, biblically speaking?

The Lord Jesus Christ is the sole and absolute Head of the church. This is not a title of mere honor, but a declaration of His sovereign authority and His life-giving relationship to His people. As the Apostle Paul clearly writes, "And He is the head of the body, the church; He is the beginning and the firstborn from among the dead, so that in everything He might have the supremacy" (Colossians 1:18).

This headship means that Christ is the source of the church's life, the director of her mission, and the only Lawgiver for her worship and conduct. No human being—whether a pastor, a king, or a religious leader—can rightly claim to be the head of the church. To suggest that a sinful man could hold such a position is a grave error that denies the unique glory of Christ. While God appoints elders and pastors to serve as under-shepherds, they possess no independent authority. Their only duty is to faithfully administer the Word of the true Shepherd, to whom they must one day give an account (1 Peter 5:2-4; Hebrews 13:17).

The Scriptures consistently emphasize this reality:
*   God "placed all things under His feet and appointed Him to be head over everything for the church" (Ephesians 1:22).
*   "For the husband is the head of the wife as Christ is the head of the church, His body, of which He is the Savior" (Ephesians 5:23).

It is a profound comfort to know that the church does not depend on the frailty of men, but rests upon the finished work and ongoing rule of the Son of God. Because Jesus is the Head, He is the only source of our salvation. We were all separated from God by our sin and rebellion, but God, in His great mercy, sent His Son to reconcile us to Himself. Jesus lived a life of perfect obedience, died a substitutionary atoning death on the cross to satisfy the justice of God, and was buried. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, demonstrating His victory over sin and death. He is now exalted at the right hand of the Father, ruling over all things.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What is it that might be keeping you from placing your full trust in Him today?

As a practical next step in your walk with Him, I encourage you to read through the book of Ephesians this week. As you do, pay close attention to how Christ’s authority as Head provides every spiritual blessing and guides how we are to live together in the local church. Seek out a faithful, Bible-teaching church where Christ is honored as the supreme authority in all things.
Who is the ancient serpent of Revelation 12:9?

The ancient serpent mentioned in Revelation 12:9 is explicitly identified by the Apostle John as the devil and Satan, the one who deceives the whole world. This title "ancient" draws a direct line back to the beginning of human history in the Garden of Eden, where he appeared as a serpent to tempt our first parents into rebellion against God (Genesis 3:1; Genesis 3:14-15).

In the economy of Scripture, this being is not a mere metaphor for human fallenness but a real, personal spiritual adversary. He is a fallen angel who led a revolt against the Creator and now seeks to devour souls and accuse the people of God day and night (1 Peter 5:8; Revelation 12:10). His primary weapons are lies and deception, as he masquerades as an angel of light to lead many away from the simplicity of the Gospel (2 Corinthians 11:14).

However, the same passage that identifies him also proclaims his ultimate defeat. While the serpent successfully struck the heel of the Savior at the cross, the Lord Jesus Christ—the promised Seed of the woman—crushed the serpent’s head through His finished work (Genesis 3:15). By His sinless life, substitutionary death, and bodily resurrection, Jesus disarmed the powers and authorities, triumphing over them (Colossians 2:15).

If you feel the weight of temptation or the accusations of the enemy today, remember that the serpent is a defeated foe for those who are in Christ. You can find total forgiveness and protection by turning away from sin and trusting in Jesus alone. He has already won the war, and through faith in Him, you are transferred from the kingdom of darkness into the kingdom of His beloved Son (Colossians 1:13). What is preventing you from resting fully in the victory Christ has won for you?

As a practical step in your walk with God, I encourage you to immerse yourself in the Word of God daily, for it is the "sword of the Spirit" by which we resist the schemes of the devil (Ephesians 6:17). Stand firm in the truth that because Christ lives, the ancient serpent has no final claim on your soul.
Book of 1 John - Bible Survey

The First Epistle of John is a pastoral letter written to provide believers with the absolute certainty of their salvation while defending the orthodox faith against those who would distort the person and work of Jesus Christ. The Apostle John, writing with the heart of a fatherly shepherd, presents a series of "tests" by which a person may know they possess eternal life. 1 John 5:13.

The historical context of the letter involves a direct confrontation with early forms of Gnosticism, specifically an error known as Docetism, which claimed that Jesus did not truly possess a physical body but only "seemed" to be human. John strikes at this error immediately, testifying that he had heard, seen, and touched the Word of Life who was manifested in the flesh. 1 John 1:1-3. To deny the full humanity or the full deity of Jesus is to possess the spirit of the antichrist. 1 John 4:2-3.

John organizes his instruction around three primary themes often called the "Tests of Life":

1.  **The Doctrinal Test:** True believers must confess that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, who came in the flesh to be the propitiation for our sins. 1 John 2:22-23; 1 John 4:10; 1 John 4:15. This theological precision is non-negotiable, as salvation is found in no other person.
2.  **The Moral Test:** Those who truly know God will walk in obedience to His commandments. While John is clear that no one in this life is without sin, the true believer does not persist in a lifestyle of lawless rebellion. 1 John 1:8-10; 1 John 2:3-4; 1 John 3:4-10. Sanctification—the process of being made holy—is the necessary fruit of a genuine relationship with God.
3.  **The Social Test:** Love for fellow believers is the outward evidence of the inward work of the Holy Spirit. John insists that it is impossible to claim to love God, whom we cannot see, while hating a brother whom we can see. 1 John 3:14-15; 1 John 4:20-21.

At the heart of John's message is the glorious truth that God is light and God is love. 1 John 1:5; 1 John 4:8. Because God is light, we cannot fellowship with Him while walking in the darkness of unconfessed sin. Because God is love, He took the initiative to rescue us through the sacrifice of His Son.

If you find yourself questioning your standing before God, John encourages you to look to the finished work of Christ rather than your own fluctuating feelings. The Gospel declares that God is holy and we are sinners deserving of judgment, yet in His great mercy, Jesus Christ—the righteous One—took our place on the cross. He died for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death. 1 John 2:1-2; 1 John 5:11-12.

You can turn to God right now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for your forgiveness and peace with God. What is it that holds you back from resting entirely in His grace today?

As a practical next step in your discipleship, I encourage you to read through the five chapters of 1 John once a week for the next month. As you do, ask the Holy Spirit to show you where your life aligns with these tests and to deepen your confidence in the sufficiency of Jesus. Seek out a local body of believers where the Word of God is preached faithfully, for we were never meant to walk in the light alone. 1 John 1:7.
What happens at the final judgment?

The final judgment is the day when the Lord Jesus Christ will return in power and glory to sit upon His throne and render a perfect, inescapable, and eternal verdict upon every human being who has ever lived. It is not a religious metaphor but a historical certainty guaranteed by the resurrection of Jesus from the dead. Acts 17:31. On that day, the secrets of every heart will be disclosed, and God’s perfect justice and infinite mercy will be fully displayed to the entire universe. Romans 2:16.

For those who have rejected God, the judgment is a day of account based on their own works. The Bible describes "books" being opened that record every thought, word, and deed. Revelation 20:12. Since God’s standard is absolute holiness and no one is without sin, those judged by their own merits will face the "second death," which is eternal separation from God in the lake of fire. Revelation 20:15; Romans 3:23. This is the heavy reality of God's righteous wrath against sin.

However, for those who belong to Christ, the final judgment is a day of ultimate vindication and joy. While believers will also give an account of their lives, they do not face the judgment of condemnation. Romans 8:1. This is because of the glorious truth of justification: through faith, the believer’s sins were legally transferred to Jesus at the cross, where He endured the penal substitutionary punishment we deserved. In exchange, His perfect righteousness is credited to the believer. 2 Corinthians 5:21. The names of the redeemed are found in the "Lamb’s Book of Life," and they are welcomed into the New Heavens and the New Earth to dwell with God forever in a state of perfect peace and holiness. Revelation 21:1–4.

The Gospel is the only hope for any of us on that day. God is holy and just, and our sin has separated us from Him. Yet, in His great love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who took on human nature, lived a sinless life, and died a sacrificial death on the cross to pay the debt for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and His authority as Lord of all. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works of our own.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical step, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John in the Bible this week to see the character of Jesus, the Judge who became our Savior. If you have questions about how to follow Him, seek out a faithful, Bible-teaching local church where the elders can pray with you and guide you in the truth of God’s Word. James 5:16.
In what ways is becoming a Christian becoming an entirely new man / woman?

To become a Christian is not to undergo a mere moral renovation or a superficial change of habits, but to experience a supernatural resurrection of the soul. The Bible describes this transformation as being a "new creation," where the old life has passed away and an entirely new life has begun. Second Corinthians 5:17. This change is total, affecting your standing before God, your internal nature, and your ultimate purpose in the world.

First, becoming a new person involves a radical change in your legal standing before God, a doctrine known as justification. Apart from Christ, every person is "dead in trespasses and sins," living under the righteous condemnation of God’s law. Ephesians 2:1; Romans 3:23. However, when you trust in Christ, God credits His perfect righteousness to your account. You are no longer viewed as a guilty rebel, but as a justified child of God, completely forgiven and at peace with your Creator. Romans 5:1; Romans 8:1.

Second, there is a fundamental change in your nature through regeneration, or being "born again." John 3:3. God does not simply patch up your old heart; He replaces it. He promises in the Scriptures, "I will give you a new heart and put a new spirit within you; I will remove your heart of stone and give you a heart of flesh." Ezekiel 36:26. This means your deepest desires are transformed. Where you once loved sin and were indifferent toward God, the new man or woman possesses a genuine love for holiness and a hunger for God’s Word. This is the work of the Holy Spirit indwelling you, providing both the desire and the power to walk in obedience. Philippians 2:13.

Third, your identity and relationship to the world are redefined. You are moved from the kingdom of darkness into the kingdom of God’s beloved Son. Colossians 1:13. This is an adoption into the family of God, where you can now cry out to Him as "Abba, Father." Romans 8:15. As a new creation, you no longer live for temporary, selfish ambitions, but for the glory of the One who died and rose again for you. Second Corinthians 5:15.

While the "old man"—the sinful flesh—remains a source of struggle until we reach heaven, the "new man" is the true reality of who you are in Christ. This leads to progressive sanctification, where you daily "put off" the old self and "put on" the new self, which is being renewed in knowledge according to the image of its Creator. Colossians 3:9–10.

If you have not yet experienced this new life, know that it cannot be earned through your own efforts or religious rituals. It is a gift of God's sovereign grace. You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died for your sins and rose bodily from the grave to give you eternal life. What is it that holds you back from trusting Him and receiving this new heart today?

As a practical next step in walking as a new creation, I encourage you to begin reading the Gospel of John in the Bible. Set aside time each morning to ask God to speak to you through His Word, and seek out a local, Bible-believing church where you can be supported by other believers in this new life.
What are the Pauline epistles?

The Pauline epistles are the thirteen letters in the New Testament written by the Apostle Paul under the inspiration of the Holy Spirit to provide theological instruction, pastoral oversight, and practical guidance to the early Church. These letters serve as a primary foundation for Christian doctrine, explaining the mystery of Christ and the implications of His resurrection for the believer's life.

The collection is traditionally divided into two main groups based on their recipients:

**Letters to Churches**
These nine letters were addressed to specific congregations to address local issues and establish core doctrines:
*   **Romans:** A comprehensive presentation of the Gospel, focusing on the righteousness of God and justification by faith (Romans 1:16–17; Romans 3:21–26).
*   **1 Corinthians and 2 Corinthians:** Letters to a troubled church dealing with divisions, morality, spiritual gifts, and the defense of Paul's apostolic authority (1 Corinthians 1:10; 2 Corinthians 5:17–21).
*   **Galatians:** A sharp defense of the Gospel of grace against those who sought to require works of the law for salvation (Galatians 2:16; Galatians 5:1).
*   **Ephesians:** An exploration of the believer's spiritual blessings in Christ and the unity of the Church (Ephesians 1:3–14; Ephesians 2:8–10).
*   **Philippians:** A letter of joy and encouragement, highlighting the humility and exaltation of Christ (Philippians 2:5–11; Philippians 4:4).
*   **Colossians:** A focus on the preeminence and sufficiency of Jesus Christ over all creation and philosophy (Colossians 1:15–20; Colossians 2:9).
*   **1 Thessalonians and 2 Thessalonians:** Letters providing comfort regarding the return of Christ and exhortations to holy living (1 Thessalonians 4:13–18; 2 Thessalonians 2:1–12).

**Letters to Individuals**
These four letters, often called the Pastoral and Personal Epistles, were sent to specific coworkers and friends:
*   **1 Timothy and 2 Timothy:** Written to Paul’s young protégé, Timothy, regarding church leadership, sound doctrine, and perseverance in ministry (1 Timothy 3:1–13; 2 Timothy 3:16–17).
*   **Titus:** Instructions to Titus on organizing the church in Crete and the link between good works and sound grace (Titus 2:11–14; Titus 3:4–7).
*   **Philemon:** A personal appeal for mercy and Christian brotherhood regarding a runaway slave named Onesimus (Philemon 1:15–17).

Central to all these writings is the "hypostatic union"—the truth that Jesus is fully God and fully man—and the glorious reality of "penal substitutionary atonement," where Christ died in the place of sinners to satisfy the justice of God. Paul tirelessly preached that salvation is a gift received through faith, not earned by merit.

If you find yourself burdened by your own efforts to be "good enough" for God, I encourage you to look to the Christ Paul proclaimed. You can turn to God today, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died for your offenses and was raised for your justification. Have you ever considered what it means to rest entirely in the finished work of Jesus?
How can I be sure I am praying according to the will of God?

To pray according to the will of God is to bring our heart’s desires into harmony with His revealed Word and to submit our unknown future to His perfect wisdom. This assurance does not come from a mystical feeling or a private sign, but from the objective truth found in the Holy Scriptures. 

The first step in praying according to His will is to distinguish between what God has revealed and what He has kept secret. Deuteronomy 29:29 teaches that the secret things belong to the LORD our God, but the things revealed belong to us and to our children forever. When we pray for our sanctification, for the salvation of the lost, or for the strength to endure trials with joy, we can be absolutely certain we are praying according to His will because He has explicitly commanded these things in the Bible. As the apostle John wrote, this is the confidence we have in approaching God: that if we ask anything according to his will, he hears us. And if we know that he hears us, whatever we ask, we know that we have what we asked of him (1 John 5:14-15).

When you are praying for specific matters where the Bible does not give a direct command—such as which job to take, where to live, or the timing of a physical healing—you pray according to His will by adopting the posture of Christ in the Garden of Gethsemane. Jesus petitioned the Father with His honest desire but concluded with the ultimate expression of trust: "Yet not as I will, but as You will" (Matthew 26:39). This is not a prayer of apathetic resignation, but a prayer of deep faith in the character of a Father who is too wise to be mistaken and too good to be unkind.

We also have the wonderful promise that the Holy Spirit assists us in our limitations. We often do not know what we ought to pray for, but the Spirit himself intercedes for us through wordless groans. And he who searches our hearts knows the mind of the Spirit, because the Spirit intercedes for God’s people in accordance with the will of God (Romans 8:26-27). This means that even when your words are clumsy or your understanding is clouded, the Spirit translates the cries of a sincere heart into the perfect will of the Father.

To grow in this assurance, I encourage you to begin "praying the Bible." Open to a passage such as the Lord’s Prayer in Matthew 6:9-13 or the apostolic prayers in Ephesians 1:15-23 and use those inspired words as the foundation for your own. When you pray the very words God gave us, you can be sure you are speaking His language.

Ultimately, remember that our access to the Father is granted only through the person and finished work of Jesus Christ. He is the one Mediator between God and men, who died for our sins and rose again to give us new life (1 Timothy 2:5). If you have not yet placed your trust in Him, I invite you to turn from your sin and believe in the Lord Jesus today. What holds you back from trusting the One who loves you perfectly and intercedes for you even now? 

As a practical next step, try setting aside ten minutes today to pray through Psalm 23, turning each verse into a personal request for God's guidance and provision. Trust that as you delight yourself in the Lord through His Word, He will sovereignly align the desires of your heart with His own (Psalm 37:4).
What does the Bible say about busyness / being too busy?

The Bible views busyness not merely as a matter of a full calendar, but as a condition of the heart that can lead to spiritual distraction and the neglect of our primary devotion to the Lord. While Scripture commends diligent labor and warns against laziness, it frequently cautions against the "anxious toil" that arises when we forget that God is the sovereign sustainer of all things (Psalm 127:2).

The most vivid example of the danger of busyness is found in the account of Martha and Mary. Martha was "distracted by all her preparations," while Mary sat at the feet of Jesus. Our Lord did not rebuke Martha for her desire to serve, but for her "anxious and troubled" heart, noting that only one thing is truly necessary (Luke 10:41–42). This teaches us that even good activities—ministry, family care, or honest work—become harmful when they pull us away from the presence of Christ. 

Biblically, chronic over-busyness often reveals a lack of trust in God’s providence. When we feel we must do everything ourselves, we are acting as if the world rests on our shoulders rather than His. James warns those who make endless plans without acknowledging the Lord’s will, reminding us that our lives are a "mist that appears for a little while and then vanishes" (James 4:13–15). True wisdom involves numbering our days so that we may gain a heart of wisdom, prioritizing eternal fruit over temporary checklists (Psalm 90:12).

Furthermore, the command to observe a Sabbath rest is a perpetual reminder that we are creatures, not the Creator (Exodus 20:8–11). By resting, we testify that God is the one who provides and that our worth is not found in our productivity but in our relationship with Him. This is a vital part of our sanctification, as we learn to bring every thought and every hour under the lordship of Christ.

If you find yourself overwhelmed, the first step is to bring your burdens to the Lord in prayer. Consider your schedule and ask if your "busyness" is actually a form of idolatry where work or the approval of others has taken the place of God. A practical next step is to set aside a specific time each morning for the "one thing necessary"—reading the Word and seeking God’s face—before the demands of the day begin.

Ultimately, the remedy for a frantic soul is the Gospel. We often stay busy because we are trying to prove our worth or justify our existence through our achievements. But the Bible tells us that we are justified by grace alone through faith alone in the finished work of Jesus Christ (Ephesians 2:8–9). You do not have to earn God’s love; it was demonstrated when Christ died for us while we were still sinners (Romans 5:8).

Jesus offers a rest that the world cannot give, saying, "Come to Me, all who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest" (Matthew 11:28). This invitation is not just for a physical nap, but for the soul-rest that comes from knowing your sins are forgiven and your future is secure in Him. You can turn to God now, repent of the pride of self-sufficiency, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What is it that keeps you from resting in His finished work today?
What are the seven baptisms mentioned in the Bible, and what do they mean?

The word baptism in the New Testament translates from the Greek word *baptizo*, which fundamentally means to dip, plunge, or immerse. While many people think only of water when they hear this word, the Scriptures use it to describe several distinct types of "immersions" or identifications. Understanding these helps us see the depth of God's work in history and in the heart of a believer.

Here are the seven baptisms found in the Bible:

**1. The Baptism of Moses**
This refers to the Israelites being "immersed" into the leadership and providence of Moses as they passed through the Red Sea. They were identified with him as their God-appointed leader and deliverer from Egypt.
*   **Supporting Passage:** 1 Corinthians 10:1-2

**2. The Baptism of John (Repentance)**
John the Baptist preached a baptism of repentance for the forgiveness of sins, preparing the way for the Messiah. It was an outward sign of an internal heart change, signaling that the individual was ready to receive the coming King, Jesus Christ.
*   **Supporting Passage:** Matthew 3:11; Mark 1:4; Luke 3:3

**3. The Baptism of Jesus (His Suffering)**
Jesus spoke of a baptism He had to undergo, referring not to water, but to His impending ordeal on the cross. He was "immersed" in the wrath of God and the sorrows of death to secure our redemption. It highlights the reality of His substitutionary atonement for our sins.
*   **Supporting Passage:** Mark 10:38-39; Luke 12:50

**4. The Baptism of Fire**
This is often misunderstood as a spiritual blessing, but in context, it refers to the fire of God’s judgment. When John the Baptist said Jesus would baptize with the Holy Spirit and fire, he followed it by describing the winnowing fork and the burning of the "chaff" with unquenchable fire. It is the final immersion of the unrepentant into judgment.
*   **Supporting Passage:** Matthew 3:11-12; Luke 3:16-17

**5. The Baptism of the Holy Spirit**
At the moment of conversion, the Holy Spirit immerses the believer into the body of Christ, the Church. This is a sovereign work of God that joins all true believers together regardless of their background, making them one in Christ.
*   **Supporting Passage:** 1 Corinthians 12:13; Acts 1:5

**6. The Baptism into Christ (Identification)**
Closely related to Spirit baptism, this describes our spiritual union with Jesus. When we trust in Him, we are identified with His death, burial, and resurrection. Our old self is crucified with Him, and we are raised to walk in newness of life. This is the ground of our justification and sanctification.
*   **Supporting Passage:** Romans 6:3-4; Galatians 3:27

**7. The Baptism of Water (Believer’s Baptism)**
This is the Christian ordinance commanded by Jesus. It is a public profession of faith where a believer is immersed in water to symbolize what has already happened spiritually: they have died to sin and been raised with Christ. While water baptism does not save the soul, it is a vital act of obedience and a beautiful picture of the Gospel.
*   **Supporting Passage:** Matthew 28:19; Acts 2:38-41; Acts 8:36-38

It is vital to recognize that while water baptism is a beautiful sign, the most important reality is your spiritual "baptism into Christ." Without being joined to Him by faith, the water of baptism is merely a bath. The Scriptures are clear that we are saved by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

The Lord Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, lived a perfect life, died a sacrificial death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord over all. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. 

Have you ever considered whether you have truly been united to Christ by faith, or have you perhaps relied on an outward ritual? I encourage you to read the Gospel of John to see the beauty of the Savior who invites you to come to Him.
What do you think about the Bible codes? Is there any validity to them?

The pursuit of "Bible codes," often referred to as Equidistant Letter Sequencing (ELS), is a modern phenomenon that suggests hidden messages are embedded within the Hebrew text of the Old Testament. While these patterns can be mathematically intriguing to some, we must approach them with great pastoral caution and theological discernment. The Bible is not a puzzle box to be solved by computers, but a divine revelation to be heard, believed, and obeyed.

The validity of the Bible codes is deeply questionable for several reasons, primarily because they conflict with the historical Christian understanding of how God speaks to His people.

First, we must uphold the doctrine of the perspicuity, or clarity, of Scripture. This means that the central message of the Bible—the way of salvation and the nature of God—is made plain enough for the simplest person to understand through the diligent use of ordinary means, such as reading and preaching. God did not hide the Gospel in a cryptographic cipher; He proclaimed it openly through prophets and ultimately through His Son. As it says in Deuteronomy 29:29, "The secret things belong to the LORD our God, but the things revealed belong to us and to our children forever, so that we may follow all the words of this law."

Second, we believe in the sufficiency of Scripture. The Apostle Paul instructs us that the written Word is breathed out by God and is profitable for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for training in righteousness, so that the man of God may be complete and equipped for every good work (2  Timothy 3:16–17). If we need a computer algorithm to find "hidden" truths, we are essentially claiming that the plain text of the Bible is insufficient for our spiritual needs. This shifts our focus away from the clear commands of Christ and toward a hunt for sensational novelties.

Third, the method itself is fraught with danger. Critics have demonstrated that similar "codes" can be found in any sufficiently large body of text, such as secular novels, simply because of the laws of probability. When we treat the Bible this way, we risk turning the holy Word of God into a tool for divination or a book of magic. We are called to interpret the Bible according to its literary context, historical setting, and grammatical meaning, rather than searching for patterns that the original authors and the Holy Spirit did not intend to convey.

The true "code" of the Bible is not hidden at all; it is the person and work of Jesus Christ, who is the theme of every book from Genesis to Revelation. The most important message you will ever find in the pages of Scripture is that God is holy, we are sinners deserving of judgment, and yet God in His mercy sent Jesus Christ to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins. He rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4).

I encourage you to set aside the search for hidden codes and instead feast upon the plain, majestic truths of the Gospel. You do not need a secret key to know God; you need only to trust in the Savior He has clearly revealed. You can turn to Him today, repent of your sins, and find full forgiveness and eternal life through faith in Jesus Christ alone.

As a practical step, I suggest you commit to reading one of the Gospels, such as the Gospel of John, and ask the Holy Spirit to show you the glory of Christ in the plain meaning of the text. Trust in what God has openly spoken, for His Word is a lamp to our feet and a light to our path (Psalm 119:105).
How can I see the hand of God moving in my life?

To see the hand of God moving in your life, you must first look through the lens of Holy Scripture, for the Bible is the lamp that illuminates the path of His providence. We often expect God to move only in the miraculous or the extraordinary, yet He most frequently works through the quiet, steady governance of our daily circumstances and the internal transformation of our hearts.

God's hand is best recognized when we understand that He is sovereign over every detail of existence. This biblical truth, often called Divine Providence, means that God is not a distant observer but an active participant in the lives of His children. You can see His movement by looking for His work in these specific areas:

**The Lens of His Word**
You cannot recognize what God is doing if you do not know who God is. The more you saturate your mind with Scripture, the more your "spiritual eyes" are trained to see His character at work. When a verse provides comfort in a trial or a command brings conviction of sin, that is the Holy Spirit moving within you. As it is written, "Your word is a lamp to my feet and a light to my path" Psalm 119:105.

**The Work of Sanctification**
One of the clearest evidences of God’s hand is the change in your own desires. If you find yourself hating the sin you once loved, or desiring to forgive someone who has wronged you, that is not a product of human willpower. It is the work of God. We are promised that "He who began a good work in you will carry it on to completion until the day of Christ Jesus" Philippians 1:6. This internal growth in holiness, or sanctification, is a sure sign of His active presence.

**The Mystery of Sovereignty and Suffering**
We often struggle to see God in the "frowning providences" of life, such as loss or disappointment. However, the Bible teaches that God moves even through these shadows to produce endurance and trust. You can see His hand in the way He sustains you when your own strength fails. "And we know that God works all things together for the good of those who love Him, who are called according to His purpose" Romans 8:28. Even when we cannot see His face, we can trust His heart, knowing that His hand is always guiding the scalpel of the Great Physician for our ultimate healing.

**The Provision of Common and Special Grace**
Look at the "coincidences" of your day. Perhaps a timely word from a friend, a provision of a need just as it arose, or an unexpected door opening. These are not accidents; they are the intentional movements of a Father who cares for His children. "Every good and perfect gift is from above, coming down from the Father of the heavenly lights, who does not change like shifting shadows" James 1:17.

The most profound way to see God's hand is to look at the person and work of Jesus Christ. If you ever doubt God’s involvement in your life, look to the Cross. There, God’s hand was most active, bruising His own Son to provide a way for you to be reconciled to Him. This is the Gospel: that although we are sinners deserving of judgment, God in His mercy sent Jesus to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day.

I invite you to consider your standing before this sovereign God. You can turn to Him now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for your salvation. What is it that holds you back from fully trusting His hand today?

As a practical step, I encourage you to begin a "Providence Journal." At the end of each day, write down three specific moments where you saw God's character—His patience, His provision, His wisdom, or even His gentle correction. When you make it a habit to look for Him, you will find that He has been there all along, for "He is not far from each one of us" Acts 17:27.
Are we to love the sinner but hate the sin?

While the exact phrase "love the sinner, hate the sin" is not found in the pages of Scripture, the principle it seeks to express is woven throughout the Gospel. It calls us to a narrow and holy path: to maintain an uncompromising hatred for the rebellion that destroys the soul and dishonors God, while simultaneously possessing a self-sacrificing, redemptive love for the person who is created in the image of God.

To hate sin is a mark of a heart that has been renewed by the Holy Spirit. God is perfectly holy, and because He is light, in Him there is no darkness at all as stated in 1 John 1:5. Therefore, we are commanded to "Abhor what is evil; hold fast to what is good" in Romans 12:9. Sin is not a peripheral issue; it is a lawless rejection of the Creator that brings death and judgment. We hate sin because it grieves the heart of God, it necessitated the agonizing death of our Lord Jesus Christ, and it enslaves the people we are called to love.

However, our hatred for sin must never be used as a cloak for animosity toward our fellow man. We must remember that we ourselves were once defined by our rebellion. The Bible reminds us that "God proves His love for us in this: While we were still sinners, Christ died for us" in Romans 5:8. If God had waited for us to stop sinning before He loved us, we would all be utterly lost. 

In our interactions with a fallen world, we are called to follow the example of Christ, who was "full of grace and truth" in John 1:14. Truth without love is harsh and drives people away from the remedy they so desperately need. Love without truth is sentimental and leaves people comfortably on the road to destruction. True biblical love seeks the highest good of the neighbor, and the highest good for any person is their reconciliation to God through Jesus Christ. 

We see this balance in the exhortation to "snatch others from the fire and save them; to others show mercy, mixed with fear—hating even the clothing stained by corrupted flesh" in Jude 1:23. We move toward the sinner with mercy, but we do so with a cautious fear of the sin itself, refusing to minimize its danger or affirm its practice.

The heart of the Gospel is this: God is a righteous Judge who must punish sin, yet He is a merciful Father who desires to save sinners. He resolved this by sending His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived the perfect life we could not live and died the death we deserved. Through His bodily resurrection, He broke the power of sin and death. Salvation is not earned by our efforts to be "better," but is received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. When a person turns from their sin and trusts in Jesus, they are justified—declared righteous in God's sight—and begin the lifelong process of sanctification.

If you find yourself struggling to love those whose sins you find repulsive, look first to the cross. See there the price of your own forgiveness. If you are the one feeling weighed down by the guilt of your own sin, know that the Savior’s arms are open wide. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What is it that prevents you from resting in His grace today?

As a practical step, I encourage you to choose one person in your life whose lifestyle or actions you find difficult to stomach. Spend time this week praying specifically for their soul, asking God to grant them repentance and to fill your heart with a genuine, Christ-like compassion that desires their salvation more than your own comfort.
Is the Buddhist concept of Zen compatible with the Christian faith?

The Buddhist concept of Zen and the Christian faith are fundamentally incompatible because they rest upon entirely different foundations regarding the nature of God, the condition of humanity, and the way of salvation. While Zen may offer a certain temporal calmness or mindfulness that appeals to a restless world, its core principles deny the essential truths revealed in the Holy Scriptures.

At the heart of Zen is the pursuit of "satori" or enlightenment through self-effort and meditation, often focusing on the concept of "emptiness" or the realization that the individual self is an illusion. In stark contrast, Christianity is built upon the objective reality of a personal, sovereign, and holy God who created the heavens and the earth (Genesis 1:1). The Bible teaches that we are not called to empty our minds into a void, but to fill our minds with the truth of God’s Word (Psalm 1:2; Philippians 4:8).

Consider these three primary areas where the two paths diverge:

First, the nature of God is the most significant divide. Zen is essentially non-theistic, viewing "ultimate reality" as an impersonal force or state of being. The Christian faith, however, rests upon the Trinity: one God eternally existing in three persons—Father, Son, and Holy Spirit (Matthew 28:19). God is not a "nothingness" to be realized, but a glorious Person to be known, loved, and worshiped.

Second, the human problem is viewed differently. Zen suggests that our suffering stems from ignorance of our true nature. The Bible, however, diagnoses our condition far more seriously, teaching that our problem is sin—a moral rebellion against a holy God that separates us from Him (Romans 3:23). We do not need to "awaken" to our own inner divinity; we need to be reconciled to our Creator because we are spiritually dead in our trespasses (Ephesians 2:1).

Third, the means of restoration are irreconcilable. Zen relies on the individual’s discipline and meditation to achieve clarity. Christianity teaches that salvation is a gift of God’s grace, impossible to achieve by human effort. It is secured only through the historical death and bodily resurrection of Jesus Christ, who is the only mediator between God and man (1 Timothy 2:5). Jesus did not come to teach us how to save ourselves through meditation; He came to save us by giving His life as a ransom for many (Mark 10:45).

The true peace that the human heart craves is not found in the cessation of thought or the denial of the self, but in the forgiveness of sins and the gift of eternal life through Jesus Christ. He said, "I am the way and the truth and the life. No one comes to the Father except through Me" (John 14:6).

The Gospel is this: God is perfectly holy, and we have all sinned against Him. Yet, in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ—truly God and truly man—to live the perfect life we could not live and to die the sacrificial death we deserved. He was buried and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Salvation is by grace alone, through faith alone, in Christ alone.

You can turn to God even now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. In Him, you will find a peace that surpasses all understanding, grounded not in your own efforts, but in His finished work on the cross (Philippians 4:7). What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is the Wailing Wall / Western Wall?

The Western Wall, often referred to as the Wailing Wall or the Kotel, is an ancient limestone retaining wall located in the Old City of Jerusalem. It was originally constructed as part of the massive expansion of the Second Temple courtyard begun by Herod the Great around 19 BC. While it was not a wall of the Temple building itself, it served as a support for the vast platform upon which the Temple stood.

When the Roman legions destroyed Jerusalem and the Temple in 70 AD, as Jesus had precisely foretold in Matthew 24:1–2, this section of the outer retaining wall was left standing. For centuries, it has remained the holiest site where Jewish people are permitted to pray, chosen for its proximity to the site of the Holy of Holies. The term "Wailing Wall" was coined by European observers who witnessed the Jewish people gathered there to weep and mourn over the loss of their Holy House and the exile of their nation.

To understand the significance of this wall, we must look at the purpose of the Temple it once supported. The Temple was the center of biblical worship, the place where the presence of God dwelt among His people and where animal sacrifices were offered to atone for sin. However, the Scriptures teach that these earthly structures and ceremonies were a "shadow of the things to come," pointing toward a greater reality (Colossians 2:17).

Jesus Christ, in His incarnation, identified Himself as the true Temple (John 2:19–21). He is the place where God and man are perfectly reconciled. While the stones of the Western Wall remind us of a vanished era of animal sacrifices, the Gospel announces that Jesus has offered one sacrifice for sins for all time (Hebrews 10:12). By His death and bodily resurrection, He became the "chief cornerstone" of a new, spiritual house made of "living stones"—believers from every nation (1 Peter 2:4–6).

There is a profound sadness in seeing many seek God at a wall of silent stone, yet there is also a great hope. The wall stands as a historical witness to the truth of God's Word and the fulfillment of Christ's prophecies. We no longer need to travel to a specific mountain or wall to find God's favor, for Jesus told the woman at the well that the hour has come when true worshipers will worship the Father in spirit and truth (John 4:23–24).

If you find yourself moved by the history of the Temple and the longing for God’s presence, I encourage you to look to the One the Temple was intended to foreshadow. You do not need an ancient wall to reach God; you can turn to Him directly today. By repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose again for you, you can experience the forgiveness and peace that the Temple sacrifices could only point toward but never fully provide. 

What is it about the history of the Temple that most interests you? As a practical step in your study, you might find it helpful to read the Book of Hebrews, which beautifully explains how Jesus Christ is the fulfillment of everything the Old Testament Temple represented.
What is Taizé worship, and is it biblical?

Taizé worship is a style of Christian prayer and music that originated in the Taizé Community, an ecumenical monastic order in France founded in the 1940s. It is characterized by short, simple songs—often called chants or canons—that are repeated many times to foster a meditative atmosphere, interspersed with long periods of silence, Scripture readings, and often the use of icons and candles.

In evaluating whether Taizé worship is biblical, we must look at both its form and its foundation with a heart that loves the truth and seeks the glory of Christ.

The practice of silence and meditation is indeed found in Scripture. We are told to "Be still, and know that I am God" in Psalm 46:10, and we see the value of meditating on God's Word day and night in Psalm 1:2. In a world of constant noise, the discipline of quietness before the Lord is a precious thing. However, biblical meditation is never an emptying of the mind or a pursuit of a mystical "feeling"; it is the active filling of the mind with the objective truths of God’s revelation.

There are several areas where Taizé worship raises significant concerns for the believer committed to the sufficiency of Scripture:

First, the nature of the repetition in Taizé songs can border on what Jesus warned against in the Sermon on the Mount. He said, "And when you pray, do not keep on babbling like pagans, for they think they will be heard because of their many words" in Matthew 6:7. While singing a chorus more than once is not inherently sinful, the goal of worship should be the engagement of the intellect with the truth. Worship must be "in spirit and in truth" as stated in John 4:24. If repetition is used to induce a trance-like state or an emotional "high" rather than to deepen our understanding of God’s character, it drifts away from the biblical model.

Second, the ecumenical foundation of Taizé is a matter of serious doctrinal concern. The community intentionally brings together individuals from Roman Catholic, Orthodox, and Protestant backgrounds by minimizing the "dogmatic" differences between them. While peace and unity are beautiful, true Christian unity can only exist where there is agreement on the essentials of the Gospel. If a worship service purposefully obscures the doctrine of justification by faith alone or the unique mediatorial work of Christ to avoid "offending" various traditions, it ceases to be truly apostolic. We must remember that there is "one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus" in 1 Timothy 2:5, and any worship that softens this exclusive truth for the sake of a generic spiritual experience is on dangerous ground.

Third, the use of icons and specific liturgical elements often found in Taizé can lead toward a form of mysticism that replaces the clear preaching of the Word. The Bible teaches that "faith comes from hearing, and hearing through the word of Christ" in Romans 10:17. A service that prioritizes atmosphere, aesthetics, and subjective feelings over the bold proclamation of the Law and the Gospel risks leaving the soul malnourished.

If you are drawn to the quietness of Taizé because your soul is weary, I encourage you to find that rest in the person of Jesus Christ through His Word. True peace is not found in a candlelit room or a repetitive chant, but in the finished work of Christ on the cross, where He bore our sins and reconciled us to God. 

I suggest you spend time in the Gospel of John, reading of the Savior who is the Light of the World. Seek out a local church where the Bible is preached with clarity and where the hymns and songs are rich with theological truth that informs your heart. You might also find it helpful to set aside ten minutes each morning to pray through a Psalm, letting the inspired words of God guide your thoughts into a deep, biblical stillness.

What is most important is your standing before God. We have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, but God, being rich in mercy, sent His Son to die and rise again so that all who repent and trust in Him might have eternal life. You can turn to God even now, renouncing self-reliance and trusting in Jesus Christ alone for the forgiveness of your sins. Have you placed your full weight and hope on Christ alone for your salvation?
Does the Old Testament truly predict a second advent of the Messiah?

The Old Testament does indeed predict a second coming of the Messiah, though these prophecies are often interwoven with descriptions of His first coming. While the first advent of Jesus Christ was characterized by humility, suffering, and the atonement for sin, the prophets also spoke of a day when the Messiah would appear in glory to judge the nations and establish a visible, eternal kingdom.

The reason many in Israel missed the distinction is often described as prophetic foreshortening. Just as a traveler looking at two distant mountain peaks might see them as one because they are in the same line of sight, the Old Testament prophets often saw the two advents of Christ as a single event of redemption and judgment.

Consider the vision given to the prophet Daniel, who saw "One like a Son of Man, coming with the clouds of heaven" to be given "dominion, glory, and a kingdom, that all peoples, nations, and languages should serve Him" in Daniel 7:13-14. While Jesus applied the title "Son of Man" to Himself during His earthly ministry, the full manifestation of this universal dominion and the visible "coming with the clouds" awaits His return.

The prophet Zechariah provides a startlingly clear picture of this transition from suffering to glory. In Zechariah 12:10, the Lord says, "And I will pour out on the house of David and on the people of Jerusalem a spirit of grace and prayer, and they will look on Me, the One they have pierced." This points back to the crucifixion, but the context describes a future day of national repentance and deliverance. Furthermore, Zechariah 14:3-4 describes a day when the Lord's feet will "stand on the Mount of Olives," and He will come to fight for His people and reign as King over all the earth as seen in Zechariah 14:9.

The necessity of a second advent is also found in the promises regarding the throne of David. Isaiah 9:6-7 promises a Child who is the "Mighty God" and "Prince of Peace," stating that "of the increase of His government and peace there will be no end." While Christ is currently reigning at the right hand of the Father, the complete subduing of all enemies and the final renewal of the cosmos will be realized when He returns personally and bodily.

This truth is not merely a matter of academic interest but is the "blessed hope" of the believer. In His first coming, Jesus Christ came as the Lamb of God to take away the sin of the world through His sacrificial death and bodily resurrection. He lived the perfect life we could not live and died the death we deserved, satisfying the justice of God so that all who repent and trust in Him might be saved by grace alone through faith alone.

If you have not yet placed your trust in Him, I encourage you to consider that the same scriptures which accurately predicted His birth, His rejection, and His death also guarantee His return as Judge. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you.

As a practical step in your study, I suggest reading through the book of Hebrews, which beautifully explains how Christ fulfilled the Old Testament types and shadows in His first coming and why we now wait for Him to appear a second time, not to bear sin, but to bring salvation to those who are waiting for Him as stated in Hebrews 9:28. What do you find most challenging about the idea of Christ returning to judge the world?
Contemporary Christian music - is it honoring to God? Should it be used in church services?

The question of whether contemporary music honors God is less about the date of its composition and more about the depth of its devotion to the truth. Music in the church is a vehicle for the Word of Christ to dwell in us richly, as we are commanded to teach and admonish one another in all wisdom through psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs (Colossians 3:16). Whether a song honors God depends primarily on three factors: its theological accuracy, its focus on the glory of God, and the posture of the heart of those singing.

To honor God, music must be anchored in sound doctrine (orthodoxy). If a song contains vague lyrics that could apply to a human romance as easily as to the Creator, or if it presents an imbalanced view of God’s character by ignoring His holiness and justice while overemphasizing His friendship, it fails to provide the spiritual nourishment the flock requires. True worship must be in spirit and in truth (John 4:24). When the lyrics of a contemporary song are biblically faithful, clear, and Christ-centered, they can be a powerful tool for doxology, which is praise directed toward God for who He is and what He has done.

The use of contemporary music in church services is considered a tertiary issue, meaning it is a matter of Christian liberty where faithful believers may disagree while remaining in fellowship. There are two primary historic views on this matter:

*   The Traditional or Regulative View: Some churches prefer to stick to the historic hymns and psalms of the faith. The reasoning is that these songs have been tested by time, are often deep in theological "meat," and provide a sense of continuity with the church throughout the ages. They worry that contemporary styles may lean too heavily on emotionalism or mimic worldly entertainment patterns that distract from the gravity of God's presence.
*   The Contemporary or Normative View: Other churches embrace modern styles, citing the biblical call to "sing to Him a new song" (Psalm 33:3; Psalm 96:1). They argue that every generation should express its devotion using its own cultural "language" and musical instruments, provided the content remains biblically sound. They believe that a modern musical setting can make the truths of the Gospel more accessible to the current generation without compromising the message.

The ultimate goal of worship is not to please our own musical tastes but to bring an offering that is acceptable to God through Jesus Christ. We must be careful not to let "worship wars" over style cause division, which grieves the Holy Spirit. Instead, we should pursue what makes for peace and mutual upbuilding (Romans 14:19).

As a practical next step, I encourage you to take the lyrics of the songs your church sings and compare them directly to the Scriptures. If a song speaks of God's love, look for where that love is grounded in the sacrifice of Christ. If it speaks of our response, ensure it leads toward repentance and faith rather than mere emotional experience.

Worship is the natural response of a heart that has been transformed by the Gospel. We were once separated from God by our sin, but God, being rich in mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day (Ephesians 2:4-5; 1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone. If you have not yet done so, you can turn to God today, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ for the forgiveness and eternal life that only He can provide. Have you ever considered what it means to truly rest in the finished work of Christ for your standing before God?
What does it mean to test the spirits?

To test the spirits is the vital practice of comparing every teaching, message, or spiritual claim against the absolute standard of God’s Word to discern its true origin. The Apostle John commands us, "Beloved, do not believe every spirit, but test the spirits to see whether they are from God, for many false prophets have gone out into the world" (1 John 4:1). This is not an invitation to a spirit of cynicism, but a call to a holy and loving discernment that protects the soul from the "spirit of error" (1 John 4:6).

The primary test is a doctrinal one, specifically concerning the person and work of Jesus Christ. Any spirit that denies that Jesus is the Son of God come in the flesh, or that seeks to diminish His finished work on the cross, is not from God (1 John 4:2; 1 John 4:3). We must also weigh every claim against the apostolic testimony preserved for us in the Holy Scriptures. Just as the Bereans were commended for examining the Scriptures daily to see if what Paul said was true (Acts 17:11), we must be diligent students of the Word so that we are not "tossed to and fro by the waves and carried about by every wind of doctrine" (Ephesians 4:14).

Furthermore, we test the spirits by observing the fruit of the teaching. Truth according to God's Spirit always leads toward holiness, humility, and a love for the brothers, whereas the spirit of the world fosters pride, sensuality, and division (Matthew 7:15; Matthew 7:16; Matthew 7:17; Matthew 7:18; Matthew 7:19; Matthew 7:20; 1 John 4:7; 1 John 4:8).

If you find yourself uncertain or easily swayed, let this be your steady anchor: God has not left us in the dark. He has given us His Spirit to guide us into all truth (John 16:13) and His Word as a lamp to our feet (Psalm 119:105).

As a practical step, make it a habit to bring your Bible to every sermon and study. When you hear a claim about God or the spiritual life, ask yourself, "Where is this written?" and "Does this exalt the Lord Jesus Christ as He is revealed in Scripture?"

True discernment begins with a heart that has been reconciled to God. We are all by nature prone to follow the spirit of this world until we are born again by the grace of God. Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, lived the perfect life we could not live and died the death we deserved for our sins, rising bodily on the third day to secure salvation for all who believe. You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for your forgiveness and peace. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Why should I get married?

Marriage is not a human invention or a mere social convenience, but a divine ordinance established by God Himself at the dawn of creation. It is a holy covenant between one man and one woman, intended for the glory of God and the good of mankind. Scripture provides several profound reasons why you should consider the calling of marriage.

First, marriage provides the blessing of companionship and mutual help. When God created the first man, He declared, "It is not good for the man to be alone; I will make him a helper fit for him" (Genesis 2:18). Life in a fallen world is often filled with trials, and God in His kindness provides a spouse to be a steady companion, a source of encouragement, and a partner in the labors of life.

Second, marriage is the God ordained setting for human intimacy and the building of family. It serves as a safeguard for purity, providing a holy channel for the desires God has placed within us. The Apostle Paul writes that "because of the temptation to sexual immorality, each man should have his own wife and each woman her own husband" (1 Corinthians 7:2). Furthermore, marriage is the foundation for the family, the means by which children are brought forth and raised in the "discipline and instruction of the Lord" (Ephesians 6:4).

Third, and most significantly, marriage is designed to be a living parable of the Gospel. It is a physical earthly picture of the spiritual heavenly reality of Christ’s relationship with His people. Paul explains that a husband is to love his wife "as Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her" (Ephesians 5:25). When a man and woman commit to one another in a lifelong covenant of faithfulness, they show the world a glimpse of the sacrificial, enduring, and unconditional love that Jesus Christ has for those He has redeemed. This is why marriage is referred to as a "profound mystery" that refers to Christ and the church (Ephesians 5:32).

Marriage is a high and holy calling that requires self denial and a heart of service.
