It is a primary means of sanctification, as God uses the closeness of the relationship to mold you more into the image of His Son. If you are considering marriage, I encourage you to begin by praying for a heart that seeks God’s glory above your own happiness. Reach out to a faithful pastor or a mature Christian couple in your local church to discuss how you can prepare your heart for such a significant covenant.

Ultimately, even the best marriage is only a shadow of the greatest relationship you can ever have. While earthly marriage ends at death, the union between Christ and His people lasts forever. We have all sinned and fallen short of God’s holiness, but God demonstrated His love by sending His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die on a cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. He is the true Bridegroom who died to save His bride. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and the promise of eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Is Sunday the Christian Sabbath?

Whether Sunday is the Christian Sabbath is a question that faithful believers have weighed with great care throughout church history. While all orthodox Christians agree that the first day of the week is uniquely set apart for the worship of our risen Lord, they often differ on whether the Old Testament Sabbath command specifically transfers to Sunday. This is considered a secondary doctrine where Christians may hold different convictions while remaining in warm fellowship.

There are two primary historic perspectives on this matter.

The first view, often called Sabbatarianism, holds that Sunday is indeed the Christian Sabbath. This perspective teaches that the principle of "one day in seven" is a moral law established at Creation and codified in the Ten Commandments. While the specific day changed from the seventh to the first to honor Christ’s resurrection, the obligation to rest from common labor and devote the day to holy exercises remains. Proponents point to the fact that the early church met on the first day of the week to break bread and collect offerings as seen in Acts 20:7 and 1 Corinthians 16:1-2. They also see the "Lord’s Day" mentioned in Revelation 1:10 as the fulfillment of the Sabbath for the New Covenant people of God. In this view, the Fourth Commandment is a perpetual gift and obligation. Exodus 20:8-11.

The second view holds that Sunday is the Lord's Day but not a "Sabbath" in the legal sense of the Old Covenant. This perspective emphasizes that the Sabbath was a shadow or a type that pointed toward the substance, which is Jesus Christ. According to this view, Christians find their "Sabbath rest" every day by trusting in the finished work of Jesus. Therefore, while Sunday is the appropriate day for corporate worship because of the Resurrection, believers are not bound by the specific regulations of the Jewish Sabbath. This position often highlights the words of the Apostle Paul, who taught that no one should be judged regarding a Sabbath day, as these were a shadow of things to come. Colossians 2:16-17; Romans 14:5-6.

Despite these differing interpretations, there is a beautiful unity in the practice of the church. Whether one calls it the Christian Sabbath or the Lord's Day, the following truths remain constant:

1. Jesus Christ rose from the dead on the first day of the week, signifying a new creation. Matthew 28:1.
2. The gathering of the local church for the preaching of the Word, prayer, and the sacraments is a vital and non-negotiable part of the Christian life. Hebrews 10:24-25.
3. Human beings were designed by God to need rhythms of rest and spiritual refreshment. Mark 2:27.

The most crucial reality is that our ultimate rest is found not in a calendar day, but in a Person. Jesus Christ lived the perfect life we could not live and died the death we deserved for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death. We are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our ability to keep a day perfectly. Hebrews 4:9-10.

If you find yourself weary and burdened by the pressures of life or the weight of your own failures, I encourage you to look to Jesus. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. He offers a rest for your soul that no legalistic observance could ever provide.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to prioritize the gathering of your local church this coming Lord's Day. Set aside the distractions of work and digital devices to focus your heart on the goodness of God and the fellowship of the saints. If you are struggling to find rest, perhaps you might meditate on the promises of Matthew 11:28-30 and ask the Lord to help you trust in His finished work.
What does it mean that those who humble themselves will be exalted (Matthew 23:12)?

To humble oneself means to adopt a right and honest view of who we are in relation to the holy and sovereign God, abandoning all claims to self-righteousness, personal merit, or social status. In the context of Matthew 23:12, Jesus is rebuking the religious leaders of His day who loved the places of honor and sought to be noticed by men for their supposed piety. He warns that those who strive to lift themselves up in pride will be brought low by God’s judgment, while those who acknowledge their own spiritual poverty will be lifted up by His grace.

True humility is not a performance or a feeling of worthlessness, but a radical shift in focus from self to God. It is the recognition that we are creatures and He is the Creator, that we are sinners and He is the Savior. Scripture tells us that God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble (James 4:6). This humility is a requirement for entering the kingdom of heaven, as we must come to God as helpless children, setting aside our pride to receive the gift of salvation (Matthew 18:3-4).

The "exaltation" that Jesus promises is not a worldly promotion or the satisfaction of our ego. Rather, it is the divine favor and honor that God bestows on those who trust Him. This exaltation begins with justification, where God declares a repentant sinner righteous because of the work of Christ, and it culminates in eternal glory when we are raised to live in His presence forever (Luke 18:14).

We see the perfect model of this principle in the person of Jesus Christ. Though He was truly God, He did not cling to His heavenly rights but emptied Himself by taking the form of a servant and being born in human likeness (Philippians 2:6-7). He humbled Himself further by becoming obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross, to atone for our sins. Because of this ultimate act of humility, God has highly exalted Him and bestowed on Him the name that is above every name (Philippians 2:8-9).

The truth is that our natural hearts are factories of pride, constantly seeking to justify ourselves and prove our worth. Yet, the Gospel invites us to a different path. You are invited to stop the exhausting work of self-exaltation and instead rest in what Christ has done. Real life is found not in being served, but in serving, and not in being known, but in knowing the Lord.

If you find yourself struggling with the weight of trying to prove your own goodness or importance, I encourage you to turn to God in prayer. Confess your need for Him and ask the Holy Spirit to grant you a heart like Christ's. You can turn to God right now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to find the honor that comes from Him alone. What is it that makes it difficult for you to let go of your own reputation and trust in His?

As a practical step this week, look for one opportunity to serve someone else in a way that no one will notice. Let that secret act of service be a physical reminder that your value is held securely by your Father who sees in secret (Matthew 6:4).
How should poetry in the Bible be interpreted?

Poetry in the Bible is the inspired Word of God expressed through literary artistry, purposefully designed to engage both the mind and the affections of the believer. To interpret it rightly, we must recognize that God uses these beautiful forms not to obscure truth, but to deepen our understanding of His character and our relationship with Him. Unlike prose, which moves in a linear, narrative fashion, biblical poetry often moves in echoes, using a structure known as parallelism to emphasize and expand upon divine revelation.

The first key to interpreting biblical poetry is understanding this Hebrew parallelism. Rather than relying on rhyme or meter like much of Western poetry, Hebrew verse repeats or contrasts thoughts. In synonymous parallelism, the second line reinforces the first by saying the same thing in a different way, as seen in Psalm 119:105: "Your word is a lamp to my feet and a light to my path." In antithetic parallelism, the second line provides a contrast to sharpen the truth, such as in Proverbs 10:1: "A wise son brings joy to his father, but a foolish son is a grief to his mother." By identifying these patterns, you can see how the Holy Spirit intends to highlight specific facets of God's wisdom.

A second principle is the proper handling of figurative language. Biblical poetry is rich with metaphors, similes, and personification. When the Psalmist declares, "The LORD is my rock, my fortress, and my deliverer" in Psalm 18:2, he is not suggesting that God is a literal stone, but he is stating a literal, historical fact about God's unshakeable nature and protective power. We must never allow the presence of symbols to lead us into a "spiritualizing" that denies the objective reality of God’s intervention in history. These figures of speech are windows into the soul, helping us to feel the weight of God’s holiness and the warmth of His mercy.

Thirdly, we must interpret poetry within the context of the whole of Scripture, following the principle that the Word of God is its own best interpreter. This means that a poetic expression should never be used to contradict a clear doctrinal teaching found elsewhere. For example, when a poem expresses deep human despair or honest questioning of God’s timing, it does not negate the truth of God’s absolute sovereignty and goodness. Instead, it shows us how to bring our rawest emotions before a holy God in the language of worship and lament.

Ultimately, all biblical poetry points us toward the wisdom and glory of Christ. The Psalms speak of His sufferings and His coming kingdom, while the Proverbs reveal the wisdom that is found perfectly in Him alone. The goal of reading these passages is not merely to gain information, but to have our hearts stirred to love the Lord more deeply.

As a practical step, I encourage you to read one Psalm aloud each day this week. Pay close attention to how the second line of each verse relates to the first, and ask God to show you how that specific truth applies to your current circumstances. If you find yourself burdened by sin or distance from God, remember that the greatest "poetry" of God is the Gospel itself, the Word made flesh. Jesus Christ lived the perfect life we could not live and died the death we deserved so that through faith in Him alone, we might be reconciled to God. You can turn to Him now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What is it that prevents you from resting fully in His grace today?
What is the significance of the statement in Romans 1:22 that “professing themselves to be wise, they became fools”?

In Romans 1:22, the Apostle Paul identifies the ultimate irony of the human condition: when men and women reject the clear revelation of God in order to establish their own intellectual or moral autonomy, they do not find enlightenment, but instead descend into spiritual absurdity. This "professing to be wise" is a description of human pride that suppresses the truth of God's existence and character, which God has made plain to all through the things He has created (Romans 1:19; Romans 1:20).

The significance of this verse lies in the "noetic effects of sin," a theological term referring to how sin has darkened the human mind and corrupted our ability to reason correctly about our Creator. By turning away from the source of all wisdom, humanity becomes "futile in their thinking," and their "senseless hearts" are darkened (Romans 1:21). This is not a lack of worldly intelligence or education, but a profound moral and spiritual failure. Instead of worshiping the incorruptible God, people begin to worship the creation, whether that be literal idols of wood and stone or modern idols like secular materialism, the self, or human achievement (Romans 1:23; Romans 1:25).

The Bible teaches that true wisdom begins with the fear of the Lord (Proverbs 9:10), yet the natural man seeks a world where he is the measure of all things. This path leads only to the judgment of God, who eventually gives people over to the sinful desires of their hearts (Romans 1:24). It is a sobering reminder that there is no such thing as a neutral mind; we are either bowing to the Truth or manufacturing a lie to justify our own independence from our Maker.

Yet, dear friend, there is great hope even for those who have walked the path of folly. While we have all exchanged the glory of God for lesser things and deserve His righteous wrath, God demonstrated His love for us in this: while we were still sinners, Christ died for us (Romans 5:8). Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, took on human nature, lived a perfectly righteous life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the debt for our sins. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death and His authority as Lord of all.

The path out of spiritual darkness and into true wisdom is not found in more human philosophy, but through humble repentance and faith. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As you consider these things, I encourage you to read the first three chapters of the book of Romans to see the full depth of our need and the height of God's grace. Pray that the Holy Spirit would grant you a heart that seeks Him, and find a local church where the Bible is honored as the final authority, so that you may grow in the grace and knowledge of our Lord.
What does it mean that sin is lawlessness?

To say that sin is lawlessness means that every sin, no matter how small it may seem to us, is a direct act of rebellion against the authority and sovereign rule of God. The Apostle John provides the definitive scriptural definition when he writes, "Everyone who practices sin also practices lawlessness; and sin is lawlessness" (1 John 3:4). This definition teaches us that sin is not merely a mistake, a lapse in judgment, or a social faux pas, but is a fundamental rejection of the Lawgiver Himself.

Lawlessness, derived from the Greek word *anomia*, describes a state of living as if there were no law or as if one is above the law. It is the heart's declaration of independence from God, a spiritual anarchy where the creature attempts to sit on the throne that belongs only to the Creator. Because God’s law is a perfect reflection of His holy and righteous character, to break His law is to assault His very nature. When we sin, we are not just breaking a rule, we are expressing a preference for our own will over the perfect will of our Holy God.

The Bible teaches that this condition is universal. Since the fall of our first parents, every human being is born with a nature that is prone to this lawlessness (Romans 3:23; Psalm 51:5). We see this lawlessness manifested in two primary ways: transgression and omission. Transgression is "crossing the line" by doing what God has forbidden, while omission is failing to do what God has commanded. In both cases, the root is the same: a heart that refuses to submit to the Lordship of Jesus Christ.

However, the weight of our lawlessness is exactly why the Gospel is such glorious news. While we were yet sinners and rebels, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, into the world. Jesus is the only human being who ever lived a life of perfect lawfulness, fulfilling every requirement of God's righteous standards. He then took the penalty for our lawlessness upon Himself at the cross. This is the doctrine of penal substitutionary atonement: Christ died in our place, bearing the wrath of God that our sin deserved, so that we might be forgiven (2 Corinthians 5:21; 1 Peter 2:24). 

By His bodily resurrection on the third day, Jesus proved His victory over sin and death, offering eternal life to all who turn to Him. We are not saved by our own attempts to keep the law, for "by the works of the law no human being will be justified in his sight" (Romans 3:20). Instead, we are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

If you recognize the lawlessness in your own heart and the burden of your guilt before a Holy God, there is hope. You can turn to God right now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive full forgiveness and a new heart that desires to obey Him. I invite you to consider: what is keeping you from surrendering your will to the one who loved you and gave Himself for you?

For a practical next step in your walk with the Lord, I encourage you to read through the Gospel of John and the First Letter of John this week. As you read, ask the Holy Spirit to show you the beauty of Christ’s obedience and to reveal any areas of your life where you are still clinging to your own way instead of His. Seek out a faithful local church where the Word of God is preached with clarity, so that you may grow in the grace and knowledge of our Lord.
Has anyone ever seen God?

The Bible teaches that in His divine essence, no human being has ever seen God. Scripture is explicit on this point, stating that God is spirit and dwells in unapproachable light, whom no man has seen or can see (1 Timothy 6:16; John 1:18). Because God is holy and we are fallen, the direct sight of His full, unveiled glory would be consuming. As the Lord told Moses, "You cannot see My face, for no man can see Me and live" (Exodus 33:20).

However, there is a beautiful tension in Scripture because it also records instances where people are said to have "seen" God. These events are understood in two primary ways:

First, God revealed Himself through "theophanies," which are visible manifestations of His presence. When Moses spoke with God "face to face" (Exodus 33:11), or when Isaiah saw the Lord "high and lifted up" (Isaiah 6:1), they were seeing a mediated representation of His glory, not His naked, infinite essence. These were gracious condescensions where God took on a form that human eyes could endure.

Second, and most importantly, the invisible God has made Himself fully known in the person of Jesus Christ. This is the miracle of the Incarnation. The Apostle John writes, "No one has ever seen God, but the one and only Son, who is Himself God and is at the Father’s side, has made Him known" (John 1:18). Jesus is the "image of the invisible God" (Colossians 1:15). When Philip asked Jesus to show them the Father, Jesus responded, "Anyone who has seen Me has seen the Father" (John 14:9). In the face of Christ, we see the character, love, and holiness of God perfectly displayed in human form.

The reason we cannot see God in His fullness now is the veil of our own sin, which separates us from His perfect holiness. But the heart of the Gospel is that Jesus Christ came to remove that veil. He lived the perfect life we could not live, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over death. 

Through faith in Him, we are justified by grace and promised a future where our sanctification will be complete. In that day, the "beatific vision" will be realized, and we shall see Him as He is (1 John 3:2; Matthew 5:8). Revelation 22:4 promises that His servants "will see His face, and His name will be on their foreheads."

You can turn to God even now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive the forgiveness that allows you to know Him. What is it that makes you desire to see God today?
Are books such as 90 minutes in Heaven and 23 minutes in Hell biblically based?

Accounts of personal visits to Heaven or Hell, often categorized as "heavenly visit" narratives, are not biblically based because they elevate subjective personal experience above the objective and sufficient Word of God. While these stories may be intended to offer comfort or warning, they often present a version of the afterlife that adds to or contradicts the witness of Scripture.

The Bible is the inspired, inerrant, and final authority for the believer. When we look to the accounts of those in Scripture who were given glimpses of the heavenly realm, we see a stark contrast to modern bestsellers. The Apostle Paul, for instance, spoke of being "caught up into Paradise" but noted that he "heard inexpressible words, which a man is not permitted to speak" in 2 Corinthians 12:4. Likewise, Lazarus, whom Jesus raised from the dead after four days, is never recorded as providing any description of his experience in the grave. The silence of these biblical figures suggests that God has intentionally limited what we are to know about the specifics of the afterlife.

The danger of these books lies in their challenge to the sufficiency of Scripture. If the Bible is truly enough for our faith and practice, we do not need the extra-biblical testimonies of men to confirm the reality of Heaven or the terrors of Hell. Jesus addressed this very issue in the account of the rich man and Lazarus, where Abraham declares, "If they do not listen to Moses and the Prophets, they will not be persuaded even if someone rises from the dead" in Luke 16:31. We are called to walk by faith in God's Word, not by sight in another person's visions. To seek truth in these narratives is to risk being led astray by the imaginations of the human heart, which can be deceptive. We must remember the warning in 1 Corinthians 4:6 "not to go beyond what is written."

Our confidence regarding eternity should not rest on the shifting sand of human experience but on the rock of Christ’s work. Scripture teaches that God is perfectly holy and that our sin separates us from Him. Yet, in His mercy, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to take on human nature, live a sinless life, and die a substitutionary death on the cross to satisfy the justice of God. His bodily resurrection on the third day is the historical fact that guarantees the hope of the believer. You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and the gift of eternal life.

As an actionable next step, I encourage you to study the final chapters of the Bible. Instead of relying on modern accounts, spend time reading Revelation 21:1 through Revelation 22:5. These verses provide the God-breathed, authoritative description of the New Jerusalem and the eternal state, where God Himself will dwell with His people. What holds you back from trusting solely in the testimony of Scripture for your understanding of the life to come?
Why does God not seem to care about the millions of starving children in the world?

The sight of a starving child is a profound weight upon the heart, and your question echoes the cries of the psalmists who often asked God why He seemed far off in times of trouble. We must begin by affirming that God is not indifferent to suffering, for the Bible reveals Him as a God who is intimately acquainted with grief and deeply concerned for the vulnerable.

The reality of starvation and systemic poverty is not a sign of God’s lack of care, but rather a devastating testimony to the fallenness of our world. When humanity rebelled against God in the beginning, the entire created order was subjected to a curse, as recorded in Genesis 3:17–19. This introduced "total depravity" into the human heart and decay into the natural world. Much of the hunger we see today is not due to a lack of God’s provision, as He provides "common grace" through the fruitfulness of the earth, but is instead the result of human sin, including war, greed, corruption, and the selfish hoarding of resources by those in power. 

God expresses His care by commanding His people to be the instruments of His mercy. He identifies Himself as the one who "executes justice for the oppressed and gives food to the hungry," as seen in Psalm 146:7. He warns that those who ignore the cry of the poor will themselves not be heard, in Proverbs 21:13. In the New Testament, Jesus makes it clear that how we treat "the least of these" is how we treat Him personally, in Matthew 25:35–40. The church is called to be the hands and feet of Christ, actively working to alleviate the suffering that sin has wrought.

Most importantly, God demonstrated His care by entering into our suffering Himself. In the person of Jesus Christ, God took on human flesh and experienced hunger, thirst, and the agony of a broken world. He is the "Man of Sorrows," as described in Isaiah 53:3. He did not remain distant; He stepped into the darkness to conquer the root cause of all suffering, which is sin and death.

The ultimate hope for the starving child and the grieving observer is the Gospel of Jesus Christ. Because of Christ’s perfect life, His substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and His bodily resurrection, there is a promise of a new heaven and a new earth where hunger will be no more and "He will wipe away every tear from their eyes," in Revelation 21:4. 

This suffering reminds us that the world is not as it should be and that we desperately need a Savior. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive the hope of eternal life and the power to serve others in His name. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical next step, I encourage you to look into a gospel-centered relief organization or talk to your local church elders about how you can participate in bringing both physical bread and the Bread of Life to those in need. Pray that God would use you to manifest His care to the hurting. 

Supporting Passages: 
Genesis 3:17–19; Psalm 10:14; Psalm 146:7–9; Proverbs 21:13; Isaiah 53:3; Matthew 25:35–40; Romans 8:20–22; Revelation 21:4.
What is postmodern therapy, and is it biblical?

Postmodern therapy is a broad category of psychological approaches, such as Narrative Therapy and Solution-Focused Brief Therapy, that operates on the premise that objective truth does not exist or cannot be known. These methods suggest that reality is socially constructed through language and personal "stories," meaning there is no single "correct" way to view the world or one's own identity. Instead of looking for a root cause or a moral standard, the therapist helps the individual "deconstruct" their current problems and "re-author" their life according to their own preferences and goals.

When we hold these ideas up to the light of the Holy Scriptures, we see that postmodern therapy, in its core philosophy, stands in direct opposition to the Christian faith. While it may offer temporary relief or helpful communication techniques, its foundation is built upon the shifting sands of human subjectivity rather than the solid rock of God's Word.

The first and most critical point of conflict is the nature of truth. Postmodernism claims that truth is relative to the individual, but our Lord Jesus Christ declared, "I am the way and the truth and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me" (John 14:6). God has revealed objective truth to us through His creation and, most perfectly, through the inspired, inerrant, and infallible Scriptures. To deny that there is a fixed, moral, and spiritual reality governed by God is to deny the very character of the Creator who is the source of all truth (John 17:17).

Furthermore, postmodern therapy often views the individual as the ultimate expert on their own life. However, the Bible teaches the doctrine of total depravity, which means that because of the fall of man into sin, our hearts are deceptive and our understanding is darkened (Jeremiah 17:9; Ephesians 4:18). We are not the experts of our own souls; God is. We do not need to "re-author" our lives through our own imagination; we need to be transformed by the renewing of our minds through the Holy Spirit (Romans 12:2). True change does not come from changing our "narrative" to suit our desires, but from repentance and alignment with God's sovereign will.

In terms of identity, postmodernism suggests that we can define ourselves however we wish. The Bible, however, teaches that our identity is found in being created in the *Imago Dei*, the image of God (Genesis 1:27), and for the believer, being a "new creation" in Christ (2 Corinthians 5:17). Our problem is not a "bad story" we have been told, but the reality of sin that separates us from a holy God. The solution is not a new personal narrative, but the historical and objective work of Jesus Christ on the cross.

If you are struggling with deep pain or confusion, I encourage you to seek counsel that is grounded firmly in the Bible. True healing is found when we stop trying to invent our own truth and instead submit to the One who made us. We must remember that we are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, and it is in Him that we find our true purpose and peace.

The most important reality you will ever face is not your own personal story, but God’s story of redemption. He is holy and just, and we have all sinned against Him, deserving His righteous judgment. Yet, in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord over all. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and eternal life. 

As a practical next step, I encourage you to find a local, Bible-believing church where the Word of God is preached faithfully and where you can receive care from elders who shepherd the flock with biblical wisdom (Hebrews 13:17). What holds you back from trusting in the objective promises of God today?
I am a Christian who is addicted to smoking. How can I quit?

The struggle you are facing is one that many believers have walked through, and I want you to know that there is profound hope and power available to you in Jesus Christ. To begin the journey of quitting, we must first look at the biblical reality of who you are in Him. Your body is not your own; it is the temple of the Holy Spirit, who lives in you and was given to you by God (1 Corinthians 6:19). Because you were bought with a price—the precious blood of Christ—you are called to glorify God in your body (1 Corinthians 6:20). Smoking is often a form of bondage that masters the senses and harms the physical vessel God has entrusted to you, but the Gospel promises that sin and addiction shall not be your master, for you are under grace (Romans 6:14).

Quitting an addiction is an act of progressive sanctification, which is the lifelong process of being set apart for God’s purposes and becoming more like Christ. Here is a path forward grounded in Scripture and pastoral wisdom:

First, treat this struggle as a spiritual battle that requires repentance. Repentance is more than just feeling sorry; it is a "change of mind" that leads to a change of direction. Acknowledge to God that this habit has become a "weight" that clings so closely and hinders your race (Hebrews 12:1). Ask Him to replace the temporary relief of nicotine with the lasting peace of His Presence.

Second, you must practice the "mortification" of this habit. To mortify means to "put to death" the deeds of the body by the power of the Spirit (Romans 8:13). This involves practical steps to remove the temptation. Just as the Bible tells us to flee from immorality, you should flee from the environments and triggers that prompt you to smoke. If there are certain times of day or specific people that make you want to light a cigarette, you must wisely create new patterns. No temptation has overtaken you except what is common to mankind, and God is faithful; He will not let you be tempted beyond what you can bear, but will provide a way out (1 Corinthians 10:13).

Third, do not fight this in isolation. God has placed you within the body of Christ for mutual support. Confess your struggle to a mature brother or sister in your local church or to your elders. The prayer of a righteous person is powerful and effective (James 5:16). Having someone to whom you are accountable provides a layer of protection that the enemy hates. 

As a practical next step, I encourage you to begin a "Scripture displacement" rhythm. Whenever the urge to smoke arises, instead of reaching for a cigarette, commit to reciting a specific verse of Scripture aloud, such as Philippians 4:13: "I can do all things through Christ who strengthens me." This redirects your mind from the physical craving to the spiritual reality of God's strength.

Remember, your identity is not "an addict" or "a smoker"; your identity is a beloved child of God, justified by grace alone through faith alone (Galatians 2:16). Even if you stumble, do not despair. Get back up, look to the cross where your sins were paid for, and continue to walk in the Spirit. The Lord is patient and full of mercy, and He will complete the good work He began in you (Philippians 1:6). 

If you find yourself overwhelmed by the weight of your sin or feel that you cannot change, remember the heart of the Gospel: Jesus Christ died for our failures and rose again to give us new life. If you have not yet fully surrendered your life to His Lordship, you can turn to Him now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for your salvation and your strength. What is holding you back from resting entirely in His power today?
What is mortification of sin / the flesh?

Mortification of sin is the lifelong, Spirit-empowered practice of weakening and destroying the power of sinful desires and habits in the life of a believer. It is the active, daily labor of "putting to death" the deeds of the body so that the life of Christ may be more clearly seen in us. This is not an attempt to earn God’s favor through self-effort, but is the fruit of a heart already reconciled to God through the finished work of Jesus Christ.

The Apostle Paul provides the foundational command for this work in Romans 8:13, where he writes, "For if you live according to the flesh, you will die; but if by the Spirit you put to death the deeds of the body, you will live." He further instructs us in Colossians 3:5 to "Put to death, therefore, whatever belongs to your earthly nature: sexual immorality, impurity, lust, evil desires and greed, which is idolatry."

This process is a vital part of progressive sanctification. While every believer is justified once and for all by grace through faith, the presence of indwelling sin remains a constant foe until we are glorified in heaven. Mortification involves two primary movements: the weakening of the "old man" with its corrupt desires, and the strengthening of the "new man" through the grace of the Holy Spirit.

To practice mortification effectively, one must recognize that it cannot be done by human willpower alone. It is a work done "by the Spirit." When we gaze upon the holiness of God and the immense price Christ paid for our redemption on the cross, the Holy Spirit changes our affections. Sin begins to look loathsome because it is the very thing that pierced our Savior. True mortification does not merely mask outward behavior; it goes to the root of the heart, starving sinful impulses of their fuel and replacing them with a greater love for Jesus.

If you find yourself weary in the struggle against a particular sin, remember that Christ has already won the victory over sin and death. His resurrection guarantees that sin no longer has legal dominion over you. You are invited to come to Him just as you are, for His grace is sufficient for your weakness.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to begin each day with a specific prayer, asking the Holy Spirit to reveal the "roots" of sin in your heart and to provide the strength to turn from them. Combine this with the daily reading of Scripture, particularly the Psalms and the Gospels, to saturate your mind with the beauty of Christ.

If you have never trusted in Jesus, understand that no amount of self-improvement can bridge the gap between a holy God and sinful humanity. We have all sinned and fallen short of God’s glory. However, God in His great mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What is holding you back from resting in His grace today?
Is God’s love reckless?

To describe God’s love as reckless is to use a term that contradicts the very nature of the Almighty. In our common language, recklessness implies a lack of caution, a disregard for consequences, or an impulsive action taken without foresight. However, the Scriptures present God as the fountain of all wisdom and the sovereign architect of all things. Nothing God does is rash, thoughtless, or accidental. His love is not a gamble, nor is it a desperate risk; it is a purposeful, eternal, and perfectly executed commitment to His people.

The Bible teaches that God’s actions are always consistent with His holy character and His perfect knowledge. He is omniscient, meaning He knows the end from the beginning, as stated in Isaiah 46:9-10. Because He knows all things and ordains all things, He can never be "reckless." When God sets His heart on a sinner, He does so with full knowledge of the cost and with the absolute power to bring His purpose to pass. 

We see this divine intentionality most clearly in the plan of redemption. Before the world was even created, God planned the salvation of His people. Ephesians 1:4-5 teaches that He chose us in Christ before the foundation of the world to be holy and blameless in His sight, having predestined us for adoption through Jesus Christ according to the good pleasure of His will. This was not a reactive or haphazard decision, but a covenant made in the halls of eternity.

While the word reckless is sometimes used by people to describe how lavish or "extravagant" God’s grace feels to us, we must be careful to use language that honors His perfection. God’s love is indeed overwhelming and sacrificial, but it is always wise. Romans 5:8 declares that God proves His own love for us in this: While we were still sinners, Christ died for us. This was a calculated act of mercy. He knew exactly what our salvation would cost—the life of His own Son—and He paid that price with infinite resolve.

The Gospel is the ultimate proof of this intentional love. God is perfectly holy, and our sin is a direct rebellion that deserves His righteous judgment. However, out of His great mercy, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, took on human nature through the virgin birth to save us. He lived a life of perfect obedience and then offered Himself as a substitute on the cross. His sacrificial death satisfied the justice of God, and His burial and bodily resurrection on the third day confirmed His victory over sin and death, as recorded in 1 Corinthians 15:3-4. He is now the exalted Lord who offers salvation to all who believe.

Salvation is not earned by our efforts but is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Does the Bible teach that there is a gift of celibacy?

The Bible does teach that there is a gift of singleness, or celibacy, which is a special enablement from the Holy Spirit for the sake of the Kingdom of God. In his first letter to the church at Corinth, the Apostle Paul speaks of his own singleness and says, "I wish that all men were as I am. But each has his own gift from God; one has this gift, another has that" (1 Corinthians 7:7). This word for gift is the same Greek word used for other spiritual gifts, indicating that for those who possess it, God provides a unique grace to live contentedly without a spouse.

Our Lord Jesus also spoke of this reality when He discussed those who have made themselves "eunuchs for the sake of the kingdom of heaven," adding the instruction, "The one who can accept this should accept it" (Matthew 19:11–12). This gift is not a mark of superior holiness or a higher spiritual rank, but rather a practical provision for undistracted service to the Lord. Paul explains that the unmarried person is able to be "anxious about the things of the Lord, how he may please the Lord," whereas the married person is rightly concerned with the responsibilities of pleasing their spouse (1 Corinthians 7:32–35).

It is important to understand that this gift is not merely the absence of marriage, but a divinely granted capacity to remain single without being consumed by sexual temptation. The Bible is clear that if a person does not have this specific gift and finds themselves "burning with passion," they should seek to marry, for marriage is the God-ordained provision for human companionship and the expression of desire (1 Corinthians 7:9). Both marriage and singleness are good gifts from a sovereign Father, and neither state makes a person more or less accepted by God.

Whether you are currently single or married, your primary identity and satisfaction must be found in the person and work of Jesus Christ. If you are single, do not view this time as a "waiting room" for life to begin, but as a purposeful season to be poured out for the Gospel. If you find yourself struggling with loneliness or desire, take those burdens to the Lord in prayer, for He is a sympathetic High Priest who understands our every weakness (Hebrews 4:14–16).

If you find yourself searching for fulfillment in relationships or anything else this world offers, remember that true peace only comes through reconciliation with God. We are all sinners separated from a holy God, but Jesus Christ, being truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and eternal life. Seek fellowship in a local, Bible-believing church where you can be encouraged in your walk with Him, regardless of your marital status.
What did it mean that Satan wanted to be like the Most High in Isaiah 14:14?

In Isaiah 14:14, the statement "I will make myself like the Most High" represents the ultimate sin of pride and the desire for autonomous sovereignty. It describes the heart of a creature attempting to usurp the unique glory, authority, and worship that belong solely to the Creator.

While the immediate context of Isaiah 14 concerns a taunt against the proud King of Babylon, many faithful students of the Word recognize that the language transcends any earthly ruler. It points back to the primordial fall of the "Day Star" or "Lucifer," the angelic being who became Satan. When he sought to be "like the Most High," he was not seeking God’s holiness or goodness, but rather God’s position. He desired to be the center of the universe, the source of law, and the object of adoration. 

This rebellion is characterized by five "I wills" found in Isaiah 14:13–14, culminating in this attempt to rival God. This is the essence of sin: the creature’s refusal to remain in his assigned place and the arrogant attempt to transcend the boundaries set by God. It is the same lie that the serpent later whispered in the Garden of Eden, telling Eve that she would "be like God" (Genesis 3:5).

We can understand the gravity of this pride by looking at the following points:

*   **Usurpation of Authority:** Satan sought to rule where only God has the right to reign. This is a denial of God's absolute sovereignty (Psalm 103:19).
*   **The Robbery of Glory:** God declares that He will not give His glory to another (Isaiah 42:8). Seeking to be "like the Most High" is an attempt to steal the honor that belongs to the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit alone.
*   **The Contrast of Christ:** The pride of Satan stands in stark contrast to the humility of Jesus Christ. While Satan, who was a creature, sought to be like God, Jesus, who is truly God, "emptied Himself by taking the form of a servant" (Philippians 2:7). Satan sought to go up and was cast down; Christ came down and was highly exalted (Philippians 2:8–9).

This passage serves as a solemn warning that pride is the way of destruction. The fall of the "Day Star" teaches us that no creature can find life or peace by attempting to sit on God's throne. We are designed to find our joy not in being God, but in being known by Him and worshipping Him.

If you find your heart struggling with the desire for control or the craving for the praise of others, remember that this is the very path that led to the adversary’s ruin. Instead, look to Jesus, who conquered the pride of the devil through His perfect obedience and sacrificial death.

Because of our sin, we all have shared in this rebellion, choosing our own will over God's. Yet, the Gospel offers a way of reconciliation. Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, bore the judgment for our pride on the cross and rose again to give us new life. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and a restored relationship with your Creator.

As a practical step, I encourage you to read through Philippians 2:1–11 this week. Ask the Holy Spirit to reveal any areas of "I will" in your heart that need to be surrendered to Christ's "Thy will," and seek the fellowship of a local, Bible-believing church where you can grow in humility alongside other believers.

What is it about the character of God that makes His position as "Most High" something we should celebrate rather than envy?
What does the Bible say about the hornet?

In the Bible, the hornet is presented as a divine instrument of judgment and assistance that God sent to drive out the enemies of Israel from the Promised Land. This creature appears in the context of the Exodus and the conquest of Canaan, signifying that God Himself fights on behalf of His covenant people.

The Scriptures highlight this work of God in three specific passages:

In Exodus 23:28, the Lord promises His people, "I will send the hornet before you to drive out the Hivites, Canaanites, and Hittites from your path."

In Deuteronomy 7:20, Moses encourages the congregation by saying, "Moreover, the LORD your God will send the hornet among them until those who are left and hide themselves from you have perished."

In Joshua 24:12, as Joshua recounts God's faithfulness at the end of his life, he declares, "I sent the hornet before you, which drove them out before you, even the two kings of the Amorites. It was not by your sword or bow."

These accounts demonstrate the absolute sovereignty of God over His creation. Whether these hornets were literal swarms of stinging insects that caused mass panic or a metaphorical "terror" that stung the hearts of the Canaanites, the theological truth remains the same: the victory belonged to the Lord. It was not the military prowess, the sharp swords, or the sturdy bows of the Israelites that secured their inheritance, but the power of God working through means that man could neither control nor resist.

For the believer today, this is a profound reminder of God’s providence and protection. Just as God cleared the way for Israel, He is sovereign over every obstacle we face in our walk with Him. While we are called to be faithful and obedient, we must never lean on our own understanding or strength. We serve a God who can use the smallest parts of His creation to accomplish His grandest purposes.

However, the most significant "enemy" that any human being faces is not a physical army, but the spiritual reality of sin and the judgment it deserves. No amount of human effort can drive away the guilt of our transgressions. This is why the Gospel is such glorious news. Jesus Christ, the Son of God, took on human flesh to fight the battle we could not win. By His perfect life, His substitutionary death on the cross, and His bodily resurrection, He has triumphed over sin, death, and the power of the devil.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

As a practical step in your discipleship, I encourage you to read through the book of Joshua this week. As you read, take note of how often God intervenes in ways that exceed human ability, and let those accounts lead you into a deeper rhythm of prayer, trusting God with the "giants" in your own life. What is currently causing you fear that you might need to surrender to the Lord's sovereign care?
What were the key events in the life of Jesus Christ?

The life of Jesus Christ is the central hinge of all human history, representing the moment the eternal Word became flesh to dwell among us for our redemption. His life was not merely a sequence of biographical markers but a deliberate, sovereign journey to accomplish the salvation of His people through His perfect obedience and sacrifice.

**The Incarnation and Virgin Birth**
The journey began with the miraculous conception of Jesus by the Holy Spirit in the womb of the Virgin Mary. This event established the hypostatic union, meaning Jesus is truly God and truly man in one person. He was born in Bethlehem, fulfilling ancient prophecies that the Savior would come from the line of David. (Isaiah 7:14; Matthew 1:21-23; Luke 2:1-7; John 1:14).

**His Baptism and Temptation**
Before beginning His public ministry, Jesus was baptized by John the Baptist in the Jordan River. At this moment, the Trinity was clearly manifested as the Spirit descended like a dove and the Father declared His divine approval. Immediately following this, Jesus was led into the wilderness to be tempted by the devil. Unlike the first Adam, Jesus remained perfectly sinless, defeating the enemy with the Word of God. (Matthew 3:13-17; Matthew 4:1-11; Hebrews 4:15).

**His Public Ministry and Teaching**
For approximately three years, Jesus traveled through Israel preaching the Gospel of the Kingdom, calling all people to repent and believe. He performed countless miracles, such as healing the blind and raising the dead, which served as divine credentials of His authority. His teaching focused on the holiness of God, the depth of human sin, and the necessity of a new birth. (Mark 1:14-15; John 3:3; John 20:30-31).

**The Transfiguration**
On a high mountain, Jesus allowed Peter, James, and John to see a glimpse of His divine glory. His face shone like the sun and His clothes became white as light, confirming that He is the Lord of Glory who surpasses the Law and the Prophets. (Matthew 17:1-5; 2 Peter 1:16-18).

**The Passion and Crucifixion**
The climax of His earthly life occurred in Jerusalem during the Passover. After the Last Supper with His disciples, where He instituted the Lord's Supper, Jesus was betrayed, arrested, and subjected to illegal trials. He was then crucified on a Roman cross. This was not a tragic accident but a planned act of penal substitutionary atonement. Jesus took the full weight of the wrath of God that was due to us for our sins, dying in our place to satisfy divine justice. (Matthew 26:26-28; Romans 5:8; 1 Peter 2:24).

**The Resurrection**
Three days after His burial, Jesus rose bodily from the grave, leaving an empty tomb. His resurrection is a historical fact that proves His victory over sin and death and confirms that the Father accepted His sacrifice as sufficient. It is the guarantee of the future resurrection for all who believe in Him. (Luke 24:1-6; 1 Corinthians 15:3-8; 1 Corinthians 15:20).

**The Ascension and Promised Return**
After forty days of appearing to His disciples and teaching them, Jesus ascended into heaven. He is currently seated at the right hand of God the Father, reigning as King and interceding for His Church. He promised that He will return personally and bodily to judge the living and the dead and to make all things new. (Acts 1:9-11; Hebrews 7:25; Revelation 21:5).

Because of these events, the way to God is open. You are invited to turn from your sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What holds you back from placing your full trust in Him today? (John 3:16; Romans 10:9).

As a practical next step, I encourage you to read through the Gospel of John. Notice how Jesus repeatedly points to His identity as the only source of life. If you have not done so, find a local, Bible-preaching church where you can hear the Word of God explained and join in fellowship with other believers. (Hebrews 10:24-25).
Do Christians need a day of rest?

The need for a day of rest is woven into the very fabric of our humanity by our Creator. Christians do indeed need a day of rest, not merely as a physical necessity for our weary bodies, but as a spiritual rhythm that reorients our hearts toward God.

From the very beginning, God established a pattern of work and rest. Although He never tires, He rested on the seventh day to set a "creation mandate" for all mankind (Genesis 2:2-3). This principle was later formalized in the Ten Commandments, where God commanded His people to "Remember the Sabbath day by keeping it holy" (Exodus 20:8-11). For the believer, this is not a heavy burden of legalism but a gracious gift from a loving Father who knows our limitations. As the Lord Jesus taught, "The Sabbath was made for man, not man for the Sabbath" (Mark 2:27).

While Christians agree on the necessity of rest, there are different historic views regarding how this is practiced under the New Covenant:

*   **The Sabbatarian View:** Many Christians, particularly in the Reformed tradition, believe the Fourth Commandment is a moral law that remains in effect. They believe the Sabbath was changed from the seventh day of the week to the first day (Sunday) to commemorate the resurrection of Jesus. This is often called "The Lord’s Day," a day set apart for corporate worship and a cessation from worldly labors and recreations.
*   **The Fulfillment View:** Other Christians believe that the Old Testament Sabbath was a "shadow of the things to come" and that its substance is found in Christ (Colossians 2:16-17). In this view, every day is holy, and while a day of rest is wise and biblical for human flourishing, the strict regulations of the Mosaic Law are no longer binding (Romans 14:5).

Regardless of these secondary distinctions, the spiritual reality remains: we are prone to forget God when we are consumed by constant toil. Taking a day for rest and worship serves as a "means of grace," a designated time to nourish our souls through the Word of God, prayer, and the fellowship of the local church. It is a weekly declaration that the world does not rest on our shoulders, but on His.

Ultimately, all physical rest points us to the eternal rest found only in the person and work of Jesus Christ. We are all naturally restless, seeking to justify ourselves through our own efforts and works. Yet, Jesus invites us with words of profound tenderness: "Come to Me, all who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest" (Matthew 11:28). This is the "soul rest" that comes through the Gospel.

The Gospel is the good news that Christ lived the perfect life we could not live and died the substitutionary death we deserved for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, conquering death and providing the only way for us to be reconciled to a holy God. Salvation is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

If you find yourself restless and burdened by the weight of sin or the pressures of this life, I encourage you to look to Jesus. You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. He provides the only rest that truly satisfies.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to set aside the first day of the week to join a local, Bible-believing church. Use that day to put aside your ordinary work, feast upon the Scriptures, and enjoy the company of other believers. What prevents you from taking this time to rest and reflect on God’s goodness this coming week?
How can I be a good Christian?

To be a "good" Christian is first to recognize that no human being is good on their own merit, for the Scriptures tell us that "all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God" (Romans 3:23). Christianity is not a self-help program or a ladder of moral achievements that we climb to reach God; rather, it is the historical reality that God reached down to us in the person of Jesus Christ to do for us what we could never do for ourselves.

The foundation of the Christian life is **justification**, which is a legal declaration by God that a sinner is righteous in His sight. This does not happen because of our good works, but because of the perfect life, substitutionary death, and bodily resurrection of Jesus Christ. We receive this status by grace alone through faith alone (Ephesians 2:8-9). To be a follower of Christ, you must first stop trying to save yourself and rest entirely in what He has already finished on the cross.

Once you are united to Christ by faith, the "goodness" you seek is called **sanctification**. This is the lifelong process where the Holy Spirit works within you to make you more like Jesus. It is not a way to earn God's love, but a way to live out of the love He has already given you.

A faithful life in Christ generally flows through several vital channels:

First, devote yourself to the Word of God and prayer. Jesus said, "If you love Me, you will keep My commandments" (John 14:15). You cannot follow a Lord you do not know, and you cannot know Him apart from the Bible. Seeking Him daily in Scripture allows His truth to renew your mind and direct your steps.

Second, walk in obedience and love toward others. A Christian is called to reflect God’s character by acting justly, loving mercy, and walking humbly (Micah 6:8). Your good works should be like light reflecting off a mirror, intended to point others toward the goodness of your Father in heaven (Matthew 5:16).

Third, join yourself to a local, Bible-believing church. We are not designed to be solitary believers. We need the preaching of the Word, the support of elders, and the fellowship of other Christians to encourage us and hold us accountable (Hebrews 10:24-25).

If you are currently carrying the weight of your own failures or trying to prove your worth to God, I invite you to turn to Him today. Repent of your sins and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to begin reading the Gospel of John to see the glory of Christ for yourself. Additionally, reach out to a local pastor this week to discuss how you can grow in your faith and be baptized if you have not yet made that public profession. What is it that draws your heart toward wanting to follow Jesus more closely today?
What does the Bible say about lawsuits / suing?

The Bible teaches that believers should prioritize reconciliation, forgiveness, and the internal wisdom of the church over seeking redress in secular courts, especially when the dispute is with a fellow Christian. The primary concern in Scripture is not merely the recovery of personal property or rights, but the preservation of the testimony of the Gospel and the glory of God.

When a dispute arises between brothers and sisters in Christ, the Apostle Paul provides clear instruction that taking one another to a secular court is a failure in the life of the church. He asks, "Does any of you, having a matter against another, dare to go to law before the unrighteous, and not before the saints?" in 1 Corinthians 6:1. Paul argues that because the saints will one day judge the world and even angels, they should be fully capable of appointing wise mediators from within their own fellowship to settle smaller, earthly matters as noted in 1 Corinthians 6:2-5.

This teaching emphasizes the doctrine of the sufficiency of Scripture and the wisdom of the Holy Spirit dwelling within the body of Christ. To stand before an unbelieving judge to settle a grievance against a brother suggests to the world that the Gospel does not have the power to produce peace or justice among God’s people. Paul goes so far as to say in 1 Corinthians 6:7, "To have lawsuits at all with one another is already a defeat for you. Why not rather suffer wrong? Why not rather be defrauded?" This reflects the sacrificial heart of Jesus, who suffered the ultimate injustice to secure our salvation.

For disputes in general, the Lord Jesus gave a specific process for reconciliation in Matthew 18:15-17. First, go to the person privately. If they do not listen, take one or two witnesses. If the matter remains unresolved, bring it to the church. The goal is always the restoration of the brother or sister, not their public humiliation or financial ruin. Furthermore, in the Sermon on the Mount, Jesus encourages a spirit of incredible generosity and quick settlement, saying, "Settle matters quickly with your adversary who is taking you to court" in Matthew 5:25.

However, the Bible does recognize the role of the civil magistrate as a servant of God for the purpose of maintaining order and punishing manifest evil as described in Romans 13:1-4. While the Bible forbids "brother suing brother" in a way that shames the church, it does not categorically prohibit a Christian from seeking legal protection or justice in cases involving non-believers, criminal activity, or complex civil matters that require the state’s jurisdiction, provided the motivation is justice and not malice or greed. Even in these cases, the believer must act with integrity, truthfulness, and a desire to reflect the character of Christ.

If you are currently facing a legal dispute, your first step should be to seek the counsel of your local church elders. They are called to shepherd your soul and can provide biblical mediation that honors God. Pray for a heart that values the peace of the church more than earthly gain, and ask the Lord for the grace to forgive as you have been forgiven.

Ultimately, our hope is not found in the verdicts of human courts, but in the perfect justice and mercy of God. We were all once debtors to God’s law, unable to pay the price for our transgressions. Yet, God, being rich in mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to take our judgment upon Himself. Through His death and bodily resurrection, He satisfied the requirements of divine justice. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive the forgiveness that no earthly court can grant. What prevents you from trusting in His perfect advocacy today?
What is kingdom now teaching?

Kingdom Now theology is the belief that the Church is mandated to take dominion over all spheres of society to establish God’s Kingdom on earth, effectively preparing the way for Christ’s return through human institutional control. This teaching suggests that the Great Commission is primarily a mandate for societal transformation, often asserting that Jesus cannot or will not return until the Church has successfully reclaimed the world’s systems from the influence of Satan.

At its core, this is an over-realized eschatology, which is a theological term meaning it attempts to claim the full blessings and authority of the future age in the present day. While we certainly pray for God’s will to be done on earth as it is in heaven, Kingdom Now teaching often errs by shifting the focus from the sovereign work of God to the political and social efforts of men. It tends to minimize the biblical truth that we are sojourners and exiles in a world that is passing away, as noted in 1 Peter 2:11.

The Bible provides a different perspective on the nature of the Kingdom and the timing of its consummation:

*   **The Nature of the Kingdom:** Jesus explicitly told Pilate, "My kingdom is not of this world. If My kingdom were of this world, My servants would fight, so that I would not be delivered to the Jews; but now My kingdom is not from here" (John 18:36).
*   **Our Citizenship:** We are told that "our citizenship is in heaven, from which we also eagerly wait for the Savior, the Lord Jesus Christ" (Philippians 3:20). 
*   **The Final Consummation:** The Bible teaches that the full restoration of all things occurs only at the second coming of Christ, when He personally and bodily returns to judge the world and create a new heaven and a new earth (Revelation 21:1–5).

This teaching can lead to significant pastoral dangers, such as spiritual burnout or disillusionment when society does not yield to Christian control. It can also tempt believers to seek secular power at the expense of the clear biblical call to preach the Gospel and make disciples of all nations (Matthew 28:19–20). 

As a practical next step, I encourage you to fix your eyes on the sufficiency of Christ and the local church. Rather than feeling the weight of the world’s political transformation resting on your shoulders, be faithful in your primary calling: to love your neighbor, share the Gospel of grace, and grow in holiness within your local body of believers. 

If you find yourself wondering whether you have done enough to "bring the Kingdom," remember that salvation and the ultimate victory belong to the Lord alone. True peace is found not in our ability to fix the world, but in Christ’s finished work on the cross and His certain return. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to give you an inheritance that can never perish, spoil, or fade (1 Peter 1:4). What holds you back from resting in His sovereign plan today?
What is The Infinite Way?

The Infinite Way is a spiritual movement and system of mystical teaching founded by Joel S. Goldsmith in 1947. It emphasizes the attainment of a "spiritual consciousness" and the inward realization of oneness with God. While this movement frequently utilizes Christian terminology and quotes the Bible, its foundational beliefs are a departure from the historic, orthodox Christian faith, aligning more closely with New Thought and gnostic mysticism.

In the light of Holy Scripture, we must evaluate these teachings with both clarity and tenderness, for the enemy often uses familiar words to lead souls away from the simplicity of the Gospel. There are several core areas where The Infinite Way conflicts with the truth revealed by God.

First, concerning the nature of God, The Infinite Way teaches that God is an impersonal "Divine Consciousness" or an "Infinite Principle" that dwells within every person as their true identity. However, the Bible reveals that God is a personal, sovereign, and transcendent Being who is distinct from His creation. He is the holy Creator, and we are His creatures. He is not a principle to be harnessed, but the Lord to be worshipped (Genesis 1:1; Isaiah 40:25; Romans 1:25).

Second, the movement redefines the person of Jesus Christ. It often distinguishes between "Jesus" the human man and "the Christ" as a universal state of consciousness that anyone can achieve. This is a denial of the hypostatic union—the reality that Jesus is the Christ, eternally God and truly man in one person. Scripture warns us that whoever denies that Jesus is the Christ is a liar (1 John 2:22). There is no "Christ-consciousness" apart from the person of Jesus of Nazareth, who is the only Mediator between God and men (1 Timothy 2:5; John 14:6).

Third, The Infinite Way treats sin, suffering, and evil as "illusions" or "errors of belief" that vanish once a person realizes their oneness with God. This is a dangerous teaching because it minimizes the gravity of our condition. The Bible teaches that sin is a real, objective rebellion against a holy God (Romans 3:23). It is not a misunderstanding in our minds, but a corruption of our nature that deserves divine judgment and requires a bloody sacrifice for atonement (Hebrews 9:22).

The true "way" is not found by looking inward to discover our own divinity, but by looking upward and outward to the finished work of Jesus Christ. We are not divine beings who have forgotten our identity; we are fallen sinners in need of a Savior. Jesus Christ took on human flesh, lived a perfect life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the debt for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4).

Salvation is not an achievement of consciousness, but a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8–9). I encourage you to turn away from any teaching that points you toward your own "inner light" and instead trust in the Light of the World, Jesus Christ. You can turn to Him now, repent of your sins, and find true forgiveness and eternal life in the One who died and rose for you.

As a practical step for your growth, I suggest you read the Gospel of John. Pay close attention to how Jesus speaks of Himself as the unique Son of God. What holds you back from trusting in Him as your only hope for reconciliation with God?
Does God have favorites?

God does not show partiality or have favorites based on human merit, social status, or outward appearance. Scripture explicitly states that "God does not show favoritism" (Acts 10:34) and "there is no partiality with God" (Romans 2:11). Unlike human beings who are often swayed by wealth, beauty, or influence, the Almighty judges with perfect equity and looks directly at the heart (1 Samuel 16:7).

However, we must distinguish between human partiality and divine sovereignty. While God is not "impressed" by anyone, He does exercise His sovereign right to bestow His special, saving grace on whom He chooses. This is known as election. This choice is not based on the person being a "favorite" because of their own goodness, for "all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God" (Romans 3:23). Instead, God sets His love upon His people according to the good pleasure of His will, so that all the glory goes to His mercy rather than human effort (Romans 9:15-16; Ephesians 1:4-5).

In this sense, God has a unique, covenant love for those who are in Christ Jesus that is distinct from His general benevolence toward all creation. He is a Father to those who have been adopted into His family through faith, and He promises to work all things together for their good (Romans 8:28). This favor is a gift of grace, completely unearned and found only in the person and work of the Lord Jesus Christ.

The central truth is that you do not need to compete for God's attention or earn the status of a favorite. We are all equally lost in our sin, yet God in His great love sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to take on human nature, live a sinless life, and die an atoning death on the cross to reconcile us to Himself. He rose bodily from the grave on the third day, conquering death for all who believe.

You can turn to God right now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you. By faith, you are united to Christ, and the Father looks upon you with the same love and favor He has for His own Son. What holds you back from trusting in His grace today?

As a practical next step, I encourage you to read the first chapter of the book of Ephesians. It beautifully describes the spiritual blessings and the secure position that every believer has in Christ, regardless of their background or past. Seek out a local, Bible-teaching church where you can grow in the understanding of God's sovereign grace and find fellowship with other believers.
How can I live a holy life?

To live a holy life is to be set apart by God and for God, reflecting His character in a world that often ignores Him. It is not a ladder we climb to reach God, but a fruit that grows because God has already reached down to us in Jesus Christ. Holiness begins with a right understanding of the Gospel: you are not made holy by your own efforts, but you are first declared righteous through faith in Christ alone, and then empowered by the Holy Spirit to walk in a way that pleases Him.

The foundation of a holy life is the doctrine of justification. Before you can do anything for God, you must rest in what He has done for you. The Bible teaches that "He made Him who knew no sin to be sin for us, so that in Him we might become the righteousness of God" in 2 Corinthians 5:21. Once you are united to Christ by faith, you are no longer a slave to sin, and you are given a new nature that desires to honor Him.

To grow in this holiness, which theologians often call progressive sanctification, you must diligently use the "means of grace" that God has provided. 

First, saturate your mind with the Word of God. Jesus prayed, "Sanctify them by the truth; Your word is truth" in John 17:17. You cannot walk in the light if you do not know where the path is. Make it your daily rhythm to read, meditate upon, and obey the Scriptures.

Second, live a life of constant prayer. Holiness is a supernatural work, and you need the strength of the Holy Spirit to put to death the deeds of the body. As the Apostle Peter exhorted, "As He who called you is holy, so be holy in all your conduct" in 1 Peter 1:15. You must ask God for the desire and the power to obey Him, for without Him you can do nothing.

Third, commit yourself to a local, Bible-believing church. Holiness is not a solo project. We are called to "encourage one another daily" so that none of us are "hardened by the deceitfulness of sin" in Hebrews 3:13. The fellowship of the saints, the preaching of the Word, and the accountability of elders are essential safeguards for your soul.

Finally, you must deal radically with sin. Do not coddle it or make excuses for it. When you stumble, do not run away from God in shame, but run to Him in repentance. We have the beautiful promise that "if we confess our sins, He is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness" in 1 John 1:9.

If you find yourself struggling to find the power to live this way, I invite you to look again at the Lord Jesus. True holiness is found only in Him. If you have never done so, turn from your sin and trust in Jesus Christ, who died for your sins and rose again to give you new life. You can turn to God even now, repenting of your self-reliance and trusting in His finished work for your salvation.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to begin this week by reading through the First Epistle of John. It is a letter written specifically so that we might not sin and that we might know the joy of walking in the light. What is the one area of your life today where you find it hardest to obey God? Bring that specific struggle to Him in prayer right now.
What is the sinner's prayer?

The sinner’s prayer is a verbal expression of repentance and faith offered to God by an individual who recognizes their need for salvation through Jesus Christ. It is not a magical formula that grants eternal life through the mere repetition of words, but rather a sincere cry for mercy from a heart that God has awakened to its own spiritual poverty (Luke 18:13-14; Romans 10:9-10).

In the historic Christian tradition, such a prayer is understood as a person’s first response to the Gospel. The Gospel is the good news that although we have all sinned and fallen short of the glory of God (Romans 3:23), God has provided a Way of reconciliation. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a life of perfect obedience, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day to defeat death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). We are justified, or declared righteous before God, not by any works or prayers we perform, but by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9).

The essence of this turning to God is found in two movements of the soul: repentance and faith. Repentance is a change of mind that leads to a turning away from sin and self-governance, while faith is a wholehearted trusting in the person and finished work of Jesus Christ as one’s only hope for salvation. A prayer of this nature usually involves confessing one's sinfulness, acknowledging Jesus as Lord and Savior, and asking for the gift of the Holy Spirit to lead a new life (Acts 2:38).

If you feel the weight of your sin and a desire to be reconciled to God, know that He is patient and full of lovingkindness toward those who seek Him. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John in the Bible to see the character of the Savior more clearly, and to find a local church where the Word of God is faithfully preached. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
How can a Christian avoid being a doormat for other people?

A Christian avoids being a doormat by recognizing that biblical meekness is not the same as worldly weakness. To be a servant of all, as Christ commanded, does not mean submitting to the sinful whims or manipulations of others, but rather submitting oneself entirely to the will of God. True Christian humility is strength under the control of the Holy Spirit, rooted in the foundational truth that we are accountable to God alone.

The primary reason many believers feel like doormats is the "fear of man," which the Scripture describes as a snare. When we prioritize the approval of people over the commands of God, we have traded our identity in Christ for a form of idolatry. Proverbs 29:25 states, "The fear of man begins a snare, but whoever trusts in the LORD is kept safe." If your "yes" to a person requires a "no" to God’s calling on your life or your stewardship of the resources He has given you, you are not serving that person; you are enabling their selfishness.

Consider the example of our Lord Jesus Christ. While He was the perfect servant who laid down His life for His sheep, He was never a doormat. He frequently withdrew from crowds when they sought to make Him king by force, as seen in John 6:15.
