As a practical next step, I encourage you to read through 1 Corinthians 13 this week. Reflect on how your interactions within your local church reflect the patient and kind love of Christ, and ask God to help you lay aside any pride or divisiveness for the sake of the Gospel.

If you have not yet trusted in Christ, know that He is the only way to be reconciled to God. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
How do I identify my spiritual gift?

Spiritual gifts are divine enablements sovereignly distributed by the Holy Spirit to every believer for the purpose of glorifying God and edifying the Church. These are not natural talents alone, nor are they intended for personal prestige, but are given so that the body of Christ may be built up in love and unity (1 Corinthians 12:7; 1 Corinthians 12:11).

To identify how the Lord has equipped you, you might consider these four biblical and practical avenues:

**1. Examine Your Desires and Burdens**
God often works through the sanctified desires of our hearts. Is there a particular need in the church that consistently moves you to action? Do you find a deep, holy joy in teaching, or perhaps a quiet satisfaction in behind-the-scenes service or acts of mercy? While we are called to be obedient in all areas, God frequently gives us a particular affinity for the work He has prepared for us (Romans 12:6-8; 1 Peter 4:10).

**2. Engage in Diligent Service**
Gifts are rarely discovered in a vacuum of inactivity; they are usually identified in the "workshops" of the local church. As you serve in various capacities—whether it be hospitality, administration, or teaching—you will begin to notice where your contributions most effectively meet the needs of others. The more you labor for the sake of the Gospel, the more clearly your specific stewardship will emerge (1 Peter 4:11).

**3. Seek the Affirmation of the Body**
Because spiritual gifts are for the benefit of the community, the community is often the best judge of where your strengths lie. Ask your pastors, elders, and mature brothers and sisters in Christ what they observe in your life. Do they see the fruit of your teaching? Do they find themselves encouraged by your gift of exhortation? The Holy Spirit speaks through the collective wisdom and observation of the local church (Proverbs 11:14; Acts 13:2-3).

**4. Look for Spiritual Fruit**
A spiritual gift will produce spiritual results. This does not mean every effort will be a massive success, but there will be a visible effectiveness in your service that brings glory to God and strength to other believers. If you have the gift of teaching, people will grow in their understanding of the Word. If you have the gift of help, the burden of others will be genuinely lightened (1 Corinthians 14:12).

It is important to remember that while some gifts were foundational and miraculous for the apostolic age, every gift currently active in the church is a manifestation of God's grace. Whether your gift is prominent or quiet, it is essential to the health of the body.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to set aside time this week to pray specifically for God to show you where you can best serve your local congregation. Afterward, schedule a brief meeting with one of your church elders to ask for their perspective on your strengths and where the church has the greatest need.

If you find yourself searching for purpose and have not yet placed your trust in Jesus Christ, know that these gifts are given only to those who belong to Him. The greatest gift of all is the Gospel: that we are sinners separated from a holy God, but through the perfect life, sacrificial death, and bodily resurrection of Jesus, we can be forgiven and reconciled to Him. You can turn to God even now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for your salvation and new life. Have you ever come to a place where you have surrendered your life to Him?
What is lacking in Christ’s afflictions (Colossians 1:24)?

To understand what the Apostle Paul meant in Colossians 1:24, we must first firmly establish that nothing is lacking in the atoning merit, power, or sufficiency of Jesus Christ's sacrifice on the cross. When Jesus cried out, "It is finished" in John 19:30, He declared that the work of redemption was complete. The writer of Hebrews confirms that Christ offered one sacrifice for sins for all time (Hebrews 10:12-14). Therefore, we must reject any notion that our own suffering adds to the price paid for our justification.

What is "lacking" is not the value of the sacrifice, but the personal presentation of that sacrifice to a world that did not see Him hang on the tree. Paul is speaking of ministerial or representational suffering. Because the Church is the Body of Christ, the world continues to direct its hatred toward the Master by striking His servants (Acts 9:4). In this sense, Paul is "filling up" the remaining quota of trials that the Body of Christ must endure as it carries the message of the Gospel to the nations. It is a suffering of application and extension, not a suffering of expiation.

As a believer, you are in a vital union with Christ. When you face trials or persecution for your faith, you are not paying for your sins, but you are identifying with your Savior. Your suffering becomes a living bridge that allows others to see the character and love of Jesus through your endurance. This is the great privilege of the Christian, to be so closely identified with the Lord that the world treats the servant just as it treated the Master.

If you are walking through a season of hardship or opposition because of your devotion to Christ, do not be discouraged. See your trial as an opportunity to manifest the life of Jesus in your mortal flesh. I encourage you to bring your burdens to Him in prayer today, asking Him not only for relief, but for the grace to reflect His glory through your steadfastness.

If you have never placed your trust in this Savior, know that His death and resurrection are entirely sufficient to forgive your every sin and reconcile you to God. God is holy and just, and our sins have separated us from Him, but Christ took the penalty we deserved through His bodily death and proved His victory through His resurrection. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for your salvation. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
How can I get my prayers answered by God?

Prayer is the breath of the believer, a holy communication with our Heavenly Father that is made possible only through the finished work of Jesus Christ. To have your prayers heard and answered, you must understand that prayer is not a means of bending God’s will to match your desires, but a gracious gift by which God aligns your heart with His sovereign and perfect purposes.

The first and most essential requirement for answered prayer is that we must approach God through the mediation of His Son, Jesus Christ. Because God is holy and we are by nature sinful, we have no right to enter His presence on our own merit. It is only because Jesus Christ, the God-man, died for our sins and rose again that we can draw near to the throne of grace with confidence. Jesus said, "And I will do whatever you ask in My name, so that the Father may be glorified in the Son" (John 14:13). To pray in His name means to pray according to His character, His authority, and His specific will.

Secondly, our prayers must be offered in accordance with the Word of God. We are promised that "if we ask anything according to His will, He hears us" (1 John 5:14). If we ask for things that contradict Scripture or serve only our own selfish passions, we should not expect a favorable answer. James warns us, "You ask and do not receive, because you ask with wrong motives, so that you may spend it on your pleasures" (James 4:3). Therefore, the best way to ensure your prayers are answered is to saturate your mind with the Bible so that your requests begin to mirror the very promises and priorities of God.

Thirdly, unconfessed sin acts as a barrier in our relationship with the Lord. While God in His mercy often hears the cries of the distressed, the Scripture plainly teaches that "if I had cherished sin in my heart, the Lord would not have listened" (Psalm 66:18). Walking in obedience and maintaining a heart of repentance keeps the channel of communion open and clear. This does not mean we must be perfect to pray, for none of us are, but it means we must be honest about our failings and turn from them toward Christ.

Finally, we must pray in faith, trusting that God is both able to do what we ask and wise enough to know what is best for us. Sometimes God’s answer is "yes," sometimes it is "no," and often it is "wait." In every case, His answer is motivated by His love for His children and His zeal for His own glory.

If you find that your prayers feel distant or that you do not yet know the Father, it may be because you have not yet been reconciled to Him through the Gospel. We are all separated from God by our sin, but God demonstrates His love in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us (Romans 5:8). He bore the punishment we deserved so that we might be forgiven and adopted into His family. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive the gift of eternal life and the privilege of true prayer.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to begin your prayer time by reading a Psalm, such as Psalm 23 or Psalm 103, and using those inspired words to shape your own requests. This helps keep your focus on who God is before you bring Him what you need. What is the primary burden on your heart today that you are bringing before the Lord?
What is the more excellent way (1 Corinthians 12:31)?

The more excellent way mentioned in 1 Corinthians 12:31 is the path of love, which serves as the supreme motivation and governing principle for every spiritual gift and Christian action. While the Apostle Paul spent the previous verses explaining the necessity and diversity of spiritual gifts, such as prophecy, teaching, and tongues, he concludes the chapter by showing that these gifts are profitless if they are not exercised through love (1 Corinthians 13:1-3).

Love is more excellent because it is eternal and reflects the very nature of God, for God is love (1 John 4:8; 1 John 4:16). In the kingdom of God, spiritual gifts are temporary tools given for the building up of the church in this present age, and they will one day pass away when we see the Lord face to face. However, love never fails and will endure throughout eternity (1 Corinthians 13:8-13).

This love, known as agape, is not a fleeting emotion or a mere human affection. It is a sacrificial, volitional commitment to the well-being of others, modeled after the character of Christ. It is described perfectly in 1 Corinthians 13:4-7 as being patient, kind, and not seeking its own way. We see the ultimate expression of this more excellent way at the cross, where Jesus Christ gave His life for sinners. The Bible teaches that God demonstrates His own love toward us, in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us (Romans 5:8).

To walk in this way is to prioritize the glory of God and the good of our neighbor above our own recognition or spiritual status. Without love, the most impressive spiritual feats are nothing more than a noisy gong or a clanging cymbal. The more excellent way is not about what we can do for God, but about the heart of devotion and grace through which we do it.

If you find yourself striving to serve or seeking to understand your purpose, remember that the greatest calling is to receive and reflect the love of Christ. True life and the power to love others in this way begin when we recognize our sin and turn to the One who is Love incarnate. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

As a practical next step in following this way, I encourage you to read through 1 Corinthians 13 daily for the next week. As you read, consider how Christ has shown this love to you, and ask the Holy Spirit to help you manifest these qualities in your specific relationships at home, in your church, or at your place of work. What is one relationship in your life right now where you are struggling to show this kind of selfless love?
What is the open heaven concept?
What is the origin of baptism?

The origin of baptism is found in the unfolding redemptive history of God, moving from the ceremonial washings of the Old Testament to the specific command of Jesus Christ. While ritual cleansings were a part of Jewish life for centuries, the baptism we recognize today was uniquely established through the ministry of John the Baptist and the authority of the Lord Jesus.

In the Old Testament, God sovereignly appointed various washings for the priestly family and for those who had become ceremonially unclean. These "ablutions" served as a shadow of the internal cleansing that only God can provide. For example, the Law required washing with water for purification from various uncleanness, as seen in Leviticus 15:13 and Numbers 19:7. These acts were historical facts that pointed toward the need for a pure heart before a holy God.

The immediate forerunner of Christian baptism was the ministry of John the Baptist. John appeared in the wilderness preaching a baptism of repentance for the forgiveness of sins, as recorded in Mark 1:4 and Luke 3:3. His baptism was a distinct, divinely commissioned sign that prepared the people for the arrival of the Messiah. Even our Lord Jesus, though He was without sin, chose to be baptized by John to fulfill all righteousness, which we read in Matthew 3:13–17.

However, the formal institution of Christian baptism as an ordinance for the church comes from the Great Commission. After His glorious resurrection and before His ascension to the right hand of the Father, Jesus commanded His disciples, saying, "Go therefore and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit," in Matthew 28:19.

Theologically, baptism serves as a visible sign of a believer's union with Christ. It is a public declaration that a person has died to their old self and has been raised to new life through the power of the Holy Spirit. It depicts the washing away of sin through the blood of Jesus and the believer's identification with Christ in His death, burial, and resurrection, as described in Romans 6:3–4 and Colossians 2:12.

In the early church, we see this practiced immediately following the preaching of the Gospel. On the day of Pentecost, after Peter proclaimed the death and resurrection of Christ, he told the convicted crowd, "Repent and be baptized, every one of you, in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins, and you will receive the gift of the Holy Spirit," in Acts 2:38.

The true beauty of baptism is not found in the water itself, but in the Savior to whom it points. We are all born in sin, separated from a holy God by our rebellion. Yet, in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for sinners, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. Salvation is a gift of grace alone, received through faith alone in Christ alone.

I invite you to turn from your sin today and place your full trust in Jesus Christ, who died for your sake and rose again to give you eternal life. What is it that would keep you from trusting Him today?

If you have already placed your faith in Christ, your next step of joyful obedience is to speak with the elders of a local, Bible-believing church about being baptized. This public profession is a precious means of grace that strengthens your walk and encourages the body of believers. Seek the Lord in prayer, asking Him to lead you to a faithful congregation where the Word of God is honored and the Gospel is clearly proclaimed.
What is historical criticism?

Historical criticism, often called the historical-critical method, is a way of studying the Bible that investigates its origins, the historical context of its human authors, and the development of its texts over time. It seeks to understand the "world behind the text" by looking at the literary forms, the sources used by the writers, and the social and political environment of the ancient Near East or the Greco-Roman world.

While understanding the historical context of Scripture is a helpful tool for interpretation, we must be very cautious. Much of modern historical criticism is built upon the faulty foundation of naturalism, which is the belief that only natural laws and forces operate in the world, excluding the supernatural. This often leads scholars to treat the Bible as a merely human book, questioning the historical reality of miracles, the accuracy of prophecy, or the divine authorship of the Word.

As believers, we hold that the Bible is the inspired, inerrant, and infallible Word of God (2 Timothy 3:16-17; 2 Peter 1:20-21). It is not a collection of religious myths or evolving traditions, but the objective, historical record of God’s work in the world. When scholars use historical criticism to deny the literal Creation, the global Flood, or the bodily Resurrection of Jesus, they err by placing human reason above the authority of God’s Voice. We do not judge the Word; the Word judges us (Hebrews 4:12-13).

The primary theological danger in skeptical historical criticism is the denial of the Bible’s organic inspiration. This is the truth that God so sovereignly moved the human authors that their words were exactly what He intended to say, without error. When we approach the Bible, we must do so with humility, recognizing that Jesus Christ Himself treated the Old Testament as historical fact and absolute authority (Matthew 19:4-5; John 10:35).

My dear friend, do not let the sophisticated language of modern academia shake your confidence in the Gospel. The most important historical fact is that "the Word became flesh and dwelt among us" (John 1:14). If you find yourself wrestling with the claims of critics, I encourage you to bring your questions to the feet of Christ in prayer and to study the Scriptures within the fellowship of a faithful local church that honors the Bible as God's final authority.

The truth of history is found in the person of Jesus Christ. He is the Creator who entered His own creation to save those who were lost. Because of our sin, we are separated from a holy God, but Jesus lived the perfect life we could not live and died the death we deserved. He rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. I invite you to turn from your sin today and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What holds you back from trusting in Him today?
What does the Bible say about paying taxes?

The Bible teaches that Christians have a clear, God-given obligation to pay taxes to the civil authorities under which they live. This requirement is not based on whether we agree with every government policy or how the funds are distributed, but rather on the recognition that God is the one who establishes earthly rulers for the sake of social order and the restraint of evil.

When the religious leaders of His day sought to trap Him with a question about the Roman census tax, Jesus Christ provided the foundational principle for the believer's relationship with the state. He said, "Give to Caesar the things that are Caesar’s, and to God the things that are God’s" (Matthew 22:21). By this, Jesus acknowledged that the civil government has a legitimate claim to taxes, but He also reminded us that our ultimate allegiance and our very souls belong to God alone.

The Apostle Paul further expanded on this truth, explaining that human government is a divine appointment. In his letter to the Romans, he writes, "This is also why you pay taxes, for the authorities are God’s servants, who give their full time to governing. Give to everyone what you owe them: If you owe taxes, pay taxes; if revenue, then revenue; if respect, then respect; if honor, then honor" (Romans 13:6-7). This passage teaches that the civil magistrate serves a role ordained by God to maintain justice and peace. Therefore, paying our taxes is an act of conscientious obedience to God Himself.

While it is true that we must obey God rather than men if a government commands us to sin (Acts 5:29), the general command to pay taxes remains firm. Even when taxes are burdensome or the authorities are secular, the scriptures urge us to submit for the Lord’s sake (1 Peter 2:13-17). Our honesty in financial matters, including tax season, serves as a powerful witness to the world of our trust in God’s sovereignty and His promise to provide for our needs.

As a practical step, I encourage you to approach your financial obligations with prayerful integrity. When you pay your taxes, use it as an opportunity to pray for your leaders, as we are commanded to do in 1 Timothy 2:1-2, asking that God would grant them wisdom and that we might live peaceful and quiet lives in all godliness and holiness.

Ultimately, while we fulfill our earthly debts, we must remember the greatest debt we could never pay: the debt of our sin against a holy God. Scripture tells us that we have all fallen short of God’s glory, but Jesus Christ, being truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the full forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What prevents you from trusting in His finished work today?
Should Christian women wear pants?

The question of whether Christian women should wear pants is a matter of Christian liberty, where faithful believers throughout history have reached different conclusions based on their understanding of modesty and gender distinction. While the Bible does not provide a specific dress code for modern fashion, it offers timeless principles to guide the heart and the outward life of a disciple.

There are two primary ways Christians typically approach this topic:

*   **The Principle of Distinction:** Some believers point to Deuteronomy 22:5, which states, "A woman must not wear men’s clothing, nor a man wear women’s clothing, for the LORD your God detests anyone who does this." These brothers and sisters believe that God established a permanent moral distinction between the sexes that should be reflected in our attire. In many historical and cultural contexts, pants were seen exclusively as male garments, leading to the conviction that women should wear dresses or skirts to honor God's design for gender clarity.

*   **The Principle of Cultural Context and Modesty:** Other believers argue that what constitutes "men's" or "women's" clothing is largely defined by culture and intent. They note that in the Ancient Near East, both men and women wore tunic-like garments that would look like dresses to a modern eye. In this view, as long as a woman is wearing clothing designed for women and is maintaining a modest appearance, she is not violating the biblical prohibition against cross-dressing or blurring gender lines. The focus here is on the New Testament command for women to "adorn themselves in modest apparel, with propriety and moderation," as seen in 1 Timothy 2:9 and 1 Peter 3:3–4.

The heart of the matter for every Christian woman is not merely the type of fabric she wears, but the posture of her soul before a holy God. Scripture places the highest value on "the hidden person of the heart, with the incorruptible beauty of a gentle and quiet spirit, which is very precious in the sight of God," according to 1 Peter 3:4.

When navigating these tertiary issues, we must apply the principles of Christian liberty found in Romans 14. Each person should be fully convinced in her own mind, seeking to honor the Lord without judging those who hold a different conviction. We are called to "pursue the things which make for peace and the things by which one may edify another," as stated in Romans 14:19.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to spend time in prayer, asking the Holy Spirit to align your heart with God's Word. Consider the culture of your local church and seek the wisdom of godly women and elders in your congregation. Your primary goal in dress, as in all of life, is to reflect the glory of Christ and to ensure that your outward appearance does not become a hindrance to the Gospel or a cause for others to stumble. 

If you find yourself burdened by rules or feeling a sense of guilt that you cannot resolve, remember that our standing before God is not based on our choice of clothing, but on the finished work of Jesus Christ. We are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. If you have not yet placed your trust in Him, I invite you to turn from your sin and look to the Savior who died and rose again to give you new life and true freedom. What prevents you from trusting in His perfect righteousness today?
Why wasn't Jesus named Immanuel?

Jesus was given the personal name Jesus because it describes His mission as Savior, while Immanuel is a descriptive title that reveals His divine nature. In the ancient Hebrew context, a person could have a specific name for identification and several titles that described their character or role.

The angel Gabriel instructed Joseph to name the child Jesus, which is the Greek form of the Hebrew name Joshua or Yeshua, meaning "The LORD saves" or "Yahweh is Salvation." Matthew 1:21 records this command, stating, "She will give birth to a son, and you are to give Him the name Jesus, because He will save His people from their sins." This name was chosen by God to announce the primary purpose of the Incarnation, which was the redemption of fallen humanity through the substitutionary sacrifice of Christ.

The prophecy from Isaiah 7:14, which says, "Therefore the Lord Himself will give you a sign: Behold, the virgin will be with child and will give birth to a son, and she will call His name Immanuel," was fulfilled not by Jesus using Immanuel as a legal name, but by His being the literal presence of God among men. Matthew 1:23 confirms this by saying, "Behold, the virgin will be with child and will give birth to a son, and they will call His name Immanuel (which means, 'God with us')."

While His name tells us what He came to do (save us), His title tells us who He is (God with us). This is the wonder of the Person of Christ, He is truly God and truly man. Because He is Immanuel, He has the infinite capacity to bear the weight of our sins. Because He is Jesus, He has the sovereign right to forgive them.

This truth is not merely a matter of historical nomenclature, but the very foundation of your hope. Scripture teaches that God is holy and just, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, earning the wages of death. In His great love, God the Son took on human flesh to dwell among us as Immanuel. He lived a perfect life, died on a cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over death. 

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and eternal life. Is there anything preventing you from placing your trust in the Savior who is "God with us" today?

As a practical step in your walk with Him, I encourage you to read through the first chapter of the Gospel of Matthew and reflect on how God perfectly fulfills His promises. You might also find great comfort in speaking directly to Him in prayer, thanking Him that He is not a distant God, but one who came near to save you. If you are not already part of a local church, seeking out a body of believers where the Word of God is faithfully preached will help you grow in understanding these beautiful truths.
How is choosing a good name better than choosing riches (Proverbs 22:1)?

A good name is more desirable than great riches because wealth is a fleeting commodity that can be lost in a moment, whereas a reputation built on integrity and the fear of the Lord carries eternal weight. While silver and gold can provide physical comfort, they cannot purchase a clear conscience, the trust of one's neighbors, or the favor of God (Proverbs 22:1).

The Scripture teaches that "a good name is better than fine perfume" (Ecclesiastes 7:1). In the biblical context, a name is not merely a label but a reflection of a person’s character and essence. Riches are external and temporary, but character is internal and, through the work of the Holy Spirit, is part of our progressive sanctification. Wealth can often become a snare, tempting the heart toward pride and self-sufficiency, but a good name rooted in truth serves as a testimony to the grace of God in a believer's life.

Consider that even the greatest earthly treasures are subject to "moth and rust" that destroy (Matthew 6:19-21). However, a life lived with integrity, honoring God in secret and in public, leaves a legacy that points others toward the Gospel. True favor is found not in the size of one’s bank account, but in being known as a faithful servant of the Lord.

Ultimately, we must recognize that none of us possesses a perfectly "good name" by our own merit. Because of our sin, our names were associated with rebellion and spiritual debt before a holy God. The Gospel is the glorious news that Jesus Christ, who has "the name that is above every name" (Philippians 2:9-11), took our shame upon Himself through His sacrificial death on the cross. By His burial and bodily resurrection, He demonstrated victory over sin and death, offering us His own righteous standing as a gift of grace.

When you repent of your sin and trust in Christ alone, you are given a new identity and a name written in the Lamb’s Book of Life. This "good name" is secured by His work, not your own.

As a practical step, I encourage you to spend time this week in the book of Proverbs, asking the Lord to reveal any areas where the pursuit of gain has overshadowed the pursuit of godliness. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive a name that will never fade. What is it that truly holds your heart's highest value today?
What is the lust of the eyes?

The lust of the eyes is the sinful desire to possess what we see or to find our ultimate satisfaction in the external appearance and glory of the world rather than in God. It is an illicit craving of the heart that is triggered by the sight of things that are not ours to have or that we value more than the Creator Himself.

The Apostle John identifies this struggle as one of the three great enemies of the soul, alongside the lust of the flesh and the pride of life, as recorded in 1 John 2:16. While the lust of the flesh concerns internal physical appetites, the lust of the eyes is a form of covetousness that enters through the "windows of the soul." It is the deceptive belief that if we could only obtain what we see, we would be happy, secure, or significant.

We see this tragic pattern throughout history. In the garden of Eden, Eve fell when she "saw that the tree was good for food and pleasing to the eye," leading her to reach for what God had forbidden in Genesis 3:6. Generations later, King David allowed his gaze to linger on Bathsheba, turning a look into a lingering lust that birthed adultery and murder in 2 Samuel 11:2. These examples remind us that while the eye is a gift from God, it can become a snare if it is not governed by a heart surrendered to Christ.

In a modern context, the lust of the eyes often manifests as a restless discontentment fueled by consumerism, social media, or pornography. It tells us that our lives are lacking because we do not have the house, the body, the spouse, or the lifestyle we see on a screen. This is a denial of the sufficiency of God's grace and a violation of the tenth commandment, which forbids coveting anything that belongs to our neighbor in Exodus 20:17.

The only remedy for the wandering eye is a heart that has been captured by the "expulsive power of a new affection," which is the love of Jesus Christ. Because of our sin, we are all naturally prone to chase after shadows. We have turned away from the fountain of living waters to hew out broken cisterns that can hold no water, as described in Jeremiah 2:13. But God, in His great mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who was tempted in all ways as we are, yet remained without sin in Hebrews 4:15. Christ lived the perfect life we could not live and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sinful desires.

If you find your heart constantly reaching for the things of this world, I encourage you to turn to God today. Repent of the sin of finding your worth in what you see and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose again to give you a new heart and eternal life.

To walk in victory, you must practice the mortification of sin by being intentional about what you allow before your eyes. A practical step is to pray the words of the Psalmist daily: "Turn my eyes away from worthless things; preserve my life according to Your word" in Psalm 119:37. Consider establishing a rhythm of looking away from the world's distractions to fix your eyes on Jesus, the author and perfecter of our faith, in Hebrews 12:2. What is it that you are looking to today for your happiness? Is it something that will fade, or is it the Lord who endures forever?
What does the Bible say about Voodoo? How should a Christian view Voodoo?

The Bible identifies the practices found in Voodoo, such as seeking the counsel of the dead, invoking spirits, and using charms or rituals to influence the physical or spiritual world, as forbidden acts of spiritism and idolatry. While Voodoo is often presented as a cultural tradition or a way to connect with ancestors, Scripture reveals it to be a dangerous engagement with demonic forces that are set against the one true God.

The Lord is very clear in His Word that He alone is to be worshipped and that His people must not turn to mediums or spiritists to be defiled by them (Leviticus 19:31). In the book of Deuteronomy, God describes these practices as an abomination, warning His people not to follow those who practice divination, tell fortunes, interpret omens, or function as sorcerers or those who inquire of the dead (Deuteronomy 18:10-12). From a biblical perspective, the spirits invoked in Voodoo, often called "loas" or "mysteries," are not benign helpers or ancestors but are what the Apostle Paul describes as demons. He warns that when people offer sacrifices to such entities, they are sacrificing to demons and not to God (1 Corinthians 10:20).

For a Christian, the view of Voodoo must be one of absolute rejection coupled with deep compassion for those bound by its influence. We recognize that Voodoo is a form of syncretism, which is the attempt to blend the holy truths of the Gospel with pagan rituals. However, light cannot fellowship with darkness (2 Corinthians 6:14). To participate in Voodoo is to deny the sufficiency of Jesus Christ, who is the only mediator between God and man (1 Timothy 2:5). Christ has already triumphed over all principalities and powers through His cross, disarming the spiritual forces of evil (Colossians 2:15).

If you or someone you know is involved in these practices, understand that the Bible speaks of a spiritual bondage that leads to fear and death, but God offers total liberation. The Gospel is the good news that God is holy and we are sinners separated from Him, yet out of His great love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus lived a perfect life, took our sin upon Himself, and died a substitutionary atoning death on the cross to satisfy God's justice. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin, death, and the power of the devil. He is now exalted as Lord over all creation. Salvation is found by grace alone, through faith alone, in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. He offers you a peace that no spirit ritual can provide and a security that no charm can guarantee.

As a practical next step, if you have any items associated with Voodoo or the occult, the biblical response is to destroy them completely, as the early believers did in Ephesus (Acts 19:19). I encourage you to begin reading the Gospel of John to see the beauty and power of Jesus, and to find a local, Bible-believing church where you can be supported in your walk with God. What is it that might be preventing you from placing your full trust in Jesus today?
What is the meaning of Lent?

Lent is a period of forty days, excluding Sundays, that leads up to the celebration of Easter. Historically, it has been observed by many Christians as a season of fasting, prayer, and repentance, intended to mirror the forty days Jesus spent fasting in the wilderness before beginning His public ministry, as recorded in Matthew 4:1-2 and Luke 4:1-2. The primary focus of Lent is to prepare the heart for the commemoration of Christ's suffering, death, and resurrection.

In the landscape of Christian practice, Lent is considered a tertiary matter, meaning it is an area where faithful believers may hold different convictions based on their understanding of Scripture and tradition. Because the Bible does not explicitly command the observance of Lent, it falls under the principle of Christian liberty described in Romans 14:5-6.

There are two primary ways Christians approach this season:

*   Some believers follow the liturgical calendar closely, viewing Lent as a helpful spiritual discipline. They use these weeks to "give something up" or take on specific acts of service to cultivate self-denial and focus more intently on their need for a Savior. For them, it is a structured way to obey the call to return to the Lord with all their heart, as seen in Joel 2:12-13.
*   Other believers, particularly those in the Reformed tradition, emphasize that every day should be a season of repentance and looking to Christ. They may be cautious of Lent if it is treated as a requirement for holiness or if it becomes a form of legalism. These Christians prefer to rely on the "means of grace" (the preaching of the Word, prayer, and the sacraments) throughout the entire year rather than during a specific forty-day window.

Regardless of whether one observes a formal Lenten season, the heart of the matter is the reality of our sin and the sufficiency of Jesus Christ. True repentance is not merely an outward ritual or a temporary change in diet; it is a turning of the soul from sin to God. The prophet Isaiah reminds us that our own righteous acts are like "filthy rags," as stated in Isaiah 64:6. We cannot earn God's favor through fasting or self-deprivation.

The beauty of the Gospel is that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us, according to Romans 5:8. He lived the perfect life we could not live and died the death we deserved, satisfying the justice of God through His penal substitutionary atonement on the cross. His bodily resurrection on the third day is the historical fact that proves His victory over sin and death, as declared in 1 Corinthians 15:3-4.

If you find yourself reflecting on your need for forgiveness during this season, do not look to your own efforts or traditions for peace. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the total washing away of your guilt and the gift of eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What doth every sin deserve?

Every sin, because it is a transgression against the law of an infinitely holy and sovereign God, deserves His righteous wrath and curse, both in this life and in the eternal life to come. The Bible tells us that the wages of sin is death (Romans 6:23), and that anyone who fails to abide by everything written in the Book of the Law is under a curse (Galatians 3:10).

To understand the weight of sin, we must understand the majesty of the One we have offended. God is perfectly holy, and His justice is a fundamental part of His character. Because He is an infinite Being, an offense against Him carries an infinite weight of guilt. Even what the world might call a small mistake is, in reality, a strike at the very throne of God. Scripture warns that whoever keeps the whole law and yet stumbles at just one point is guilty of breaking all of it (James 2:10). We are, by our natural birth and our personal choices, children of wrath (Ephesians 2:3).

However, dear friend, the truth of our guilt serves only to highlight the magnificent grace of the Gospel. While we were helpless and deserving of judgment, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live a perfect life we could not live and to die the death we deserved. On the cross, Jesus endured the penal substitutionary suffering necessary to satisfy the justice of the Father. He was the propitiation for our sins, meaning He turned away the wrath of God by taking it upon Himself.

The Gospel is the good news that Christ's historical death, burial, and bodily resurrection on the third day have secured victory over sin and death. Salvation is not a reward for our works, but a gift of grace alone, received through faith alone, in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

As a practical step in your walk with God, I encourage you to read through the first eight chapters of the book of Romans. It provides a clear and powerful explanation of our need for a Savior and the glorious freedom found in Christ. What holds you back from trusting in Him today?
What does the Bible say about diligence?

The Bible presents diligence as the steady, earnest, and energetic application of one’s mind and body to the duties God has set before us. It is not merely a secular habit for success, but a spiritual discipline and an act of worship, reflecting the character of our Creator who is always at work.

In the Book of Proverbs, diligence is repeatedly contrasted with laziness or "slothfulness." The Scriptures make it clear that God honors the hard-working soul. Proverbs 10:4 states, "Lazy hands make a man poor, but diligent hands bring wealth." Furthermore, Proverbs 12:24 teaches that "The hand of the diligent will rule, but the lazy will be put to forced labor." These are not just observations of how the world works, but are expressions of God's design for human stewardship. We are called to be faithful with the time, talents, and resources He has entrusted to us.

For the believer, diligence extends far beyond our earthly careers. It is essential for our spiritual growth and our walk with Christ. The Apostle Peter instructs us to "make every effort to add to your faith virtue, and to virtue knowledge" in 2 Peter 1:5. He later adds in 2 Peter 1:10, "Therefore, brothers, be all the more diligent to make your calling and election sure." This "making every effort" is the theological concept of progressive sanctification, where we, by the power of the Holy Spirit, actively strive to put sin to death and grow in holiness.

Even our inner life requires constant watchfulness. Proverbs 4:23 warns us, "Guard your heart with all diligence, for from it flow the springs of life." If we are careless with our thoughts and affections, we quickly drift into spiritual apathy.

When you approach your daily tasks, remember that you are serving the Lord Christ. Colossians 3:23-24 says, "Whatever you do, work at it with all your heart, as working for the Lord and not for men, since you know that you will receive an inheritance from the Lord as a reward. It is the Lord Christ you are serving." Whether you are cleaning a floor, raising children, or managing a business, you do it before the face of God (Coram Deo) for His glory.

However, we must never mistake our diligence for the cause of our salvation. While the Bible commands us to be diligent, our standing before God rests entirely upon the finished work of Jesus Christ. He was perfectly diligent in fulfilling the Law and enduring the cross on our behalf. It is because He has already won the victory that we can now labor with joy and hope rather than fear.

If you find yourself struggling with a spirit of procrastination or spiritual lethargy, I encourage you to take one concrete step today. Identify one responsibility you have been neglecting, and commit it to the Lord in prayer. Ask Him for the strength to begin that task immediately as an offering of thanks for the grace He has shown you.

Have you ever considered that your work is actually a way to love God and your neighbor? If you are feeling overwhelmed or unsure of how to start, I would be glad to talk more about how Christ’s strength is made perfect in our weakness. What area of your life feels most difficult to be diligent in right now?
What is agnosticism?

Agnosticism is the philosophical position that the existence of God, the divine, or the supernatural is either unknown or inherently unknowable. The term is derived from the Greek word *agnōstos*, which means "without knowledge," and it generally asserts that human reason and scientific observation are insufficient to provide certain proof for or against the existence of a higher power.

While the agnostic often seeks to maintain a position of intellectual humility by admitting the limits of human observation, the Bible teaches that the problem is not a lack of evidence, but the human tendency to suppress what God has already made plain. Scripture affirms that God has revealed Himself to all people through "general revelation," which is the testimony of His power and divine nature woven into the very fabric of the created order (Romans 1:19-20; Psalm 19:1-2). Because the world is His handiwork, no one is truly in the dark regarding the reality of a Creator.

Furthermore, Christians believe that God has not remained a distant or "unknown God." He has provided "special revelation" through the Holy Scriptures and, most supremely, through the person of Jesus Christ. In the incarnation, the eternal Son of God took on human flesh to reveal the Father to us in a way we could understand and relate to (John 1:14; John 1:18). Jesus Christ is the "hypostatic union," truly God and truly man, who came to bridge the chasm between a holy God and sinful humanity (Colossians 1:15; Hebrews 1:1-3).

The uncertainty of agnosticism is ultimately answered by the historical facts of the Gospel. Because of our sin, we are naturally alienated from God and unable to find Him on our own terms. However, God in His mercy sent Jesus to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the dead on the third day. This resurrection is the objective, historical proof that Jesus is Lord and that God is knowable (Acts 17:30-31).

You can turn to God today by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive the gift of eternal life and a personal relationship with your Creator. As a practical next step in seeking the truth, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John in the Bible and ask God to reveal Himself to you as you read. What is it that holds you back from trusting that He has made Himself known?
What does it mean to “be angry and do not sin” (Psalm 4:4)?

To be angry and not sin means to feel a deep, righteous displeasure toward evil or injustice while remaining under the sovereign control of the Holy Spirit, refusing to let that emotion turn into personal malice, bitterness, or retaliation. This command acknowledges that anger is not inherently sinful, as God Himself expresses holy anger against sin (Psalm 7:11). However, because of our fallen nature, human anger quickly becomes a foothold for the enemy and can lead to devastating consequences (Ephesians 4:26–27).

The context of Psalm 4:4 provides the remedy for sinful anger: "Tremble and do not sin; when you are on your beds, search your hearts and be silent." True, godly anger, often called righteous indignation, is directed at the offense against the holiness of God rather than a personal slight against our own pride. To avoid sinning in our anger, we must bring our emotions into the light of the presence of God, pondering His character and trusting in His final judgment rather than taking vengeance into our own hands (Romans 12:19).

In the process of sanctification, the believer learns to distinguish between the heat of the flesh and the zeal of the Spirit. While we may feel the surge of frustration, we are called to be slow to anger (James 1:19), remembering that the anger of man does not produce the righteousness of God (James 1:20). Sinful anger seeks to tear down, but the anger of a believer should be governed by love and a desire for God's truth to prevail.

If you struggle with the fire of anger or the guilt of having let it master you, look to Jesus Christ. He is the only one who ever expressed perfect, sinless anger, yet He willingly took the wrath we deserved upon Himself at the cross. Through His death and bodily resurrection, He offers forgiveness and a new heart to all who believe. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for the peace that surpasses all understanding.

As a practical step this week, when you feel anger rising, commit to a moment of silence. Follow the instruction of the Psalmist to be still before the Lord, asking Him to reveal if your anger is rooted in His glory or your own wounded ego. What is the primary thing that makes you angry today, and have you laid it before the Lord in prayer?
How will the covenant with death be annulled (Isaiah 28:18)?

The covenant with death mentioned in Isaiah 28:18 is annulled by the sovereign decree and judgment of Almighty God, who sweeps away the false refuges of men to establish the only sure foundation, which is Jesus Christ. In the immediate historical context, the leaders of Jerusalem believed they had secured their safety through political alliances and deceptive treaties, effectively making a "covenant with death" to avoid the coming scourge of the Assyrian invasion (Isaiah 28:15). God declares that He will cancel this foolish agreement by bringing the very judgment they sought to avoid, proving that any security found outside of His Word is a refuge of lies.

The process of this annulment involves several divine actions:

First, God exposes the inadequacy of human schemes. He uses justice as the measuring line and righteousness as the plumb line (Isaiah 28:17). When God’s perfect standard is applied, the "hiding place" of lies is overwhelmed by the hail and floods of His providence. No man-made treaty or spiritual compromise can withstand the scrutiny of a holy God.

Second, God provides a superior foundation. Before He speaks of the annulment in verse 18, He promises in Isaiah 28:16 to lay a stone in Zion, a tested stone, a precious cornerstone for a sure foundation. The New Testament identifies this stone as Jesus Christ (1 Peter 2:4-6). The covenant with death is annulled because God offers a Covenant of Grace. Where the leaders of Jerusalem trusted in death to save them from death, God provides the Prince of Life to conquer death entirely.

Ultimately, this passage points to the finished work of Christ. Because of our sin, every human being is naturally aligned with death and judgment (Romans 6:23). We cannot negotiate with the grave or bribe the afterlife with our own works. This "covenant" is only broken when God, in His mercy, executes judgment upon His own Son in our place. Through the substitutionary atonement of Jesus, the power of death is broken for all who believe. By His bodily resurrection, He demonstrated victory over Sheol, making the "agreement with the grave" void for His people.

If you are currently looking for security in your own strength, your bank account, or your moral standing, you are resting on a foundation that will be swept away. God invites you to abandon those "refuges of lies" and stand upon the precious cornerstone. 

You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and eternal life. What is preventing you from laying hold of this sure foundation today?

As a practical step, I encourage you to read through the Gospel of John, specifically John 11:1-44, to see how Jesus proves Himself to be the Resurrection and the Life. Take time today to speak directly to God in prayer, confessing your need for His protection and thanking Him for the firm foundation found in Christ. Seek out a local, Bible-believing church where the Word of God is preached faithfully so that you may grow in this truth.

Isaiah 28:16; Isaiah 28:17; Isaiah 28:18; Romans 6:23; 1 Peter 2:4; 1 Peter 2:5; 1 Peter 2:6; John 11:25; John 11:26
What is the Hindu caste system?

The Hindu caste system, traditionally known as "varna," is a social hierarchy that categorizes individuals into four primary groups based on birth and religious duty. These groups include the Brahmins (priests and teachers), the Kshatriyas (warriors and rulers), the Vaishyas (merchants and farmers), and the Shudras (laborers and servants), while those outside this structure are often referred to as Dalits or "untouchables."

This system is historically and religiously rooted in the concept of "karma," where a person's current social standing is viewed as the direct result of their actions in a previous life. It suggests an inherent spiritual inequality among human beings, where some are born "pure" and others are born "polluted" or inferior.

From the perspective of the Holy Scriptures, we must recognize that this view of humanity conflicts with the truth revealed by our Creator. The Bible teaches that every single person, regardless of their family lineage or social status, is created in the "Imago Dei," which is the image of God (Genesis 1:26-27). This means that human dignity is not earned by deeds or assigned by birth, but is a gift granted by God to all people equally. God made from one man every nation of mankind to live on all the face of the earth (Acts 17:26).

The Gospel of Jesus Christ specifically shatters the walls of division that humans build to exalt themselves over others. While the caste system suggests that one must work through many lifetimes to achieve a higher status or spiritual liberation, the Bible declares that we are all equally lost in sin and equally offered salvation as a free gift through faith in Christ alone (Romans 3:22-24). In the kingdom of God, there is no Jew or Greek, slave or free, for all who believe are one in Christ Jesus (Galatians 3:28).

Our Lord Jesus, the very Son of God, demonstrated this by touching the lepers and eating with the outcasts, showing that no person is beyond the reach of His love or beneath the dignity of His grace. He took the form of a servant and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for the sins of all who trust in Him, rising again to offer new life to everyone who calls upon His name.

If you have ever felt that your value is defined by your background or your failures, I encourage you to look to Jesus. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive full forgiveness and a new identity as a child of God. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical step, I suggest you read the Gospel of John to see how Jesus treated those whom society had rejected. Additionally, pray for those who are currently oppressed by rigid social hierarchies, asking that the light of the Gospel would bring them true freedom and hope.
What does the Bible say about rest and relaxation?

The Bible teaches that rest is not merely a cessation of labor, but a holy pattern established by God at creation for the benefit of His people. From the beginning, God sanctified the seventh day, not because He was weary, but to set a precedent for mankind to find refreshment and to acknowledge His sovereign provision over their lives.

Scripture presents rest in three primary dimensions:

**1. The Pattern of Creation and Law**
God Himself rested on the seventh day after finishing His work of creation, as seen in Genesis 2:2-3. This was later codified in the Ten Commandments, where God instructed His people to remember the Sabbath day and keep it holy in Exodus 20:8-11. By resting, we acknowledge that the world continues to turn under God’s providential care even when our hands are still. It is an act of trust, declaring that our survival and success depend on His grace rather than our own frantic efforts.

**2. Physical Refreshment and Stewardship**
Jesus Christ, though being truly God and truly man, experienced human exhaustion and recognized the necessity of physical withdrawal for prayer and recovery. In Mark 6:31, He told His disciples, "Come away by yourselves to a desolate place and rest a while." Relaxation is a matter of wise stewardship of the body, which is the temple of the Holy Spirit. However, Scripture distinguishes between holy rest and the sin of slothfulness. While rest honors God, laziness is warned against in passages such as Proverbs 6:6-11, which reminds us that our work should be diligent so that our rest is well-earned and purposeful.

**3. Spiritual Rest in Christ**
The ultimate fulfillment of rest is found not in a day of the week, but in a Person. The Law provided a shadow, but the substance belongs to Christ. Because of our sin, we are naturally restless and burdened by the weight of trying to earn God's favor through works. Jesus offers the only true rest for the weary soul, saying in Matthew 11:28-30, "Come to Me, all who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest. Take My yoke upon you and learn from Me, for I am gentle and humble in heart, and you will find rest for your souls."

This spiritual rest is the "Sabbath rest" that remains for the people of God, as mentioned in Hebrews 4:9-11. It is the peace that comes from knowing that the work of salvation is finished by Christ on the cross.

**A Pastoral Application**
Beloved, if you find yourself overwhelmed by the demands of life, remember that your value is not found in your productivity, but in your identity as a child of God. I encourage you to set aside a specific time this week to intentionally turn away from your labors. Use this time to contemplate God's Word and thank Him for His sustaining power. Let your physical rest be a physical sermon to your own heart, preaching that God is in control and you are safe in His hands.

If you are burdened by a deeper restlessness—a guilt or a fear that sleep cannot cure—know that this is often the weight of sin and separation from your Creator. God is holy and just, and our transgressions rightfully separate us from Him. Yet, in His great love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live the perfect life we could not live and to die the death we deserved. By His bodily resurrection, He defeated death and offers eternal life to all who believe. 

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to find the perfect rest for your soul. What is it that keeps you from resting in His finished work today?
What is deism? What do deists believe?

Deism is the belief in a supreme creator who established the laws of the universe but has since remained distant, uninvolved, and indifferent to human affairs. It often describes God using the metaphor of a "Clockmaker" who wound up the machinery of the world and then stepped back to let it run on its own without further intervention.

While deists acknowledge that a designer is necessary to explain the complexity of the cosmos, they reject the core tenets of the Christian faith. Specifically, deists generally believe the following:

*   **Rejection of Revelation:** Deists believe that God is known only through human reason and the observation of nature, rather than through the inspired Word of God or prophetic messages. They deny the inerrancy and authority of the Bible.
*   **Denial of Miracles:** Because deists view the universe as a closed system of cause and effect, they reject the possibility of divine intervention, including the virgin birth and the bodily resurrection of Jesus Christ.
*   **A Distant God:** In deism, God is transcendent (above and beyond the world) but not immanent (present and active within the world). They do not believe in a personal relationship with God or the efficacy of prayer.
*   **Moralism without Redemption:** Many deists hold to a form of natural morality, believing that doing good is a duty to the Creator, but they see no need for a Savior or the substitutionary atonement of Christ.

From a biblical perspective, deism is a profound error because it strips God of His love, His providence, and His holiness. The Scriptures teach that God did not merely start the universe; He actively sustains it by the word of His power, as noted in Hebrews 1:3. He is not a silent architect, but a Father who hears the cries of His people and intervenes in history.

The greatest refutation of deism is the Incarnation. The Bible tells us that "the Word became flesh and dwelt among us," as recorded in John 1:14. God did not remain distant; He entered our broken world in the person of Jesus Christ to rescue those separated from Him by sin. Paul the Apostle reminded the thinkers in Athens that God "is not far from each one of us," in Acts 17:27.

While deism offers a cold, mechanical god of the mind, the Gospel offers the living God of the heart. We are not cosmic orphans left to fend for ourselves. We are fallen image-bearers who can be reconciled to our Maker through the historical death and resurrection of Jesus Christ. If you find yourself searching for a God who is more than a distant force, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John and see the character of the God who pursues you.

True life is found not in human reason alone, but in turning from sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died for our sins and rose again to give us eternal life. You can move beyond a mere intellectual acknowledgement of a Creator and enter into a personal relationship with the Lord today by placing your faith in Him. What is it that prevents you from trusting in a God who loves you enough to enter into your suffering?
Is the distinction between clergy and laity biblical?

In the New Testament, there is a clear distinction regarding the offices and functions within the church, but there is no distinction in the spiritual status or worth of its members. While the specific terms "clergy" and "laity" are later ecclesiastical developments, the Bible distinguishes between those called to the office of leadership and the rest of the congregation they serve.

The Scripture teaches the priesthood of all believers, meaning that every person who trusts in Christ has direct access to God and a spiritual calling to serve Him. There is no special class of Christians who possess a higher degree of holiness or a more direct line to the Father. Peter writes to the whole church, "But you are a chosen people, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people for God’s own possession, to proclaim the virtues of Him who called you out of darkness into His marvelous light" (1 Peter 2:9). Because of the work of Christ, every believer is a priest who offers spiritual sacrifices to God.

However, God is a God of order, and He has established specific offices for the healthy functioning of His church. These offices are elders (also called overseers or pastors) and deacons. Elders are specifically charged with the tasks of teaching, oversight, and soul-care, and they are held to a particular standard of accountability before God. Paul instructs Titus to "appoint elders in every town" (Titus 1:5) and provides specific qualifications for those who would hold such a position (1 Timothy 3:1–7; Titus 1:6–9). These leaders are not masters over the flock but are undershepherds serving the Chief Shepherd, Jesus Christ.

The distinction is therefore functional and ministerial, not ontological or essential. In the body of Christ, the eye is not more "important" than the foot, yet it has a different task. "And He gave some to be apostles, some to be prophets, some to be evangelists, and some to be pastors and teachers, for the equipping of the saints for the work of ministry, for the building up of the body of Christ" (Ephesians 4:11–12). The role of the "clergy" is to equip the "laity" so that the entire body grows together in maturity.

It is a grave error to treat the ministry as a professional caste that performs religious duties on behalf of a passive audience. Every member of the local church is a vital part of the body, and the leaders are servants who facilitate the spiritual growth of that body. You are called to "obey your leaders and submit to them, for they keep watch over your souls as those who must give an account" (Hebrews 13:17), yet you are also called to test everything by the Word of God and to exercise your own spiritual gifts for the common good.

If you find yourself feeling like a mere spectator in the church, I encourage you to seek out your elders and ask how your specific gifts might be used to serve the body. Remember that your primary identity is not found in a church title, but in your union with Jesus Christ.

This beautiful order exists because of the Gospel. We were all once rebels, separated from God by our sin and deserving of His holy wrath. But God, in His great mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live the perfect life we could not live and to die the death we deserved. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, victorious over sin and death. He is now the exalted Lord of all. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What is it that might be keeping you from fully trusting in Him today?
What does it mean to have a wilderness experience?

A wilderness experience is a divinely appointed season of spiritual testing, refining, and isolation where God strips away a believer's worldly comforts to cultivate a deeper dependence on Himself. It is not a sign of God’s absence, but rather a purposeful classroom where He humbles His children and prepares them for future service or greater intimacy with Him.

In the history of God’s dealings with man, the wilderness is a recurring setting for significant spiritual growth. The Israelites wandered for forty years in the desert, a journey intended to teach them that man does not live by bread alone, but by every word that comes from the mouth of the Lord, as recorded in Deuteronomy 8:2-3. Similarly, the Holy Spirit led Jesus into the wilderness for forty days of fasting and temptation to demonstrate His perfect obedience and victory over the devil, according to Matthew 4:1-11. These events are historical facts that show God uses the dry places of life to prove the reality of our faith and the sufficiency of His grace.

Theologically, the wilderness is a vital part of progressive sanctification. In these seasons, you may feel as though your prayers are hitting a brass heaven or that your usual sources of joy have dried up. This is often the Lord’s providence at work, removing idols of self-reliance so that you might learn to rest in His sovereignty alone. As the great Charles Spurgeon often noted, the Lord gets His best soldiers out of the highlands of affliction and the deserts of trial. The wilderness forces a believer to move from a theoretical knowledge of God to a practical, desperate reliance on His character.

If you find yourself in such a season, do not interpret your struggle as a loss of God's love. Instead, recognize it as an invitation to seek Him with renewed intensity. The primary actionable step in a wilderness season is to saturate your mind with the Word of God and persist in prayer, even when you do not feel like it. You should also remain tethered to a local church, as the enemy often uses isolation to breed despair. Trust that the one who led you into the wilderness is the same Good Shepherd who will lead you through it to still waters.

The most beautiful truth of the wilderness is that Jesus Christ entered the ultimate wilderness for us. He experienced the loneliness of the desert and the total abandonment of the cross so that we would never have to face God’s wrath or be truly alone. He took our sin, died in our place, and rose again on the third day to secure our eternal peace with God. 

If you have never placed your trust in Him, you are in a spiritual wilderness without a guide or a destination. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and new life. What is it that holds you back from trusting Him today?
What does Malachi 2:16 mean when God says, ‘I hate divorce’?

Malachi 2:16 is a profound declaration of God’s holy character and His design for human relationships. When the Lord says, "I hate divorce," He is expressing His grief over the breaking of a sacred covenant that He Himself established and witnessed. To understand this verse, we must look at the heart of the Speaker and the context of those He was addressing.

In the days of the prophet Malachi, the men of Israel were acting with great treachery. They were abandoning the "wives of their youth"—the women they had made solemn promises to—often to marry women who worshipped foreign gods (Malachi 2:11; Malachi 2:14). This was not merely a social problem; it was a spiritual betrayal. God hates divorce because it is a violation of the "one flesh" union He created in the beginning (Genesis 2:24). When a husband and wife are joined, God is the one who joins them, and what God has joined, no man should tear apart (Matthew 19:6).

God’s hatred of divorce is rooted in three primary truths:

First, God is the God of the Covenant. He is perfectly faithful to His promises, and He expects His children to reflect His likeness by being faithful to theirs. When a man divorces his wife without biblical cause, he deals "treacherously" against a companion and a partner (Malachi 2:14).

Second, marriage is intended to be a living picture of the Gospel. It illustrates the relationship between Christ and His Church (Ephesians 5:32). Just as Christ will never divorce or abandon His people, the marriage bond is meant to show the world a love that is enduring and sacrificial. Divorce mars this beautiful illustration of God’s steadfast love.

Third, God cares deeply for the vulnerable. In the ancient world, a divorced woman was often left without protection, provision, or social standing. By saying He hates divorce, God is standing as a defender of the discarded and a witness against the cruelty of an unfaithful heart. It is important to remember that while God hates the act of divorce and the sin that leads to it, He does not hate the divorced person. He is "near to the brokenhearted" (Psalm 34:18).

If you find your marriage in a season of struggle, or if you are reeling from the pain of a broken covenant, do not lose heart. The Lord calls us to "guard our spirits" so that we do not act treacherously (Malachi 2:15). Seek the help of your local church elders and commit yourself to a path of reconciliation and prayer, trusting that God can restore what is broken.

Ultimately, our hope is found in the one Husband who is perfectly faithful. Though we have all been unfaithful to God through our sin—a spiritual form of adultery—Jesus Christ took the punishment for our unfaithfulness upon Himself. He died on the cross and rose again to bring us into an eternal covenant of grace that can never be broken. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. What is it that prevents you from resting fully in His unchanging love today?
How transparent should church finances be?

Church finances should be handled with the highest level of transparency and integrity, as every cent given to the church is a sacred trust dedicated to the service of God. Because the church belongs to Jesus Christ and not to any man, those who manage its resources must be above reproach, ensuring that the congregation is fully informed about how their tithes and offerings are being utilized for the advancement of the Gospel.

The Bible teaches that we must be careful to do what is right, not only in the eyes of the Lord but also in the eyes of men, as stated in 2 Corinthians 8:21. When the Apostle Paul handled the collection for the saints in Jerusalem, he insisted on having others travel with him to avoid any suspicion of financial impropriety, as noted in 2 Corinthians 8:20. This principle of accountability is vital for the health of the local church. It protects the reputation of the elders, guards the hearts of the members against cynicism, and ensures that the ministry is not hindered by scandal.

From a practical standpoint, transparency usually involves several key elements. First, the church leadership should provide a regular, detailed report of income and expenditures to the congregation, often through quarterly or annual meetings. Second, there should be a plurality of leaders involved in financial decisions to prevent any single individual from having unchecked control. Third, the church should follow sound accounting practices, such as having an annual budget approved by the members and, in many cases, having an independent review or audit.

The theological foundation for this openness is the doctrine of stewardship. We believe that God is the owner of all things, as declared in Psalm 24:1, and church leaders are merely stewards who will one day give an account to Him for how they managed His household. When a church is secretive about its money, it often creates a breeding ground for the sin of greed or the appearance of evil, both of which are strictly forbidden for leaders in 1 Timothy 3:3 and 1 Peter 5:2.

If you are concerned about the financial health or transparency of your local congregation, a helpful and actionable next step is to request a copy of the most recent annual budget or financial report from your church office. Approaching your elders or deacons with a spirit of gentleness and a desire to understand can foster a culture of trust. You might also offer to serve on a finance or audit committee if you have the professional skills to assist.

Ultimately, our handling of earthly wealth reveals the state of our hearts. Just as Christ was perfectly faithful and gave Himself entirely for our salvation, we should seek to be faithful in the "very little" things of this world, according to Luke 16:10. If you find yourself unsettled by a lack of transparency, remember that the Lord Jesus is the true Head of the Church, and He calls His people to walk in the light just as He is in the light, as written in 1 John 1:7. Trusting in Him provides the wisdom and courage needed to seek order and truth in the house of God.
Is intinction the correct way to do communion?

Intinction, the practice of dipping the bread into the wine or juice during the Lord's Supper, is a method used by various Christian traditions. Whether it is the correct way is a matter of secondary doctrine where faithful Christians may differ in their interpretation and practice.

The Bible does not explicitly command or forbid the practice of intinction, but it does provide a pattern for the ordinance. Here are the primary views regarding the administration of the elements:

*   The Pattern of Distinct Elements: Many believers, particularly in the Reformed Baptist tradition, emphasize following the specific pattern seen in the Gospels. During the Last Supper, Jesus took the bread, broke it, and gave it to His disciples, saying, "Take, eat; this is My body" (Matthew 26:26). Then He took the cup, gave thanks, and gave it to them, saying, "Drink from it, all of you" (Matthew 26:27). This view holds that because Jesus gave two distinct commands to eat and to drink, the church should maintain these as two separate actions to fully honor the symbolism of His broken body and His poured-out blood (1 Corinthians 11:23-26).

*   The Practice of Intinction: Other traditions, including many Anglican and some Lutheran congregations, practice intinction for reasons of hygiene, efficiency, or to emphasize the unity of Christ's sacrifice. Those who practice intinction believe that as long as both the bread and the wine are received, the spiritual requirements of the ordinance are met. They often point to the fact that Christ is fully present in the sacrament, and receiving the elements together does not diminish the grace being communicated.

The differences exist because of varying emphases on the literal imitation of the Last Supper versus the practical administration of the meal in a communal setting. While the method is a matter of Christian liberty and church order (Romans 14:5), the essential truth remains that the Lord’s Supper is a visible word that proclaims the death of the Lord until He returns.

When we approach the Table, the posture of our hearts is of greater weight than the physical method of delivery. We must examine ourselves and partake in a worthy manner, recognizing the body of the Lord (1 Corinthians 11:27-29). The bread and the cup point us to the glorious Gospel: that Jesus Christ, being truly God and truly man, died a substitutionary death on the cross to reconcile sinners to a holy God. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, victorious over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

Salvation is found by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. If you have not yet placed your trust in Him, no outward ritual can provide the forgiveness you need. I invite you to turn from your sin and trust in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive eternal life.

As a practical step, I encourage you to seek the counsel of your local church elders. Ask them about the biblical convictions that lead to their specific way of celebrating communion. This will help you participate with a clear conscience and a heart full of worship. What is it that draws your heart to the Lord as you consider His sacrifice?
What is the church suffering?

The suffering of the Church is the various trials and afflictions that believers endure as they follow Jesus Christ through a fallen and rebellious world. Scripture teaches that suffering is not an accidental tragedy for the believer but is often an expected part of the Christian life, serving as a means by which God refines our faith and conforms us to the image of His Son.

First, the Church suffers because of its identity with Jesus Christ. Our Lord told His disciples that if the world hated Him, it would also hate those who follow Him (John 15:18; John 15:20).
