This suffering often takes the form of outward persecution, ranging from social exclusion and mockery to physical imprisonment and martyrdom. This is particularly evident today for our brothers and sisters in many parts of the world who risk their lives to gather in the name of Jesus.

Second, the Church suffers from the common effects of the Fall. Because we live in a world cursed by sin, believers are not exempt from the pains of sickness, the grief of death, or the weight of poverty and natural disaster. Romans 8:22-23 explains that the whole creation groans, and we ourselves groan inwardly as we wait eagerly for the redemption of our bodies.

Third, the Church experiences internal suffering through the struggle against indwelling sin and the spiritual attacks of the enemy. We are in a constant battle against the flesh, the world, and the devil (Ephesians 6:11-12). This sanctification process can be painful as God uses trials to prune away our worldliness and teach us total dependence on His grace (Hebrews 12:6-11).

We must remember that for the Christian, suffering is never purposeless. God is sovereign over every trial, and He promises to use all things for the good of those who love Him (Romans 8:28). Our suffering allows us to share in the "fellowship of His sufferings" (Philippians 3:10), preparing us for an eternal weight of glory that far outweighs our light and momentary troubles (2 Corinthians 4:17).

If you are currently walking through a season of deep pain, do not lose heart. The Lord is near to the brokenhearted (Psalm 34:18). I encourage you to bring your burdens to Him in prayer and to seek the support of a faithful local church. The Body of Christ is designed so that when one member suffers, all suffer together (1 Corinthians 12:26).

If you have not yet placed your trust in Christ, you must know that while all people suffer, only those in Christ have a hope that transcends the grave. We have all sinned and fall short of the glory of God (Romans 3:23). But God, being rich in mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die on the cross as a substitute for sinners, and rise bodily from the dead on the third day. He took the judgment we deserved so that through faith in Him, we might be forgiven and receive eternal life. 

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for your salvation. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What does it mean that a person will not inherit the kingdom of God (1 Corinthians 6:9-11)?

To not inherit the kingdom of God is to be excluded from the eternal life, joy, and presence of God in His perfected and coming reign, remaining instead under His righteous judgment for sin. The Apostle Paul provides a solemn warning in 1 Corinthians 6:9-11 that those who live a life characterized by unrepentant sin and rebellion against God’s design have no share in the inheritance reserved for His children.

When Paul lists behaviors such as sexual immorality, idolatry, adultery, homosexual practice, theft, greed, drunkenness, reviling, and swindling, he is describing a lifestyle of persistent, willful practice. This is not a warning that a believer who stumbles into a single sin loses their salvation, but rather that those whose lives are defined by these patterns demonstrate that they have not been transformed by the grace of God. Scripture is clear that the "unrighteous" (1 Corinthians 6:9) are those who remain in their natural state of fallenness, separated from God by their transgressions (Romans 3:23).

The heart of this passage, however, is found in the glorious transformation described in verse 11: "And such were some of you." This short phrase carries the weight of the entire Gospel. It reminds us that no one is beyond the reach of God's mercy, but it also insists that a person cannot remain as they were once they have truly encountered the living Christ. Paul uses three specific theological terms to describe this change:

1. **Washed:** This refers to the cleansing from the filth of sin. Through the blood of Christ, the stains of our past are removed (Titus 3:5).
2. **Sanctified:** This is the process by which God sets a person apart for His holy purposes. While we are declared holy the moment we believe, we are also progressively changed into the likeness of Jesus by the power of the Holy Spirit (1 Thessalonians 4:3).
3. **Justified:** This is a legal declaration where God, the righteous Judge, declares a guilty sinner to be "not guilty" and righteous in His sight. This happens not because of our own works, but because the righteousness of Christ is credited to us through faith (Romans 5:1).

The warning exists to wake us up to the reality that sin is a deadly master. To "inherit the kingdom" requires a new birth (John 3:3). One cannot claim to belong to the King while walking in the very darkness that Christ died to destroy. True faith always produces the fruit of repentance—a turning away from sin and a turning toward God.

If you find your life currently described by the list in verses 9 and 10, do not fall into despair, but do not remain in your current state. The Lord is holy, and His wrath against sin is real, yet He is also gracious and merciful. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live the perfect life we could not live and to die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. On the third day, Jesus rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

Salvation is found by grace alone, through faith alone, in Christ alone. You can turn to God right now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ for forgiveness and new life. He is the only mediator who can reconcile you to the Father (1 Timothy 2:5). What is it that holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical next step, I encourage you to begin reading the Gospel of John to see the character of Jesus, and to find a local, Bible-preaching church where you can hear the Word of God and find fellowship with other believers. Spend time in prayer daily, asking the Holy Spirit to give you a heart that loves what God loves and hates what God hates.
Is it wise for Christian teens to be dating?

The wisdom of dating during the teenage years is not addressed by a direct command in Scripture, as modern dating is a relatively recent cultural development. However, the Bible provides profound principles of wisdom, purity, and stewardship that should guide every young believer in their season of youth.

To determine if dating is wise, one must first look at the biblical purpose of romantic intimacy. In the Scriptures, romantic pursuit is inextricably linked to the covenant of marriage, which is a union intended to reflect the relationship between Christ and His Church (Ephesians 5:31-32). Because marriage is the God ordained goal of romance (Genesis 2:24), dating serves best as a season of intentional evaluation for marriage. For many teens, marriage is not a practical or immediate possibility, which often makes recreational dating a pursuit that invites unnecessary temptation without a godly exit.

The Scriptures warn us to guard our hearts with all diligence, for from it flow the springs of life (Proverbs 4:23). Entering into a romantic relationship involves a significant emotional and spiritual investment. When a young person engages in dating before they are ready for the responsibilities of marriage, they risk "awakening love before its time," a caution repeated in the Song of Solomon 2:7; Song of Solomon 3:5; Song of Solomon 8:4. 

Furthermore, every Christian is called to the process of progressive sanctification, which is the work of the Holy Spirit in conforming us to the image of Christ. For a teenager, this season is often best spent focusing on growth in character, devotion to the Word, and service within the local church. Paul encouraged Timothy to "flee youthful passions and pursue righteousness, faith, love, and peace, along with those who call on the Lord from a pure heart" (2 Timothy 2:22). This suggests that the primary focus of youth should be the cultivation of godly friendships and spiritual maturity rather than the pursuit of a singular romantic partner.

There are varying views among faithful Christians regarding this topic:
*   Some believe that dating is acceptable if it is done with high levels of parental oversight and clear boundaries, viewing it as a way to learn how to relate to the opposite sex with honor.
*   Others advocate for a "courtship" model or waiting until adulthood, arguing that it protects the heart from the pain of multiple breakups and preserves one's purity for their future spouse.

Because this is an area of Christian liberty and wisdom, you should seek the counsel of your parents and the elders of your local church. A practical next step would be to sit down with a trusted, mature mentor to discuss your motivations for wanting to date and to ask them to hold you accountable to the standard of purity described in 1 Thessalonians 4:3-5.

Ultimately, your greatest need is not a romantic partner, but the steadfast love of Jesus Christ. He lived the perfect life of purity that we cannot live and died on the cross to pay for our sins, including our failures in the area of relationships. By His bodily resurrection, He offers us new life and the power to live for His glory. If you have not done so, you can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for your hope and your future. He is the only one who can truly satisfy the deepest longings of your heart.
Why does God allow us to go through trials and tribulations?

God allows trials and tribulations not because He is indifferent to our pain, but because He is a loving Father who uses the pressures of this life to shape us into the image of His Son, Jesus Christ. While we often view suffering as an unwelcome interruption, Scripture reveals that God sovereignly uses it as a purposeful instrument of His grace.

First, trials serve to refine the genuineness of our faith. Just as gold is purified in a furnace to remove dross, our faith is tested by fire so that it may be proven true and result in praise, glory, and honor at the revelation of Jesus Christ (1 Peter 1:6–7). This process of sanctification often requires the removal of our self-reliance so that we might learn to depend entirely upon God.

Second, tribulations are the means by which God produces spiritual endurance and maturity. We are told to count it all joy when we meet trials of various kinds, knowing that the testing of our faith produces steadfastness, which makes us perfect and complete, lacking in nothing (James 1:2–4). In the economy of God's kingdom, suffering produces perseverance, perseverance produces character, and character produces a hope that does not put us to shame (Romans 5:3–5).

Third, trials display the sufficiency of God's grace in the midst of human weakness. When the Apostle Paul begged for the removal of his suffering, the Lord responded by saying, "My grace is sufficient for you, for My power is perfected in weakness" (2 Corinthians 12:9–10). When we reach the end of our own resources, we are positioned to experience the sustaining power of Christ in a way that prosperity could never teach us.

Finally, we must recognize that we live in a world broken by the fall and the reality of sin. However, for those who are called according to His purpose, God promises that He is working all things together for good (Romans 8:28). This "good" is our conformity to the likeness of His Son. Even the most painful circumstances are held within His sovereign hand and are limited by His mercy.

If you are currently walking through a season of deep trial, remember that Jesus Christ is the "Man of Sorrows" who is intimately acquainted with grief. He endured the greatest tribulation of all, the wrath of God on the cross, to pay for our sins and secure our eternal peace. Through His bodily resurrection, He has secured victory over every sorrow and death itself.

I invite you to turn to Him in your distress, for He is a high priest who is able to sympathize with our weaknesses (Hebrews 4:15–16). You can trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of sins and the gift of eternal life. What holds you back from resting in His finished work today? As a practical step, I encourage you to spend time in the Psalms, bringing your honest heart to God in prayer, and to seek the fellowship and prayers of a faithful local church.
What does Deuteronomy 10:12 tell us God requires of us?

Deuteronomy 10:12 provides a beautiful and comprehensive summary of what God desires from His covenant people: to fear the LORD your God, to walk in all His ways, to love Him, to serve the LORD your God with all your heart and with all your soul, and, as the following verse adds, to keep His commandments for your own well-being. This passage reveals that God is not a distant tyrant seeking burdensome tribute, but a loving Father who calls us into a relationship characterized by reverence, obedience, and total devotion.

To fear the LORD is to have a profound, holy awe of His majesty and a righteous trembling before His sovereign authority. This is not the cowering fear of a slave before a cruel master, but the deep respect of a child for a perfect Father. Walking in His ways refers to the practical outworking of this fear in our daily conduct, ensuring our paths align with His revealed will. Love is the inward motivation that fuels this walk, while serving Him with all the heart and soul speaks to the totality of our existence being offered as a living sacrifice to His glory.

This divine standard reflects the perfect holiness of God. However, as we look honestly at these requirements, we must acknowledge that we have all fallen short of loving and serving Him with our whole hearts. Our failure to keep this law highlights our desperate need for a Savior. Scripture teaches that while the law was given to show us the right way, it also serves to lead us to Jesus Christ, the only one who ever fulfilled these requirements perfectly. 

Christ’s life of perfect obedience, His substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and His bodily resurrection provide the only way for us to be reconciled to God. Through faith in Him, we are not only forgiven but are also given the Holy Spirit, who begins the work of progressive sanctification in us, enabling us to truly love and serve God from the heart.

I encourage you to take a moment today to reflect on your own heart. Is there an area of your life where you have been walking in your own ways rather than His? You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. As a practical step, choose one of the five elements mentioned in Deuteronomy 10:12 and ask the Lord in prayer to deepen that specific area of your devotion this week.

Deuteronomy 10:12; Deuteronomy 10:13; Romans 3:20; Galatians 3:24; Romans 12:1
Is it okay to get tattoos if they are of a Christian nature?

Beloved, the question of whether a Christian may adorn their body with tattoos, even those of a sacred nature, is one that touches upon the beautiful reality of our liberty in Christ and our solemn responsibility as stewards of God’s temple. In the history of the church, this has been regarded as a tertiary issue—a matter of personal conscience and Christian liberty where faithful believers may reach different conclusions.

The Holy Scriptures provide us with principles rather than a direct "yes" or "no" for the New Covenant believer. We must approach this with both the boldness of truth and the tenderness of a shepherd’s heart.

**The Context of the Law**
Many who argue against tattoos point to the Old Testament command: "You shall not make any cuts on your body for the dead or tattoo yourselves. I am the LORD" (Leviticus 19:28). To understand this rightly, we must see it in its historical and theological context. This prohibition was given to the nation of Israel to distinguish them from the surrounding pagan nations, who practiced ritualistic cutting and tattooing as part of idolatrous mourning rites and the worship of false gods.

As those under the New Covenant, we recognize that the ceremonial laws of Israel, which served as a wall of separation, have been fulfilled in Jesus Christ. While the moral law of God remains our perfect standard of righteousness, the specific prohibitions against cultural marks of that era are not strictly binding on the Christian in the same legal sense.

**The Principle of Stewardship**
The primary concern for a child of God is the truth that we are not our own. The Apostle Paul writes, "Do you not know that your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit who is in you, whom you have received from God? You are not your own; you were bought at a price. Therefore glorify God with your body" (1 Corinthians 6:19–20).

Because our bodies belong to the Lord, any decision to alter them must be weighed with great care. A Christian should ask:
1.  **Motive:** Is the desire for this tattoo rooted in a genuine wish to testify to the grace of God, or is it an expression of worldly rebellion or a craving for attention?
2.  **Modesty and Witness:** Will this mark hinder your gospel witness in your specific community or family? We are called to "give no offense in anything, so that our ministry may not be blamed" (2 Corinthians 6:3).
3.  **Wisdom:** Tattoos are largely permanent. Is this an expression of a passing season of life, or a lasting truth you are prepared to carry to the grave?

**The Matter of Conscience**
Because the Bible does not explicitly forbid tattoos for the believer today, it falls under the category of things "indifferent," governed by the conscience. Romans 14 teaches us that "each one should be fully convinced in his own mind" (Romans 14:5). If your conscience pricks you or causes you doubt, then for you, it would be sin to proceed, for "everything that does not come from faith is sin" (Romans 14:23). Conversely, if a brother or sister proceeds in faith, seeking to honor God, we must not judge them harshly.

**A Pastoral Path Forward**
If you are considering a Christian tattoo, I encourage you to take these actionable steps:
*   **Pray for Discernment:** Ask the Lord to reveal the true intentions of your heart.
*   **Consult Godly Counsel:** Speak with your elders or mature believers in your local church to see how such a decision might affect your fellowship and ministry.
*   **Wait:** Give yourself a season of time—perhaps several months—to see if the desire remains steady and remains focused on the glory of Christ.

Whether you choose to wear a mark of faith on your skin or not, remember that the most vital "mark" of a Christian is the inward seal of the Holy Spirit and the fruit of love that flows from a transformed life. "So whether you eat or drink or whatever you do, do it all to the glory of God" (1 Corinthians 10:31).

If you find yourself searching for identity or a way to show you belong to Him, remember that the greatest testimony of ownership is not in ink, but in the blood of Christ shed for you. You are saved by grace alone through faith alone in Him. If you have not yet trusted Him, turn from your sin today and rest in the finished work of the One who was pierced for our transgressions (Isaiah 53:5). What holds you back from fully trusting in His sufficiency today?
What day is the Sabbath, Saturday or Sunday?

In the historical and biblical record, the Sabbath is the seventh day of the week, known as Saturday, while Sunday is observed by the Christian church as the Lord’s Day in honor of the resurrection of Jesus Christ. 

From the beginning of creation, God instituted the seventh day as a day of rest, as seen in Genesis 2:2-3, and later codified this in the Ten Commandments in Exodus 20:8-11. For the people of Israel under the Mosaic Covenant, the Sabbath was a sign of their relationship with God and a mandatory day of physical and spiritual rest. 

However, with the coming of Jesus Christ, a significant shift occurred. Jesus rose from the dead on the first day of the week, which we call Sunday, as recorded in Matthew 28:1 and Mark 16:2. This day marks the beginning of the new creation and the completion of the work of redemption. Consequently, the early church began to meet on the first day of the week for worship, the breaking of bread, and the collection of offerings, as seen in Acts 20:7 and 1 Corinthians 16:2. In the book of Revelation 1:10, the Apostle John refers to this as the Lord’s Day.

Within the Christian tradition, there are different perspectives on how this applies to believers today:

*   **The Christian Sabbath View:** Many historic Protestant traditions, including the Reformed Baptist tradition, believe that the moral principle of the Sabbath (one day in seven set apart for God) remains in effect but that the day was changed from the seventh to the first day of the week to commemorate Christ’s resurrection. This is often called the Christian Sabbath.
*   **The Fulfillment View:** Other Christians believe that the Old Testament Sabbath was a shadow or "type" that pointed toward the true rest found in Jesus Christ, as suggested in Colossians 2:16-17 and Hebrews 4:9-10. In this view, Christ is our permanent Sabbath rest, and while gathering on Sunday is a vital New Testament practice, it is not a direct continuation of the Mosaic Sabbath law.
*   **The Seventh-day View:** A minority of Christian groups maintain that the Sabbath remains strictly on Saturday and should be observed according to the Old Testament timing.

The differences in these views often stem from how one understands the continuity between the Old and New Covenants. However, the consistent testimony of the New Testament is that the church honors the Lord’s Day (Sunday) because it celebrates the victory of our Savior over sin and death.

The ultimate reality is that no amount of day-keeping can save a soul. The Law points us to our need for a Savior because we cannot keep it perfectly. Jesus Christ, the Lord of the Sabbath, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rose bodily on the third day. He offers true rest for your soul, a rest that comes not from your own works, but through His finished work. You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for your salvation and eternal rest.

As a practical step, I encourage you to seek out a faithful local church where the Word of God is preached and to join with other believers this coming Lord’s Day to worship the One who has called us out of darkness into His marvelous light. What is it that concerns you most regarding the observance of these days?
Why did God punish Pharaoh for Abram’s lie (Genesis 12:17)?

God struck Pharaoh with severe plagues because He was sovereignly protecting the covenant promise He had made to Abram. While Pharaoh was unaware that Sarai was Abram’s wife due to Abram’s deception, God intervened to preserve the purity of the family line through which the Messiah, Jesus Christ, would eventually be born.

We must first be clear that Abram’s lie was a sin. It was a failure of faith born out of fear, as he chose to rely on his own cleverness rather than God’s protection. However, God had given Abram a specific promise in Genesis 12:1-3 that he would become a great nation and that all the families of the earth would be blessed through him. If Sarai had remained in Pharaoh’s house as his wife, that promise would have been compromised. The plagues were not a declaration of Pharaoh's personal moral guilt in the deception, but a necessary act of providence to stop a greater evil from occurring and to force the truth to light.

In this event, we see the doctrine of God’s covenant fidelity. God is holy and just, but He is also a protector who watches over His people even when they falter. The plagues served as a "stop" sign to Pharaoh, preventing him from violating the marriage bond and ensuring that Sarai was returned to her husband. It demonstrates that God's plans cannot be thwarted by human weakness or pagan power. Even when we are faithless, He remains faithful, for He cannot deny Himself as noted in 2 Timothy 2:13.

This account is a profound reminder of the Gospel. Like Abram, we often find ourselves acting out of fear and failing to trust the God who has called us. We deserve the "plagues" of judgment for our own deceptions and sins. Yet, God in His mercy sent Jesus Christ—the perfect descendant of Abram and Sarai—to take the punishment we deserved. Christ was struck so that we might be spared, and He rose again to secure our eternal hope.

If you are currently struggling with fear or the weight of past failures, a practical next step is to meditate on the character of God as your Shield. Read through Psalm 3 and Psalm 27, and in your own prayers, confess any areas where you have tried to control your circumstances through worldly wisdom or dishonesty. Ask the Holy Spirit to strengthen your trust in His sovereign care.

The same God who preserved Sarai in the house of Pharaoh is the God who offers you grace today. You can turn to Him now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting God's protection in the difficult areas of your life today?
Christmas Story — Day 4 — Do Christians worship Mary?

Christians do not worship Mary. According to the Holy Scriptures, worship is reserved exclusively for the triune God—Father, Son, and Holy Spirit.

While we do not worship Mary, we hold her in high esteem as a faithful and chosen servant of God. She was the "blessed among women" who uniquely bore the Son of God in His humanity (Luke 1:42). However, Mary herself recognized her position as a creature in need of a Creator. In her beautiful song of praise, she declared, "My soul magnifies the Lord, and my spirit rejoices in God my Savior" (Luke 1:46-47). By calling God her Savior, Mary acknowledged her own need for the redeeming grace that only her Son, Jesus Christ, could provide.

The Bible is clear that there is only one Mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus (1 Timothy 2:5). To offer Mary the worship or prayer that belongs to God alone would be to depart from the clear teaching of the Word. When Jesus was tempted in the wilderness, He responded with the definitive standard for all believers: "Worship the Lord your God and serve Him only" (Matthew 4:10).

We honor Mary best when we follow her own example and her only recorded command in the Gospels: "Do whatever He tells you" (John 2:5). Her life points us away from herself and toward the Lord Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. The Christmas story is the account of God becoming flesh to do what no human being, including Mary, could ever do: satisfy the justice of God and take away the sins of the world.

As you reflect on the role of Mary this Christmas, I encourage you to read her song of praise in Luke chapter 1 and use it as a guide for your own heart to magnify the Lord.

The message of Christmas is that Jesus Christ came to save sinners by His perfect life, His substitutionary death on the cross, and His bodily resurrection. This salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God right now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What is preventing you from placing your full trust in Him today?
What is Kemetic spirituality / Kemetism?

Kemetic spirituality, also known as Kemetism, is a modern neopagan religious movement that seeks to revive or reconstruct the beliefs and practices of ancient Egypt. The name is derived from "Kemet," the ancient name for Egypt meaning "the black land" (Psalm 105:23; Psalm 106:22). Adherents typically worship a pantheon of deities known as the Netjeru, such as Ra, Isis, or Osiris, and emphasize the concept of "Ma’at," which refers to a cosmic order of truth, balance, and justice.

While some are drawn to this path to reconnect with African ancestry or ancient mysteries, we must speak the truth in love: this system is a form of idolatry that replaces the Creator with the created (Romans 1:25). The Bible is clear that there is only one God, and the gods of the nations are mere idols (Psalm 96:5). When God brought His people out of Egypt, He explicitly stated that He would execute judgment "against all the gods of Egypt," demonstrating that they have no power and are not gods at all (Exodus 12:12; Numbers 33:4). To follow Kemetism is to pursue shadows and myths rather than the living God who has revealed Himself through His Word and His Son.

The human heart longs for the order and justice that the concept of Ma'at attempts to describe, but true righteousness cannot be achieved through our own efforts or by returning to ancient paganism. God is perfectly holy, supreme, and sovereign. Because of our sin, we are all naturally separated from Him and under His just judgment. No ritual or adherence to a moral code can bridge this gap.

However, the Gospel is the good news that God, in His mercy, provided the only way of reconciliation. Jesus Christ is truly God and truly man (the hypostatic union), born of a virgin, and lived a perfect, sinless life. He died a substitutionary, atoning death on the cross to pay the debt for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). He is now exalted as Lord of all. Salvation is a gift of grace alone, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works of our own.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. I encourage you to set aside the pursuit of these ancient idols and read the Gospel of John to see the glory of the true God. What is it that draws you toward Kemetic spirituality, and have you considered how the historical reality of Jesus satisfies the deepest needs of your heart?
Should Christians be concerned about the idea of Sharia Law?

Christians have a valid reason for concern regarding Sharia Law because it represents a legal and social system built upon a foundation that explicitly denies the person and work of Jesus Christ. As those who hold the Bible to be the infallible Word of God, we recognize that any system claiming divine authority while rejecting the Gospel of grace is a spiritual error that leads people away from the only source of life (John 14:6; 1 John 2:22-23).

The concern for the believer is multifaceted, touching on the nature of salvation, the role of the state, and the freedom to fulfill the Great Commission.

First, there is a fundamental conflict regarding how a person is made right with God. Sharia is a system of legalism, focusing on outward adherence to a code of conduct to earn divine favor. In contrast, the Bible teaches the doctrine of justification by faith alone. We are saved not by our own works of the law, but by the perfect obedience and sacrificial death of Jesus Christ on our behalf (Romans 3:20-24; Galatians 2:16). Any system that replaces the grace of Christ with a yoke of human effort is a departure from the truth that sets men free (Galatians 5:1).

Second, there is a concern regarding religious liberty and the jurisdiction of the state. The New Testament establishes a distinction between the "things that are Caesar’s" and the "things that are God’s" (Matthew 22:21). While Christians are called to be exemplary citizens and submit to earthly authorities (Romans 13:1-7; 1 Peter 2:13-17), we believe the state’s power is limited to civil order and justice. Sharia Law merges the civil magistrate with religious enforcement, often resulting in the criminalization of the Christian faith and the suppression of the Gospel. This directly hinders the command to make disciples of all nations (Matthew 28:19-20).

Furthermore, Sharia often mandates harsh penalties for those who leave Islam to follow Christ. We must have deep compassion and concern for our brothers and sisters in Christ who live under such systems, facing persecution for their testimony (Hebrews 13:3).

While we may be concerned about the spread of systems that oppose the Gospel, our response must not be rooted in fear or animosity. We are called to love our neighbors, including those who hold to different faiths, and to pray for those who persecute the church (Matthew 5:44). The ultimate "weapon" of the Christian is not political maneuvering, but the bold and humble proclamation of the Word of God, which has the power to pull down spiritual strongholds (2 Corinthians 10:4-5).

As a practical step, I encourage you to pray daily for the "Persecuted Church" in countries where Sharia is the law of the land. Ask God to give them boldness and to protect them as they witness for Him. Additionally, look for opportunities to share the hope of the Gospel with your neighbors, demonstrating the love of Christ in both word and deed.

At the heart of this issue is a question of who holds the final authority. The Bible tells us that God is holy and we are sinners separated from Him by our transgressions. No amount of law-keeping can bridge that gap. However, God in His mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. He lived a sinless life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. He is the only Mediator between God and man (1 Timothy 2:5).

You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and eternal life. What holds you back from trusting in His finished work today?
What is the meaning of the foxes in Song of Solomon 2:15?

The "little foxes" mentioned in Song of Solomon 2:15 symbolize the subtle, seemingly insignificant sins, attitudes, or distractions that threaten to ruin the budding fruitfulness of a godly relationship or a believer's intimacy with Christ. In the context of this poem, the vineyard represents the blossoming love between the bride and the bridegroom, which requires protection from anything that would hinder its growth. "Catch for us the foxes, the little foxes that ruin the vineyards, for our vineyards are in bloom" Song of Solomon 2:15.

While a large animal might be easily spotted, these "little foxes" are dangerous because they are small, crafty, and often go unnoticed until the damage is done. In our spiritual lives, these can represent "respectable sins" such as irritability, a prayerless spirit, a lack of gratitude, or a small root of bitterness. If left unaddressed, these small habits of the heart can gnaw at the tender vines of our sanctification and rob us of the joy of our salvation.

From a pastoral perspective, this verse serves as a call to vigilance and repentance. We often guard against the "great" sins while allowing small compromises to flourish in the secret corners of our hearts. Yet, even the smallest sin is an affront to a holy God and a threat to our spiritual vitality. The vineyard of the soul is most beautiful when it is kept clear of these intruders through the constant application of the Word of God and the power of the Holy Spirit.

You cannot catch these foxes by your own strength or through mere moral effort. True protection for your heart is found only in Jesus Christ, who is the True Vine (John 15:1). Because of His perfect life, His substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and His bodily resurrection, we have been given the grace not only to be forgiven but to be transformed.

A practical step for you today is to set aside a few moments for quiet reflection before the Lord. Ask the Holy Spirit to reveal one "little fox"—perhaps a habit of speech or a hidden pride—that has been nipping at your spiritual fruitfulness. Bring this specifically to God in prayer, repenting of it and asking for His strength to "catch" it before it causes more harm.

If you find yourself struggling to maintain this intimacy because you have never truly known the peace of God, remember that Christ came to reconcile sinners to Himself. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive a new heart and a life that flourishes in His grace. What is it that might be keeping you from fully surrendering these "little things" to His Lordship?
What is the false cause fallacy?

The false cause fallacy is a lapse in reasoning where one incorrectly assumes that because two events are related or happen in sequence, one must have caused the other. In formal logic, this is often described by the phrase "correlation does not imply causation." It occurs when a person sees a connection between two things and jumps to a conclusion about their relationship without sufficient evidence.

In our walk with the Lord, we see this error frequently when people attempt to interpret the providence of God through the lens of human intuition rather than the light of His Word. This is the mistake of assuming that every specific earthly trial is the direct result of a specific personal sin, or that every earthly success is a sign of God's special approval. While God is the sovereign primary cause of all that occurs (Ephesians 1:11), He governs the world through various secondary causes and for purposes that often remain hidden from our immediate sight.

A clear biblical example of this fallacy appears in the Gospel of John. When the disciples encountered a man who had been blind from birth, they asked Jesus, "Rabbi, who sinned, this man or his parents, that he was born blind?" (John 9:2). They were operating under a false cause assumption, believing that the man's blindness was necessarily caused by a particular act of disobedience. Jesus corrected their error, stating, "Neither this man nor his parents sinned, but this happened so that the works of God might be displayed in him" (John 9:3).

As those who love the truth, we must be careful not to judge our neighbors or ourselves based on these simplistic and often legalistic assumptions. We must remember that even the righteous suffer, as seen in the life of Job, whose friends were rebuked by God for their "folly" in wrongly attributing Job's misery to hidden wickedness (Job 42:7-8). Our duty is not to play the judge of God's secret providences, but to trust His character. We know with certainty that for those who love God, all things work together for good, for those who are called according to His purpose (Romans 8:28).

If you are currently facing a difficult season and are tempted to believe God is punishing you for a specific past failure, I encourage you to turn your eyes to the cross of Christ. For the believer, the penalty for sin has been paid in full by Jesus (Romans 5:1). Instead of searching for a "false cause" in your own merit or failure, seek the Lord in prayer and ask for the peace that surpasses understanding (Philippians 4:7). A practical next step would be to spend time in the Psalms, specifically Psalm 73, which addresses the confusion we often feel when observing the complicated causes and effects of life in a fallen world.
Is it a sin to share copyrighted material on the internet?

Sharing copyrighted material without permission or proper payment is a sin, as it violates the biblical principles of submission to authority and the prohibition against theft. While the digital age makes the duplication of files appear harmless or intangible, the Word of God speaks clearly to the integrity of our hearts and our conduct before a watching world.

First, God has commanded every person to be subject to the governing authorities, for there is no authority except from God, and those that exist have been instituted by Him Romans 13:1. In most nations, copyright laws are established to protect the labor and livelihoods of creators. When we bypass these laws, we are in rebellion against the order God has established for a just society Romans 13:2. As Christians, we are called to be model citizens, obeying the law not only to avoid punishment but for the sake of conscience before God 1 Peter 2:13-14.

Second, the Eighth Commandment declares, "You shall not steal" Exodus 20:15. Theft is the taking of something that does not belong to you without the owner's consent. Intellectual property—the music, writing, software, or art produced by another—is the fruit of their labor. To take or distribute this work without following the terms set by the creator is a form of digital theft. We are commanded instead to work honestly with our own hands so that we may have something to share with those in need, rather than taking what we have not earned Ephesians 4:28.

Furthermore, we must consider the "Golden Rule" given by our Lord Jesus: "So in everything, do to others what you would have them do to you" Matthew 7:12. If you had spent months or years creating something of value, you would rightly expect to be compensated or to have your distribution rights respected. To share copyrighted material is to fail in the Great Commandment to love our neighbor as ourselves Matthew 22:39.

Our use of the internet is a matter of stewardship and sanctification. We represent the Lord Jesus Christ in every click and every download. If you have been involved in sharing or consuming copyrighted material illegally, the path forward is one of repentance. This involves acknowledging the sin to God, turning away from the practice, and, where possible, making restitution—either by deleting the files or purchasing legitimate copies.

We must remember that none of us can stand before God on the basis of our own law-keeping. We all fall short of His perfect standard of holiness. This realization should drive us to the feet of Jesus Christ. He lived the perfect life of integrity that we have failed to live, and He died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the debt for our sins. He was buried and rose again bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. In Him, you are not only forgiven but given the Holy Spirit to help you walk in a manner worthy of the Gospel. What is it that makes it difficult for you to trust God’s provision and obey His Word in this area of your life?
What does the Bible say about Christian fathers?

Beloved, the Bible portrays the role of a father as a high and holy calling, meant to be a visible mirror of the invisible God. A Christian father is tasked with the spiritual, emotional, and physical care of his household, acting as a shepherd within the home.

The primary duty of a father is the spiritual instruction of his children. Scripture commands fathers not to provoke their children to anger, but to bring them up in the discipline and instruction of the Lord, as we see in Ephesians 6:4. This is not a passive role, but one that requires a father to have the Word of God written upon his own heart first, so that he may teach it diligently to his children throughout the rhythm of daily life, whether sitting in the house or walking along the road, as described in Deuteronomy 6:6-7.

Furthermore, a father is called to lead with a combination of firm authority and tender compassion. While he must provide for the physical needs of his family, for any who do not provide for their own household have denied the faith and are worse than an unbeliever, according to 1 Timothy 5:8, he must also ensure he does not embitter his children so that they do not become discouraged, as commanded in Colossians 3:21. This balance mirrors the character of God, who is both a righteous Judge and a merciful Father to His people. A father's leadership is not meant to be a heavy handed tyranny, but a sacrificial service that points his family toward the leadership of Christ.

A Christian father must recognize that he cannot fulfill this calling in his own strength. We all stumble, and the weight of leading a family often reveals our own deep need for sanctification. This realization should drive a father to the feet of Jesus Christ. If you find yourself weary or feeling the sting of past failures in your home, remember that the Gospel is for fathers too. Christ lived the perfect life we could not live and died the death we deserved, rising again to secure our justification. You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you. He provides the grace not only for your forgiveness but for your transformation as a parent.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to begin a simple rhythm of family worship. This can be as brief as reading one passage of Scripture together each evening and praying for your children by name. What is one specific way you can show the love and patience of Christ to your household this week?
What was the process of deciding on the Old Testament canon?

The process of deciding on the Old Testament canon was not a matter of human beings choosing which books they liked best, but rather a humble recognition by God's people of the words He had already spoken. The word "canon" comes from a Greek term meaning a "rule" or "measuring stick." For the Old Testament, the canon consists of those books that were breathed out by God through His prophets and recognized by the community of faith as having unique, divine authority.

This recognition began with the very first books of the Bible. When Moses wrote the Law, it was immediately recognized as the commandment of the Lord and placed beside the Ark of the Covenant for safekeeping as recorded in Deuteronomy 31:24-26. As God raised up later prophets, their writings were added to this collection because they spoke with the same "Thus says the LORD" authority. By the time of the return from the Babylonian exile, the Jewish people had a settled understanding of the "Law and the Prophets."

The ultimate authority for the Old Testament canon is the Lord Jesus Christ Himself. During His earthly ministry, Jesus frequently referred to the Scriptures in a way that mirrored the established Hebrew canon of His day. In Luke 24:44, He spoke of "the Law of Moses, the Prophets, and the Psalms," which represented the three recognized sections of the Hebrew Bible: the Torah, the Nevi'im (Prophets), and the Ketuvim (Writings). By affirming these sections, Jesus set His divine seal of approval on the books we now hold as the thirty-nine books of the Old Testament.

We must understand that these books are self-authenticating. This means they carry the marks of divine authorship within themselves, showing forth the majesty, purity, and consistency of God’s character. While some later traditions included the Apocrypha—books written during the period between the Old and New Testaments—the historic Christian faith, following the example of the Jewish people and the Apostles, does not recognize them as inspired Scripture. These books were never quoted as authoritative by Jesus or the Apostles, and they often contain historical and doctrinal errors that conflict with the established Word of God.

As a practical matter, you can rest your soul upon the Old Testament with total confidence. It is not a collection of ancient myths but a historically accurate record of God’s redemptive work. When you read the accounts of Creation in Genesis 1 or the crossing of the Red Sea in Exodus 14, you are reading historical facts recorded by the inspiration of the Holy Spirit.

The primary purpose of these Scriptures is to point us to the Promised Seed, the Messiah. From the first promise of a Savior in Genesis 3:15 to the closing warnings of Malachi, the Old Testament is a herald announcing the coming of Jesus Christ. If you find yourself searching these ancient texts, remember that they are intended to lead you to the living Word, Jesus Christ. 

The same God who spoke to the fathers by the prophets has now spoken to us by His Son as stated in Hebrews 1:1-2. This Jesus, being truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily on the third day to secure eternal life for all who believe. You can turn to Him today, repenting of your sin and trusting in His finished work alone for your salvation.

To grow in your understanding of the canon, I encourage you to spend time reading the Psalms daily. Notice how often the psalmists express a deep, unshakable trust in the "law of the LORD" as perfect and sure, as described in Psalm 19:7-11. Let their devotion to the Word of God mirror your own as you submit your life to its authority.
Is cyber-sex / phone sex a sin?

Yes, the Bible identifies sexual activity outside the covenant of marriage between a man and a woman as sin. Scripture uses the term sexual immorality—or *porneia* in the original Greek—to describe any sexual engagement, whether physical or virtual, that occurs outside the boundaries God established for our protection and His glory (1 Corinthians 6:18; Hebrews 13:4).

God designed human sexuality to be a physical, emotional, and spiritual union expressed exclusively within the marriage of one man and one woman (Genesis 2:24). Cyber-sex and phone sex violate this design by seeking sexual gratification through lust and the objectification of others. Our Lord Jesus Christ made it clear that the heart is the root of the matter when He taught that anyone who looks at another person lustfully has already committed adultery in their heart (Matthew 5:27–28). Even if there is no physical contact, these acts involve intentional arousal and the indulgence of desires that are meant to be reserved for a spouse.

Furthermore, Christians are called to a life of sanctification, which is the process of being set apart for God’s holy purposes. We are told to treat others with absolute purity (1 Timothy 5:2) and to let no hint of sexual immorality exist among us (Ephesians 5:3). Engaging in virtual sexual acts trains the heart to seek self-centered pleasure rather than the self-giving love that characterizes a walk with Christ. It grieves the Holy Spirit and creates a barrier in our fellowship with God.

However, there is profound hope for those who have stumbled into these behaviors. The same Bible that names sin also proclaims a Savior who is "faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness" (1 John 1:9). God is holy and His justice demands a payment for sin, but in His great love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus lived a perfectly pure life, died on the cross as a substitute to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and the power of sin.

Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God right now, repent of this sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. He does not merely offer a clean slate; He offers a new heart and the power of the Holy Spirit to walk in purity.

As a practical step toward healing, I encourage you to confess this specifically to God and then seek out a trusted elder or mature believer in a local, Bible-believing church. Bringing these hidden struggles into the light of Christian fellowship is essential for finding lasting freedom and accountability (James 5:16). What is it that makes you hesitate to bring this struggle before the Lord today?
Do unbelievers immediately go to hell when they die?

The Holy Scriptures teach that at the moment of death, the soul of an unbeliever enters an immediate, conscious state of separation from God and undergoes punishment for sin. This is often referred to as the intermediate state, where the soul awaits the final resurrection and the last judgment.

In the account of the rich man and Lazarus, the Lord Jesus describes the rich man dying and being "in torment" in Hades immediately, even while his brothers were still alive on earth (Luke 16:22–24). This makes it clear that there is no "soul sleep" or period of unconsciousness for the deceased. Furthermore, Hebrews 9:27 declares that "it is appointed for man to die once, and after that comes judgment." There is no second chance for repentance or a middle ground like purgatory after one passes from this life.

While this immediate state involves conscious suffering, it is distinct from the final eternal state. The Bible teaches that when Christ returns, there will be a bodily resurrection of both the righteous and the unrighteous (John 5:28–29). Those who have rejected Christ will stand before the Great White Throne to be judged according to their deeds (Revelation 20:11–13). Because no human can stand before a holy God on the merit of their own works, those whose names are not found written in the Book of Life are cast into the Lake of Fire, which is the final and eternal place of punishment (Revelation 20:14–15).

This is a solemn and heavy truth, but it is the testimony of the Word of God. God is perfectly holy and just, and His wrath against sin is a real and necessary consequence of His goodness. However, the same God who is Just is also the Justifier of those who have faith in Jesus. In His immense love, God the Father sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live the perfect life we could not live and to die a substitutionary atoning death on the cross. By taking the punishment that sinners deserved, Jesus satisfied the demands of God's justice. His bodily resurrection on the third day proved His victory over sin and death.

Salvation is not earned by our efforts or our goodness, but is a gift secured by Christ's historical work. We are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting in Him today?
How should Christians react to persecution against the LGBTQ community?

The Christian response to any form of unjust violence, dehumanization, or cruelty must be rooted in the dual reality of God’s absolute truth and His profound love for His image-bearers. We are called to hold fast to the biblical standard of holiness while simultaneously acting as agents of mercy and peace in a broken world.

First, Christians must recognize that every person, regardless of their lifestyle or self-identification, is created in the image of God (Genesis 1:26-27). Because of this inherent dignity, the Bible forbids us from harboring malice, acting in cruelty, or remaining silent in the face of physical harm or unjust treatment toward others. To hate our neighbor is a violation of the Great Commandment to love our neighbor as ourselves (Matthew 22:39). We are instructed, as much as it depends on us, to live peaceably with all men and to never avenge ourselves, but rather to overcome evil with good (Romans 12:18-21).

However, true love never rejoices in unrighteousness but rejoices in the truth (1 Corinthians 13:6). We must distinguish between "persecution"—which involves violence, legal injustice, or dehumanizing abuse—and the "offense" of the Gospel. While we must oppose cruelty, we cannot affirm or normalize behavior that the Scripture clearly identifies as sin. The Bible describes homosexual practice as a departure from God’s created order (Romans 1:26-27; Leviticus 18:22). Therefore, it is not an act of hate to call all people to repentance; rather, it is the highest form of love to warn someone of the spiritual danger of sin and point them toward the forgiveness found in Jesus Christ.

Our reaction to the LGBTQ community should mirror the heart of our Lord Jesus. He was "full of grace and truth" (John 1:14). We should be the first to offer a helping hand to those in physical distress or facing unjust violence, just as the Good Samaritan cared for one who was culturally and religiously an outcast (Luke 10:25-37). Yet, we must also be the ones to speak clearly about the need for a new birth. 

The Gospel offers a path for everyone, declaring that though we were all once defined by our sins, we can be washed, sanctified, and justified in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and by the Spirit of our God (1 Corinthians 6:9-11). If you see someone being mistreated, stand for their human dignity because they belong to God by right of creation. At the same time, pray for the opportunity to share the hope of the Gospel, which is the only power that can truly transform a human heart.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
How did Satan and other angels sin if heaven is sinless?

The Scriptures reveal that the origin of sin did not begin on earth, but in the heavenly realms through a voluntary rebellion of the heart. While God created all things good (Genesis 1:31), He alone possesses the attribute of immutability, which means He is unchangeable in His holiness. Angels, though created in a state of original righteousness and splendor, were finite creatures with a will, and they were capable of turning away from their Creator.

The fall of Satan, often identified as the "anointed guardian cherub" in prophetic lamentations, occurred because pride took root in his heart. Ezekiel 28:15 states, "You were blameless in your ways from the day you were created until wickedness was found in you." This wickedness was not something God planted in him, for God is light and in Him is no darkness at all (1 John 1:5). Rather, it was a turning of the will away from God toward the self. Satan’s sin was the desire to usurp the glory that belongs to God alone, as seen in the "I will" statements of Isaiah 14:13–14, where he declared, "I will ascend to heaven; I will raise my throne above the stars of God... I will make myself like the Most High."

Other angels followed him in this rebellion, choosing to leave their designated positions of authority. Jude 1:6 explains that "the angels who did not stay within their own position of authority, but left their proper dwelling, He has kept in eternal chains under gloomy darkness until the judgment of the great day." Their sin was a deliberate rejection of God’s sovereignty in favor of their own autonomy.

It is a profound mystery how a perfect being in a perfect environment could conceive of sin. However, we must distinguish between the "sinlessness" of heaven as a place of God’s manifest presence and the "moral stability" of a creature. Only God is necessarily holy by His very nature. The angels were holy by grace and by their initial creation, but they were not yet confirmed in that holiness until they passed a period of probation. Those who did not fall are now "elect angels" (1 Timothy 5:21), forever upheld by God’s power, while those who rebelled were cast out of God's favorable presence (2 Peter 2:4).

This history serves as a solemn warning that pride is the swiftest path to destruction. Even in the presence of God's glory, the heart that looks away from Christ and toward itself finds only darkness. While the fallen angels have no hope of redemption, the glorious truth of the Gospel is that God the Son became man to save fallen human beings. Jesus Christ came to destroy the works of the devil (1 John 3:8) and to reconcile us to God through His death and resurrection.

I encourage you to examine your own heart today. Are you resting in your own strength, or are you looking to the only One who can keep you from falling? You can turn to God right now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and the gift of eternal life. What is preventing you from placing your full trust in Him today?
What is the greatest sin?

The greatest sin is the violation of the greatest commandment: the failure to love the Lord your God with all your heart, soul, mind, and strength. Jesus Christ explicitly identified this supreme duty in Matthew 22:37–38, stating that loving God is the first and greatest commandment. Consequently, to withhold from the Creator the honor, worship, and affection that are rightfully His is the root and summit of all human rebellion.

While we often categorize sins by their outward effects on society, the Bible teaches that all sin is primarily an affront to a holy and sovereign God. This is the essence of unbelief. To live as though God does not exist, or to treat His Word with indifference, is to commit high treason against the King of Heaven. In a specific sense, Jesus also spoke of the "unpardonable sin," which is blasphemy against the Holy Spirit, as recorded in Matthew 12:31–32. This represents a final, heart-hardened rejection of the Spirit's testimony to the person and work of Christ. It is not a sin one commits by accident, but a settled, defiant refusal of God’s grace.

The gravity of sin is seen most clearly at the cross of Calvary. Because God is perfectly just, He cannot simply overlook transgression. Our sin—every lie, every act of selfishness, and our fundamental rebellion—rendered us spiritually dead and separated from Him as described in Romans 3:23 and Romans 6:23. Yet, in His profound mercy, God the Father sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived the perfect life of love toward God that we failed to live, and then He died a substitutionary death, bearing the full weight of God's righteous wrath in our place. His bodily resurrection on the third day proved His victory over sin and death, offering a way for sinners to be reconciled to God.

If you feel the weight of your failings or the distance between yourself and your Creator, know that there is no sin so great that the grace of Christ is not greater still. The Gospel is not a call to fix yourself, but a call to trust in the One who has already finished the work of redemption. You can turn to God even now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive full forgiveness and eternal life. What is it that might be keeping you from resting in His grace today?
What does the Bible say about sedition?

The Bible teaches that sedition, which involves inciting rebellion or stirring up strife against governing authorities, is a sin that disrupts the order God has placed over human society. Scripture identifies such dissensions and factions as "works of the flesh" (Galatians 5:19–21), contrasting them with the peaceable fruit produced by the Holy Spirit.

In Romans 13:1–2, the Apostle Paul writes, "Everyone must submit himself to the governing authorities, for there is no authority except that which is from God. The authorities that exist have been appointed by God. Consequently, whoever rebels against the authority is opposing what God has instituted, and those who do so will bring judgment on themselves." This passage establishes that because God is sovereign over the nations, to resist lawful authority without a biblical mandate is to resist God’s providential placement of those leaders.

Similarly, the Apostle Peter exhorts believers to "Submit yourselves for the Lord’s sake to every human institution, whether to the king as the supreme authority, or to governors as those sent by him to punish those who do wrong and to praise those who do right" (1 Peter 2:13–14). The Christian is called to be an exemplary citizen, not out of a blind devotion to men, but out of a deep reverence for Christ, who is the true Head of all things.

However, the Bible provides a clear boundary for this submission. Scripture does not demand obedience if a government commands a believer to sin or forbids what God has explicitly commanded. When the apostles were ordered to stop preaching the Gospel, they replied in Acts 5:29, "We must obey God rather than men." This is not sedition in the sense of a lawless or self-serving spirit, but a display of higher loyalty to the King of Kings.

In your daily life, a faithful way to honor God’s word is to transform criticism into intercession. A practical step is to follow the instruction to "make petitions, prayers, intercessions, and thanksgiving for everyone, for kings and all those in authority, that we may live peaceful and quiet lives in all godliness and holiness" (1 Timothy 2:1–2).

Ultimately, our greatest problem is not the failings of human government, but our own natural rebellion against God’s holy law. We have all sought to be our own authorities and have turned away from His righteous path. Yet, God in His great mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who perfectly submitted to the Father and even to unjust human trials. He died for our rebellions and rose bodily from the grave to offer us reconciliation and peace with God. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from fully trusting in His sovereignty today?
What is the International Christian Church (ICC), and what do they believe?

The International Christian Church (ICC), also known as the Sold-Out Discipling Movement, is a restorationist organization founded in 2006 by Kip McKean. It emerged as a splinter group from the International Churches of Christ (ICOC) after McKean called for a return to what he described as "biblical" principles of total commitment and aggressive evangelism. While they use the language of Scripture, their core convictions often depart from the historical and biblical understanding of the Gospel, particularly regarding how a person is made right with God.

The ICC holds several distinct beliefs that define their practice and identity:

First, they teach a form of baptismal regeneration. They believe that salvation occurs only at the moment of water baptism by immersion and that this baptism is only valid if the person has first become a "sold-out disciple." In their view, those baptized in other Christian denominations or as infants are not truly saved. This teaching conflicts with the biblical truth that justification is by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, as seen in Ephesians 2:8-9 and Romans 3:24-25.

Second, the ICC practices what they call "discipling" or "statutory discipling." Every member is assigned a discipler to whom they must submit and report their daily lives. This often leads to an authoritative environment where leaders make or heavily influence personal decisions regarding finances, dating, and career choices. While Scripture encourages us to sharpen one another, as noted in Proverbs 27:17, it warns leaders not to lord their authority over those in their care, as stated in 1 Peter 5:2-3.

Third, they maintain a strong sense of exclusivity. The ICC often teaches that they are the only true movement of God on earth today and that all other professing Christians are "lost" because they do not follow the ICC's specific model of discipleship. This contradicts the biblical reality that the body of Christ is comprised of all who truly believe in Jesus across many nations and tongues, as shown in Revelation 7:9 and John 10:16.

My friend, it is vital to recognize that when a group adds human requirements or organizational loyalty to the finished work of Jesus, they cloud the beauty of the Gospel. True salvation is not found in our "total commitment" or in the oversight of a human discipler, but in the perfect obedience and sacrificial death of Jesus Christ. He is the only mediator between God and man, as taught in 1 Timothy 2:5.

If you are seeking peace with God, do not look to your own performance or a specific organization. Look to Christ. The Bible tells us that if you confess with your mouth that Jesus is Lord and believe in your heart that God raised Him from the dead, you will be saved, according to Romans 10:9. I encourage you to find a local church that honors the absolute authority of the Bible and preaches the sufficiency of God's grace.

You can turn to God right now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. He promises that whoever comes to Him, He will never cast out, as said in John 6:37. What is it that holds you back from trusting in His grace alone today?
Do some Christmas traditions have pagan origins?

It is a matter of historical record that several customs commonly associated with Christmas have roots in the pre-Christian world. The choice of December 25th, for example, coincided with the Roman festival of Saturnalia and the winter solstice. Similarly, the use of evergreen branches, which represented life in the midst of winter for various ancient peoples, and the burning of logs were elements of pagan festivals such as Yule.

However, as we navigate these historical realities, we must distinguish between the origin of a symbol and its current meaning in the life of a believer. For the Christian, the celebration of Christmas is not the continuation of a pagan rite, but a focused commemoration of the greatest event in human history: the Incarnation of the Lord Jesus Christ. This is an area of tertiary doctrine, meaning it is a matter of Christian liberty where faithful believers may disagree in their practice (Romans 14:5; Colossians 2:16).

There are generally two faithful ways Christians approach these traditions:

*   Some believers choose to abstain from specific traditions or the holiday altogether, desiring to keep their worship free from any association with non-Christian history. They emphasize the principle of worshiping God only as He has specifically commanded in His Word.
*   Other believers choose to "redeem" the season, taking elements that once pointed to false gods or natural cycles and using them as tools to point to the one true God. For them, an evergreen tree becomes a symbol of the eternal life offered through Christ, and the lights represent the Light of the World who has come to dispel our darkness (John 1:4-5; John 8:12).

The essential truth is that Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, was miraculously conceived by the Holy Spirit and born of the Virgin Mary. This is not a "religious story" but a historical fact. He came into a world darkened by sin to fulfill the requirements of God’s perfect holiness. Because we are all sinners separated from a holy God, we could never save ourselves through our own works or traditions. Therefore, Christ lived a perfect life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to satisfy the righteous wrath of God against our sin. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

If you find yourself concerned with the origins of holiday traditions, I encourage you to set your mind primarily on the person and work of Jesus. Use this season to ground your heart in the Scriptures and to fellowship with your local church. You can turn to God even now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of sins and the gift of new life.

As you consider these things, what is your primary concern regarding these traditions? Is it a matter of your own conscience before God, or are you concerned about how these traditions might affect your witness to others?
Where does the Old Testament predict the coming of Christ?

The Old Testament is a grand tapestry of promises, types, and shadows that point directly to the person and work of Jesus Christ. From the very beginning of human history, God began revealing His sovereign plan to redeem a fallen world through a coming Savior. These prophecies are not mere "religious stories," but are historical declarations of God's faithfulness that were fulfilled with literal precision in the life of Jesus.

The first promise of the Gospel, often called the *protoevangelium*, is found in Genesis 3:15, where God declares that the seed of the woman will crush the head of the serpent. This foretold that a human descendant would one day destroy the power of sin and Satan. As the centuries passed, God narrowed the lineage of this coming King, promising that He would come through the line of Abraham in Genesis 22:18, the tribe of Judah in Genesis 49:10, and the royal house of David in 2 Samuel 7:12–13.

Specific details regarding the birth of Christ were also revealed long before He walked the earth. The prophet Isaiah declared that the Messiah would be born of a virgin in Isaiah 7:14, and Micah identified the small town of Bethlehem as His birthplace in Micah 5:2. These prophecies establish that Jesus is truly God and truly man, the long-awaited Immanuel.

The mission and sacrificial work of Christ are most vividly described in the "Suffering Servant" passages. Isaiah 53:3–7 provides a stunningly accurate portrait of Christ’s penal substitutionary atonement, explaining that "He was pierced for our transgressions, He was crushed for our iniquities; the punishment that brought us peace was upon Him, and by His stripes we are healed" (Isaiah 53:5). Furthermore, Psalm 22 describes the specific agonies of the crucifixion centuries before that method of execution was even practiced, including the piercing of His hands and feet in Psalm 22:16 and the casting of lots for His clothing in Psalm 22:18.

The ultimate significance of these prophecies is that they demonstrate God's absolute holiness and His amazing grace. Because God is just, He must punish sin, but because He is loving, He provided His own Son to bear that punishment in our place. This is the heart of the Gospel: Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, took on human nature, lived a perfect life, and died a sacrificial death on the cross to reconcile sinners to God. He did not remain in the grave but rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death.

If you find your heart stirred by these ancient promises, I encourage you to consider your own standing before God. We have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, but salvation is found in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life.

What holds you back from trusting in Him today?

As a practical next step in your study, I suggest reading the Gospel of Matthew. Matthew wrote specifically to show how Jesus fulfilled these Old Testament prophecies, and he frequently cites the passages mentioned above to demonstrate that Jesus is indeed the promised Messiah. Seek the Lord in prayer as you read, asking Him to open your eyes to the beauty of His truth.
What is spiritual adultery?

Spiritual adultery is the betrayal of the exclusive covenant relationship between God and His people through the pursuit of idols, worldly devotions, or anything that usurps God’s rightful place in the human heart. In the Scriptures, God frequently uses the metaphor of marriage to describe His relationship with those He has redeemed, which means that when we turn our primary affections away from Him, it is viewed not merely as a mistake, but as a profound breach of marital-like fidelity.

In the Old Testament, the prophet Hosea provides a vivid and heart-wrenching illustration of this concept. God commanded Hosea to marry a woman who would be unfaithful to him, serving as a living sign of how Israel had abandoned the Lord to worship the Baals and other false deities of the surrounding nations as seen in Hosea 2:2; Hosea 3:1. This is not a matter of God being "insecure," but rather a reflection of His holy jealousy for the truth and for the well-being of His people, for He alone is the source of life and joy. To seek ultimate satisfaction in anything else is to pursue a lie that will eventually fail and destroy the soul.

In the New Testament, this theme continues with even greater clarity regarding the heart. The apostle James writes with a sharp, pastoral boldness to those who claim to follow Christ but still crave the approval and the ways of the fallen world. He says, "You adulteresses! Do you not know that friendship with the world is hostility toward God? Therefore, whoever chooses to be a friend of the world renders himself an enemy of God" (James 4:4). Here, spiritual adultery is defined as worldliness, which is the adoption of the values, priorities, and lusts of a society that rejects the Lordship of Christ.

Modern spiritual adultery often takes the form of subtle idolatry. It may not involve bowing before a carved image, but it involves the "high places" of the heart, where we might worship our careers, our families, our comfort, or our public image. When these things become the foundation of our identity or the source of our deepest security, we have committed spiritual adultery by giving to the creation what belongs only to the Creator.

The gravity of this sin is immense because it treats the precious blood of Christ as a common thing. However, the Gospel offers a stunning remedy. While we are like the unfaithful spouse, God remains the perfectly faithful Husband. He does not merely wait for us to return; He pursues us. Jesus Christ, the true Bridegroom, gave His life to wash His Bride, the Church, and make her holy and blameless as described in Ephesians 5:25-27. His death on the cross satisfied the righteous wrath of God against our spiritual infidelities, and His resurrection provides the power for us to live a new, faithful life.

A practical step for self-examination is to look at your "prayer life" and your "calendar." Consider what occupies your thoughts when they are at rest and what you are most afraid of losing. If you find that something has taken the place of God, do not despair, but go to Him in honest confession. True repentance involves a turning away from the idol and a renewed clinging to the Savior.

The Lord is gracious and full of compassion, ready to receive any who come to Him in sincerity. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. Is there anything in this world currently competing for the place that only Christ should hold in your heart?
How can we rely on the power of God?

To rely on the power of God is to abandon the fragile reed of self-sufficiency and to lean wholly upon the Almighty arm of our Creator and Redeemer. It begins with the humble acknowledgment that we are, in our own strength, utterly helpless to overcome sin or to navigate the trials of this life. The Bible teaches that God's power is not a distant force to be summoned for our personal convenience, but a personal reality experienced by those who walk in subjection to His will.

The Apostle Paul discovered that divine strength is most clearly seen when our own resources are exhausted. The Lord told him, "My grace is sufficient for you, for My power is perfected in weakness," as recorded in 2 Corinthians 12:9. When we stop trying to manufacture our own righteousness or peace and instead cast ourselves upon Him, we find that He is more than capable of sustaining us. This reliance is expressed through a life of constant prayer, where we admit our need, and through the study of Scripture, where we align our minds with His truth. Proverbs 3:5-6; Isaiah 40:29-31.

True reliance on God's power, however, is only possible for those who have first relied on His power to save them. We are all born in rebellion against a holy and just God, separated from Him by our sin. We cannot bridge this gap by our own efforts or moral improvements. But God, in His great love, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to take on human nature.
