Jesus lived a perfectly sinless life and died a substitutionary death on the cross, bearing the wrath of God that we deserved. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, demonstrating His total victory over sin and death. This is the Gospel: that salvation is by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. Romans 1:16; Ephesians 2:8-9.

If you have never trusted in Him, the greatest display of God's power you can experience is the new birth. You can turn to God right now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and eternal life. What holds you back from trusting in His finished work today?

For the believer, relying on God’s power is a daily rhythm of sanctification. You can practice this by setting aside a specific time each morning to meditate on a passage of Scripture, such as Psalm 121, and then committing your specific anxieties to the Lord in prayer. Ask Him for the strength to obey Him in the very tasks that feel most overwhelming, trusting that the same power that raised Jesus from the dead is at work within you. Philippians 4:13; Ephesians 1:19-20.
What does it mean that Jesus fulfilled the law, but did not abolish it?

Jesus fulfilling the law means that He perfectly satisfied its righteous requirements through His sinless life and bore its righteous curse through His sacrificial death. He did not abolish the law by casting it aside as irrelevant or flawed; rather, He brought it to its intended completion and established the only way for sinners to be counted righteous before a whole and holy God.

In the Sermon on the Mount, Jesus declared, "Do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets; I have not come to abolish them but to fulfill them" (Matthew 5:17). To abolish the law would be to suggest that God's standard of holiness had changed or that His previous commands were mistaken. Instead, Jesus honored the law in several profound ways.

First, He fulfilled the law through His active obedience. As the God-man, Jesus lived the only life of perfect conformity to God's will, never stumbling in thought, word, or deed. He loved the Lord with all His heart and His neighbor as Himself (Matthew 22:37; Matthew 22:38; Matthew 22:39; Matthew 22:40). Because He kept the law perfectly, He earned the merit that is now credited, or imputed, to those who believe in Him.

Second, He fulfilled the law through His substitutionary atonement. The law demands death for sin, for "the wages of sin is death" (Romans 6:23). By dying on the cross, Jesus took the penalty we deserved, satisfying the justice of God so that we might be forgiven. As the Apostle Paul writes, "Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us" (Galatians 3:13).

Third, He fulfilled the ceremonial and sacrificial systems of the Old Covenant. These laws were shadows and types that pointed toward a coming Reality (Hebrews 10:1). Jesus is the final High Priest and the perfect Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world (John 1:29). While we no longer offer animal sacrifices, we do not ignore those laws, we see them realized and completed in Christ.

For the believer, this means we are no longer under the law as a way to earn God's favor or escape His judgment. We are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. However, the moral law remains a precious guide for our sanctification, showing us how to live lives that please the Father who has already accepted us in His Son.

If you find yourself weary from trying to "be good enough" for God, look to Jesus. He has already done what you cannot do. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What holds you back from trusting Him?

As an actionable step in your discipleship, I encourage you to read through the Gospel of Matthew chapters 5 through 7 this week. As you read, do not look at these commands as a ladder to reach God, but as a description of the perfect righteousness Jesus achieved for you and the heart He is now forming within you by His Spirit.
What are the Major Prophets and Minor Prophets?

The terms Major Prophets and Minor Prophets refer to the length of the biblical books written by these messengers of God, not to their importance or the authority of their message. All of these books are part of the inspired and inerrant Word of God, given to lead His people to repentance and to point them toward the coming Messiah, Jesus Christ.

The Major Prophets consist of five books that are generally longer in length. They include Isaiah, Jeremiah, Lamentations, Ezekiel, and Daniel. These books provide expansive oracles concerning the holiness of God, the falling away of Israel, and the glorious hope of a future King who would reign in righteousness. For example, the prophet Isaiah provides some of the most profound descriptions of Christ's sacrificial death for our sins in Isaiah 53:1–12.

The Minor Prophets, often referred to in the Hebrew tradition as The Twelve, are shorter books but are equally vital to the canon of Scripture. This group includes Hosea, Joel, Amos, Obadiah, Jonah, Micah, Nahum, Habakkuk, Zephaniah, Haggai, Zechariah, and Malachi. Though their writings are brief, they are packed with theological weight, addressing God’s justice, His requirement for mercy, and the promise of the New Covenant. Micah, for instance, precisely foretold that the Savior would be born in Bethlehem in Micah 5:2.

It is a beautiful truth that God did not leave us in the dark regarding His plans. In former times, He spoke to our fathers by the prophets, but in these last days, He has spoken to us by His Son, Jesus Christ, as noted in Hebrews 1:1–2. Every prophet, whether "major" or "minor," serves as a signpost pointing to the life, death, and resurrection of Jesus, through whom we find forgiveness and peace with God. As Jesus Himself told His disciples, all the things written in the Law of Moses, the Prophets, and the Psalms concerning Him had to be fulfilled, which is recorded in Luke 24:44.

I encourage you to set aside time this week to read through one of the shorter books, such as Zephaniah or Habakkuk. As you read, look for the character of God, His hatred of sin, and His incredible mercy toward those who trust in Him.

The prophets warn us that we cannot save ourselves from the judgment our sins deserve, but they also offer the hope of a Redeemer. This hope is found in Jesus Christ alone, who took the punishment we deserved so that we might be justified by grace through faith. You can turn to God today, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. Have you ever considered how the ancient words of these prophets might be calling you to trust in the Savior today?
Why believe in Jesus?

We believe in Jesus Christ because He is the unique and final revelation of God to mankind, the only Savior who addresses the deepest need of the human heart, which is reconciliation with our Creator. The Bible teaches that Jesus is not merely a moral teacher or a historical figure, but the eternal Son of God who took on human flesh to do for us what we could never do for ourselves.

The primary reason to believe in Jesus is the reality of who He is and what He has accomplished. Scripture declares that Jesus is truly God and truly man, the "radiance of God’s glory and the exact representation of His nature" (Hebrews 1:3). He lived a life of perfect obedience to God’s law, a life that we, in our fallen state, have failed to live. We must face the somber truth that "all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God" (Romans 3:23). Our sin is not a minor lapse in judgment but a rebellion against a holy and just God, carrying the penalty of eternal separation from Him.

Jesus came to earth on a mission of rescue. Through His substitutionary atonement, He took the guilt of our sins upon Himself and suffered the wrath of God that we deserved. On the cross, a divine exchange took place: our sin was placed on Him, and His righteousness is credited to those who believe. This is the heart of the Gospel—that we are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. As the Apostle John wrote, "For God so loved the world that He gave His one and only Son, that everyone who believes in Him shall not perish but have eternal life" (John 3:16).

Furthermore, we believe in Jesus because of the historical fact of His bodily resurrection. This was not a "religious story" or a metaphor; it was a physical event witnessed by hundreds. The resurrection is the Father’s "amen" to the Son’s work on the cross, proving that death has been defeated and that Jesus is indeed the Lord of life. Paul reminds us that "if Christ has not been raised, your faith is futile; you are still in your sins" (1 Corinthians 15:17). But because He lives, we have a living hope.

Believing in Jesus is not about joining a religion or following a list of rules; it is about being restored to a relationship with the Living God. He offers peace that surpasses understanding, the forgiveness of all your transgressions, and the promise of a renewed life guided by the Holy Spirit. 

The weight of eternity rests upon how you respond to Him. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

To begin this journey, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John in the Bible to see the character of Jesus for yourself. Seek out a local, Bible-believing church where the Word of God is preached faithfully, and speak to the elders there about what it means to follow Christ. Commit yourself to daily prayer, speaking honestly to God about your doubts, your needs, and your desire to know the truth.
What is a Bible Baptist Church, and what do Bible Baptists believe?

A Bible Baptist Church is a local, autonomous body of believers that identifies the Holy Scriptures as the sole and sufficient authority for all matters of faith and practice. These churches are characterized by a firm commitment to the historic Baptist distinctives, primarily the conviction that the Bible is the inerrant Word of God and that baptism is reserved strictly for those who have made a personal profession of faith in Jesus Christ.

While "Bible Baptist" can refer to a specific fellowship of churches, it more broadly describes congregations that hold to several foundational pillars:

**The Authority of Scripture**
Bible Baptists believe in the verbal, plenary inspiration of the Bible, meaning every word of the original manuscripts was breathed out by God. They hold to "Sola Scriptura," believing that the Bible is the only infallible rule for life and the only source of divine revelation for the church today (2 Timothy 3:16; 2 Peter 1:20-21).

**The Nature of Salvation**
The core of their teaching is the Gospel of Jesus Christ. They believe that salvation is a work of God’s grace alone, received through faith alone in Christ alone. This involves "justification," a legal act where God declares a sinner righteous because the merit of Christ’s perfect life and his substitutionary death has been credited to the believer (Ephesians 2:8-9; Romans 5:1).

**Believer’s Baptism**
Following the pattern found in the New Testament, these churches practice "credo-baptism," which is the immersion of a person in water after they have repented of sin and trusted in Christ. They reject infant baptism, viewing baptism not as a means of saving grace, but as a public testimony of a believer’s identification with the death, burial, and resurrection of Jesus (Acts 2:41; Romans 6:3-4).

**The Autonomy of the Local Church**
Each Bible Baptist Church is self-governing and self-supporting. While they may associate with other like-minded churches for missions or fellowship, they recognize no higher human authority than the local congregation and its leadership of elders or pastors and deacons. They believe Jesus Christ is the only Head of the Church (Colossians 1:18).

**Individual Soul Liberty and Separation of Church and State**
Bible Baptists historically emphasize that every individual is responsible to God alone in matters of faith and conscience. They believe that the state should not interfere in the religious life of the church, nor should the church seek to use the power of the state to enforce its doctrines.

The historical reality of the Christian faith is that God is holy and just, while humanity is fallen and rebellious. Because of our sin, we deserve the righteous judgment of God. However, in His mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to take on human nature through a miraculous virgin birth. He lived the life we could not live and died the death we deserved to die. His bodily resurrection from the dead is an objective historical fact that confirms His victory over sin and the grave (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

You can turn to God right now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What prevents you from placing your full weight upon the finished work of Christ today?

If you are looking for a practical next step, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John to see the person of Jesus clearly. Additionally, seeking out a local church that faithfully preaches the Bible will provide you with a community of believers who can walk alongside you in your spiritual journey.
What is sexual ethics?

Sexual ethics is the framework of moral principles and divine commands that govern human sexuality, which for the Christian is rooted entirely in the character and creative design of God as revealed in the Holy Scriptures. It is not a collection of societal traditions or evolving human opinions, but the application of God’s unchanging truth to our bodies and desires.

At its foundation, biblical sexual ethics begins with the created order. God created humanity as male and female and established the covenant of marriage as the exclusive setting for sexual intimacy. Genesis 1:27; Genesis 2:24. This "one flesh" union between one man and one woman is designed for the procreation of the human race, the deepening of companionship, and the protection of the family unit.

Because God is the author of sexuality, He alone possesses the authority to define its proper use and its many perversions. The Bible teaches that any sexual activity outside the bond of marriage—including fornication, adultery, and homosexual behavior—is sin and a violation of God’s holy standard. 1 Corinthians 6:18-20; Hebrews 13:4. These boundaries are given not to restrict our joy, but to preserve our holiness and to reflect the spiritual reality of Christ’s faithful, sacrificial love for His Church. Ephesians 5:31-32.

When we consider sexual ethics, we must recognize that our hearts are naturally prone to wander from God’s design. We all, in various ways, have failed to honor God with our bodies and thoughts. Matthew 5:27-28. However, the Gospel of Jesus Christ provides a path of restoration. Through the sacrificial death and bodily resurrection of Jesus, there is full forgiveness for every sexual sin for those who repent and believe.

If you find yourself burdened by past failures or current struggles in this area, do not hide in shame. Turn to Christ, who died to wash us clean and rose to give us power over sin. You might begin by reading through 1 Corinthians 6:9-11 and asking God to align your heart with His Word. I encourage you to seek the fellowship of a faithful local church where you can find accountability and grow in the grace and knowledge of our Lord.

The message of the Bible is clear: you can be made new. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and a life of holy purpose. What prevents you from trusting Him with your life and your desires today?
What is spiritual sickness? What does it mean to be spiritually sick?

Spiritual sickness is a condition of the soul where sin, neglect of God, or rebellion has dampened a person's spiritual vitality and fellowship with their Creator. In its most fundamental sense, every human being is born spiritually terminal, dead in trespasses and sins, and in desperate need of the life giving power of the Holy Spirit (Ephesians 2:1; Ephesians 2:5). For the believer, spiritual sickness often manifests as a period of backsliding, where the heart becomes cold, the conscience becomes seared, or the joy of salvation is eclipsed by the pursuit of worldly idols.

To be spiritually sick means that your soul's "appetite" for the things of God has been suppressed. Just as a physical illness robs a person of their desire for food, spiritual sickness causes a person to lose interest in the Word of God, prayer, and the fellowship of the saints. The prophet Isaiah described the state of a rebellious people by saying, "The whole head is sick, and the whole heart faint" (Isaiah 1:5). This sickness is not merely a lack of "wellness" but is a profound misalignment with the holiness of God.

The symptoms of this condition often include:
1. A lack of desire for Scripture and prayer.
2. A persistent struggle with known sin without a corresponding sense of grief or repentance.
3. A heart that is easily provoked to anger, bitterness, or envy.
4. A reliance on one’s own strength and wisdom rather than the grace of God.

King David experienced the heavy weight of spiritual sickness when he attempted to hide his sin. He wrote, "When I kept silent, my bones wasted away through my groaning all day long. For day and night Your hand was heavy upon me; my strength was drained as in the summer heat" (Psalm 32:3; Psalm 32:4). Spiritual sickness often carries a heavy psychological and even physical toll, as the soul was never designed to carry the burden of unconfessed sin or the distance from God’s presence.

The only cure for spiritual sickness is the Lord Jesus Christ, whom the Bible presents as the Great Physician. He declared, "It is not the healthy who need a doctor, but the sick. I have not come to call the righteous, but sinners to repentance" (Luke 5:31; Luke 5:32). Through His substitutionary death on the cross, Jesus took the penalty for our sins, and through His bodily resurrection, He offers us new and vibrant life. He does not merely patch up the old self but grants a new heart and a new spirit (Ezekiel 36:26).

If you feel the weight of spiritual sickness today, do not despair. The first step toward healing is an honest diagnosis before God. You might take a moment to sit quietly with Psalm 51, reading it as your own cry to God, and asking the Holy Spirit to reveal any hidden idols or unconfessed sins that have hindered your walk with Him. Seek out a faithful local church where the Word of God is preached and where you can find the accountability and encouragement of other believers (Hebrews 10:24; Hebrews 10:25; James 5:16).

If you have never trusted in Christ, recognize that the "sickness" you feel is the separation from God caused by sin. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the full forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What is holding you back from trusting Him today?
What is fellowship with God?

Fellowship with God is the intimate, shared life and spiritual communion between a believer and their Creator, established through the person and work of Jesus Christ. It is far more than a mere acquaintance or a religious duty; it is a vital union where the soul rests in God's love, hears His voice through His Word, and responds in prayer and obedience. The Apostle John describes this reality by saying, "and indeed our fellowship is with the Father and with His Son, Jesus Christ" (1 John 1:3).

To understand this fellowship, we must first recognize the holiness of God. God is light, and in Him there is no darkness at all (1 John 1:5). Because of our sin, we were naturally alienated from God, walking in darkness and unable to approach His presence. True fellowship requires a shared nature and a common ground, which we could never provide on our own.

This is the glory of the Gospel: God initiated the way for us to be brought near. Fellowship is built upon the foundation of reconciliation. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, took on human nature, lived a sinless life, and offered Himself as a substitutionary sacrifice on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, victorious over death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). When a person turns from their sin and trusts in Christ alone, they are justified by grace through faith. At that moment, the barrier of sin is removed, and the believer is adopted into God’s family (Galatians 4:4-7).

Fellowship is the ongoing experience of this relationship. It involves:

1. Walking in the Light: As we grow in grace, we seek to live in agreement with God's character. When we stumble, fellowship is maintained through the honest confession of sin, trusting in the finished work of Christ our Advocate (1 John 1:9; 1 John 2:1).
2. The Indwelling of the Holy Spirit: God does not leave us to seek Him from a distance. He dwells within the believer, providing the "Spirit of adoption" by which we cry out, "Abba, Father" (Romans 8:15-16).
3. The Means of Grace: We enjoy fellowship by listening to God through the study of the Bible and speaking to Him in prayer. This is a rhythmic, daily dependence on His strength (John 15:4-5).
4. Corporate Union: Fellowship with God is inextricably linked to fellowship with His people. We experience the life of Christ as we gather with the local church to worship and serve one another (Hebrews 10:24-25).

True fellowship is not a feeling that fluctuates with our emotions, but a legal and spiritual reality grounded in the unchanging promise of God. It is the beginning of eternal life, which Jesus defined as knowing the only true God and Jesus Christ whom He sent (John 17:3).

If you find yourself distant from God or weighed down by the guilt of sin, know that the door to this fellowship is open through Christ. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. In Him, you will find not a judge to fear, but a Father to love. What is it that prevents you from resting fully in His grace today?

As a practical step in nurturing this fellowship, I encourage you to set aside a specific time each morning to read a portion of Scripture—perhaps starting in the Gospel of John—and to speak honestly with God about your needs, your failures, and your gratitude. Fellowship thrives where there is consistent, humble communication.
What role does Iran play in the end times?

The Bible’s primary mention of the region we now call Iran is found under its ancient name, Persia. For centuries, this name identified the people and the land until it was changed in 1935. In the context of the end times, or eschatology, many students of Scripture point to the prophecies of Ezekiel as the most significant reference to Iran’s future role.

In the book of Ezekiel, specifically within a section describing a future conflict often called the "War of Gog and Magog," Persia is listed as a key ally in a vast coalition of nations that descends upon the restored land of Israel Ezekiel 38:5. According to this prophecy, this alliance is led by a figure described as "Gog, of the land of Magog," and includes other nations from the north and south Ezekiel 38:1–6. The text states that this invasion occurs in the "latter years" to seize spoil, but God intervenes with supernatural judgment to demonstrate His holy name and sovereignty to the nations Ezekiel 38:16; Ezekiel 38:23; Ezekiel 39:1–8.

Because the interpretation of prophecy is a tertiary matter—meaning it is an area where faithful, Bible-believing Christians may disagree while remaining in fellowship—there are several ways believers approach these passages:

*   **The Futurist View:** Many Christians believe these events describe a literal, geopolitical conflict that will take place shortly before or during the return of Jesus Christ. In this view, Iran’s current political stance toward Israel is seen as a preparation for the fulfillment of Ezekiel’s prophecy.
*   **The Symbolic or Idealist View:** Other Christians see these descriptions as symbolic of the perpetual spiritual conflict between the kingdoms of this world and the Kingdom of God. They emphasize that while the nations may rage against God’s people, the ultimate focus is on the certain victory of Christ over all earthly powers at the end of the age.

Regardless of the specific timing or nature of these events, the Scriptures make it clear that God is the sovereign ruler over the history of nations. He raises up leaders and brings them down according to His divine purposes Daniel 2:21. The central truth of all prophecy is not to provide a map for political speculation, but to testify to the glory and authority of Jesus Christ Revelation 19:10.

While world events may seem uncertain or even frightening, the child of God is called to live with hope rather than fear. The reality that nations will one day be judged reminds us of our own standing before a holy God. We must all recognize that we have sinned and fallen short of His glory, but God, in His great mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death. 

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and eternal life.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to spend time reading the book of Ezekiel alongside a reliable commentary, and more importantly, to anchor your heart in the Gospel of John to see the character of the Savior who holds the future in His hands. Consider joining a local, Bible-teaching church where you can study these truths in the safety of a faithful community. What aspect of these future events causes you the most concern or curiosity?
What does Jesus mean when He says, “Verily, verily” or “Truly, truly,” in the gospels?

When Jesus says "Truly, truly" (or "Verily, verily" in older translations), He is using the Greek word *Amen*. In the ancient world, people typically said "Amen" at the end of a prayer or a statement to show agreement or to say, "Let it be so." Jesus, however, did something entirely unique and authoritative. He moved the "Amen" to the beginning of His statements. By doing this, He was declaring that His words did not need anyone else’s confirmation to be true. He was speaking with His own divine authority as the Son of God.

The doubling of the word—"Truly, truly"—is a way of emphasizing the absolute certainty and supreme importance of what follows. It is a divine "pay attention" signal. When Jesus uses this phrase, He is telling us that the truth He is about to reveal is foundational, non-negotiable, and essential for our souls to grasp. It is the language of a King who is not merely giving an opinion, but is declaring the laws and realities of His kingdom.

You see this most clearly in the Gospel of John, where Jesus uses this phrase to introduce the most vital truths about salvation. For example, He tells Nicodemus, "Truly, truly, I tell you, no one can see the kingdom of God unless he is born again" (John 3:3). He also uses it to promise eternal life: "Truly, truly, I tell you, whoever hears My word and believes Him who sent Me has eternal life and will not come under judgment, but has crossed over from death to life" (John 5:24).

These statements bring us face to face with the weight of the Gospel. The Bible teaches that God is perfectly holy and that our sin has separated us from Him. We cannot bridge that gap by our own works or good intentions. However, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, came to earth, lived a sinless life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now the exalted Lord who offers salvation as a free gift of grace.

When Jesus says "Truly, truly," He is inviting you to rest your entire life on the reliability of His character. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. Because He is "the way and the truth and the life" (John 14:6), His promises are the only firm ground in a world of shifting shadows.

As you read the Gospels, I encourage you to pause whenever you see these words. Ask yourself, "Am I listening to this truth with a heart ready to obey?" What holds you back from fully trusting Him and His word today?
What should we learn from the tribe of Asher?

The tribe of Asher provides a profound study of God’s abundant provision, the subtle dangers of worldly comfort, and the enduring faithfulness of a humble remnant. As the eighth son of Jacob, Asher’s very name means "happy" or "blessed," and his tribal history reflects a journey from material prosperity to spiritual realization in the person of Jesus Christ.

One of the primary lessons we learn from Asher is that God is a lavish provider who delights in the flourishing of His people. In the prophetic blessings of the patriarchs, Asher was promised a life of exceptional plenty. Jacob declared that Asher’s food would be "rich" and he would provide "royal delicacies" (Genesis 49:20). Later, Moses echoed this by describing Asher as "most blessed of sons" and one who would "dip his foot in oil" (Deuteronomy 33:24). This imagery points to the fertile lands they inherited, rich in olive groves. It serves as a reminder of God’s common grace and His sovereignty in distributing secondary blessings. We are reminded that every good gift comes from the Father, and we are called to receive His provision with gratitude rather than entitlement.

However, the history of Asher also carries a solemn warning regarding the danger of ease and spiritual complacency. When the Israelites entered the Promised Land, the tribe of Asher failed to fully drive out the Canaanites from their territory (Judges 1:31-32). Unlike some other tribes who struggled due to military weakness, Asher simply "dwelt among the Canaanites." Their material comfort seems to have led to a compromise with the world. Later, when Deborah and Barak called the tribes to war against Sisera, Asher "remained on the coast" and "stayed by his landings" (Judges 5:17). Here we see that prosperity can sometimes dull our sense of spiritual urgency and lead us to prefer our own security over the cause of God’s kingdom. We must be vigilant in our own sanctification, ensuring that the comforts God gives us do not become idols that keep us from total obedience.

In the midst of this mixed history, we see the beauty of God’s grace toward those who humble themselves. Centuries later, when King Hezekiah sent a call throughout Israel to return to the Lord and keep the Passover, many mocked the messengers. Yet, Scripture records that some men from Asher "humbled themselves and came to Jerusalem" (2 Chronicles 30:11). This teaches us that no matter our tribal or family history of compromise, the door to repentance is always open. God honors the broken and contrite heart that seeks to worship Him in truth.

Finally, the tribe of Asher reaches its spiritual crescendo in the New Testament through the person of Anna. After the birth of Jesus, as He was brought to the temple, he was met by Anna, a prophetess of the tribe of Asher (Luke 2:36). Though her tribe had often been overlooked in the geopolitical shifts of Israel, God had not forgotten them. Anna’s life of prayer and fasting culminated in her seeing the Messiah. She began to "give thanks to God and speak of the Child to all who were looking for the redemption of Jerusalem" (Luke 2:38). This demonstrates that God preserves a remnant in every generation and that the true "happiness" of Asher is found only in recognizing and proclaiming Jesus Christ.

This same Jesus, whom Anna recognized, is the only hope for all people today. He lived a perfect life of obedience, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily on the third day, conquering death and the grave. Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, apart from any works or merit of our own. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you.

As a practical step in your walk with God, consider meditating on the promise given to Asher in Deuteronomy 33:25: "As your days, so shall your strength be." Take time today to specifically ask the Lord for the strength required for your current burdens, trusting that His grace is sufficient for each moment. What is one area of comfort in your life that might be hindering your obedience to Him?
Why does the Bible speak against braided hair?

The Bible’s instructions regarding braided hair, found primarily in the letters of the apostles Paul and Peter, are not a legalistic prohibition against a specific hairstyle. Instead, they serve as a call for believers to prioritize the humility of the heart over the ostentatious display of wealth and status.

In the first century Greco-Roman world, "elaborate hairstyles" often involved complex braids interwoven with gold, silver, pearls, and expensive jewels. This was a common way for wealthy women to flaunt their social standing and riches. When Paul wrote to Timothy, he said, "I also want the women to dress modestly, with decency and propriety, adorning themselves, not with elaborate hairstyles or gold or pearls or expensive clothes, but with good deeds, as is proper for women who profess to worship God" (1 Timothy 2:9-10). The concern was that such displays of luxury within the church would cause distractions, breed envy among the poor, and shift the focus away from the worship of the Lord.

Similarly, the apostle Peter encouraged women to find their identity in Christ rather than in outward fashion. He wrote, "Your beauty should not come from outward adornment, such as elaborate hairstyles and the wearing of gold jewelry or fine clothes. Instead, it should be that of your inner self, the unfading beauty of a gentle and quiet spirit, which is of great worth in God’s sight" (1 Peter 3:3-4). This is a matter of the heart’s orientation. The "elaborate hairstyles" mentioned here refer to the same kind of prideful, costly ornamentation that characterized the pagan culture of the day.

The biblical principle at work is modesty, which in the Greek (aidos) refers to a sense of shamefacedness or a heart that shrinks from overstepping the bounds of propriety. It is a fruit of the Spirit’s work in sanctification, leading a believer to desire that others see Christ in them rather than being dazzled by their personal wealth or fashion.

As you consider this, reflect on the motive behind your outward appearance. While there is nothing inherently sinful about braiding hair, the Scriptures invite us to ask if our choices in dress and grooming serve to glorify God or to draw attention to ourselves. A helpful next step is to pray and ask the Holy Spirit to cultivate in you a heart that treasures the "unfading beauty" of Christlikeness above any worldly fashion.

Ultimately, our worth is not determined by how we adorn our bodies, but by how we have been adorned by God. Through the Gospel, we learn that we were once stripped of all righteousness because of our sin, yet God in His great mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ. By His perfect life, His substitutionary death on the cross, and His bodily resurrection, He provides the "garment of salvation" to all who repent and trust in Him alone. You can turn to God even now, trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you, to receive the forgiveness and new life that only He can give. What is it that might be keeping you from fully resting in the identity He provides?
What is the difference between a covenant and a contract?

A contract is a legal agreement between parties involving an exchange of goods or services, whereas a covenant is a solemn, life-binding commitment that creates a relational bond between persons. In a contract, the relationship is secondary to the transaction, but in a covenant, the transaction serves the relationship.

Contracts are typically conditional and protective, often containing clauses that allow for termination if one party fails to fulfill their duty. In contrast, a biblical covenant is a "cutting" of an agreement, often involving a sacrifice, that signifies a permanent change in status and identity. For example, in a marriage covenant, two become one flesh, and in the New Covenant, God makes us His own children. This is the difference between saying "I will give you this if you do that" and "I give myself to you."

The Bible presents God’s relationship with His people through the lens of covenantal faithfulness. While human contracts depend on mutual performance, God’s Covenant of Grace is grounded in His own unchanging character and sovereign decree. Because we broke the covenant of works through sin in Adam, our federal head, we are all naturally born under the shadow of death and judgment. However, God, in His infinite mercy, sent Jesus Christ as the Mediator of a better covenant. Jesus fulfilled every requirement of the law and bore the penalty for our covenant-breaking through His penal substitutionary death on the cross.

Through Christ’s sacrifice and bodily resurrection, He has secured an eternal inheritance for all who trust in Him. This is the heart of the Gospel: that God's justice is satisfied and His love is poured out through the blood of the New Covenant. Matthew 26:28; Hebrews 9:15; Jeremiah 31:33.

Salvation is not a contract you negotiate with God based on your own merits or religious effort. It is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to be brought into this everlasting covenant relationship. What holds you back from trusting in Him today?

As a practical step in your spiritual walk, I encourage you to read through the book of Hebrews, specifically chapters 8 through 10. As you read, pray that God would show you the security of being held by His faithful hand rather than your own strength. Hebrews 8:10–12; Hebrews 13:20–21.
If Jesus was God, why did He say "No one is good but God alone"?

When Jesus asked the rich young ruler, "Why do you call Me good? No one is good except God alone," He was not denying His own divinity, but rather challenging the man’s superficial understanding of goodness and of Jesus’ own identity. By pointing to the absolute holiness of God, Jesus was inviting the man to consider the logical conclusion of his own words: if Jesus is truly good, then Jesus is truly God.

In this encounter, recorded in Mark 10:17–18 and Luke 18:18–19, the young man approached Jesus with a title of respect, "Good Teacher," yet he likely viewed Jesus merely as a human rabbi. Jesus used this as a teaching moment to reveal that "goodness" is not a relative human standard, but an absolute divine attribute. Throughout the Gospels, Jesus repeatedly affirmed His deity, stating elsewhere, "I and the Father are one" in John 10:30, and accepting worship that belongs to God alone, as seen in John 20:28. He is the express image of the Father’s person, the Word made flesh, possessing the fullness of the divine nature in what theologians call the hypostatic union, being truly God and truly man.

The heart of the matter is that we often use the word "good" lightly, but in the presence of the Holy One, our own "righteous acts are like filthy rags," as described in Isaiah 64:6. Jesus was showing the young man that if he wanted to inherit eternal life through his own goodness or by following a "good teacher," he would fail, because only God is perfectly good. This realization is meant to drive us to the mercy of Christ, who is the only truly Good Shepherd who lays down His life for the sheep, as stated in John 10:11.

Because Jesus is the sinless, good God in human flesh, He was able to offer Himself as a perfect sacrifice for our sins. The Bible teaches that we have all fallen short of God’s glory and are separated from Him by our transgressions. However, out of His immense love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die an atoning death on the cross, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. Through His resurrection, He triumphed over death and now offers salvation as a free gift. We are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own attempts at being "good."

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. He is not merely a "good teacher" to be admired, but the Lord of life to be worshiped. What prevents you from placing your full trust in His goodness today?

To grow in your understanding of who Jesus is, I encourage you to read through the Gospel of John, specifically focusing on the "I Am" statements of Jesus. It may also be helpful to speak with a pastor at a local, Bible-believing church who can walk through these scriptures with you.
What does it mean that God will reveal the hidden things (Daniel 2:22 and 28)?

To say that God reveals the hidden things means that He is the sovereign Lord of all knowledge, possessing an absolute and exhaustive understanding of everything that remains a mystery to mankind. In the context of Daniel 2, this truth is demonstrated by God revealing a specific prophetic dream to Daniel that the world’s wisest counselors could not perceive. This reveals three profound realities about the character of God and His relationship with His creation.

First, it establishes God’s omniscience. Nothing is hidden from His sight, whether it is the vast reaches of the future or the deepest recesses of the human heart. Because light dwells with Him, as stated in Daniel 2:22, the darkness of human ignorance or the secrecy of the grave cannot obscure anything from His gaze. The Bible affirms this in Psalm 139:12, saying, "even the darkness is not dark to You; the night shines like the day, for darkness is as light to You."

Second, it demonstrates God’s sovereignty over human history. When God revealed to King Nebuchadnezzar "what will happen in the latter days" in Daniel 2:28, He was not merely predicting the future, but declaring what He had already ordained. He reveals the secrets of rising and falling empires because He is the one who removes kings and sets up kings, as recorded in Daniel 2:21. History is not a series of accidents, but a purposeful unfolding of God's divine decree.

Third, this truth carries a weight of moral accountability. The revealing of hidden things means that every secret act, every unspoken thought, and every hidden motive will eventually be brought into the light of His perfect judgment. Scripture warns us in 1 Corinthians 4:5 that the Lord "will bring to light what is hidden in darkness and will expose the motives of the heart." This is a sobering reality for those who think their sins are concealed, for "God will bring every deed into judgment, including every hidden thing, whether it is good or evil," as stated in Ecclesiastes 12:14.

Ultimately, the greatest "hidden thing" or mystery that God has revealed is the Gospel of Jesus Christ. For ages, the full plan of how God would redeem a sinful people was shadowed in types and ceremonies, but it has now been made clear through the incarnation, death, and resurrection of His Son. Colossians 1:26 describes this as "the mystery that has been kept hidden for ages and generations, but is now disclosed to the Lord’s people."

In light of these truths, there is no place to hide from God, but there is a place to hide in Him. While our hidden sins merit His righteous judgment, the Gospel offers us a refuge. Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, lived the perfect life we could not live and died on the cross as a substitute for sinners, bearing the full weight of God’s justice. He was buried and rose again bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for your forgiveness and peace. What holds you back from trusting Him today? 

As a practical step, I encourage you to read through the Gospel of John, paying close attention to how Jesus brings light into a dark world. Pray and ask the Lord to reveal the truth of His Word to your own heart, trusting that He is the one who gives wisdom to the wise and knowledge to the discerning, as promised in Daniel 2:21.
What items should be included in a church constitution?

The local church is called to be the pillar and foundation of the truth (1 Timothy 3:15), and a constitution serves as a faithful map to ensure that all things are done decently and in order (1 Corinthians 14:40). While the Scriptures are our sole and supreme authority, a constitution summarizes how a specific congregation intends to apply biblical principles to its corporate life. A well-ordered constitution protects the purity of the Gospel, the peace of the flock, and the honor of the Lord Jesus Christ.

The following essential elements are typically included in a biblically grounded church constitution:

**1. Name and Purpose**
This section identifies the church and explicitly states its reason for existence. The primary purpose of any true church is the glory of God through the worship of His name, the edification of the saints, and the proclamation of the Gospel to the world (Matthew 28:18-20; Ephesians 4:11-16).

**2. Statement of Faith**
This is the doctrinal heart of the document. It should clearly define the church's stance on core doctrines such as the Trinity, the inerrancy of Scripture, the deity of Christ, and justification by grace alone through faith alone (Jude 1:3). For those in the Reformed tradition, this often aligns with historic standards like the 1689 London Baptist Confession of Faith.

**3. Church Covenant**
A covenant is a solemn promise made before God and one another to live according to the commands of Christ. It outlines the practical responsibilities of members, such as walking in love, attending services, and supporting the ministry (Hebrews 10:24-25).

**4. Membership**
Scripture speaks of believers being added to the number of the church (Acts 2:41; Acts 2:47). This section defines the qualifications for membership, such as a credible profession of faith and believer's baptism, as well as the process for joining or leaving the local body.

**5. Church Officers**
The Bible recognizes two primary offices: elders (also called pastors or overseers) and deacons (1 Timothy 3:1-13; Titus 1:5-9). The constitution should detail the qualifications, selection process, and specific duties of these men, emphasizing that elders lead through the ministry of the Word and prayer, while deacons serve the physical needs of the congregation.

**6. The Ordinances**
A constitution should specify how the church administers the two ordinances established by Christ: Baptism and the Lord’s Supper (Matthew 28:19; 1 Corinthians 11:23-26). It should clarify who may administer them and who is eligible to receive them.

**7. Church Discipline**
In love, the church must seek to restore those who wander into persistent sin or false doctrine. Following the pattern of Matthew 18:15-17 and Galatians 6:1, this section outlines the steps for formative and corrective discipline, always with the goal of repentance and the preservation of the church's holiness.

**8. Governance and Meetings**
This clarifies how decisions are made. Many faithful churches practice a form of elder-led congregationalism, where the elders provide spiritual leadership and the congregation votes on significant matters such as the annual budget, the calling of pastors, or the removal of members.

**9. Finances and Property**
To ensure integrity, the constitution should address the handling of tithes and offerings, stating that all resources belong to the Lord and must be managed with transparency and stewardship (2 Corinthians 8:20-21).

**10. Amendments and Dissolution**
A process for changing the constitution should be clearly defined to prevent hasty or unbiblical alterations. Additionally, a dissolution clause explains how remaining assets would be distributed to other ministries of like faith and practice if the church were ever to close.

Beloved, remember that while documents provide structure, the Holy Spirit provides life. If you are involved in drafting or reviewing a constitution, a wise next step is to compare each article against the clear teaching of the Word of God and to seek the counsel of mature, godly elders.

As you consider the order of your local church, have you personally settled the most important question of all? We all stand in need of the grace found only in Jesus Christ. You can turn to God even now, repenting of sin and trusting in the Lord Jesus, who died for our sins and rose again to give us eternal life (Romans 10:9-10). What prevents you from trusting Him today?
What is topical preaching? Should a pastor preach topically?

Topical preaching is a method of instruction where the sermon is organized around a specific subject, such as "marriage," "justification," or "suffering," rather than following the consecutive verses of a single book of the Bible. In this approach, the preacher gathers various passages from across the Old and New Testaments to provide a comprehensive biblical perspective on that chosen theme.

Whether a pastor should preach topically is a matter of pastoral wisdom and homiletic priority. Within the historic Christian tradition, particularly among those who hold to the high authority of Scripture, there are two primary perspectives on how this method should be used.

The first perspective emphasizes the priority of expository preaching, which is the practice of preaching through books of the Bible verse-by-verse. This approach is often seen as the "staple diet" of the local church because it ensures that the pastor proclaims the "whole counsel of God" as mentioned in Acts 20:27. By following the text as God inspired it, the preacher is prevented from dwelling only on his favorite "hobby horses" or avoiding difficult doctrines. It compels both the speaker and the hearer to confront the text on God's terms, ensuring that the meaning of the passage remains the message of the sermon.

The second perspective acknowledges that topical preaching is a valuable and necessary tool for specific pastoral needs. There are times when a congregation requires focused instruction on a particular doctrine or a pressing ethical issue. For example, if a church is struggling with a lack of prayer, a series specifically on the nature and practice of prayer can be deeply edifying. Furthermore, some of the greatest sermons in history, including many by Charles Spurgeon, were thematic in nature, weaving together the "analogy of faith"—the principle that Scripture interprets Scripture—to highlight the glories of Christ from many angles.

However, topical preaching carries specific risks that a pastor must guard against with fear and trembling. The greatest danger is "eisegesis," which is the act of reading one's own ideas into the Bible rather than "exegesis," which is drawing the meaning out of the text. When a preacher goes "shopping" for verses to support a pre-conceived point, he risks taking those verses out of their original context, thereby misrepresenting the voice of God. 

A faithful shepherd should ensure that the primary weight of his ministry is grounded in the steady, sequential opening of God's Word. As Paul charged Timothy, "Preach the word; be prepared in season and out of season; reprove, rebuke, and encourage, with every form of patience and instruction" in 2 Timothy 4:2. Whether the sermon is topical or expository, it must remain a slave to the biblical text.

Ultimately, every topic in Scripture, whether it be about money, family, or trials, must eventually lead us to the person and work of Jesus Christ. The Bible is not a manual for self-improvement but a testimony of God’s redemptive work. Every subject finds its fulfillment in the Gospel: that God is holy, we are sinners deserving of judgment, and yet in His great love, He sent His Son to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death for our sins. He rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death, as stated in 1 Corinthians 15:3-4.

If you are evaluating the preaching you hear, a practical next step is to follow along in your own Bible during the sermon. Ask yourself: Is the preacher's point actually found in the verses he is reading, or is he using the verses merely as "props" for his own opinions? Pray that God would give you a hunger for the pure milk of the Word, so that you may grow in your salvation, as urged in 1 Peter 2:2.

You can turn to God even now, repenting of your sins and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and eternal life. Is there anything in your heart today that keeps you from fully trusting in His finished work?
What are tribulation saints?

Tribulation saints are individuals who come to saving faith in Jesus Christ during the period of intense global judgment and suffering known as the Great Tribulation. These believers are prominently described in the book of Revelation as a vast multitude from every nation, tribe, people, and language who stand before the throne of God, having remained faithful to Christ despite severe persecution and even death.

The primary biblical description of these saints is found in Revelation 7:14, where they are identified as those who "have come out of the great tribulation; they have washed their robes and made them white in the blood of the Lamb." This imagery emphasizes the core doctrine of justification by faith; their standing before God is not based on their own endurance or merit, but solely on the finished work of Christ’s substitutionary atonement.

Because the timing and nature of the end times are considered tertiary doctrines—areas where faithful, Bible-believing Christians may hold differing interpretations—the identity of these saints is understood in a few different ways:

*   **The Post-Rapture View:** Many Christians, particularly those holding a dispensational premillennial perspective, believe that the Church will be caught up to meet Christ before the Tribulation begins. In this view, tribulation saints are those who are reached by the Gospel after the Church has departed, often through the witness of the two witnesses or the 144,000 Jewish believers mentioned in Revelation 11:3 and Revelation 7:4.
*   **The Enduring Church View:** Other Christians, including those with historic premillennial or amillennial views, believe that the "tribulation saints" represent the Church itself, which must pass through the final period of testing before the return of Christ. From this perspective, these saints are the faithful remnant of God’s people who are preserved through the fire of trial, as seen in Revelation 13:10 and Revelation 14:12.

Regardless of the specific timing one holds, the existence of these saints serves as a powerful testament to the sovereign grace of God. Even in a season of divine wrath and human rebellion, the Holy Spirit continues to draw hearts to repentance. Revelation 20:4 notes that many of these believers are martyred for "the testimony of Jesus and for the word of God," refusing to worship the beast or receive his mark.

The reality of the tribulation saints should move us to deep gratitude for the patience of our Lord, who is not wishing for any to perish but for all to come to repentance as noted in 2 Peter 3:9. It also serves as a solemn reminder of the exclusivity of Christ; there is no other name under heaven by which we must be saved as stated in Acts 4:12.

If you find yourself wondering about the future or feeling the weight of the world's brokenness, remember that the same Jesus who sustains the saints through the tribulation is available to you today. You do not need to wait for a time of global catastrophe to find peace with God. You can turn to Him now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ—who died for your sins and rose again—for forgiveness and eternal life.

As a practical step, I encourage you to read through the book of Revelation, chapters 7 and 14, and spend time in prayer, asking the Lord to strengthen your own faith and to give you a heart for those who do not yet know Him. What aspects of God's sovereignty over the future give you the most comfort right now?
Who are the Black Hebrews / Black Israelites?

The Black Hebrew Israelites, or Black Hebrews, are a diverse group of movements whose members believe that people of color, particularly African Americans, are the true physical descendants of the ancient Israelites mentioned in the Bible. While there is a broad spectrum within this movement, ranging from those who hold to traditional Christian beliefs to those who embrace radical, non-Christian ideologies, the central tenet remains a focus on biological lineage as a primary marker of identity and spiritual standing.

In the light of the Truth, we must address these claims with the tenderness of a shepherd but the firmness of the Word of God. The Holy Scriptures warn us not to devote ourselves to "endless genealogies, which promote speculations rather than the stewardship from God that is by faith" (1 Timothy 1:4). While human history is complex and the dignity of all people is rooted in being created in the image of God, the Bible teaches that our standing before the Father is never determined by our physical ancestry or the color of our skin.

Many branches of the Black Israelite movement fall into grave error by emphasizing the keeping of the Mosaic Law as a requirement for salvation or by suggesting that only certain ethnic groups are the objects of God's redeeming love. We must be clear: any teaching that adds to the finished work of Jesus Christ or limits the scope of the Gospel to a single race is a departure from the faith delivered once for all to the saints. The Apostle Paul, himself a "Hebrew of Hebrews," wrote by the inspiration of the Holy Spirit that "no one is a Jew who is merely one outwardly, nor is circumcision outward and physical. But a Jew is one inwardly, and circumcision is a matter of the heart, by the Spirit, not by the letter" (Romans 2:28–29).

Furthermore, the New Covenant established by the blood of Jesus Christ has broken down every wall of ethnic hostility. In Christ, "there is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither slave nor free, there is no male and female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus" (Galatians 3:28). To seek a primary identity in one's DNA rather than in the Spirit is to return to the "weak and worthless elementary principles of the world" (Galatians 4:9).

The heart of the matter is not who your earthly ancestors were, but whether you have been born again into the family of God. Our Lord Jesus told Nicodemus, a ruler of the physical Israelites, "Truly, truly, I say to you, unless one is born again he cannot see the kingdom of God" (John 3:3). Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, whose death and resurrection are sufficient to save anyone from any nation who calls upon His name.

If you are searching for your true identity, look not to your lineage, but to the Cross. You are a sinner in need of a Savior, and Jesus Christ is that Savior. He does not offer a tribal religion, but a cosmic redemption. You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive a new name and an eternal inheritance that can never fade away. 

I encourage you to seek out a local, Bible-believing church where the Word is preached faithfully and the Gospel is the center of all things. Study the Book of Galatians and the Book of Hebrews to see how Christ is the fulfillment of all the Old Testament promises. What is it that draws you to seek identity in an earthly lineage? Is it possible that the peace you seek is found only in being a child of the Living God through faith?
What does the Bible say about church-hopping?

While the Bible does not use the specific phrase "church-hopping," it speaks extensively about the nature of the local church as a committed, covenant community. The New Testament assumes that every believer is an active, identifiable part of a local body of believers where they are known, cared for, and held accountable. Church-hopping—the practice of moving frequently from one congregation to another without ever settling or committing—often runs contrary to the biblical design for spiritual growth and mutual edification.

The Bible describes the church not as a building or a weekly event to attend, but as a living body. In 1 Corinthians 12:12-27, the Apostle Paul explains that just as a human body has many parts that rely on one another, the local church is composed of individual members who are indispensable to the whole. When a person "hops" from church to church, they are like a limb trying to function without being attached to a body. You cannot exercise your spiritual gifts for the benefit of others, nor can you receive the necessary care from the other "parts," if you do not remain long enough to be joined to them.

Furthermore, the Scriptures command believers to submit to the leadership of God-appointed elders. Hebrews 13:17 says, "Obey your leaders and submit to them, for they watch over your souls as those who must give an account. To do this with joy and not with groaning, for that would be of no benefit to you." It is impossible for a pastor to "watch over your soul" or for you to biblically "submit" to leadership if you are constantly in transit. Biblical oversight requires a stable relationship between the shepherd and the sheep.

There are, of course, legitimate reasons to leave a local congregation. This is a matter of Christian liberty where faithful believers may disagree on the timing or specific reasons for a move. Valid reasons include:
*   The departure of a church from the core doctrines of the faith, such as the deity of Christ or the authority of Scripture.
*   Persistent unrepentant sin in leadership or a refusal to practice biblical church discipline.
*   Relocation to a new geographical area.
*   A lack of biblical preaching where the Word of God is not being rightly handled.

However, many people today move between churches for consumeristic reasons—searching for better music, more entertaining programs, or to avoid the discomfort of a personal conflict. This mindset treats the bride of Christ like a product rather than a family. True sanctification, the process by which the Holy Spirit makes us more like Jesus, often happens through the very friction and challenges of staying in a community of imperfect people.

If you find yourself frequently moving between churches, I encourage you to examine your heart in prayer. Ask yourself if you are seeking a "perfect" church—which does not exist on this side of heaven—or if you are perhaps avoiding the accountability that comes with being truly known.

Our commitment to a local church is a small reflection of Christ’s unwavering commitment to His people. He did not "hop" from one people to another; He loved the church and gave Himself up for her, as seen in Ephesians 5:25. It is through His historical death on the cross and His bodily resurrection that we are reconciled to God and brought into His family. If you have not yet trusted in Him, I invite you to turn from your sin and place your faith in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose again to give you new life and a place in His eternal kingdom.

An actionable next step is to identify a biblically sound local church and meet with one of the elders. Ask them about their process for formal membership and how you can begin to serve and be held accountable within that specific body of believers.
Dance in worship - what does the Bible say?

The Bible presents dance as a physical expression of spiritual joy and thanksgiving, primarily appearing in the Old Testament during moments of national celebration or personal triumph. This subject is a tertiary matter of Christian liberty and worship style, meaning that faithful believers may disagree on how it should be applied in the life of the church today.

In the Old Testament, dance was often a spontaneous response to God’s deliverance. Miriam and the women of Israel danced to celebrate the Lord's victory over the Egyptian army at the Red Sea in Exodus 15:20-21. King David famously danced before the Lord with all his might as the Ark of the Covenant was brought into Jerusalem, an act of humble and exuberant devotion recorded in 2 Samuel 6:14. Furthermore, the Psalms explicitly call the faithful to praise His name with dancing in Psalm 149:3 and Psalm 150:4. From this perspective, dance is seen as a legitimate way for a believer to engage their whole being in the exaltation of God.

However, many Christians throughout history, particularly within the Reformed tradition, have exercised caution regarding dance in corporate worship. They observe that the New Testament instructions for the gathered church emphasize the preaching of the Word, prayer, and the singing of psalms and hymns, as seen in Colossians 3:16 and 1 Timothy 4:13. These believers prioritize the "Regulative Principle," which suggests that we should only include elements in formal worship that are specifically commanded in the New Testament. The primary concern here is to maintain order and ensure that the focus remains entirely on the Gospel and the edification of the body, rather than on a physical performance that might distract the heart, following the principle in 1 Corinthians 14:40 that all things should be done decently and in order.

The differences in these views often stem from how a church interprets the transition from the ceremonial celebrations of Israel to the more sober and Word-centered gatherings of the early church. While some see David’s dance as a timeless model for all believers, others see it as a cultural expression unique to that historical context.

Regardless of where a local church lands on this issue, the ultimate standard is the heart’s orientation toward Jesus Christ. True worship must be "in spirit and in truth," as our Lord taught in John 4:24. Our greatest reason for joy is not found in our own movements, but in the finished work of Christ. Through His death and bodily resurrection, He has defeated sin and death, reconciling us to a holy God. This is the Gospel that provides the "oil of joy instead of mourning," as mentioned in Isaiah 61:3. 

If you are considering how to express your praise, I encourage you to examine your motives and the context of your local church. Ask yourself if your expression is intended to glorify God and build up your brothers and sisters in Christ. You may find it helpful to speak with your elders or pastors to understand the convictions of your local body of believers.

If you have not yet placed your trust in the Savior, I invite you to turn to Him today. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and the gift of eternal life. What is it that might be holding you back from trusting Him with your whole life?
What is secular humanism?

Secular humanism is a comprehensive worldview that exalts human reason, ethics, and social justice as the ultimate authorities while explicitly rejecting the existence of God and the reality of the supernatural. It is an attempt to live in God’s world without acknowledging the God who made it, relying instead on scientific naturalism and human autonomy to define truth and morality. At its core, it is a philosophy that seeks to answer the fundamental questions of life by looking inward at ourselves rather than upward to our Creator.

The Bible describes this inclination not as a modern intellectual breakthrough, but as a rejection of the clear evidence God has provided of Himself in the things He has made. The Apostle Paul notes that those who suppress the truth in unrighteousness "exchanged the truth of God for a lie, and worshiped and served the creature rather than the Creator" (Romans 1:25). By removing the Lord from the center of the universe, secular humanism loses the only objective foundation for human dignity and moral law. If we are merely the products of blind, material forces, then there is no ultimate purpose to our lives beyond what we temporarily invent for ourselves.

While secular humanism often speaks of the inherent goodness of humanity and the power of progress, Scripture provides a more honest and somber assessment: "The heart is deceitful above all things and beyond cure" (Jeremiah 17:9). No amount of education, scientific discovery, or social engineering can fix the spiritual brokenness caused by our sin. The humanist dream of a self-perfected society fails because it ignores the reality that we are fallen creatures in need of a Savior.

We must be careful not to be led astray by such thinking. Scripture warns us, "See to it that no one takes you captive through philosophy and empty deception, which are based on human tradition and the spiritual forces of the world rather than on Christ" (Colossians 2:8). Our value as human beings does not come from our own reason or achievements, but from the fact that we were created in the image of God (Genesis 1:27). True wisdom begins not with the exaltation of man, but with the fear of the Lord (Proverbs 1:7).

The tragedy of secular humanism is that it offers a high view of man's potential but provides no hope for man’s soul. Our Creator has not left us to wander in the darkness of our own limited understanding. He has spoken clearly through His Word and perfectly through His Son, Jesus Christ, who came to reconcile us to God.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Does the Bible predict the destruction of Damascus?

Yes, the Bible contains explicit prophecies concerning the destruction of Damascus, which is historically recognized as one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world. The most prominent of these predictions is found in the book of Isaiah.

The prophet Isaiah declares that Damascus will cease to be a city and will instead become a heap of ruins (Isaiah 17:1). This prophecy further describes the desertion of its surrounding cities and the fading of the fortified city of Ephraim and the royal power of Damascus (Isaiah 17:2-3).

Other prophets also spoke of God's judgment upon this city. The prophet Amos recorded that God would break the gate bar of Damascus and send a fire upon the house of Hazael that would consume the fortresses of Ben-hadad (Amos 1:3-5). Similarly, the prophet Jeremiah described Damascus as having grown feeble and turning to flee, as anguish and sorrows took hold of her (Jeremiah 49:23-27).

In history, many of these judgments saw a significant fulfillment when the Assyrian King Tiglath-Pileser III conquered Damascus in 732 BC. However, because Damascus remains a city today, many students of the Bible believe these prophecies also point toward a final, ultimate fulfillment in the context of the Lord's dealings with the nations at the end of the age.

These prophecies serve as a sobering reminder of the absolute sovereignty of God over the nations. He is the one who raises up kingdoms and brings them low according to His righteous will (Daniel 2:21; Daniel 4:17). While the fall of a great city is a matter of historical and physical gravity, it points us to the spiritual reality that all human structures and earthly securities are temporary.

The certainty of biblical prophecy demonstrates that God's Word is true and His judgments are just. This same God who judges the nations also offers a refuge through His Son, Jesus Christ. Just as the prophets warned of coming ruin, they also pointed toward the hope of a King whose kingdom will never be destroyed.

I invite you to consider your own standing before this sovereign God. You can turn to Him now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to find eternal safety in His grace. What is it that holds you back from placing your full trust in Him today?

As a practical step in your study, I encourage you to read through Isaiah 17 and Jeremiah 49, asking the Holy Spirit to give you an understanding of God’s holiness and His control over history. Pray also for the people living in that region today, that they might hear and receive the peace of the Gospel.
What does it mean to watch and pray?

To watch and pray is the biblical command to maintain spiritual alertness while remaining in constant, dependent communion with God. This dual discipline ensures that the believer is not overtaken by temptation or lulled into spiritual sleep by the distractions and trials of this world (Matthew 26:41; Mark 13:33).

Watching is the exercise of spiritual vigilance. It is the act of a sentry who stands upon the wall, looking outward to discern the approach of the enemy and looking inward to guard against the treachery of his own heart. The Bible describes this as being sober-minded and alert, recognizing that the devil prowls around like a roaring lion seeking someone to devour (1 Peter 5:8). Watching also has an expectant quality, as we look forward with hope to the return of Jesus Christ, ensuring that our hearts are ready for His appearing (Mark 13:35–37). Without watching, we are like a city with broken walls, vulnerable to every passing deception.

Praying is the necessary companion to watching. While watching identifies the danger, prayer lays hold of the divine power required to overcome it. Prayer is the humble admission that we lack the strength to persevere on our own. It is the means of grace by which we receive help in our time of need (Hebrews 4:16). When we continue steadfastly in prayer, being watchful in it with thanksgiving, we are fortifying our souls against the anxieties and deceptions that seek to derail our progressive sanctification (Colossians 4:2; 1 Thessalonians 5:17).

In a practical sense, you can implement this by practicing a morning inventory. Before your day begins, consider where you are most vulnerable to sin, whether it be through pride, irritability, or worldly distraction. Having watched for these specific pitfalls, immediately pray for the Holy Spirit to provide the specific strength and self-control needed to resist them.

This command was most poignantly given by Jesus in the Garden of Gethsemane. While the disciples slept, Christ watched and prayed in perfect obedience, preparing to go to the cross to atone for our failures and spiritual lethargy. His sacrificial death and bodily resurrection are the only grounds by which we are forgiven and empowered to live for Him. If you find yourself struggling with the weight of sin or spiritual exhaustion, you can turn to Jesus Christ today, repent of your sins, and trust in Him alone for the grace to stand firm. What is it that currently hinders you from fully trusting in His strength?
How is eternity in Hell a fair punishment for sin?

The fairness of eternal punishment is found in the infinite dignity and holiness of the One who is offended. In any system of justice, the severity of a punishment is not determined by how long it took to commit the crime, but by the nature of the crime and the status of the person against whom it was committed. If a man lies to a child, it is a moral failing, if he lies under oath to a high court, it is perjury, and if he commits treason against his country, the consequences are life-altering. Because God is an infinite, eternal, and perfectly holy Being, any sin against Him is an offense of infinite proportions.

The Bible teaches that God is "Holy, holy, holy," a repetitive emphasis used to show that His purity is His most defining attribute in Isaiah 6:3. Because He is the source of all life and goodness, to rebel against Him is to choose a path away from the only source of life. Sin is not merely a mistake, it is high treason against the Sovereign of the universe. Therefore, the punishment must match the infinite value of the Lawgiver who was defied, as seen in Revelation 20:11–15 and Matthew 25:46.

Furthermore, Hell is the final place for those who persistently refuse to be with God. It is the destination for those who have said to their Creator, "Go away and leave me alone." In His justice, God eventually grants that request. Those in Hell do not cease to be rebels, they continue in their rejection of God for eternity, which necessitates an ongoing judgment.

This weight of divine justice is exactly why the Gospel is such glorious news. God, who is perfectly just, is also perfectly loving. He did not leave us to face this infinite debt alone. The Lord Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, entered our world through the virgin birth and lived a life of perfect obedience. He then suffered the penal substitutionary death on the cross, meaning He took the infinite weight of God's holy wrath that we deserved upon Himself. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death, as recorded in 1 Corinthians 15:3–4 and Romans 4:25.

Salvation is not something we earn by being "good enough," for we have all fallen short of God’s glory in Romans 3:23. Instead, justification is by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. When a person trusts in Jesus, His perfect righteousness is credited to them, and their sins are forgiven because they were paid for at the cross.

If you find yourself troubled by the reality of judgment, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John in the Bible to see the character of the One who calls you to Himself. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
How is Satan god of this world (2 Corinthians 4:4)?

The title "god of this world" used in 2 Corinthians 4:4 does not imply that Satan is an equal power to the Almighty Creator, nor that he possesses inherent authority over the universe. Rather, it describes the pervasive and tragic influence he exercises over the present fallen age and the hearts of those who live in rebellion against God. 

Satan is the "god of this world" in the sense that he is the architect of the world's systems of thought, its false religions, and its sinful lusts. He rules over those who are spiritually dead, leading them according to the "prince of the power of the air," as described in Ephesians 2:2. He does not rule by right, but by the voluntary subjection of a humanity that has preferred darkness rather than light. As the Apostle John records, "the whole world lies under the sway of the wicked one," in 1 John 5:19.

The primary way Satan maintains this "god-like" status over the unbelieving world is through spiritual blindness.
