As a practical next step, I encourage you to spend time in prayer for the leaders of your local church. Ask the Lord to grant them humility and a deep love for the truth. If you are seeking a church home, look for one where the elders clearly submit themselves to the authority of Scripture and where they lead with the gentleness and boldness of Christ. Have you found it difficult to trust leadership in the past, or are you currently seeking a place where you can grow under godly oversight?
What is Luciferianism?

Luciferianism is a belief system that honors Lucifer, not typically as the personification of evil found in traditional Satanism, but as a symbol of enlightenment, independence, and the pursuit of hidden knowledge. Adherents often view Lucifer as a "light-bearer" who provides humanity with the means to achieve self-deification and liberation from what they perceive as the restrictive authority of the Creator. While some Luciferians may believe in a literal spiritual being, many treat Lucifer as a metaphor for the human intellect and the rebellious spirit that refuses to submit to any higher power.

From the firm foundation of Scripture, however, we must recognize this as the same ancient deception first whispered in the Garden of Eden. The heart of Luciferianism is the exaltation of the self over God, echoing the original temptation where the serpent promised, "you will be like God" (Genesis 3:5). It is the ultimate expression of human pride, which the Bible identifies as the very sin that led to the fall of the adversary. Although the name "Lucifer" is derived from the Latin translation of "shining one" or "day star" in Isaiah 14:12, the passage describes a fall from heaven resulting from the arrogant ambition to be like the Most High.

As those who love the truth, we must clearly state that Luciferianism is a dangerous spiritual error. It replaces the worship of the holy Creator with the worship of the fallen creature. The Bible warns us that "Satan himself masquerades as an angel of light" (2 Corinthians 11:14). What may appear to be a path toward intellectual freedom is actually a state of spiritual blindness and captivity to the prince of this world (Ephesians 2:2). 

True light and true life are not found within ourselves or through the pursuit of forbidden wisdom, but only in the person of Jesus Christ. He is the "Light of the World," and those who follow Him will never walk in darkness (John 8:12). 

The Gospel reveals that we are all sinners, separated from a holy and just God by our rebellion and pride (Romans 3:23). We cannot save ourselves or "evolve" into divine beings. Instead, God in His mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. He lived a sinless life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, triumphing over sin, death, and the power of the devil (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith in Christ alone, not by any works or secret knowledge we possess (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God even now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. He offers a peace and a purpose that no worldly philosophy or occult practice can provide. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical step, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John in the Bible. It portrays the true Light that has come into the darkness, a Light that the darkness can never overcome (John 1:5). If you are seeking answers, pray and ask the Lord to reveal the truth of His Word to you, and consider connecting with a local, Bible-believing church where the Gospel is faithfully preached.
What is the satisfaction or commercial theory of the atonement?

The satisfaction theory of the atonement, primarily developed by Anselm of Canterbury in the eleventh century, teaches that sin is an objective insult to the honor of God that creates a debt no human being can repay. Because God is infinitely holy, any violation of His will is an infinite offense that disturbs the moral order of the universe, requiring a satisfaction that restores what was taken from His glory.

In this framework, humanity owes God perfect obedience, but through sin, we have not only withheld that obedience but have also incurred a debt of honor. Since man committed the sin, a man must provide the satisfaction, yet because the debt is infinite, only God has the capacity to pay it. Therefore, the incarnation of Jesus Christ, the God-man, was necessary. By His voluntary death, which He did not owe because He was sinless, Jesus provided a gift of infinite value to the Father, thereby satisfying God's offended honor and winning forgiveness for believers.

While this theory rightly emphasizes the gravity of sin and the necessity of the God-man, many Reformed believers see it as a precursor to the more biblically complete doctrine of penal substitution. While Anselm focused on God's "honor," the Scriptures emphasize God's "justice" and "wrath" against sin. In penal substitution, Jesus does not merely restore God's honor as a gift, but He actually bears the legal penalty for our sins, satisfying the righteous requirements of God's law by standing in our place.

The Bible speaks of this reality when it says that all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God, and are justified by His grace as a gift, through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus, whom God put forward as a propitiation by His blood, to be received by faith. This was to show God's righteousness, because in His divine forbearance He had passed over former sins (Romans 3:23-25). Furthermore, it is written that He was pierced for our transgressions, He was crushed for our iniquities; the punishment that brought us peace was on Him, and by His wounds we are healed (Isaiah 53:5).

As you reflect on these truths, it is helpful to recognize that sin is never "free." It always carries a price that must be paid, either by the sinner or by a substitute. The depth of God’s love is seen in that He did not demand that you pay a debt you could never afford, but instead provided His own Son to pay it for you. 

Consider your own standing before the Lord today. If you recognize the weight of your debt before a holy God, I encourage you to turn from your sin and place your full trust in Jesus Christ alone. He is the only one who has satisfied the Father’s justice on your behalf. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What is it that might be holding you back from resting entirely in His finished work?
Who are the Shakers?

The Shakers, formally known as the United Society of Believers in Christ’s Second Appearing, are a religious sect that originated in England in the mid 18th century before migrating to America under the leadership of Ann Lee, often called Mother Ann. They are most widely remembered today for their simple lifestyle, communal living, and the exceptional craftsmanship of their furniture, but their origins were rooted in a particular set of mystical and restorationist beliefs.

The group received the name Shakers because of their ecstatic form of worship, which included trembling, shouting, singing, and dancing. While they sought a life of holiness and separation from the world, their core doctrines departed significantly from the historic Christian faith in several essential ways.

First, the Shakers held a view of God that was not aligned with the biblical revelation of the Trinity. They believed in a dual nature of God, both male and female. More critically, they taught that while Jesus was the male incarnation of the Christ spirit, Mother Ann Lee was the female incarnation and represented the second appearing of Christ on earth. Scripture, however, teaches that Jesus Christ is the unique and eternal Son of God who will return personally and bodily to judge the living and the dead (Acts 1:11; Hebrews 9:28). To suggest a second incarnation in another person is a denial of the sufficiency and finality of Christ's work.

Second, the Shakers practiced strict celibacy, believing that all sexual relations were the root of human sin. While the Bible honors those who are called to singleness for the sake of the kingdom, it also upholds marriage as a holy institution created by God (Genesis 2:24; Hebrews 13:4). Because they did not have children, the Shakers could only sustain their communities through converts and the adoption of orphans, which eventually led to their near-disappearance as a movement.

While we can appreciate their commitment to hard work, honesty, and peace, we must be careful to distinguish their teachings from the true Gospel. True holiness does not come from withdrawing into a communal society or through legalistic practices like mandatory celibacy, but through the finished work of Jesus Christ. We are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, and it is His Spirit within us that produces the fruit of a transformed life (Ephesians 2:8-9; Galatians 5:22-23).

If you are searching for a community that reflects the love of God, I encourage you to look to the person of Jesus Christ as He is revealed in the Bible. He is the only one who can reconcile us to the Father and provide the peace that the Shakers so earnestly sought. You can turn to Him now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. Have you ever considered the difference between trying to earn God's favor through your own lifestyle and receiving it as a free gift through Christ?
What is the favor of God, and how can I get it?

The favor of God is His gracious approval, divine kindness, and benevolent blessing bestowed upon His people. It is not a wage earned by human effort, but a gift flowing from the sovereign good pleasure of God. To have God’s favor is to be reconciled to Him, no longer under His righteous wrath for sin, but brought near as a beloved child through the work of Jesus Christ.

The Scriptures teach that we cannot earn this favor through our own merits or moral improvements, for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God (Romans 3:23). Because God is holy and just, our rebellion separates us from His favorable presence. However, God, being rich in mercy, sent His Son to be the propitiation for our sins. This means Jesus Christ satisfied the demands of God's justice on our behalf.

You receive the favor of God not by working, but by believing. It is found exclusively in the person and work of Jesus Christ. When a person turns from their sin and trusts in Christ’s historical death and bodily resurrection, they are justified by grace alone through faith alone (Ephesians 2:8-9). In this wonderful exchange, Christ takes our sin and we receive His perfect righteousness. This is the ultimate favor: to be "accepted in the Beloved" (Ephesians 1:6).

If you desire to know the favor of God, you must look away from yourself and look to Jesus. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical step in your journey, I encourage you to begin reading the Gospel of John. As you read, pray and ask God to reveal the glory of Jesus to your heart. Seek out a local, Bible-believing church where the Word of God is preached faithfully, so you may grow in the grace and knowledge of our Lord (2 Peter 3:18). Hebrews 4:16 promises that because of Christ, we can "approach the throne of grace with confidence, so that we may receive mercy and find grace to help us in our time of need."
What does it mean that Jesus is the Savior?

To say that Jesus is the Savior is to acknowledge that humanity is in a state of spiritual ruin from which we cannot escape by our own efforts. It means that Jesus Christ is the one who rescues us from the power of sin, the guilt of our rebellion, and the eternal weight of God’s righteous wrath. Matthew 1:21.

To understand Jesus as Savior, we must first look at the truth of our condition. God is infinitely holy, just, and good. He created us to know Him and reflect His glory, but every one of us has turned away in sin, which is a literal rebellion against His authority. Romans 3:23. Because God is just, He must punish sin, and the wages of that sin is death—not only physical death but eternal separation from God. Romans 6:23.

In His profound love, God did not leave us in this helpless state. Jesus Christ, who is eternally God, took on human flesh to serve as our Mediator. John 1:1; John 1:14. He lived a life of perfect obedience to God’s law, a life we could never live. Then, He willingly went to the cross to accomplish what is called penal substitutionary atonement. This means that on the cross, Jesus stood in the place of sinners, bearing the full weight of the punishment we deserved so that we might be forgiven. 1 Peter 2:24; 2 Corinthians 5:21.

His work as Savior was confirmed by His historical, bodily resurrection from the dead on the third day. 1 Corinthians 15:3–4. By rising again, He proved His victory over sin and death, demonstrating that His sacrifice was accepted by the Father. He is now exalted as Lord of all, offering the gift of salvation to all who will receive it. Acts 4:12.

The Gospel is the good news that although you are a sinner separated from a holy God, salvation is available by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. Ephesians 2:8–9. It is not something you can earn through good works, religious rituals, or moral improvement. It is a gift received by trusting in the finished work of Jesus. John 14:6.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him?

As a practical next step in understanding this great rescue, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John in the Bible. It was written specifically so that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that by believing you may have life in His name. John 20:31. Seek out a local church where the Bible is preached faithfully, and do not hesitate to ask the elders there to help you understand what it means to follow Jesus.
What is baby dedication? Is child / baby dedication biblical?

Baby dedication is a public act of worship in which parents present their child before God and the local congregation, signifying their commitment to raise the child in the "nurture and admonition of the Lord" as described in Ephesians 6:4. In this ceremony, the parents acknowledge the child as a gift from God and pledge to model a Christ-centered life, while the church community often pledges to support the family in the spiritual upbringing of the child.

Regarding its biblical basis, it is important to distinguish between a direct command and a godly precedent. There is no specific command in the New Testament for a "baby dedication" ceremony, nor is it a sacrament like Baptism or the Lord’s Supper. However, we see beautiful examples in Scripture that provide a foundation for this practice. In the Old Testament, Hannah dedicated her son Samuel to the Lord, saying, "I prayed for this boy, and the LORD has granted me what I asked of Him. So now I give him to the LORD. For his whole life he will be given over to the LORD" in 1 Samuel 1:27-28. In the New Testament, we see Joseph and Mary bringing the infant Jesus to the temple in Jerusalem "to present Him to the Lord" as recorded in Luke 2:22.

Furthermore, our Lord Jesus showed a particular tenderness toward children. When the disciples tried to turn families away, Jesus said, "Let the little children come to Me, and do not hinder them! For the kingdom of heaven belongs to such as these" in Matthew 19:14. Dedication reflects this heart, placing the child under the prayerful care of the church.

Within the Christian tradition, views on this practice vary:
*   **Credobaptist (Believer's Baptism) View:** Many churches, particularly those in the Reformed Baptist tradition, practice baby dedication instead of infant baptism. They believe baptism is reserved for those who have made a personal profession of faith in Christ. Dedication is seen as a way for parents to express their intent to lead the child toward that future day of faith, without suggesting that the ceremony itself confers salvation or church membership.
*   **Paedobaptist (Infant Baptism) View:** Other historic denominations, such as Presbyterians or Lutherans, practice infant baptism (paedobaptism) rather than dedication. They view this as the New Testament fulfillment of circumcision, marking the child as a member of the covenant community, though they also affirm that personal faith is necessary for salvation.

It is vital to understand that a baby dedication does not secure a child's salvation. Scripture is clear that we are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, as taught in Ephesians 2:8-9. A child is not born a Christian by virtue of their parents' faith or a religious ceremony, but must eventually come to a personal repentance and trust in the finished work of Jesus Christ. 

If you are a parent considering this, the most significant "dedication" happens in the quiet moments of your home. Dedicate yourself to the daily reading of Scripture and prayer, for your life is the first Bible your child will read. You might begin by reading a small portion of the Gospels to your child each night and asking the Lord for the wisdom to lead them to the Savior. 

The Gospel reminds us that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us as stated in Romans 5:8. This is the great truth we want every child to eventually embrace. If you have not yet placed your own trust in Jesus, I invite you to turn from your sin and trust in Him today. He is the only one who can forgive sin and grant eternal life through His death and bodily resurrection. What is it that might be keeping you from fully trusting Him with your life and your family?
Will God continue to forgive you if you commit the same sin over and over again?

The short and wonderful answer is that God’s mercy is indeed vast enough to forgive the same sin repeatedly when a person comes to Him with a heart of genuine repentance. We see the heart of the Father reflected in the command Jesus gave to Peter, telling him to forgive a brother not just seven times, but seventy times seven times, as recorded in Matthew 18:21-22. If God requires such extensive patience from us, we can be certain that His own reservoir of grace is infinitely deeper.

The Apostle John, writing with the heart of a shepherd, tells us, "If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness" in 1 John 1:9. This promise does not have an expiration date or a limit on the number of times it can be claimed. Because Christ’s sacrifice on the cross was a finished and perfect work, it covers the sins of the past, the present, and the future for those who belong to Him. His blood is sufficient for every failure.

However, we must also walk in the light of the truth. While God’s grace is inexhaustible, it is never intended to be a license for us to continue in sin. The Apostle Paul addresses this very concern in Romans 6:1-2, asking, "What shall we say then? Are we to continue in sin that grace may abound? By no means! How can we who died to sin still live in it?" A heart that has truly been transformed by the Holy Spirit will find no lasting peace or comfort in a cycle of habitual sin. Instead, there will be a holy dissatisfaction and a longing to be free from that which grieves the Lord.

There is a vital distinction between the "struggle" with sin and the "practice" of sin. A believer may find themselves falling into the same hole many times, yet each time they fall, they cry out to God in sorrow and get back up to continue the fight. As Proverbs 24:16 says, "For the righteous falls seven times and rises again, but the wicked are overthrown by calamity." If you are grieving over your sin and running back to Christ for help, that very grief is often a sign that the Holy Spirit is at work within you, conforming you to the image of the Son.

If you find yourself trapped in a particular pattern, I encourage you to take these practical steps:
First, bring this specific struggle to God in honest prayer, asking not only for forgiveness but for the power of the Holy Spirit to grant you new desires. 
Second, do not fight this battle in isolation. God has given us the local church for our protection and growth. I urge you to speak with a faithful pastor or a mature Christian friend who can offer biblical counsel and hold you accountable. 
Third, identify the "provisions" you might be making for that sin and, by God's grace, remove the triggers that lead you into temptation.

If you have never truly rested in the finished work of Jesus Christ, please know that He is the only one who can reconcile you to a holy God. We are all sinners separated from God by our rebellion, but God, being rich in mercy, sent His Son to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for your salvation and the gift of new life. What is it that makes you hesitate to fully surrender this struggle to Him?
Can monotheism be proven?

The existence of the one true God is not merely a philosophical theory to be weighed in the scales of human logic; it is the fundamental reality declared by the Creator Himself through both His works and His Word. Scripture does not set out to prove God's existence as a scientist might prove a hypothesis; rather, it begins with the absolute fact of His being. Genesis 1:1.

There are three primary ways the oneness of God is established: through creation, through logical necessity, and through divine revelation.

**1. The Testimony of Creation (General Revelation)**
The universe we inhabit is a "cosmos," an orderly system that reflects a single, coherent Mind. The complexity and fine-tuning of the physical laws suggest a solitary Designer rather than a committee of competing deities. The Apostle Paul explains that God’s invisible attributes, namely His eternal power and divine nature, have been clearly perceived, ever since the creation of the world, in the things that have been made. Romans 1:20. This general revelation leaves all humanity without excuse for failing to acknowledge the one Creator.

**2. The Testimony of Logic and Necessity**
From a logical standpoint, the concept of "God" implies a Being who is supreme, infinite, and all-powerful. If there were two such beings, they would necessarily limit one another, meaning neither would be truly infinite or supreme. For God to be God, He must possess "aseity," meaning He is self-existent and underived, the First Cause of all things. A universe with multiple "first causes" is a logical contradiction. As the Prophet Isaiah records, "I am the LORD, and there is no other; besides me there is no God." Isaiah 45:5.

**3. The Testimony of Scripture (Special Revelation)**
The most certain proof is found in the Bible, which is the inerrant and infallible Word of God. The central confession of the people of God for millennia has been the Shema: "Hear, O Israel: The LORD our God, the LORD is one." Deuteronomy 6:4. This monotheism is not a late human development but a truth revealed by God Himself. This one God exists eternally in three persons—the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit—yet remains one in essence. Matthew 28:19.

The ultimate "proof" of monotheism was walking among us in the person of Jesus Christ. He is the radiance of God’s glory and the exact representation of His being. Hebrews 1:3. In Christ, the one true God took on human nature to reconcile a fallen world to Himself. 

Because God is holy and just, our sin—which is a rebellion against His rightful rule—separates us from Him. However, in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary atoning death on the cross to pay for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day. He has ascended to the right hand of the Father and is the only Mediator between God and man. Romans 10:9; 1 Timothy 2:5. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and eternal life.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John in the Bible. It was written specifically so that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that by believing you may have life in His name. John 20:31. What is it that holds you back from trusting in the one true God today?
What is impartation?

Impartation is the act of sharing, giving, or communicating something to another person. In the context of the Christian faith, it refers to the way God bestows spiritual blessings, truths, or graces upon His people, often through the ministry of the Holy Spirit or the fellowship of the saints. While the word itself is simple, it carries profound significance for how we understand our growth in Christ and our relationship with one another.

To understand impartation clearly, we must distinguish it from a similar term, imputation. In our justification, Christ’s righteousness is imputed to us, which means it is legally credited to our account through faith alone, apart from our works (Romans 4:3; Romans 4:22-24). Impartation, however, is more closely related to our sanctification. It is the process by which the Holy Spirit actually communicates the life and holiness of Jesus to our inner being, enabling us to become in character what God has already declared us to be in status (2 Peter 1:3-4).

There are three primary ways the Bible speaks of impartation:

First, there is the impartation of spiritual encouragement and strength through fellowship. The Apostle Paul expressed a deep longing to visit the believers in Rome so that he might "impart" to them some spiritual gift to make them strong (Romans 1:11). He clarifies in the following verse that this is a mutual encouragement of faith. We see here that God often uses the presence, words, and prayers of fellow believers to transmit grace and perseverance to our souls (1 Thessalonians 2:8).

Second, the Bible speaks of the laying on of hands in relation to ministry. In the Old Testament, Moses laid hands on Joshua to impart authority and wisdom (Numbers 27:18-23; Deuteronomy 34:9). In the New Testament, Timothy was exhorted not to neglect the gift he received through the laying on of hands by the elders (1 Timothy 4:14; 2 Timothy 1:6). While we must be careful not to view this as a mechanical or magical transfer of power, we recognize that God sovereignly uses the formal setting apart of individuals for ministry to bestow specific graces for their calling.

Third, and most importantly, is the impartation of life from the Vine to the branches. Jesus is the source of all spiritual vitality, and through the Holy Spirit, He imparts His peace, joy, and strength to those who abide in Him (John 15:4-5; John 14:27). This is not a one time event but a continuous flow of grace that sustains the believer.

In some modern circles, the term impartation is used to describe a physical transfer of an "anointing" from one person to another through touch. While God certainly uses human means, we must remember that the Holy Spirit is sovereign and distributes His gifts to each one individually as He wills (1 Corinthians 12:11). We should never look to a human being as the source of spiritual power, but always look to Christ, who is the Head of the Church.

The greatest impartation any human can receive is the gift of new life. Because of our sin, we are spiritually dead and separated from a holy God (Ephesians 2:1-3). But God, in His great mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and was raised bodily on the third day, triumphing over death. All who turn from their sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone receive the forgiveness of sins and the gift of the Holy Spirit. 

You can turn to God even now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What is preventing you today from placing your full trust in Him?

As a practical step in your walk with God, I encourage you to seek the "means of grace" that God has provided for your spiritual nourishment. Dedicate time each day to the reading of the Word and to private prayer, and commit yourself to a faithful local church where the Gospel is preached. It is through these ordinary means that the Holy Spirit most consistently imparts the strength and wisdom you need for the Christian life (Acts 2:42).
What is Atenism? Did either Judaism or Christianity borrow from Atenism?

Atenism was a religious system in ancient Egypt established by Pharaoh Akhenaten during the fourteenth century BC, which centered on the exclusive worship of the Aten, represented as the disk of the sun. This period, often called the Amarna Period, marked a radical departure from traditional Egyptian polytheism as Akhenaten suppressed the worship of other gods and declared the Aten to be the sole, self-created deity and the sustainer of all life.

While some secular historians and critical scholars suggest that Judaism borrowed its monotheism from Atenism, this perspective conflicts with the historical facts recorded in the Holy Scriptures. The Bible reveals that the knowledge of the one true God was not a human invention or an evolutionary step in religious thought, but a direct result of God revealing Himself to mankind from the beginning of creation.

The worship of the true God, Yahweh, predates Akhenaten by centuries. God called Abraham out of Ur of the Chaldeans and established a covenant with him long before the rise of the eighteenth dynasty in Egypt, as seen in Genesis 12:1-3; Genesis 15:1-6. Furthermore, the monotheism of Israel was fundamentally different from Atenism. Akhenaten worshipped a part of the creation—the sun disk—whereas the Hebrews worshipped the Creator who stands outside of and above His creation. In Exodus 20:2-5, God explicitly forbids the worship of anything in the heavens above or the earth beneath, which would include the sun.

Christianity, being the fulfillment of the promises made to Israel through the person and work of Jesus Christ, likewise does not borrow from Atenism. The faith of the apostles is grounded in the historical incarnation, death, and resurrection of the Son of God, who is the radiance of God’s glory and the exact representation of His being, according to Hebrews 1:3. Any superficial similarities between Atenism and the biblical faith, such as the focus on a single deity, are better understood as remnants of the original knowledge of God that all humanity once possessed but suppressed in unrighteousness, as described in Romans 1:18-23.

We must hold firmly to the truth that the Bible is the inspired, inerrant Word of God and our ultimate authority. It does not present "perspectives" on God, but the actual history of God’s dealings with man. The Lord Himself declares His uniqueness and precedes all human religious systems, saying in Isaiah 45:5, "I am the LORD, and there is no other; there is no God besides Me."

The most vital truth for us to grasp is that this one true God has not remained distant or hidden behind the sun, but has made Himself known perfectly in His Son, Jesus Christ. Because of our sin, we were separated from this holy God, but through the sacrificial death of Christ on the cross and His bodily resurrection, we are offered reconciliation and eternal life by grace through faith alone. 

You can turn to God even now, repenting of your sins and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive full forgiveness and the hope of heaven. Is there anything in your heart today that makes it difficult for you to trust that God has truly spoken through His Word?
What happens to babies and young children when they die? Where do I find the age of accountability in the Bible?

The Bible does not contain the specific phrase "age of accountability," nor does it provide a specific chronological age at which a child becomes responsible for their sins before God. Instead, Scripture points us toward the perfect character of God, who is both infinitely just and boundlessly merciful, to understand the state of those who die in infancy or early childhood.

While every human being is born with a fallen nature inherited from Adam and is therefore in need of God’s grace (Psalm 51:5; Romans 5:12), many believers hold a firm and well-founded hope that God, in His sovereign mercy, saves children who die before they reach a stage of mental or spiritual development where they can consciously understand the Gospel or willfully reject God's law. This hope is not based on the "innocence" of the child, as all have a sinful nature, but on the gracious character of the Creator who applies the atoning work of Jesus Christ to those who are unable to exercise faith.

Several passages of Scripture offer comfort regarding this difficult question:

1.  **David’s Confidence:** After the death of his infant son, King David expressed a clear hope of reunion, saying, "I shall go to him, but he will not return to me" (2 Samuel 12:23). David’s comfort suggests a conviction that his child was in the presence of the Lord.
2.  **God’s Compassion for the Helpless:** In the book of Jonah, God expresses pity for the people of Nineveh, specifically noting the "more than a hundred and twenty thousand people who cannot tell their right hand from their left" (Jonah 4:11). This highlights God's unique care for those lacking moral discernment.
3.  **Jesus Welcoming Children:** Our Lord Jesus displayed a tender heart toward the little ones, rebuking His disciples and saying, "Let the little children come to Me, and do not hinder them! For the kingdom of heaven belongs to such as these" (Matthew 19:14). 
4.  **Moral Discernment:** Deuteronomy 1:39 mentions "your children who do not yet know good from evil," suggesting that God recognizes a distinction between those who knowingly rebel and those who do not yet possess the capacity to understand His commands.

The "age of accountability" is better understood as a "state of accountability." It is the point at which a person has the maturity to recognize their sinfulness and their need for a Savior. Because every child develops at a different pace, this moment is known only to God.

If you are grieving the loss of a child or concerned for the soul of a little one, I encourage you to rest in the character of God. He is the "Judge of all the earth" who will surely "do what is right" (Genesis 18:25). He loves children far more deeply than any earthly parent ever could.

The ultimate truth for all of us, regardless of age, is that salvation is a gift of God's grace through the Lord Jesus Christ. Jesus, being truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily on the third day, conquering death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). For those of us who can understand this message, the call is to repent of our sins and trust in Christ alone for our salvation (Acts 16:31).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. As a practical step, I invite you to read through the Gospel of John, paying close attention to how Jesus treats the weak and the broken, and then bring your questions and your heart to Him in prayer. What is it that makes it difficult for you to trust God with the souls of the little ones?
What is the Church of Spiritualism?

The Church of Spiritualism is a religious movement that centers on the belief that the spirits of the dead can and do communicate with the living, typically through the assistance of a medium. While it often adopts the language of "church" and mentions "God" or "Infinite Intelligence," its core tenets and practices stand in direct opposition to the historic Christian faith and the clear commands of the Bible.

The primary error of Spiritualism is its reliance on necromancy, which is the practice of attempting to communicate with the deceased. Scripture is not silent on this matter, but warns us that such activities are a spiritual snare. In the Old Testament, God spoke clearly to His people, saying, "There shall not be found among you anyone who... practices divination or tells fortunes or interprets omens, or a sorcerer or a charmer or a medium or a necromancer or one who inquires of the dead, for whoever does these things is an abomination to the LORD" (Deuteronomy 18:10-12). To seek guidance from the dead is to turn away from the living God who has revealed Himself perfectly through His Word and His Son.

From a doctrinal perspective, Spiritualism diverges from Christianity in several critical ways:

The Authority of Scripture: Spiritualism prioritizes "messages" from spirits over the written Word of God. However, the Bible is the inspired, inerrant, and final authority for all matters of faith (2 Timothy 3:16-17). Any spirit that contradicts the Word of God is not of God, for "even Satan disguises himself as an angel of light" (2 Corinthians 11:14).

The Person and Work of Jesus Christ: While some Spiritualists speak highly of Jesus, they often reduce Him to a "great medium" or a highly evolved spirit. This denies the core doctrine of the Hypostatic Union, that Jesus is truly God and truly man, the only Mediator between God and humanity (1 Timothy 2:5). Furthermore, Spiritualism generally rejects the necessity of Christ’s substitutionary atonement, the belief that Jesus died on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins.

The Nature of Salvation: Spiritualism frequently teaches a form of "infinite progression," where every soul eventually evolves or improves in the afterlife regardless of their relationship with Christ. This ignores the biblical truth of the finality of death and the reality of judgment. "And just as it is appointed for man to die once, and after that comes judgment" (Hebrews 9:27).

As a fellow traveler through this life, I urge you to see that our greatest need is not to hear from those who have passed away, but to be reconciled to the One who conquered death. We are all separated from God by our sin, and no amount of spiritual searching or personal "progression" can bridge that gap. But God, being rich in mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live the perfect life we could not live and to die the death we deserved. He rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and the grave (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

If you are searching for peace regarding the afterlife or comfort concerning those you have lost, do not look to the shadows of the séance room. Look to the Light of the World. You can turn to God even now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for your forgiveness and eternal life. He is the only one who can truly say, "I am the resurrection and the life. Whoever believes in Me will live, even though he dies" (John 11:25).

A helpful next step for you would be to pick up a Bible and read the Gospel of John. As you read, ask God to show you the truth about His Son. I also encourage you to find a local, Bible-believing church where you can hear the Word of God preached and find true fellowship in the Gospel. What is it that holds you back from trusting in Jesus Christ today?
What is Chrislam?

Chrislam is a syncretistic religious movement that attempts to merge the teachings and practices of Christianity and Islam into a single belief system. It primarily emerged in Nigeria during the 1970s, based on the claim that because both religions are monotheistic and trace their lineage back to Abraham, they can be practiced as one. Proponents often treat the Bible and the Quran as equally inspired texts and may alternate between Christian and Islamic forms of worship.

However, when we look through the lens of Holy Scripture, we must see this movement as a grave error that compromises the very foundation of the Christian faith. While we are called to live at peace with all men and to show neighborly love to those of other faiths, we cannot pretend that light and darkness can fellowship together or that two contradictory paths lead to the same destination. 2 Corinthians 6:14-15; Romans 12:18.

The Bible teaches that there are irreconcilable differences between the Gospel of Jesus Christ and the teachings of Islam. These differences involve the very nature of God, the identity of Jesus, and the way of salvation:

First, the nature of God is at the heart of the divide. The Bible reveals that there is one God who eternally exists in three persons: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. Matthew 28:19; 2 Corinthians 13:14. Christianity is rooted in the worship of the Triune God. Islam explicitly denies the Trinity and the Fatherhood of God, viewing such beliefs as a rejection of God's oneness. To merge these views is to worship a god of human imagination rather than the God who has revealed Himself in Scripture.

Second, the person and work of Jesus Christ are non-negotiable. The Bible declares that Jesus is the eternal Word made flesh, the Son of God who is truly God and truly man. John 1:1; John 1:14; Colossians 2:9. Scripture records as a historical fact that Jesus was crucified, died for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day to defeat death. 1 Corinthians 15:3-4. Islam teaches that Jesus was merely a human prophet, denies His deity, and typically denies that He was ever crucified. There is no middle ground here; if Jesus is not God and did not die for our sins, we are still in our sins and without hope.

Finally, the way of salvation is fundamentally different. The Gospel is the good news that we are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works or religious devotion. Ephesians 2:8-9; Titus 3:5. In contrast, any system that requires human effort or submission to a law to earn God's favor is a different gospel entirely, which the Apostle Paul warns is no gospel at all. Galatians 1:6-9.

The truth is that God is perfectly holy, and we have all rebelled against Him through our sin, leaving us separated from Him and deserving of judgment. Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23. In His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live the perfect life we could not live and to bear the full weight of God’s justice on the cross in our place. He rose again in victory, proving His authority over all things. Salvation is found in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved. Acts 4:12; John 14:6.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Why should I forgive?

The call to forgive is one of the most beautiful and yet most difficult paths a believer is asked to walk. We forgive not because the offense against us was small, but because our God is unimaginably large in His mercy. To understand why we must forgive, we must first look at the heart of the Gospel. 

We forgive because we have been the recipients of a mercy so vast that it outweighs every earthly grievance. Scripture teaches us that God is perfectly holy and just, and because of our sin, we were once His enemies, justly deserving His wrath. Yet, in His infinite love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to take on human nature and live a perfect life. Jesus died a substitutionary death on the cross, bearing the punishment for our sins, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death. Through His sacrifice, those who trust in Him receive *justification*, being declared righteous in God's sight by grace alone through faith alone. If the Lord of Glory has forgiven us our mountain of debt, we cannot rightly withhold forgiveness for the debts others owe us (Matthew 18:21–35).

The Bible provides us with clear reasons for this posture of the heart. First, we forgive out of obedience to our Lord. Scripture commands us to "Be kind and compassionate to one another, forgiving each other, just as in Christ God forgave us" (Ephesians 4:32). To refuse to forgive is to set ourselves up as a higher judge than the Almighty. When we forgive, we are not saying the sin against us did not matter; rather, we are handing the scales of justice over to God, who alone judges rightly (Romans 12:19).

Second, forgiveness is essential for our own *progressive sanctification* and spiritual health. Unforgiveness is like a root of bitterness that springs up to trouble the soul and defile many (Hebrews 12:15). It creates a barrier in our daily fellowship with the Father. Jesus warned that "if you do not forgive others their sins, your Father will not forgive your sins" (Matthew 6:15). This does not mean we earn our way into heaven by forgiving, but rather that a heart truly transformed by the Holy Spirit will inevitably bear the fruit of a forgiving spirit.

Third, we forgive to reflect the character of Christ to a broken world. We are never more like our Savior than when we are extending grace to those who do not deserve it (Colossians 3:13). This witness shows the world that there is a power greater than human resentment.

If you find yourself struggling to forgive because you have never known the peace of being forgiven by God, I urge you to look to Christ. Recognize that your own sin separates you from a holy God, but that Jesus’ death and resurrection provide the only way to be reconciled to Him. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical next step, I encourage you to bring the person who hurt you before the Lord in your private prayers. Do not wait until you "feel" like forgiving, as feelings often follow the act of obedience. Ask God to help you release the desire for personal vengeance and to give you a heart that desires that person’s repentance and ultimate good. You may find it helpful to meditate daily on the magnitude of God’s grace toward you by reading the account of Christ's crucifixion in Luke 23, noting His prayer for those who put Him to death.
Who is Jesus — Day 2 — Jesus the Miracle Worker

Jesus Christ is the Lord of all creation, and His miracles were not mere "religious stories" or metaphors for inner change. They were historical facts and objective demonstrations of His divine authority over every realm of existence. When we look at the ministry of Jesus, we see a man who walked the dusty roads of Galilee, yet at His word, the blind saw, the lame leaped, and the raging seas fell silent. 

In the Gospel of John, these miracles are often called "signs" because they point beyond the wonder itself to the identity of the One performing them. Jesus did not perform miracles to entertain the crowds or to gain worldly power. He performed them to reveal His glory as the eternal Son of God and to manifest the compassion of the Father toward a fallen and suffering world. As it is written, "Jesus performed many other signs in the presence of His disciples, which are not written in this book. But these are written so that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that by believing you may have life in His name" (John 20:30-31).

The miracles of Jesus fall into several clear categories that establish His total sovereignty:

**Authority over Nature**
Jesus demonstrated that the physical world obeys His voice because He is the Creator. He turned water into wine, multiplied bread to feed thousands, and walked upon the surface of the sea. When He rebuked a violent storm, His disciples were terrified and asked, "What kind of man is this? Even the winds and the sea obey Him!" (Matthew 8:27).

**Authority over Sickness and Disability**
Jesus healed every kind of disease, restoring withered limbs and opening ears that had never heard a sound. His touch was not restricted by the contagion of leprosy or the finality of long-term paralysis. These acts were a direct fulfillment of Messianic prophecy, proving He was the promised Savior who would bring restoration to the brokenness caused by the fall of man (Matthew 11:4-5).

**Authority over the Demonic Realm**
With a single command, Jesus cast out unclean spirits. The demons recognized Him even when men did not, trembling at His presence because they knew He was the Holy One of God who had come to destroy the works of the devil (Mark 1:23-27).

**Authority over Death**
The most staggering miracles involved Jesus calling the dead back to life, such as the daughter of Jairus, the widow's son at Nain, and His friend Lazarus who had been in the tomb for four days (John 11:43-44). These events served as a prelude to the greatest miracle of all: His own bodily resurrection from the dead on the third day, which forever broke the power of sin and death for all who believe.

Scripture is clear that these "miracles, wonders, and signs" were the means by which God publicly endorsed Jesus of Nazareth in the sight of all people (Acts 2:22). They prove that He is truly God and truly man, the only Mediator between God and men.

However, the miracles of Jesus were never intended to be an end in themselves. While He cared deeply for physical suffering, He came primarily to solve our greatest problem: our spiritual separation from a holy God due to our sin. We are all spiritually blind and dead in our trespasses until we are made alive by His grace. 

The same Jesus who commanded the leper to be clean and the dead man to rise is the one who offers you total forgiveness and eternal life today. He lived the perfect life you could not live and died the sacrificial death you deserved, bearing the wrath of God on the cross to reconcile you to the Father. You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of new life. What is it that holds you back from trusting Him as your Lord and Savior today?
Who are the Mormons and what do they believe?

Mormons are members of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, a religious movement that began in the United States during the 1820s under the leadership of Joseph Smith. While they use the name of Jesus Christ and value high moral standards, their core teachings differ fundamentally from the historic Christian faith delivered once for all to the saints in the Holy Scriptures. 

To understand their beliefs, one must look at their view of authority. While Christians hold that the Bible is the inspired, inerrant, and sufficient Word of God, Mormons believe the Bible is only one of four "standard works." They also follow the Book of Mormon, the Doctrine and Covenants, and the Pearl of Great Price, and they believe in ongoing modern revelation through their church presidency. This undermines the finality and sufficiency of Scripture, which warns us not to add to or take away from the words of God's revelation. 2 Timothy 3:16-17; Jude 1:3; Revelation 22:18-19.

The most significant errors in Mormonism concern the nature of God and the person of Jesus Christ. Historically and biblically, Christians worship one God who has eternally existed in three persons: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. This is the doctrine of the Trinity. Mormonism, however, teaches that God the Father was once a man who progressed to godhood and that humans can likewise become gods. This contradicts the clear testimony of Scripture that God is the eternal, unchangeable Spirit who alone is God. Numbers 23:19; Malachi 3:6; Isaiah 43:10; Isaiah 44:6; John 4:24.

Regarding Jesus Christ, Mormonism teaches that He is the firstborn spirit child of a "Heavenly Father" and "Heavenly Mother," making Him the literal elder brother of all humans and even of Lucifer. In contrast, the Bible teaches the deity of Christ as the eternal Word who was with God and was God from the beginning, the Creator of all things, who became flesh to dwell among us. John 1:1-3; John 1:14; Colossians 1:15-17.

Finally, their view of salvation deviates from the biblical doctrine of justification by faith. Mormonism emphasizes that while Christ’s grace is necessary, full salvation or "exaltation" requires personal merit, including following specific laws, ordinances, and temple rituals. However, the Gospel declares that we are saved by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works, so that no one may boast. Romans 3:24-28; Ephesians 2:8-9; Titus 3:5.

True peace with God cannot be earned through rituals or human progression; it is found only in the finished work of Jesus Christ. God is perfectly holy, and we have all fallen short of His glory through sin, which separates us from Him. Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23. In His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary atoning death on the cross to pay for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and His authority as Lord. 1 Corinthians 15:3-4; Philippians 2:9-11.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the full forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

If you are seeking to grow in your understanding of these truths, a helpful next step would be to read the Gospel of John in the Bible, focusing on how Jesus speaks of His own eternal identity and His relationship with the Father. I also encourage you to find a local church that faithfully preaches the Bible and honors the Lord Jesus Christ as the only Savior. Hebrews 10:24-25.
Is it allowable for a Christian to eat halal food?

Yes, it is biblically allowable for a Christian to eat halal food. In the New Testament, the Lord Jesus and His apostles made it clear that our standing before God is not determined by what we eat or drink, but by our standing in Christ.

The primary biblical principle regarding food is that God has created all things good, and nothing is to be rejected if it is received with thanksgiving. In Mark 7:18-19, Jesus explained that nothing entering a person from the outside can defile them, because it does not enter the heart but the stomach. By saying this, the Gospel writer notes that Jesus declared all foods clean. Furthermore, the Apostle Paul teaches in Romans 14:14 that nothing is unclean in itself.

Because halal food refers to meat prepared according to Islamic dietary laws, it is helpful to look at how the early church handled meat sacrificed to idols, which is a similar category of concern. Paul provides clear guidance on this in 1 Corinthians 10:25-26, saying, "Eat anything sold in the meat market without raising questions of conscience, for 'The earth is the Lord’s, and everything in it.'"

However, while you have the freedom in Christ to eat such food, there are two important considerations regarding Christian liberty and love:

1. **The Conscience of Others:** While you may know that an idol or a different religious ritual is nothing and that the meat is simply food provided by God, others may not have that same understanding. If eating halal food would cause a fellow believer with a sensitive conscience to stumble, or if it would signal to a non-believer that you are affirming their religious system rather than simply eating a meal, Scripture encourages us to limit our freedom out of love for them. Paul writes in 1 Corinthians 10:31-33, "So whether you eat or drink or whatever you do, do it all to the glory of God. Do not cause anyone to stumble, whether Jews, Greeks or the church of God."

2. **The Goal of Our Witness:** Our primary concern should be the advancement of the Gospel. If sitting down to a halal meal with a Muslim neighbor provides an opportunity to show the love of Christ and share the message of His grace, you should feel free to do so with a clear conscience.

True righteousness does not come from adhering to dietary restrictions or outward rituals, but through faith in Jesus Christ alone. We are all sinners separated from a holy God, unable to cleanse ourselves by our own works or diets. But God, in His great mercy, sent His Son, Jesus, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. It is through His finished work that we receive total forgiveness and eternal life.

As a practical step, when you are in a situation involving halal food, you might pause and ask the Lord for wisdom on how your choice to eat or abstain might best serve the person standing in front of you. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. Have you ever considered how the freedom found in Christ differs from a life lived under religious laws?
What does the Bible say about the role of youth pastor / minister?

The Bible does not explicitly use the modern title "youth pastor" or "youth minister," but it provides a clear and robust framework for the spiritual oversight of the local church and the specific responsibility of teaching the next generation. A youth minister functions as a specialized laborer within the body of Christ, tasked with shepherding younger members and assisting parents in the discipleship of their children.

The foundational biblical principles for this role include:

**1. The Qualifications of a Shepherd**
If a youth pastor is recognized as an elder or overseer within the church, he must meet the stringent moral and spiritual qualifications found in 1 Timothy 3:1–7 and Titus 1:5–9. Even if the role is viewed as a supportive ministry position rather than a formal eldership, the minister is called to be a godly example. Paul exhorted Timothy, who was likely a young man in leadership, by saying, "Let no one despise your youth, but be an example to the believers in word, in conduct, in love, in spirit, in faith, in purity" (1 Timothy 4:12).

**2. The Mandate to Teach Sound Doctrine**
The primary task of any minister is the faithful exposition of the Word of God. In Titus 2:6–8, Paul specifically addresses the ministry to young people: "In the same way, urge the younger men to be self-controlled. In everything set them an example by doing what is good. In your teaching show integrity, seriousness, and soundness of speech that cannot be condemned." A youth minister’s role is not primarily to entertain, but to ground young people in the "faith that was once for all entrusted to the saints" (Jude 1:3).

**3. Supporting the Household of Faith**
Scripture places the primary responsibility for the spiritual upbringing of children on parents (Deuteronomy 6:4–9; Ephesians 6:4). Therefore, a biblical youth minister does not replace the parent but serves as a partner to the family and a representative of the wider church body. The goal is to see young people integrated into the full life of the church, where they can learn from the "aged men" and "aged women" as described in Titus 2:2–5.

**4. The Goal of Spiritual Maturity**
The ultimate aim of youth ministry is the same as all pastoral ministry: "to present everyone mature in Christ" (Colossians 1:28). This involves calling young people to repentance and faith, and then guiding them through the process of progressive sanctification, where they learn to deny ungodliness and live for the glory of God.

**Tertiary Considerations on Structure**
Faithful Christians hold differing views on how youth ministry should be structured, as the Bible does not prescribe a specific "program." 
* **Age-Segregated Ministry:** Many churches believe that grouping youth by age allows for more targeted, age-appropriate teaching and fellowship. 
* **Family-Integrated Ministry:** Other believers emphasize keeping families together during all times of worship and instruction, believing this more closely mirrors the patterns seen in the Old and New Testament assemblies.
Both approaches seek to honor the biblical command to disciple the next generation, and Christians should exercise charity and liberty in these administrative decisions (Romans 14:1–5).

**A Pastoral Note**
If you are serving in or seeking a youth minister, remember that the greatest gift you can give to the youth is your own holiness and your unwavering commitment to the Truth. Young people do not need a "cool" friend as much as they need a courageous herald of the Gospel who will point them to the sufficiency of Jesus Christ. 

As an actionable step, I encourage you to read through the book of Titus this week. Observe how Paul connects sound doctrine to the conduct of different age groups. Pray that the Lord would raise up leaders in your local church who love the Word and love the souls of the next generation.

At the heart of all ministry is the message of the Gospel: that we are sinners separated from a holy God, but through the life, death, and bodily resurrection of Jesus Christ, we can be reconciled to Him. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone. If you have not yet placed your trust in Him, I invite you to turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ today for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What is it that might be keeping you from fully trusting in His grace?
Why do Jehovah's Witnesses refuse blood transfusions?

Jehovah’s Witnesses refuse blood transfusions because they believe the Bible prohibits the consumption of blood in any form, which they interpret to include the medical transfer of blood into the body. They base this position primarily on three passages of Scripture. In Genesis 9:4, God tells Noah, "But you shall not eat flesh with its life, that is, its blood." In Leviticus 17:10, the Law states, "I will set My face against that person who eats blood and will cut him off from among his people." Finally, in Acts 15:28–29, the apostles wrote to Gentile believers, telling them to "abstain from food sacrificed to idols, from blood, from the meat of strangled animals, and from sexual immorality."

For Jehovah’s Witnesses, these verses represent an eternal moral law. They believe that taking blood into one’s veins for the purpose of sustaining life is fundamentally the same as eating it, and that doing so constitutes a rebellion against God’s sovereignty over life itself. While they will often accept non-blood medical alternatives, they view a transfusion as a violation that could cost them their hope of eternal life.

From a historic Christian perspective, this is a misunderstanding of both the purpose of the Old Testament Law and the nature of the New Covenant. The prohibitions in the Old Testament were designed to highlight the sanctity of life and to point forward to the sacrificial system, where blood was reserved for the atonement of sins on the altar (Leviticus 17:11). The life was in the blood, and that life belonged to God. In the New Testament, the instruction in Acts 15 was a pastoral directive intended to preserve peace and fellowship between Jewish and Gentile Christians by asking Gentiles to be sensitive to Jewish dietary concerns. It was never intended to be a medical mandate that forbids life saving procedures.

More importantly, we must recognize that the group teaching this doctrine also denies the most essential truth of the Christian faith: the deity of Jesus Christ. They believe Jesus is a created being rather than the eternal God. When we focus so heavily on the physical symbol of blood, we risk missing the spiritual reality it was meant to foreshadow. The blood of animals could never truly take away sins, but was a shadow of the perfect sacrifice to come (Hebrews 10:1–4).

The true "life in the blood" is found only in the atoning work of Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. It is His blood alone, shed once for all on the cross, that reconciles us to God (Colossians 1:20). He did not come merely to give us a set of dietary or medical rules, but to give us life through His death and resurrection.

If you are looking for hope and security, please know that salvation does not come through our adherence to a specific medical code or our own efforts to remain "pure." Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith in the person and work of Jesus Christ. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and the promise of eternal life (John 3:16; Romans 10:9). Do you find yourself worried about whether your standing with God depends on your own obedience or on what Christ has already done for you?
Does God pursue us?

God is the great Seeker of souls, and the entire testimony of Holy Scripture confirms that He pursues us with an unyielding and holy love. From the moment humanity fell in the garden, it was God who initiated the search, calling out, "Where are you?" (Genesis 3:9). He has been pursuing His people ever since.

In our natural state, we do not seek God. Instead, we are prone to wander and flee from His presence (Romans 3:11; Isaiah 53:6). If left to ourselves, we would remain lost in the darkness of our own rebellion. Salvation, therefore, is not a matter of us finding God, but of God finding us. This is the beauty of His sovereign initiative. Jesus Christ, the Good Shepherd, explicitly stated that He came to seek and to save the lost (Luke 19:10). He does not merely wait for the lost sheep to stumble home, but He goes into the wilderness to find them and carry them back (Luke 15:4-7).

The most profound evidence of God’s pursuit is the Incarnation. God the Son took on human flesh, entering our broken world to bridge the gap that our sin had created. He pursued us even to the point of death on a cross, taking the punishment we deserved so that we might be reconciled to Him through His sacrificial atonement. As the Apostle John writes, "In this is love, not that we loved God, but that He loved us and sent His Son to be the propitiation for our sins" (1 John 4:10). We love Him only because He first loved us (1 John 4:19).

This pursuit is both personal and powerful. Jesus taught that no one can come to Him unless the Father draws them (John 6:44). This drawing is the work of the Holy Spirit, who performs what theologians call an effectual calling, opening blind eyes and softening hard hearts to respond to the Gospel. If you feel a stirring in your soul to know Him, or a weight of conviction over your sin, you are experiencing the very hand of God reaching out to you.

You do not need to run or hide any longer. The Gospel is the glorious truth that Christ died for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and sin. This salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and eternal life.

I encourage you to spend time today reading the Gospel of John to see how Jesus pursued individuals with truth and grace. What is it that holds you back from fully trusting in His pursuit of you?
Is God imaginary?

God is the ultimate reality, the self-existent Creator of all that exists, and far from being a figment of human imagination, He is the one in whom we live and move and have our being (Acts 17:28). While some suggest that God is merely a projection of human needs or a "crutch" for the weak, the Bible teaches that God has made His existence plain to every human heart through the things He has created (Romans 1:19-20). The intricate design of the universe and the moral law written on our consciences are not accidents of nature, but the fingerprints of a holy and sovereign God.

The most definitive proof that God is not imaginary is that He stepped into human history in the person of Jesus Christ. Jesus is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn over all creation (Colossians 1:15). He did not come as a myth or a legend, but as a historical person who was born of a virgin, lived a perfect life, and performed miracles that witnessed to His divine authority (John 20:30-31).

The reality of God is a matter of eternal significance because of our condition before Him. The Bible declares that God is perfectly holy and just, while we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). This sin separates us from Him and deserves His righteous judgment. However, because of His great love, God provided a way for us to be reconciled to Him. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, died on the cross as a substitute for sinners, taking the punishment we deserved. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). This is the Gospel: that salvation is a gift of grace, received through faith in Christ alone, not by our own works (Ephesians 2:8-9).

If you find yourself wondering if God is real, I encourage you to honestly examine the life and claims of Jesus by reading the Gospel of John in the Bible. You can talk to God directly in prayer, asking Him to reveal His truth to your heart, for He promises that those who seek Him with all their heart will find Him (Jeremiah 29:13).

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life. What is it that makes you feel as though God might be imaginary?
What is a mandala in Hinduism?

A mandala, a Sanskrit word meaning "circle," is a symbolic geometric design used in Hinduism to represent the universe and the sacred dwelling place of a deity. These intricate patterns are often structured with a central point from which various layers of symmetry radiate outward, symbolizing the journey from the material world into the spiritual heart of reality.

In Hindu practice, mandalas serve several specific purposes:

*   **Sacred Architecture:** The principles of the mandala are applied to the design of temples through a system known as Vastu Shastra. The ground plan of a temple, called a *Vastu Purusha Mandala*, is intended to align the physical structure with the cosmic order.
*   **Meditation and Ritual:** Practitioners use mandalas as a focal point for meditation, believing that by contemplating the design, they can internalize the divine qualities it represents. During certain rituals, temporary mandalas may be drawn on the ground using colored powders to invite the presence of a god or goddess.
*   **Symbolic Mapping:** They represent the macrocosm of the universe and the microcosm of the human soul, aiming to bring the individual into harmony with the divine.

While these designs are crafted with great care to visualize a path to the divine, we must lovingly observe that the Bible directs our search for God in a very different way. Scripture teaches that God is the Creator of the universe, yet He does not dwell in shrines or patterns made by human hands. As the Apostle Paul declared to the Athenians, "The God who made the world and everything in it is the Lord of heaven and earth and does not live in temples built by human hands" Acts 17:24.

The beauty of the Christian faith is that we do not need to visualize complex geometric symbols to reach God. Instead, God reached down to us. In the person of Jesus Christ, the infinite God took on human flesh to dwell among us, making the way to the Father clear and personal John 1:14. We do not find peace through the contemplation of a diagram, but through a relationship with the Living Word.

If you are seeking a deeper connection with the divine or a sense of order in a chaotic world, I encourage you to look to the one who said, "I am the way and the truth and the life. No one comes to the Father except through Me" John 14:6. True spiritual transformation does not come from rituals or symbols, but through the work of the Holy Spirit who dwells within those who believe.

The human heart is often restless until it finds rest in the Creator. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and new life. As a practical next step, I invite you to read the Gospel of John in the Bible to see how God has personally revealed His love and character through His Son. What is it that you are searching for in your spiritual journey?
Who is the angel Moroni?

In the teachings of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (Mormonism), Moroni is described as an angel who appeared to Joseph Smith in the early 1820s. According to their tradition, Moroni was a resurrected being and the son of a man named Mormon; he is said to have been the final prophet of a group of people called the Nephites. Joseph Smith claimed that Moroni directed him to a hill in New York where golden plates were buried, which Smith then translated to produce the Book of Mormon.

As a follower of Jesus Christ, I must gently but firmly point out that the angel Moroni does not appear anywhere in the Holy Scriptures, nor is he part of historical Christian orthodoxy. The Bible is the inspired, inerrant, and final Word of God, and it provides us with all that is necessary for life and godliness (2 Timothy 3:16–17). When a message or a messenger brings a teaching that contradicts the Gospel established by Jesus and His apostles, we are called to test those spirits with great care.

The Apostle Paul provided a solemn warning to the church in Galatia regarding visitations of this nature: "But even if we or an angel from heaven should preach a gospel other than the one we preached to you, let them be under God’s curse!" (Galatians 1:8). Furthermore, Scripture cautions us that "Satan himself masquerades as an angel of light" (2 Corinthians 11:14). We must rely solely on the Bible as our ultimate authority, for God has spoken finally and fully through His Son, Jesus Christ (Hebrews 1:1–2).

The true Gospel is not a hidden record found in plates of gold, but the public, historical, and glorious news of what God has done in Christ. God is perfectly holy, and we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, leaving us separated from Him. In His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ—who is truly God and truly man—to live a perfect life, to die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and to rise bodily from the grave on the third day. 

Salvation is found in no one else, and it is received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works or additional revelations. You can turn to God today, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive complete forgiveness and eternal life. Is there anything currently keeping you from placing your full trust in the sufficiency of Jesus Christ and His Word?
Is God a moral monster?

God is not a moral monster. On the contrary, He is the very definition and source of goodness, the ultimate standard of what is right, and the holy Creator who is light itself, and in Him there is no darkness at all (1 John 1:5). The accusation that God is "monstrous" often arises when we judge Him by our own shifting cultural standards rather than recognizing His perfect holiness and our own deep rebellion against Him.

To understand God's character, we must begin with His holiness. God is "Holy, Holy, Holy," a term that signifies His complete separation from anything sinful or common (Isaiah 6:3). Because He is perfectly holy and just, He cannot simply overlook evil. A judge who ignores a crime is not "good," he is corrupt. Therefore, when we read of God's judgments in history, such as the Flood or the removal of nations, we are seeing a righteous Judge acting against profound human depravity and the desecration of His world. We often recoil at God's judgment because we have a low view of His holiness and a high view of our own perceived innocence.

However, the most profound answer to the charge of "moral monstrosity" is found at the Cross of Jesus Christ. If God were a monster, He would be indifferent to our suffering or delight in our destruction. Instead, the Bible reveals a God who is "gracious and merciful, slow to anger and abounding in steadfast love" (Psalm 145:8). In an act of unfathomable love, God the Father sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to take upon Himself the very judgment that we deserved.

This is the heart of the Gospel:
God is perfectly holy and cannot dwell with sin (Habakkuk 1:13). All people have sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of this sin, we are separated from God and deserve His just judgment. Yet, because of His great love, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life of obedience that we could not live. He then died a substitutionary death on the cross, taking the penalty for sin upon Himself to satisfy the justice of God (Romans 3:25; 1 Peter 2:24). He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). He is now exalted as Lord of all, offering salvation to everyone who believes.

The Cross proves that God is both the "just and the justifier" of those who have faith in Jesus (Romans 3:26). He does not ignore sin, but He pays the price for it Himself. This is the opposite of a monster; it is the act of a Shepherd who lays down His life for His sheep.

You can turn to God even now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and new life.

What is it about the judgments of God in the Bible that causes you the most concern as you consider His character?
What are the main arguments against limited atonement?

The question of the extent of the atonement—whether Christ died for every individual person who has ever lived or specifically for His chosen people—is a matter of deep significance that has been discussed with both passion and piety throughout church history. While many of us find great comfort in the doctrine of Particular Redemption (often called limited atonement), which teaches that Christ’s death actually accomplished the salvation of His sheep, many faithful brothers and sisters in Christ raise earnest biblical arguments against this view.

One of the primary arguments against limited atonement is the frequent use of universal language in the New Testament. Critics point to passages that use the words "world" or "all" to describe the scope of Christ’s sacrifice. For instance, John 3:16 states, "For God so loved the world that He gave His only begotten Son, that whoever believes in Him shall not perish but have eternal life." Similarly, in 1 John 2:2, the Apostle writes, "He Himself is the atoning sacrifice for our sins, and not only for ours but also for the sins of the whole world." Those who oppose limited atonement argue that a plain reading of these texts suggests that the provision of the cross was made for every human being without exception.

Another significant argument concerns the sincerity of the Gospel offer. If Christ did not die for every person, critics ask how the Gospel can be truly and sincerely offered to everyone. They believe that for God to command all men everywhere to repent and believe, there must be a valid payment already made for them.
