They often cite 1 Timothy 2:4, which says that God "desires all men to be saved and to come to the knowledge of the truth," and 2 Peter 3:9, which declares that the Lord is "not wanting anyone to perish, but everyone to come to repentance." From this perspective, a limited atonement seems to conflict with God’s revealed desire for the salvation of all.

Furthermore, some point to "warning passages" that appear to describe individuals for whom Christ died but who ultimately fall away into destruction. A common reference is 2 Peter 2:1, which speaks of false teachers "even denying the Master who bought them, bringing swift destruction on themselves." The argument here is that if Christ "bought" those who are ultimately destroyed, then His death must extend beyond only those who are finally saved.

While these arguments carry weight for many, we must also consider the specific intent and efficacy of the cross. Those of us who hold to a Reformed perspective believe that Christ’s death did not merely make salvation possible for everyone, but actually secured it for His people. We see the cross as a successful mission where the Good Shepherd laid down His life for His sheep, as stated in John 10:11. We believe that if Christ took upon Himself the sins of every person, then no one could be justly punished in hell, for that would be a double payment for the same debt. Therefore, we emphasize that the atonement is "limited" not in its power—for the blood of Christ is of infinite value—but in its intended application to those the Father gave to the Son.

Ultimately, this is a secondary issue where faithful Christians may disagree while remaining united in the essentials of the faith. What remains crystal clear and non-negotiable is the truth that salvation is found in no one else but Jesus Christ. He is the only Mediator between God and man, as taught in 1 Timothy 2:5.

The most important question is not the theoretical extent of the atonement, but whether you have personally trusted in the Savior. The Gospel is a factual account of Christ’s historical death, burial, and bodily resurrection, which demonstrates His victory over sin and death. God is holy and just, and our sin separates us from Him, but in His great mercy, He sent His Son to be the propitiation for our sins. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of new life. What is it that might be holding you back from resting entirely in His finished work?
Christmas Story — Day 5 — Did Jesus, the Messiah, come to save the Jews only?

Jesus Christ, the promised Messiah, did not come to save the Jews only, but to be the Savior of the entire world. While it is true that salvation is from the Jews (John 4:22), as God chose the nation of Israel to be the earthly lineage of the Savior and the stewards of His holy Word, the scope of His redeeming work knows no ethnic or national boundaries. From the very beginning, God promised Abraham that through his offspring all the families of the earth would be blessed (Genesis 12:3).

When the infant Jesus was presented in the temple, the elderly Simeon, filled with the Holy Spirit, declared Him to be a light for revelation to the Gentiles, and the glory of Your people Israel (Luke 2:32). This reveals that the light of the Gospel was never meant to be hidden within the borders of Judea but was sovereignly designed to shatter the darkness of every nation. Jesus Himself made this clear when He spoke of having other sheep that are not of this fold, whom He must also bring so that there would be one flock and one shepherd (John 10:16).

The cross of Christ is the place where the justice and mercy of God meet for all who believe. Through His substitutionary atonement, Jesus paid the debt for sin that no human being could ever pay. He lived the perfect life we failed to live and died the death we deserved, rising bodily from the grave on the third day to prove His victory over sin and death. Now, justification by faith is offered freely to anyone who calls upon His name, regardless of their heritage, for there is no distinction between Jew and Greek; for the same Lord is Lord of all, bestowing his riches on all who call on him (Romans 10:12).

This is the glorious truth of the Christmas story: the Creator took on human flesh to seek and save the lost from every tribe, tongue, people, and nation (Revelation 5:9). God is perfectly holy, and our sins have separated us from Him, leaving us under a rightful sentence of judgment. Yet, in His unfathomable love, He sent His Son to be the only Mediator between God and man.

You can turn to God even now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive full forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

To grow in your understanding of this global promise, I encourage you to read the book of Romans, specifically chapters 1 through 4, which explain how the Gospel is the power of God for salvation to everyone who believes (Romans 1:16). I also urge you to find a local, Bible-preaching church where you can hear the Word of God and fellowship with others who have found their hope in the King of all nations.
How does prayer work?

Prayer is the relational communication of the redeemed soul with the living God, established as a sacred means through which we express our dependence, adoration, and petitions to our Creator. It is not a mechanism to bend God’s will to our desires, but a divine invitation to align our hearts with His sovereign purposes. As a child speaks to a loving father, the believer approaches God with the confidence that He is both all-powerful to help and all-good to listen.

The Bible teaches that prayer works through the perfect mediation of Jesus Christ and the active help of the Holy Spirit. Because God is holy and we are fallen, we have no right to approach Him on our own merit. However, because Jesus Christ died for our sins and rose again, He serves as our great High Priest who has opened the way into the heavenly sanctuary (Hebrews 4:14-16). We pray to the Father, in the name of the Son, by the power of the Holy Spirit. Even when we do not know how to pray as we ought, the Holy Spirit intercedes for us with groanings too deep for words, ensuring our prayers are aligned with God’s will (Romans 8:26-27).

A common question is why we should pray if God is already sovereign and knows our needs. The scriptures show us that while God has ordained the ends—the outcomes of history—He has also ordained the means to reach those ends. Prayer is one of those ordained means. God chooses to work through the prayers of His people to accomplish His divine decrees. In this way, prayer is not an attempt to change an immutable God, but is the way God changes us and involves us in His work on earth.

For prayer to be effective and pleasing to God, it must be offered in faith and according to His revealed Word. Jesus taught His disciples to pray by first hallowing God’s name and seeking His kingdom, which reorients our priorities before we ever ask for our daily bread (Matthew 6:9-13). When we pray "in the name of Jesus," we are not using a magical formula, but are appealing to His authority and His will. As the Apostle John wrote, if we ask anything according to His will, He hears us (1 John 5:14).

This intimacy with God is only possible because of the Gospel. By nature, our sin separates us from God, rendering us unable to fellowship with Him. But God, being rich in mercy, sent His Son to take the punishment we deserved. Through Christ’s sacrificial death and bodily resurrection, those who repent and believe are justified by grace alone and adopted into God’s family. If you have not yet trusted in Him, you can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. This is the foundation of a life of prayer.

To begin growing in this area, I encourage you to set aside a specific time each day to speak with God. You might start by praying through the Psalms, using the biblical language to help express your own heart. Prayer is a spiritual discipline that grows with practice, resting in the promise that the Lord is near to all who call on Him in truth (Psalm 145:18). What is the greatest burden or joy on your heart today that you can take to Him in faith?
Where was God on September 11th?

On September 11, as on every other day in history, God was seated upon His holy throne, exercising absolute authority and divine providence over all the affairs of men (Psalm 11:4; Psalm 103:19). He was not a distant or surprised spectator, for the Scriptures declare that God "works all things according to the counsel of His will" (Ephesians 1:11). He was present in the cockpits, He was present in the towers, and He was present in the heavens, for He is omnipresent and nothing occurs apart from His sovereign decree (Lamentations 3:37–38).

To ask where God was is to grapple with the reality of human depravity and the mystery of God's purposes in a fallen world. The events of that day were a profound expression of the wickedness that resides in the human heart since the Fall (Genesis 6:5; Jeremiah 17:9). While God is never the author of sin, He sovereignly permits evil for reasons that often remain hidden from our immediate understanding, yet we know that He is righteous in all His ways and kind in all His works (Psalm 145:17). He was near to the brokenhearted on that day, sustaining those who called upon His name and providing common grace through the courage of those who risked their lives to save others (Psalm 34:18).

The sovereignty of God is not a cold or clinical doctrine, but the only sure foundation for hope in a world of tragedy. If God were not in control of that day, we would be left to the whims of chance and the unchecked malice of men. Instead, we have the assurance that even the most horrific acts of rebellion are under the restraint of a holy God who will one day bring every deed into judgment (Ecclesiastes 12:14). 

The ultimate answer to the presence of God in human suffering is found in the person and work of Jesus Christ. God did not remain distant from our pain, but entered into it through the incarnation. On a different dark day in history, the sinless Son of God was nailed to a Roman cross. In that moment of supreme injustice, God was present, working out the salvation of His people through the penal substitutionary death of His Son (2 Corinthians 5:21). Christ took upon Himself the wrath that our sins deserve so that we might be reconciled to God. 

Because Jesus Christ died for our sins and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, we know that death and evil do not have the final word (1 Corinthians 15:54–57). He has triumphed over the grave and will return to wipe away every tear and make all things new (Revelation 21:4–5).

If you find your heart troubled by the weight of evil in this world, I encourage you to look to the cross of Christ. There, the justice and love of God meet perfectly. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to find forgiveness and eternal peace. What is it that holds you back from resting in His sovereign care today?

As a practical step, I encourage you to read through the book of Job or the Psalms, specifically Psalm 46, and spend time in prayer, honestly bringing your questions and your grief to the Lord. He is a refuge and strength, a very present help in trouble (Psalm 46:1).
Is Jesus Calling a good book? Are there any doctrinal problems with Jesus Calling?

While many people have found a sense of emotional comfort in the gentle tone of *Jesus Calling*, a discerning believer must weigh the book against the absolute standard of the Word of God. When we hold it up to the light of Scripture, several significant doctrinal concerns arise that suggest it is not a safe or sufficient guide for the Christian life.

The primary concern is the book's challenge to the sufficiency of Scripture. The author writes in the first person, as if Jesus Christ Himself is speaking directly to the reader. This format creates a form of extra-biblical revelation, where "new" words from Jesus are presented alongside or even in place of the Bible. The Apostle Paul reminds us that the Holy Scriptures are sufficient to make the man of God "complete, equipped for every good work" in 2 Timothy 3:16-17. If the Bible is truly sufficient for our faith and practice, we do not need a contemporary author to provide new messages from the mouth of Christ.

Furthermore, there is a danger in the nature of "listening prayer" that the book encourages. The author describes a process of waiting with a pen in hand for God to speak. However, the Bible teaches that God has spoken definitively through His Son, as recorded by the prophets and apostles. Hebrews 1:1-2 states, "In the past God spoke to our forefathers through the prophets at many times and in various ways, but in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son." We hear the voice of the Shepherd clearly through the written Word, and to seek His voice elsewhere risks opening our hearts to subjective feelings or even deception.

Another doctrinal problem involves the portrayal of Christ's character. The "Jesus" who speaks in these devotionals often sounds quite different from the Jesus of the Gospels. In the New Testament, Jesus is the King of kings, the Holy One who calls for repentance and warns of judgment even as He offers grace. In the book, the tone is often singularly focused on personal therapy, emotional presence, and "positive" encouragement, frequently omitting the calls to holiness, the weight of the cross, and the sovereignty of God that are central to Christ's actual teaching. As the Apostle John writes, "Sanctify them by the truth; Your word is truth" in John 17:17. We must be careful not to reshape Jesus into an image that merely makes us feel comfortable.

If you are seeking a deeper intimacy with the Lord, the most beautiful and certain path is to immerse yourself in the actual words of Jesus found in the four Gospels. To know the true Jesus is to know the One who is truly God and truly man, who lived perfectly, died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day.

As a practical step, I encourage you to set aside books that claim to speak for Jesus and instead spend time in the Gospel of John. Allow the Holy Spirit to use the inspired, inerrant Scriptures to shape your understanding of who He is. True peace is not found in a new message tailored to our feelings, but in the ancient, unchanging Gospel. 

If you have not yet placed your full trust in the Jesus of the Bible, I invite you to turn from your sin today. Trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died for you and rose again, to receive forgiveness and the gift of eternal life. What prevents you from resting fully in the words He has already spoken in His Word?
Does the Bible prophesy the coming of Muhammad?

The Bible does not prophesy the coming of Muhammad as a prophet of God. Instead, the Holy Scriptures teach that Jesus Christ is the final and supreme revelation of God to humanity, fulfilling all the Law and the Prophets that came before Him.

While some have sought to find references to Muhammad in the biblical text, a careful study of the context and the original languages shows that these passages refer to others. For example, the "prophet like Moses" mentioned in Deuteronomy 18:15-18 is identified by the Apostles as Jesus Christ, who, like Moses, mediated a covenant and spoke directly with God (Acts 3:22-26; Acts 7:37). This prophet was to be raised up from among the "brothers" of the Israelites, referring to the tribes of Israel, whereas Muhammad was of Ishmaelite descent.

Furthermore, the "Helper" or "Paraclete" promised by Jesus in the Gospel of John is explicitly identified as the Holy Spirit. Jesus describes this Helper as the "Spirit of truth" whom the world cannot receive and who would dwell within His disciples (John 14:16-17; John 14:26; John 15:26). A human prophet cannot dwell inside the hearts of believers, nor did Muhammad arrive "shortly" after Jesus’ departure as the promise implied.

The biblical narrative concludes with the understanding that the canon of revelation is centered on the person and work of the Lord Jesus. The book of Hebrews declares, "In the past God spoke to our ancestors through the prophets at many times and in various ways, but in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son, whom he appointed heir of all things, and through whom also he made the universe" (Hebrews 1:1-2). Because Christ is the "radiance of God’s glory and the exact representation of his being" (Hebrews 1:3), no further prophetic messenger is required to supplement His finished work.

This is the heart of the Gospel: God is holy and just, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over death. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works or adherence to a law. 

I encourage you to read the Gospel of John in the New Testament to see the beauty of Christ for yourself. You can turn to God even now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive eternal life. What is it that might be preventing you from placing your trust in Him today?
What is the definition of the term Gnostic?

The term Gnostic comes from the Greek word *gnosis*, which means knowledge. In a historical and theological context, Gnosticism refers to a collection of ancient heretical movements that claimed salvation is attained through a secret, mystical knowledge reserved for a spiritual elite, rather than through faith in the person and work of Jesus Christ.

Gnosticism is characterized by a sharp dualism, teaching that the spiritual realm is inherently good while the physical, material world is inherently evil. Because they believed matter was corrupt, Gnostics often denied the true humanity of Jesus, arguing that a holy God could never truly take on "evil" human flesh. This view, often called Docetism, suggests that Jesus only appeared to be human. This stands in direct opposition to the essential truth of the Incarnation, that "the Word became flesh and dwelt among us" (John 1:14).

The Apostles wrote firmly against these errors to protect the church from a gospel that was no gospel at all. To deny that Jesus came in the flesh is to deny the very means of our redemption. As 1 John 4:2-3 states, "By this you know the Spirit of God: Every spirit that confesses that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh is from God, but every spirit that does not confess Jesus is not from God."

Furthermore, Gnosticism creates a hierarchy of believers, suggesting that only those with "special insight" are truly spiritual. In contrast, the Bible teaches that the Gospel is clear, public, and available to all. We are not saved by what we know, but by whom we know. Our hope is built on the historical reality of Christ's life, death, and bodily resurrection. As Colossians 2:9 declares, "For in Him all the fullness of the Deity dwells in bodily form."

True Christianity offers no "secret" path to the divine. It is the simple, profound message that though we are sinners separated from a holy God, Christ has bridged the gap through His sacrificial death. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not a reward for intellectual or mystical achievement (Ephesians 2:8-9).

If you are searching for truth, do not look for hidden mysteries or inner enlightenment. Look to the person of Jesus Christ as He is revealed in the Scriptures. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is generalism?

Generalism is a theological term most often used to describe the view that Jesus Christ died for all people generally, rather than for a specific group of people particularly. This position, frequently called General Redemption or Unlimited Atonement, asserts that the scope of Christ’s work on the cross is universal, providing the legal basis for the salvation of any individual who chooses to believe.

Within the landscape of Christian thought, generalism is a secondary doctrine that focuses on the "extent" of the atonement. Proponents of this view believe that God’s grace is extended to all through the cross, but that this grace must be personally accepted for salvation to be realized. They often lean upon Scriptures such as 1 John 2:2, which says, "He is the propitiation for our sins, and not for ours only but also for the sins of the whole world," and 1 Timothy 2:3–4, which speaks of "God our Savior, who desires all men to be saved and to come to the knowledge of the truth."

In contrast, many in the Reformed tradition, including Reformed Baptists, hold to a view called Particular Redemption. This view emphasizes that Christ’s death was not merely a general possibility of salvation, but a specific and effective substitution that actually secured the salvation of His people. We see this in passages like John 10:11, where Jesus says, "I am the good shepherd. The good shepherd lays down His life for the sheep," and Ephesians 5:25, which states that "Christ loved the church and gave Himself up for her." From this perspective, Christ did not just make men "savable," but fully accomplished the redemption of those the Father gave to Him.

While faithful brothers and sisters may disagree on the precise intent and scope of the atonement, all orthodox Christians agree on the absolute necessity of the cross. We are all united in the truth that God is holy and just, while we are by nature sinful and separated from Him. The Gospel is the good news that Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, took on human flesh, lived a sinless life, and died a sacrificial death to satisfy the wrath of God against sin. His bodily resurrection on the third day confirmed His victory over death and His authority as Lord of all.

Whether one views the atonement as general or particular, the command of the Gospel remains the same for everyone: you must be born again. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life.

As you reflect on these truths, I encourage you to read through the Gospel of John and ask the Holy Spirit to give you clarity on the riches of Christ’s work. What is it about the nature of God's love that draws your heart to seek these answers today?
How should Christians view Men Going Their Own Way (MGTOW)?

The movement known as Men Going Their Own Way (MGTOW) presents a philosophy of life that many men find appealing because it addresses real hurts, legal frustrations, and a culture that often devalues biblical masculinity. However, when we bring this movement under the light of Holy Scripture, we must distinguish between the valid observation of a broken world and a response that leads away from God’s design for human flourishing.

The Bible acknowledges that we live in a fallen world where relationships are often fraught with pain and injustice. It is true that many men have suffered deeply from broken marriages, unfair legal systems, or a society that treats fatherhood with contempt. We must have compassion for the man who feels discarded or betrayed. Nevertheless, the solution to the brokenness of the world is never found in a retreat into the self, but in a surrender to the Lord Jesus Christ.

There are several areas where the MGTOW philosophy stands in direct opposition to the Word of God:

**1. The Rejection of God’s Created Order**
From the beginning, God declared that it was not good for man to be alone (Genesis 2:18). He created man and woman in His own image to dwell in community and, for many, to join in the sacred covenant of marriage. MGTOW often promotes a categorical rejection of marriage as a "trap" or a "losing game." While the Apostle Paul notes that singleness can be a gift used for undivided devotion to the Lord (1 Corinthians 7:32-35), the motivation of MGTOW is typically self-preservation and the avoidance of risk rather than the service of God's kingdom. To reject marriage as an institution because of the sins of others is to reject a primary means by which God displays the Gospel to the world (Ephesians 5:25-32).

**2. The Idolatry of Self-Preservation**
At its core, much of the MGTOW ideology is built on "red pill" awareness, which seeks to protect the individual man from being "used" or "exploited." While being wise and discerning is a biblical virtue, making self-protection the highest goal of one's life is a form of idolatry. Jesus tells us that whoever desires to save his life will lose it, but whoever loses his life for His sake will find it (Matthew 16:25). A life lived solely for one's own peace, hobbies, and finances is a life that misses the sacrificial essence of the Christian calling.

**3. The Danger of Bitterness and Cynicism**
The MGTOW community is often characterized by a deep-seated cynicism toward women. Scripture warns us strictly against bitterness, calling it a root that springs up to cause trouble and defile many (Hebrews 12:15). Christians are called to honor all people as image-bearers of God. To view half of the human race with inherent suspicion or disdain is a failure to love our neighbor as ourselves (Matthew 22:39).

**4. The Necessity of the Local Church**
MGTOW encourages men to "go their own way," which often leads to isolation. Scripture, however, commands us not to forsake the assembling of ourselves together (Hebrews 10:25). A man needs the accountability, fellowship, and sharpening that only comes from being part of the Body of Christ. 

If you find yourself drawn to these ideas because of past wounds, know that there is a better Way. The hurt you feel is real, but isolation and self-focus will only harden your heart. The Gospel tells us that though we were enemies of God, Christ did not "go His own way" to avoid the cost. Instead, He moved toward us in sacrificial love, bearing our sins on the cross and rising again to give us a new identity (Romans 5:8; 1 Peter 2:24).

Your primary identity is not found in your relationship status or your grievances against society, but in whether you belong to Jesus Christ. You can turn to God now, repent of the desire to live for yourself, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to seek out a healthy, Bible-believing local church where you can find older, godly men to mentor you. Do not walk this path alone; find brotherhood in the context of the Gospel, where your strength can be used for God's glory rather than your own insulation. What is it that truly draws you toward the idea of "going your own way"? Reflect on whether that desire stems from a love for God's will or a fear of man's failure.
What is corporate election?

Corporate election is the theological understanding that God’s choice of a people for salvation refers primarily to a collective group, the Church, rather than to specific individuals considered in isolation. In this view, Jesus Christ is the primary Elect One, and individuals become part of the elect only by being united to Him through faith.

This perspective emphasizes the "In Christ" language found throughout the New Testament, particularly in Ephesians 1:4, which states, "For he chose us in him before the creation of the world to be holy and blameless in his sight." Proponents of corporate election argue that just as God chose the nation of Israel as a corporate body in the Old Testament, as seen in Deuteronomy 7:6, He now chooses the Church as a corporate body in the New Covenant.

It is important to distinguish this from the view of individual election, which holds that God, in His sovereign grace, chooses specific persons for salvation before the foundation of the world. Within the historic Christian faith, this is considered a secondary doctrine where faithful believers may disagree. While many in the Reformed tradition emphasize individual, unconditional election based on passages like Romans 9:11–13, those who hold to corporate election see the biblical focus as being on the destination and identity of the group that God has set apart for Himself.

The center of this discussion is the doctrine of union with Christ. Whether one emphasizes the corporate or the individual aspect, the anchor of our hope remains the same. Through His substitutionary atonement, Jesus has purchased a people for His own possession, as noted in Titus 2:14. Our security is not found in our own choosing, but in being found in Him.

If you find yourself wondering if you are included in God's purposes, do not look inward at your own merit or outward at your own strength. Look to Jesus Christ. He is the Holy One of God who took on human flesh, lived a perfect life, and suffered the death we deserved on the cross to reconcile sinners to the Father. He rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death and His authority as Lord.

I encourage you to spend time this week reading the first chapter of Ephesians. As you do, ask the Lord to show you the riches of His grace and the security found in being part of His people. You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Did Jesus speak in tongues?

The Holy Scriptures do not record any instance of our Lord Jesus Christ speaking in tongues as the phenomenon is described in the book of Acts or the Epistles. While the Gospels provide a rich and sufficient account of His earthly ministry, they portray His communication as being consistently characterized by perfect clarity and authoritative teaching, always intended to reveal the character and will of His Father to those who would listen.

In His hypostatic union—being fully God and fully man—Jesus spoke the natural languages of His day, primarily Aramaic, while likely also possessing the ability to speak Hebrew and Greek. His mission was to be the "Word made flesh," the ultimate revelation of God to humanity, as seen in John 1:1 and John 1:14. Consequently, His speech was focused on the proclamation of the Gospel and the explanation of the Kingdom of God in ways that were understandable to His hearers.

The gift of tongues, as manifested in the early church, was a specific sign-gift sovereignly bestowed by the Holy Spirit beginning at Pentecost. Its primary purposes were to serve as a sign for unbelievers and to facilitate the spread of the Gospel across different language barriers, as noted in 1 Corinthians 14:22 and Acts 2:4-11. As the one who baptizes with the Holy Spirit, according to Matthew 3:11, Jesus is the source and giver of these gifts rather than a recipient who needed them for His own ministry. He promised that His followers would perform "greater works," which included the global expansion of the Church through the Spirit’s empowerment, as recorded in John 14:12.

While some may point to moments where Jesus sighed deeply in His spirit or spoke in intense prayer, such as in Mark 7:34 or John 11:33, these are expressions of His profound human emotion and perfect spiritual communion with the Father, not the exercise of the gift of tongues. His words were always the very "words of eternal life," as Peter acknowledged in John 6:68.

The greatest miracle is not found in the variety of tongues one might speak, but in the message that the tongue proclaims. Jesus Christ came into the world to save sinners through His perfect life, His substitutionary death on the cross, and His bodily resurrection on the third day. This is the message that every language and tongue is ultimately called to confess. You can turn to God even now, repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and new life.

As you seek to understand the work of the Holy Spirit, I encourage you to immerse yourself in the Gospels. Focus your heart on the clear teachings of Jesus, for in them you will find the narrow way that leads to life. What is it that draws you to the study of this particular gift?
What does the Bible say about dealing with regrets?

The Bible addresses the heavy burden of regret by directing our gaze away from our past failures and toward the sufficiency of God’s grace and His sovereign purposes. While the world often views regret as a dead-end cycle of "what ifs" and self-reproach, the Scriptures offer a path of redemption where even our deepest sorrows can be transformed by the power of Christ.

To deal with regret biblically, we must distinguish between two types of sorrow. The Apostle Paul explains that godly sorrow produces a repentance that leads to salvation without regret, whereas worldly sorrow produces death as seen in 2 Corinthians 7:10. Worldly regret is often rooted in pride or a lost sense of self-image, leading to despair. Godly regret, however, is a gift that leads us to the cross of Jesus Christ, where our sins are fully paid for and our guilt is removed.

If your regret stems from past sin, the remedy is found in the doctrine of justification. When you trust in Christ, God no longer counts your sins against you because they were placed upon His Son. The Prophet Micah tells us that God delights in mercy and will hurl all our iniquities into the depths of the sea as recorded in Micah 7:18-19. To continue dwelling in morbid regret over sins that God has forgotten is, in a sense, to suggest that the sacrifice of Jesus was not enough. We must believe the Gospel more than we believe our feelings of guilt.

For those regrets that are not about sin but about missed opportunities or poor wisdom, we rest in the sovereignty of God. We serve a Lord who is so powerful and kind that He works all things together for the good of those who love Him, who are called according to His purpose as stated in Romans 8:28. Your past "mistakes" are not outside the reach of His providence. He can use even the broken pieces of your life to create a beautiful tapestry of His grace.

Practically, the Bible calls us to a forward-looking faith. Paul, who had much to regret as a former persecutor of the church, wrote that he forgot what was behind and reached forward to what was ahead, pressing on toward the goal for the prize of the upward call of God in Christ Jesus in Philippians 3:13-14. 

As an actionable step, bring your specific regrets to God in prayer today. If there is a need for restitution or asking for someone's forgiveness, do so with humility. Then, intentionally choose to "put off" the old garment of regret and "put on" the new identity you have in Christ. Seek fellowship in a local church where the Word is preached, as the encouragement of other believers is a vital means of grace for a weary soul.

Ultimately, the only way to truly be free from the sting of the past is to be united to the One who holds the future. God is holy and our sin has separated us from Him, but in His great love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus lived the perfect life we could not live and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone, not by our own works or by trying to make up for our past.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. In Him, you are a new creation; the old has passed away and the new has come as promised in 2 Corinthians 5:17. What is it that makes you hesitate to leave your burden at His feet and trust Him today?
Monergism vs. synergism - which view is correct?

Monergism is the biblical truth that God alone is the acting agent in the new birth, or regeneration. This term comes from two Greek words meaning "to work alone." In contrast, synergism suggests a "working together," where God offers grace and the human will must cooperate or respond to that grace for salvation to occur. When we look to the light of Scripture, we see that while man is responsible to believe, the power to do so is a gift granted by God to those who were previously dead in their sins.

The core of the issue lies in the condition of the human heart. The Bible describes us not merely as spiritually weak or sick, but as "dead in trespasses and sins" (Ephesians 2:1). A dead man cannot cooperate in his own resurrection. Just as Lazarus did not assist Jesus in calling him out of the grave, a sinner does not assist the Holy Spirit in the work of the new birth. It is God who "made us alive together with Christ" (Ephesians 2:5). This work is entirely of His sovereign grace, so that no man may boast (Ephesians 2:8-9).

The monergistic view is precise in its order: regeneration precedes faith. We do not believe in order to be born again; we are born again so that we might believe. Jesus told Nicodemus that "unless one is born again he cannot see the kingdom of God" (John 3:3). The eyes must be opened before they can see, and the heart must be changed from stone to flesh before it can love God (Ezekiel 36:26). As the Apostle John wrote, those who believe in Jesus' name were born "not of blood nor of the will of the flesh nor of the will of man, but of God" (John 1:13).

Synergism is held by many faithful Christians, often within the Arminian or Wesleyan traditions, who wish to emphasize the genuine responsibility of man to respond to the Gospel. They often argue that God provides "prevenient grace" to everyone, which restores enough free will for a person to either accept or reject Christ. However, this view struggles to answer the question: "Why did you believe while your neighbor did not?" If the difference lies in the human will, then the individual has a small ground for boasting in their own wisdom or spiritual sensitivity. Monergism, however, gives all the glory to God, acknowledging that "it depends not on human will or exertion, but on God, who has mercy" (Romans 9:16).

This doctrine is not merely a matter of academic debate; it is the very foundation of our assurance. If my salvation depended, even in the smallest part, on my own consistent cooperation, I would have every reason to fear. But because it is God who began the good work, we can be confident that He will bring it to completion (Philippians 1:6). Our faith is a real, human action, but it is a fruit of the Spirit's secret work within us.

If you find yourself wondering whether you have been born of God, do not look inward at the quality of your own "synergy," but look outward to the sufficiency of Jesus Christ. The Gospel is the announcement that Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, took on human nature, lived a perfect life of obedience, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for the sins of His people. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, conquering death and is now exalted as Lord of all.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for your forgiveness and new life. He is a kind and capable Savior who never turns away anyone who comes to Him in faith (John 6:37). As a practical step in your journey, I encourage you to read through the Gospel of John and ask the Lord in prayer to open your heart to the wonders of His sovereign grace. What is it that holds you back from resting entirely in His finished work?
Did Old Testament believers have eternal security?

Yes, Old Testament believers possessed eternal security because their salvation rested entirely upon the sovereign grace and unchangeable promise of God. While they lived under the shadows of the Old Covenant, their standing before God was secured by the same Savior who secures us today, the Lord Jesus Christ.

The Bible teaches that salvation has always been a work of God’s grace received through faith alone. This is the doctrine of justification, which does not change based on the era in which a person lives. For example, the Scripture says of Abraham, "Abram believed the LORD, and He credited it to him as righteousness" (Genesis 15:6; Romans 4:3). Because God is the one who justifies, and because His gifts and His call are irrevocable (Romans 11:29), those whom He truly redeemed in the Old Testament were kept by His power just as surely as New Testament believers are.

The security of the Old Testament saint was rooted in several divine truths:

*   **The Character of God:** God does not change in His affections or His decrees. He declared, "For I, the LORD, do not change; therefore you, O sons of Jacob, are not consumed" (Malachi 3:6). His faithfulness to His covenant people was the anchor of their souls.
*   **The Finality of God’s Preservation:** The Psalmists frequently rejoiced in the fact that God would not abandon His holy ones. "For the LORD loves justice and will not abandon His saints. They are preserved forever, but the offspring of the wicked will be cut off" (Psalm 37:28).
*   **The Anticipated Work of Christ:** Though they lived before the incarnation, Old Testament believers were saved by looking forward to the promised Seed. Christ is the "Lamb slain from the foundation of the world" (Revelation 13:8). The efficacy of His sacrifice was applied to them retroactively, securing their eternal inheritance.

Sometimes people point to individuals like King Saul as examples of those who "lost" their salvation. However, it is vital to distinguish between a temporary anointing for a specific office or task and the internal, regenerating work of the Holy Spirit that marks a true child of God. True believers, often called the "remnant," were those whose hearts were circumcised by God (Deuteronomy 30:6) and who were kept by His grace.

If you are concerned about your own security before God, remember that your safety does not depend on the strength of your grip on Him, but on the strength of His grip on you. This security is found only in a personal relationship with Jesus Christ. The Gospel reveals that we are all sinners separated from a holy God, but Christ took the penalty for our sins on the cross and rose again to give us life.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the full assurance of eternal life. A helpful next step is to read through the Book of Romans, specifically chapters 4 and 8, to see how God’s plan of salvation remains consistent and secure across all of history. In your personal time of prayer, thank the Lord that He is the author and finisher of your faith, and ask Him to deepen your rest in His finished work.
What does it mean that the church is the bride of Christ?

The description of the Church as the Bride of Christ signifies the intimate, covenantal, and sacrificial relationship between Jesus and His redeemed people. It is a profound metaphor revealing that the Church is the primary object of Christ’s affection, purchased by His own blood and set apart for His eternal glory.

This relationship is rooted in a love that is both sovereign and sacrificial. Just as a husband is called to love his wife, the Bible teaches that Christ loved the Church and gave Himself up for her, as described in Ephesians 5:25. This was not a generic love, but a particular, redeeming love that took Him to the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. Through His death and bodily resurrection, He secured a people for Himself, justifying them by grace through faith alone.

The metaphor also speaks to the ongoing work of sanctification. Christ does not leave His Bride in her brokenness; instead, He cleanses her by the washing of water with the word, that He might present the Church to Himself in splendor, without spot or wrinkle or any such thing, as noted in Ephesians 5:26–27. This means that every trial and every word of Scripture is used by God to prepare us for the day we meet Him face to face. We are being made holy to reflect the character of our Groom.

Furthermore, this bridal identity points to an eternal hope and a future consummation. We currently live in a time of waiting, much like a betrothal period. The climax of human history is described as the "marriage supper of the Lamb," where the Church will finally be united with her Lord in a renewed creation, as seen in Revelation 19:7–9 and Revelation 21:2. This union is the ultimate fulfillment of God’s promise to dwell with His people.

Because the Church is Christ's Bride, we are called to a life of exclusivity and faithfulness. This means turning away from the "adultery" of worldliness and idolatry to find our complete satisfaction in Him. To live as the Bride of Christ is to rest in His finished work while eagerly desiring His return.

If you have never experienced the peace of being reconciled to God, know that your sin separates you from His holiness, but Jesus Christ provides the only way back. He took the wrath we deserved so that we might receive the righteousness we could never earn. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive new life. What is it that holds you back from trusting in the One who gave His life for His people?
How can I believe in the goodness of God when there is so much evil in the world?

My dear friend, your heart feels the weight of a world that is deeply fractured, and it is right to grieve over what is not as it should be. The question you ask is perhaps the most profound ache of the human soul. To look at the darkness of this world and still see the light of God’s goodness requires us to look exactly where God has revealed Himself most clearly: at the Cross of Jesus Christ.

The Bible does not ignore the reality of evil; rather, it is the only book that truly explains it and provides a remedy for it. Scripture tells us that God is light and in Him there is no darkness at all (1 John 1:5). He is the source of all that is beautiful, pure, and life-giving. Evil is not a power equal to God, nor is it a creation of God. Instead, evil is a corruption and a deprivation of the good. It entered our world through the rebellion of humanity against our Creator, a fall that brought death and suffering into every corner of existence (Romans 5:12).

You may wonder why a good God allows such rebellion to continue. We must remember that God’s patience is intended to lead us to repentance (Romans 2:4). If God were to blot out all evil this very moment, He would have to blot out every one of us, for we have all fallen short of His holy standard. Yet, in His mercy, He stays His hand of judgment to allow more people to come to know His grace.

God’s goodness is not seen in the absence of suffering, but in His presence within it. The ultimate proof of God’s goodness is that He did not remain distant from our pain. In the person of Jesus Christ, the Word became flesh and dwelt among us (John 1:14). He was "a man of sorrows and acquainted with grief" (Isaiah 53:3). The greatest evil ever committed in human history was the murder of the sinless Son of God. Yet, in His sovereign wisdom, God used that very act of evil to accomplish the greatest good: the salvation of all who trust in Him. Through the death and bodily resurrection of Jesus, God has defeated sin and death once and for all.

While we live in the "already but not yet" of God's kingdom, we have the firm promise that He is making all things new. A day is coming when He will wipe away every tear from our eyes, and death shall be no more, neither shall there be mourning, nor crying, nor pain anymore (Revelation 21:4).

If you are struggling to see God's goodness today, I encourage you to take your honest laments to Him in prayer. Read the Psalms, such as Psalm 13 and Psalm 22, where the biblical writers cry out "How long, O Lord?" and find their rest in His steadfast love. Seek out a local, Bible-believing church where you can walk alongside others who are also learning to trust God’s character in a dark world.

The most vital truth you can lay hold of is this: God demonstrated His own love for us in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us (Romans 5:8). Our sin separates us from a holy God, and we deserve His just judgment. But because of His great love, Jesus took that judgment upon Himself so that we might be reconciled to the Father. 

You can turn to God right now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and the gift of eternal life. What is it that holds you back from trusting Him with your life and your questions today?
What does "communion of the saints" mean in the ancient creeds?

The "communion of the saints" refers to the spiritual union and fellowship that all true believers share with Jesus Christ, their Head, and consequently with one another as members of His one Body. This phrase, found in the Apostles' Creed, affirms that every person redeemed by the blood of Christ is part of a singular, living community that transcends time, geography, and even the veil of death.

In the biblical sense, "saints" are not a special class of elite or canonized Christians, but rather all those who have been set apart by God, justified by grace through faith, and are being transformed by the Holy Spirit. As the Apostle John writes, "Indeed our fellowship is with the Father and with his Son, Jesus Christ" (1 John 1:3). This vertical union with God is the very foundation for the horizontal communion we enjoy with our brothers and sisters in the faith.

This communion involves several vital realities:

**Union with Christ**
By faith, believers are united to Jesus in His death, resurrection, and life. We are partakers of His righteousness and recipients of His Spirit. This is what theologians often call the "mystical union," where Christ dwells in us and we dwell in Him (John 15:4-5). Because we share in His spiritual blessings, we are never truly alone.

**Fellowship with One Another**
Because we are united to the same Christ, we are bound to one another. The Scripture describes this using the imagery of a body: "For just as the body is one and has many members, and all the members of the body, though many, are one body, so it is with Christ" (1 Corinthians 12:12). This communion compels us to care for one another, bear one another's burdens, and use our diverse spiritual gifts for the building up of the whole Church (Ephesians 4:11-16).

**A Shared Inheritance and Mission**
The communion of the saints means we share in a common hope and a common calling. We are "eager to maintain the unity of the Spirit in the bond of peace," recognizing that there is "one body and one Spirit—just as you were called to the one hope that belongs to your call—one Lord, one faith, one baptism, one God and Father of all" (Ephesians 4:3-6).

It is important to note that while all Christians affirm this communion, there are different understandings of its scope. Some traditions emphasize the "intercession of saints" in heaven, while the historic Reformed and Baptist perspective focuses on the mutual communication of spiritual and temporal blessings among the living, and the shared spiritual life we have with the "church triumphant" already with the Lord (Hebrews 12:22-24). We do not pray to those who have passed, but we are encouraged by their example and the reality that we serve the same King.

If you find yourself feeling isolated or disconnected, remember that the Christian life was never intended to be lived in solitude. I encourage you to seek out a local, Bible-believing church where this communion is lived out in practical ways through prayer, the study of the Word, and the breaking of bread.

This beautiful fellowship is only possible for those who have been reconciled to God. We are all born separated from Him by our sin, but God, in His great mercy, sent His Son to die and rise again so that we might be brought into His family. You can enter this communion today by turning from your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone as your Savior and Lord. What is it that might be keeping you from resting fully in Him and joining this eternal family?
How can I gain a passion for winning souls?

A passion for winning souls is a flame ignited by the Holy Spirit when a believer rightly perceives the glory of God, the gravity of sin, and the sufficiency of the Lord Jesus Christ. To gain this heart for the lost, you must first look upward to the majesty of God, then inward at the mercy you have received, and finally outward at the desperate condition of those without a Savior.

True evangelistic zeal begins with a deep meditation on the character of God. When we behold His holiness and His rightful claim as Creator, we realize that every person we meet is made in His image but is currently in rebellion against His kingdom. A heart that loves God will naturally grieve when His name is not honored. As you grow in your love for the Lord, you will find that your desire for His glory drives you to tell others of His goodness.

You must also cultivate a clear, biblical understanding of the human condition. The Bible teaches that every person is born in sin and is spiritually dead, separated from God and facing His righteous judgment (Ephesians 2:1-3; Romans 3:23). We must believe, as the Scripture states, that there is no other name under heaven by which we must be saved except the name of Jesus (Acts 4:12). If we do not believe that people are truly lost, we will never be urgent about their rescue. Consider the reality of eternity and the terror of the wrath to come, let that move you to a holy compassion (2 Corinthians 5:11).

The most effective way to fuel this passion is to preach the Gospel to yourself every day. Remember the "pit from which you were digged." Reflect on the fact that while you were still a sinner, Christ died for you (Romans 5:8). When the wonder of your own justification by grace alone through faith alone becomes fresh to your soul, you will find it impossible to remain silent. Christ’s love is not a pond to be contained, but a river that must flow through you to others.

The Gospel itself is the power of God for salvation (Romans 1:16). It is the message that God, who is perfectly holy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, into the world. Jesus lived a life of perfect obedience, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and the grave. He is now exalted as Lord and Savior. To have a passion for souls is to have a passion for this specific message.

If you find your heart cold, follow these practical steps to fan the flame:

First, pray specifically for the lost. Ask the Lord of the harvest to give you His heart of compassion for the multitudes who are like sheep without a shepherd (Matthew 9:36-38). Pray for specific individuals by name, asking God to open their hearts.

Second, immerse yourself in the Word of God. A passion for souls is sustained by truth, not merely by emotion. Study the book of Acts to see the boldness of the early church and the epistles of Paul to see his "great sorrow and unceasing anguish" for his kinsmen (Romans 9:1-3).

Third, take a step of obedience. Passion often follows action. Do not wait for a perfect feeling to arrive before you speak. Start by sharing a simple testimony of what Christ has done for you or by handing out a faithful Gospel tract. As you see God use your weak efforts, your joy and passion will grow.

If you have never personally trusted in this Savior, the invitation stands open for you today. You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What is it that holds you back from fully trusting Him?

For those who already know Him, remember that the results of evangelism rest in the sovereign hands of God. Our duty is to be faithful heralds of the King. Trust that His Word will not return void but will accomplish exactly what He intends (Isaiah 55:11). Go forth with the promise of His presence, for He has said, "I am with you always, even to the end of the age" (Matthew 28:20).
What is reprobation? What does it mean to be reprobate?

Reprobation is the sovereign and judicial act of God whereby He chooses to pass over certain individuals, leaving them in their self-chosen sin and justly ordaining them to punishment for their rebellion. To be reprobate is to be rejected or found disqualified after being tested, describing a person who has been given over by God to a debased mind because they have persistently rejected the truth.

In the Scriptures, the term often translates from the Greek word adokimos, which means failing to stand the test or being cast away as worthless. This reality is most clearly seen in the letter to the Romans, where the Apostle Paul explains that since people did not see fit to acknowledge God, He gave them up to a reprobate mind to do what ought not to be done, Romans 1:28. This is not a case of God forcing innocent people to sin, but rather God withdrawing His restraining grace and allowing the human heart to follow its own corrupt desires to their natural, destructive end.

The Bible teaches that God is the potter and we are the clay. He has the sovereign right to make from the same lump of fallen humanity one vessel for honorable use and another for dishonorable use, Romans 9:21. While election is an act of sheer, unmerited mercy toward those who deserve hell, reprobation is an act of perfect justice toward those who continue in their enmity against God. Those who are reprobate are described as those who were long ago designated for this condemnation, ungodly people who pervert the grace of our God into sensuality and deny our only Master and Lord, Jesus Christ, Jude 1:4.

It is important to understand that no one seeks after God on their own, Romans 3:11. Therefore, if a person finds in their heart a genuine desire to repent, a hunger for the Word of God, or a sorrow over their sin, these are signs of God's drawing grace, not of reprobation. The reprobate heart is characterized by a calloused indifference or a settled hatred toward the things of God. The Apostle Paul even urged believers to examine themselves to see whether they were in the faith, saying, Do you not realize about yourselves that Jesus Christ is in you, unless indeed you fail to meet the test? 2 Corinthians 13:5.

The doctrine of reprobation serves as a solemn warning against the hardening of the heart. It reminds us that today is the day of salvation and that we must not trifle with the patience of God. If you feel the weight of your sin or the need for a Savior, do not despair as if you are cast away. The very fact that you care about your standing before God is a reason for hope.

The Gospel declares that although we have all rebelled against a holy God and deserve His righteous judgment, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross. By His bodily resurrection, He defeated death and offers full forgiveness to all who turn to Him. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you. Will you cast yourself upon His mercy today?
What does the Bible say about how to get baptized / how to baptize?

Baptism is the solemn and beautiful ordinance commanded by our Lord Jesus Christ, acting as a public declaration of a believer's union with Him in His death, burial, and resurrection. According to the pattern of the New Testament, baptism is intended for those who have personally repented of their sins and placed their trust in Jesus Christ alone for their salvation.

The Bible describes the practice of baptism through several clear principles:

**The Proper Subjects of Baptism**
Scripture consistently presents baptism as following faith. In the book of Acts, we see that those who "received his word were baptized" (Acts 2:41) and that "when they believed Philip as he preached the good news... they were baptized, both men and women" (Acts 8:12). Because baptism is a sign of a new heart and a confession of faith, it is to be administered to those who can give a credible profession of their faith in Christ (Acts 18:8).

**The Biblical Mode of Baptism**
The word "baptize" is derived from the Greek word *baptizo*, which means to immerse, dip, or submerge. The symbolism of the act requires immersion to fully represent the believer's identification with Christ. As the Apostle Paul writes, we are "buried with Him through baptism into death" so that just as Christ was raised, we too might "walk in newness of life" (Romans 6:3–4). This is further illustrated in the account of Philip and the Ethiopian eunuch, where they both "went down into the water" and "came up out of the water" (Acts 8:38–39).

**The Biblical Formula for Baptizing**
When administering baptism, the church follows the direct instruction of the Lord Jesus to baptize "in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit" (Matthew 28:19). This acknowledges the work of the Triune God in the salvation of the sinner.

**The Spiritual Significance**
It is vital to understand that while baptism is a command to be obeyed, the water itself does not save the soul or wash away sin. Salvation is a work of sovereign grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8–9). Baptism is the "appeal to God for a good conscience—through the resurrection of Jesus Christ" (1 Peter 3:21). It is an outward sign of an inward reality—that the believer has been washed by the blood of Christ and granted the gift of the Holy Spirit.

While faithful Christians throughout history have differed on the proper subjects and mode of baptism—with some practicing infant baptism as a sign of the covenant—the clear apostolic examples in Scripture point to the baptism of believers by immersion as the normative practice for the church.

If you have recognized your need for a Savior and have turned to Jesus Christ, your next step of obedience is to seek out a faithful, Bible-teaching local church. Speak with the elders or pastors there; they are called to shepherd you and guide you through the process of making this public profession of your faith.

If you have not yet placed your trust in Jesus, know that He is the only way to the Father (John 14:6). He lived the perfect life we could not live and died the death we deserved so that all who believe in Him might have eternal life. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What is it that would keep you from trusting Him today?
What is prevenient grace?

Prevenient grace is a theological term referring to the grace of God that precedes, or "comes before," a person’s decision to trust in Jesus Christ for salvation. The word itself is derived from the Latin *praevenire*, which means to go before or anticipate. It represents the biblical truth that no person seeks after God on their own initiative because our hearts are naturally hardened by sin, and therefore, God must take the first step toward us.

Within the history of the church, there are two primary ways that faithful Christians understand how this grace operates.

**The Wesleyan or Arminian View**

Many Christians, particularly those in the Wesleyan or Methodist traditions, believe that prevenient grace is a gift given by God to all people everywhere. They argue that although human nature is corrupted by sin, God’s grace "prevenes" or moves ahead of us to restore a measure of free will. In this view, grace acts as a universal influence that enables any person to either accept or reject the Gospel when it is heard. Those who hold this position often point to passages like John 1:9, which speaks of the Light that gives light to everyone, or Titus 2:11, which states that the grace of God has appeared, bringing salvation to all people.

**The Reformed or Calvinistic View**

In the Reformed tradition, which includes the convictions of Reformed Baptists, the concept of grace "coming before" is understood differently. Rather than a universal grace that restores a neutral free will to everyone, Reformed believers speak of "effectual calling" or "irresistible grace." This view emphasizes that because we are "dead in our trespasses" as described in Ephesians 2:1, we do not simply need assistance to choose God; we need a new life altogether. 

In this perspective, God’s grace is seen as a sovereign and specific work of the Holy Spirit in the hearts of those He is drawing to Himself. This is the miracle of regeneration, where God replaces a heart of stone with a heart of flesh, as promised in Ezekiel 36:26. Here, grace does not merely make salvation possible; it effectively brings the believer to faith. This ensures that God receives all the glory for our salvation, from the first spark of interest to our final breath.

**Why This Matters for You**

While these perspectives differ on the mechanics of how grace works, they agree on a fundamental truth: you cannot save yourself. You are entirely dependent on the mercy of a loving God who reaches out to sinners. Whether one emphasizes God's universal reach or His sovereign choice, the result for the believer is a heart of deep gratitude. As the Apostle John wrote, "We love because he first loved us," in 1 John 4:19.

If you feel a tug in your heart toward God or a sense of conviction over your sin, do not ignore it. This is the work of the Holy Spirit drawing you. The Bible teaches that God is holy and just, and our sin separates us from Him. However, in His great love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. He is now the exalted Lord of all.

You can turn to God even now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for your forgiveness and new life. What is it that might be holding you back from resting entirely in His grace today?

A wonderful next step would be to spend time in the Gospel of John, specifically reading John 3:1-21 and John 6:35-40, and to ask God in prayer to reveal the beauty of His saving work to your heart. It is also vital to find a local, Bible-believing church where you can grow in the grace and knowledge of our Lord.
What does it mean that Jesus died for our sins?

To say that Jesus died for our sins is to speak of the greatest rescue mission in history. It means that Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, stood in our place to receive the judgment we deserved for our rebellion against a holy and righteous God. This central truth is often called penal substitutionary atonement, which signifies that Jesus took our penalty (penal) as our substitute (substitution) to bring us back into a right relationship with God (atonement).

The Bible teaches that every one of us has turned away from God, and the consequence of that sin is spiritual and physical death (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). Because God is perfectly just, He cannot simply ignore sin, for it must be punished. Yet, because God is perfectly loving, He sent His Son to be the propitiation, or the satisfying sacrifice, for our sins (1 John 4:10). On the cross, a divine exchange took place: Jesus, who knew no sin, was treated as if He were a sinner so that we might be credited with His perfect righteousness (2 Corinthians 5:21). He bore the wrath of God that was rightfully ours, exhausted it completely, and cried out, "It is finished," to signify that the debt was paid in full (John 19:30).

Jesus did not remain in the grave. After His sacrificial death and burial, He rose bodily from the dead on the third day, proving that His sacrifice was accepted and that sin and death are defeated (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). This means that salvation is not something we earn through our own efforts, rituals, or good works, but is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9). He is the only mediator between God and man, and there is no other name by which we must be saved (1 Timothy 2:5; Acts 4:12).

This truth changes everything because it means your debt can be canceled. You do not have to carry the weight of your guilt or fear the judgment of God if you are hidden in Christ. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is Calvinism, and is it biblical?

Calvinism is a system of biblical interpretation that centers on the absolute sovereignty of God, especially regarding the salvation of fallen humanity. Often called the Doctrines of Grace, it seeks to give all glory to God by acknowledging that salvation is entirely His work from beginning to end. Historically, these truths were summarized by the Synod of Dort in five points, commonly known by the acronym TULIP.

The first point is Total Depravity, which teaches that sin has affected every part of a human being, meaning that apart from God's grace, no one is able or willing to seek God (Romans 3:10-12; Ephesians 2:1). Second is Unconditional Election, the truth that God chose a people for Himself before the foundation of the world, not based on any foreseen merit in them, but according to His own good pleasure (Ephesians 1:4-5; Romans 9:15-16). Third is Limited Atonement, or Particular Redemption, which suggests that while Christ's sacrifice is infinitely valuable and sufficient for all, His death had a specific, saving intent for those the Father gave to Him (John 10:11; John 10:14-15). Fourth is Irresistible Grace, meaning that when the Holy Spirit sovereignly calls a person, He opens their heart and grants them the gift of faith so that they come willingly to Christ (John 6:37; John 6:44). Finally, the Perseverance of the Saints affirms that those whom God has truly saved will be kept by His power and will never finally fall away from grace (John 10:27-29; Philippians 1:6).

Regarding whether Calvinism is biblical, many faithful Christians believe these points are the most accurate way to harmonize the clear teachings of Scripture about God's power and man's responsibility. It is important to note, however, that this is considered a secondary doctrine. Sincere believers have disagreed for centuries on how to balance the sovereignty of God with the genuine choice of human beings. Other historic views, such as Arminianism, place a greater emphasis on the human will and God’s desire for all people to be saved without an unconditional decree. While these differences are significant, they do not define the core of the Christian faith.

In all of this, we must never lose sight of the central truth of the Gospel. God is holy and righteous, while we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, leaving us separated from Him. Out of His great love, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, took on human flesh, lived a perfect life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and His authority as Lord of all. Salvation is a gift of grace alone, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works of our own (Ephesians 2:8-9).

The most important question is not where you stand on a theological system, but where you stand with Jesus Christ. You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. He promises that whoever comes to Him, He will never cast out. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Who was Ishtar, and is there any connection between Ishtar and Easter?

Ishtar was the ancient Mesopotamian goddess of sexual love, fertility, and warfare, known as Inanna by the Sumerians and often symbolized by the morning and evening star, the planet Venus. In the Holy Scriptures, she is most notably associated with the "Queen of Heaven," a pagan deity whose worship provoked the Lord to anger as the Israelites fell into idolatry, offering cakes and drink offerings to her instead of the one true God (Jeremiah 7:18; Jeremiah 44:17–19).

Regarding the connection between Ishtar and Easter, there is no legitimate linguistic or historical link between the two. The idea that "Easter" is derived from "Ishtar" is a modern myth that ignores the actual etymology of the words. The name "Easter" is unique to the English and Germanic languages, likely originating from the Old English *Eostre*, which referred to a season or a month. In nearly all other parts of the world, the celebration of the Resurrection is called *Pascha*, which is the Greek and Latin form of the Hebrew word *Pesach*, meaning Passover.

As believers, we must be careful not to allow historical confusion to cloud the glorious reality of the Gospel. The celebration of Christ’s Resurrection is not a rebranded pagan fertility rite; it is the commemoration of the most significant event in human history. The early church observed this day in direct connection with the Jewish Passover, recognizing that Jesus is the true Passover Lamb who was sacrificed for our sins (1 Corinthians 5:7).

The truth is that Jesus Christ bodily rose from the dead on the third day, proving His victory over sin and the grave. This is not a religious story or a perspective, but a historical fact witnessed by many and recorded in the inerrant Word of God (1 Corinthians 15:3–8). Because He lives, those who trust in Him are granted the gift of eternal life and are no longer under the condemnation of their sins.

I invite you to consider the weight of this truth. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the hope of new life. What holds you back from placing your full trust in the risen Savior today?

As a practical step in your discipleship, I encourage you to spend time reading the Gospel of John, specifically chapters 19 and 20. Observe how the historical details of Christ's death and resurrection fulfill the promises of God and provide a firm foundation for our faith, far removed from the myths of ancient paganism. Seek out a local, Bible-teaching church where you can grow in the knowledge of the truth alongside other brothers and sisters in Christ.
Who are the Alawites, and what do they believe?

The Alawites are a secretive, syncretic religious group primarily located in Syria, as well as parts of Lebanon and Turkey. Originally emerging from Shia Islam in the ninth century, they are often referred to as Nusayris, named after their founder, Muhammad ibn Nusayr, who claimed to receive special revelations.

While they are often classified as a branch of Islam for political reasons, their theology is a complex blend of Shia tradition, Gnosticism, and ancient Middle Eastern paganism, with some outward elements borrowed from Christianity. 

At the heart of Alawite belief is a triad consisting of Ali ibn Abi Talib (the cousin and son-in-law of Muhammad), Muhammad himself, and Salman the Persian. Alawites believe that Ali is the supreme manifestation of God, a view that stands in direct opposition to the biblical truth that there is only one God, eternally existing in three persons: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. This is clearly stated in Matthew 28:19 and 2 Corinthians 13:14.

Their doctrine also includes a belief in the transmigration of souls, or reincarnation. They believe that human beings were originally stars in a world of light but were cast down to earth after committing sins of pride. To return to the stars, a person must be reborn multiple times. This stands in stark contrast to the historical fact and biblical promise that "it is appointed for man to die once, and after that comes judgment," as recorded in Hebrews 9:27.

Because of their history of being a persecuted minority, Alawites practice "taqiyya," which is the concealment of their true beliefs from outsiders. While they observe certain Christian holidays like Christmas and Easter and use bread and wine in some of their secret rituals, these actions do not reflect a biblical understanding of the Gospel. They view these elements through a mystical lens rather than as a remembrance of the finished work of Jesus Christ.

From a pastoral perspective, we must recognize that while the Alawite faith seeks a path back to the "light," it misses the only true Light of the World. They emphasize a cycle of rebirth and secret knowledge, but the Bible teaches that salvation is not a secret to be earned through cycles of life; it is a gift to be received through the person of Jesus Christ. 

The Scriptures are clear that "there is one God, and there is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus," according to 1 Timothy 2:5. Jesus Himself declared, "I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me," in John 14:6. 

If you find yourself searching for peace or trying to reconcile your past through your own efforts, please know that Jesus Christ has already accomplished everything necessary for your forgiveness. He took the judgment for our sins upon Himself on the cross, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day to give us eternal life. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive a certain hope that no cycle of reincarnation could ever provide. What is it that holds you back from trusting in His finished work today?
Why is sola fide important?

Sola fide, or faith alone, is the essential biblical truth that a person is justified—legally declared righteous in the sight of God—solely through trust in the person and finished work of Jesus Christ, apart from any human works, merit, or religious ceremony. This doctrine is the very heart of the Gospel, acting as the hinge upon which the door of salvation turns, for to lose sola fide is to lose Christ Himself as a sufficient Savior.

The importance of this truth is found in several vital areas:

**The Preservation of Grace**
If human effort were required to earn or maintain standing with God, grace would cease to be grace. The Bible teaches that salvation is a free gift received by faith, ensuring that it remains a matter of God's unmerited favor rather than a wage earned by man. Romans 4:4–5; Romans 11:6. By insisting on faith alone, we acknowledge that we are spiritually bankrupt and entirely dependent on the mercy of God. Ephesians 2:8–9.

**The Sufficiency of Christ’s Work**
Sola fide honors the perfection of Christ's sacrifice. Jesus lived a sinless life of perfect obedience and died a penal substitutionary death, taking the wrath we deserved upon Himself. When we trust in Him, His perfect righteousness is imputed—or credited—to our account. 2 Corinthians 5:21; Romans 3:21–26. To suggest that our works must be added to His sacrifice is to imply that Christ’s death was not enough to save us. Galatians 2:21.

**The Peace of the Believer**
This doctrine provides the only true ground for spiritual rest and assurance.
