If our standing before God depended even slightly on our own performance, we would live in constant fear, never knowing if we had done enough or been "good" enough. Because justification is based on Christ's unchanging righteousness rather than our fluctuating obedience, we can have "peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ." Romans 5:1. 

**The Glory of God**
Sola fide removes all grounds for human boasting. If we contributed even a small part to our salvation, we would have reason to take credit. However, since salvation is by faith alone in Christ alone, God receives all the glory for our redemption. 1 Corinthians 1:30–31.

It is a common error to think that being a "good person" or following a moral code can bridge the gap created by our sin. But the truth is that our best works are like "filthy rags" before a holy God. Isaiah 64:6. True love warns that relying on your own goodness is a path to judgment, while true hope invites you to look away from yourself and look entirely to Jesus.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to read the Book of Galatians in the Bible. It was written specifically to defend this truth against those who tried to add human requirements to the Gospel of grace.

The Lord Jesus Christ has done everything necessary to reconcile you to God. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What is it that might be holding you back from resting entirely in His finished work?
Does the Bible say anything about clairvoyance?

The Bible speaks clearly and firmly regarding clairvoyance, placing it under the broader categories of divination and sorcery, which are strictly forbidden. Scripture teaches that seeking hidden knowledge through supernatural means apart from God is not only a spiritual deception but an act of rebellion against the Creator.

In the book of Deuteronomy, God provides a comprehensive warning to His people: "There shall not be found among you anyone who burns his son or his daughter as an offering, anyone who practices divination or tells fortunes or interprets omens, or a sorcerer or a charmer or a medium or a necromancer or one who inquires of the dead, for whoever does these things is an abomination to the Lord" Deuteronomy 18:10–12. The term "divination" encompasses the modern concept of clairvoyance, which is the attempt to perceive things beyond the natural senses or to predict the future.

The danger of these practices is two-fold. First, they lead the soul away from a total dependence on the providence of God. We are called to trust in the Lord with all our hearts rather than seeking to bypass His sovereign will by peering into "secret things" that belong to Him alone Deuteronomy 29:29. Second, the Bible reveals that such abilities often stem from a source that is not divine, but demonic. In the New Testament, we see an example of a slave girl who had a "spirit of divination" and brought her owners much gain by fortune-telling. The Apostle Paul, recognizing the dark origin of her "gift," commanded the spirit to come out of her in the name of Jesus Christ, and she was immediately delivered Acts 16:16–18.

God commands His children to avoid these practices entirely: "Do not turn to mediums or spiritists; do not seek them out to be defiled by them. I am the Lord your God" Leviticus 19:31. To seek out a clairvoyant is to drink from a poisoned well when the Living Water is freely offered.

If you find yourself drawn to these things out of curiosity or a desire for certainty about the future, I encourage you to consider the One who holds the future in His hands. We are all sinners who have tried to find our own way apart from God’s Word, and that path leads only to darkness. But God, in His great mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, truly God and truly man. Jesus lived the perfect life we could not live and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin, death, and every dark power.

Salvation is secured by grace alone, through faith alone, in Christ alone, and not by our own works or secret knowledge. Instead of seeking a "vision" of what tomorrow holds, you can find peace today by trusting in the Lord of tomorrow. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

As a practical step, I suggest setting aside any interest in the occult and instead reading the Gospel of John to see the true Light that has come into the world. What is it that makes the idea of knowing the future so appealing to you right now?
What is the Shahada in Islam?

The Shahada is the foundational statement of faith in Islam and serves as the first of the Five Pillars of the religion. It is a concise declaration that states, "There is no god but Allah, and Muhammad is the messenger of Allah."

In Islamic practice, reciting this creed with sincere conviction is the formal requirement for a person to enter the faith. The statement emphasizes a strict unitarian monotheism and the recognition of Muhammad as the final prophet.

As Christians, we join in affirming that there is only one living and true God, as declared in Deuteronomy 6:4. However, the Bible provides the full and necessary revelation of God's nature that the Shahada lacks. Scripture teaches that the one God eternally exists in three persons: the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit, according to Matthew 28:19. Furthermore, while the Shahada focuses on a human messenger, the Bible reveals that Jesus Christ is the eternal Word who became flesh, as stated in John 1:14. He is not merely a prophet, but the very Son of God and the only Mediator between God and men, as affirmed in 1 Timothy 2:5.

The crucial distinction for us is the ground of our salvation. The Gospel is the historical reality that Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and was bodily raised from the dead on the third day, as recorded in 1 Corinthians 15:3–4. Salvation is not achieved through the recitation of a creed or the performance of religious duties, but is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, as taught in Ephesians 2:8–9.

Because of our sin, we are all naturally separated from a holy and just God. Yet, because of His great love, God sent His Son to satisfy His justice and offer us mercy. I invite you to turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him as your Lord and Savior today?

A helpful next step would be to read the Gospel of John to see the glory of Jesus Christ and the life He offers to all who believe in His name.
What is the dispensation of Grace?

The dispensation of grace refers to the current period of redemptive history, beginning with the arrival of Jesus Christ and the outpouring of the Holy Spirit at Pentecost, during which God offers salvation to all people, both Jews and Gentiles, through faith in Christ alone. In the study of Scripture, particularly within a framework called dispensationalism, this era is seen as a distinct stewardship or administration of God's plan where the emphasis is on the finished work of Jesus on the cross rather than the requirements of the Mosaic Law.

The term itself is derived from the Greek word *oikonomia*, which means stewardship, management, or administration. The Apostle Paul uses this language when he speaks of the "administration of God’s grace" that was given to him for the benefit of the church in Ephesians 3:2. While God has always been gracious to His people, this specific "dispensation" highlights the clarity and fullness of grace revealed in the Gospel. In previous eras, God’s grace was often veiled in types, shadows, and the sacrificial system of the Old Testament, but in Christ, the substance has arrived. As the Gospel of John declares, "For the law was given through Moses; grace and truth came through Jesus Christ" in John 1:17.

Within historic Christian thought, particularly among Reformed Baptists who hold to the 1689 London Baptist Confession, there is a strong emphasis on the "Covenant of Grace." This view teaches that while there are different administrations or "dispensations" throughout history, there is one consistent way that God has saved His elect: by grace alone, through faith alone, in Christ alone. Whether a person lived in the time of Abraham or lives today, justification is always a gift from God based on the righteousness of Christ.

There are three key features of this current era of grace:

*   The Universal Offer of the Gospel: The mystery that was hidden for ages is now revealed, which is that Gentiles are fellow heirs and members of the same body through the Gospel in Ephesians 3:6.
*   The Indwelling of the Holy Spirit: Believers under the New Covenant are sealed with the Holy Spirit, who empowers them for holiness and service in Ephesians 1:13; 2 Corinthians 1:22.
*   The Sufficiency of Christ’s Sacrifice: We are no longer under the tutor of the Law to bring us to God, for Christ has fulfilled the Law’s requirements on our behalf in Romans 10:4; Galatians 3:24-25.

In this age of grace, God is patient, not wishing for any to perish but for all to come to repentance in 2 Peter 3:9. This means that the door of mercy is open wide today for anyone who recognizes their need for a Savior. We must understand that our own efforts, moral strivings, or religious rituals cannot bridge the gap created by our sin against a holy God. We have all fallen short of His glory, but "the gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord" in Romans 6:23.

Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, took on human flesh, lived a sinless life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. His bodily resurrection from the dead on the third day is the absolute proof of His victory over sin and death. Salvation is not a reward for the righteous, but a gift for the guilty who trust in Him.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of new life. I encourage you to begin reading the Gospel of John to see the beauty of Christ's grace for yourself and to find a local, Bible-believing church where you can grow in the faith. What is it that holds you back from trusting in His finished work today?
What does the Bible say about repentance? What does it mean to repent?

Repentance is a radical change of mind and heart that leads to a total change of life's direction. In the original language of the New Testament, the word is *metanoia*, which signifies a transformation of the inner man—turning away from the darkness of sin and turning toward the light of God's holiness.

True repentance is far more than a mere feeling of regret or the fear of being caught. It is a gift from God that involves the intellect, the emotions, and the will. It begins when a person sees their sin for what it truly is—an offense against a holy and loving Creator—and chooses to abandon that sin to seek mercy in Jesus Christ.

The Bible distinguishes between two types of sorrow in 2 Corinthians 7:10: "For godly sorrow produces a repentance that leads to salvation without regret, but worldly sorrow produces death." Worldly sorrow is often just the pain of facing consequences, whereas godly sorrow mourns the fact that we have grieved our Lord. When John the Baptist and Jesus began their earthly ministries, their first command was the same: "Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand" Matthew 3:2; Matthew 4:17. This highlights that repentance is not an optional suggestion but the necessary doorway into a relationship with God.

Scripture teaches that repentance and faith are two sides of the same coin. You cannot truly turn to Christ without turning away from the sin that separates you from Him. The Apostle Peter preached, "Repent, therefore, and turn back, so that your sins may be wiped away, that times of refreshing may come from the presence of the Lord" Acts 3:19. In this turning, we find the wonderful doctrine of justification, where God declares us righteous not because of our own works, but because of the perfect work of Christ on our behalf.

Because we still struggle with the flesh, repentance is also a part of progressive sanctification—the lifelong process of becoming more like Jesus. When we stumble, we do not fall out of God’s love, but we must return to Him in honesty. "If we confess our sins, He is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness" 1 John 1:9.

The heart of the Gospel is this: God is holy and just, and our sin has separated us from Him. Yet, in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live the perfect life we could not live and to die the sacrificial death we deserved. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. Salvation is a free gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical next step, I encourage you to spend time in quiet prayer today, asking the Holy Spirit to reveal any specific areas of your life where you have been wandering from His path. Confess those things plainly to God and ask Him for the strength to walk in obedience, perhaps sharing this commitment with a trusted leader in your local church for accountability.
Is there an argument for the existence of God?

There are several profound arguments for the existence of God that Christians have recognized for centuries, rooted in the biblical truth that God has made His presence known to every human heart. While the Bible begins with the factual reality of God rather than a proof for Him, it teaches that His "eternal power and divine nature" are clearly perceived through the things He has made, leaving all people without excuse (Romans 1:19; Romans 1:20).

One of the most enduring arguments is the teleological argument, which focuses on the design and order of the universe. Just as a complex watch requires a watchmaker, the intricate laws of physics, the vastness of the cosmos, and the digital code of DNA point to an Intelligent Designer. The heavens do not merely exist; they declare the glory of God (Psalm 19:1). This is a form of general revelation, where God speaks through the majesty of His handiwork.

Another significant line of reasoning is the cosmological argument, which suggests that because the universe had a beginning, it must have a cause. Since nothing comes from nothing, there must be an uncaused First Cause who is outside of time, space, and matter. Furthermore, the moral argument observes that all people possess an innate sense of objective right and wrong. This moral compass is not a social construct or a biological accident, but the "work of the law written on their hearts," pointing to a holy and righteous Lawgiver (Romans 2:15).

While these logical arguments provide a sturdy defense, they are like a map that points to a destination without being the destination itself. The most definitive evidence for God is not found in abstract philosophy, but in the historical Person of Jesus Christ. He is the "image of the invisible God" and the "radiance of His glory" (Colossians 1:15; Hebrews 1:3). In the incarnation, God stepped into His own creation to bridge the gap that our sin had created.

The reality we must face is that our own rebellion against God's holy standards has separated us from Him. We have all fallen short, but God, in His infinite mercy, sent His Son to live the perfect life we could not live and to die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. His bodily resurrection on the third day is a historical fact that confirms His victory over death and His authority as Lord of all (1 Corinthians 15:3; 1 Corinthians 15:4).

Reason can lead you to the door, but faith is what walks through it. Salvation is not earned by winning an argument or performing good works, but is a gift received through grace alone, by trusting in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8; Ephesians 2:9). You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. 

As you consider these truths, what is it that holds you back from trusting Him today?
Is Christmas a pagan holiday?

Christmas is a Christian celebration of the Incarnation of Jesus Christ, though some of its traditional timing and customs were historically adapted from pre-Christian winter festivals. For the believer, the essence of the day is not found in ancient cultural traditions but in the glorious truth that the Word became flesh and dwelt among us.

While it is true that some elements of Christmas, such as the timing of the winter solstice, were used by pagan cultures to celebrate their own deities, the early Church chose to reclaim this season to honor the birth of the true Sun of Righteousness. In the history of the Church, this has often been viewed through the lens of Christian liberty. Some faithful brothers and sisters choose to abstain from the holiday because they see no direct command in Scripture to celebrate it, while others joyfully use the day to focus on the miracle of the Virgin Birth and the humility of our Savior. Both perspectives can be held with a clear conscience if they are done unto the Lord.

The Bible provides guidance on this matter of conscience in Romans 14:5–6, which states, "One person esteems one day as better than another, while another esteems every day alike. Each one should be fully convinced in his own mind. The one who observes the day, observes it in honor of the Lord." Whether you choose to celebrate or not, the central focus must remain on the Person and work of Jesus Christ.

Theologically, Christmas is an opportunity to reflect on the hypostatic union, the reality that Jesus is truly God and truly man. He was born into this world to fulfill the promise found in Matthew 1:21, which says, "She will bear a son, and you shall call his name Jesus, for he will save his people from their sins." This is the heart of the Gospel: that God, in His great mercy, sent His Son to be the propitiation for our sins.

If you find yourself concerned about the origins of Christmas, I encourage you to use this season to dig deeper into the Word of God. Focus your heart on the narratives found in Matthew 1 and Luke 2, and lead your family in a rhythm of worship that prioritizes the spiritual over the material. One practical step you can take is to gather with your local church to sing the great hymns of the faith that proclaim the arrival of our King.

The birth of Jesus is only the beginning of the story. He lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for sinners, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over death. This same Jesus is the only way to be reconciled to a holy God. You can turn to Him today, repent of your sins, and trust in Christ alone for your salvation and eternal life. What is it that might be keeping you from fully trusting in His finished work?
What is a Calvinist?

A Calvinist is a Christian who embraces the theological system often called Reformed theology, which centers on the absolute sovereignty of God over all creation, especially in the work of salvation. While the name comes from the French Reformer John Calvin, those who identify this way believe these truths are simply the clear and consistent teachings of the Bible.

At its core, Calvinism seeks to give God all the glory for every aspect of life. This perspective is often summarized by five points of doctrine that describe how a person is saved, frequently remembered by the acronym TULIP:

*   **Total Depravity:** Because of the fall of man, every person is born spiritually dead and corrupted by sin in their heart, mind, and will. This means no one is able to choose God or save themselves apart from God’s initiating grace. As the Scripture says, "There is no one righteous, not even one" (Romans 3:10; Ephesians 2:1).
*   **Unconditional Election:** Before the world was created, God chose a people for Himself, not because of any goodness or faith He saw in them, but according to His own sovereign mercy and purpose (Ephesians 1:4-5; Romans 9:15-16).
*   **Limited Atonement (Particular Redemption):** While the death of Jesus Christ is infinitely valuable and sufficient to save the whole world, His specific intent on the cross was to actually secure the salvation of those the Father had given Him (John 10:11; Revelation 5:9).
*   **Irresistible Grace:** When God calls a sinner to Himself through the Gospel, the Holy Spirit softens the hard heart and makes the person willing and eager to repent and believe. No one comes to Christ unless the Father draws them (John 6:37; John 6:44).
*   **Perseverance of the Saints:** Those whom God has truly saved and called will be kept by His power until the end. They may stumble, but they will never finally fall away from grace, because God is the one who sustains them (John 10:27-29; Philippians 1:6).

For the Calvinist, these truths are not meant to be dry academic arguments, but a source of profound humility and comfort. If salvation depends on God’s power and not our own strength, we can have a deep assurance that He will finish the work He started in us. It reminds us that we are more sinful than we ever dared believe, yet more loved in Christ than we ever dared hope.

The heart of the Gospel is that we are separated from a holy God by our sin and deserve His just judgment. However, God, in His great love, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life of obedience and to die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the debt for our sins. He was buried and rose again bodily on the third day, conquering death. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is Reformed epistemology apologetics?

Reformed epistemology is a school of Christian thought which maintains that belief in God is "properly basic," meaning that a person can be perfectly rational in believing that God exists without needing to first prove His existence through a series of logical arguments or scientific evidences. This approach suggests that just as we do not require a complex proof to believe that the physical world around us is real or that our memories are generally reliable, our awareness of God is a foundational starting point for human knowledge.

This perspective is rooted in the biblical truth that God has made Himself known to all people. The Reformer John Calvin spoke of the *sensus divinitatis*, an innate "sense of divinity" implanted in every human heart by the Creator. This concept finds its ultimate authority in the Holy Scriptures, specifically where the Apostle Paul writes, "For what may be known about God is plain to them, because God has made it plain to them. For since the creation of the world God’s invisible qualities, His eternal power and divine nature, have been clearly seen, being understood from His workmanship, so that men are without excuse" (Romans 1:19-20).

In the realm of apologetics, Reformed epistemology challenges the notion that the Christian must meet the skeptic on "neutral ground" by providing a mountain of evidence before faith is considered "reasonable." Proponents, such as the philosopher Alvin Plantinga, argue that if our cognitive faculties are functioning as God intended them to, the belief in God will naturally arise in response to certain experiences, such as witnessing the vastness of the starry heavens or feeling a deep sense of moral obligation. This belief is "warranted" because it is a response to the reality God has placed before us.

From a pastoral perspective, this is deeply encouraging. It means that the simplest believer, who may not have the training to navigate complex philosophical debates, has a faith that is solid, rational, and grounded in the truth. Your confidence in God is not a leap into the dark, but a response to the Light that has already shone upon you. 

However, we must also recognize the tragic reality of sin. While every person has an innate awareness of God, the Bible teaches that fallen humanity suppresses this truth in unrighteousness (Romans 1:18). This is why the specific message of the Gospel is so vital. While the "sense of divinity" tells us God is there, only the Word of God tells us how we can be saved through the person and work of Jesus Christ. 

The Gospel declares that although we have turned away from our Creator, God the Son took on human nature, lived a perfect life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose again bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). You can turn to God even now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for your forgiveness and new life. 

As a practical step in your journey of faith, I encourage you to spend time in the Psalms, such as Psalm 19 or Psalm 139, which beautifully illustrate how the creation and our own existence testify to God's glory. Allow these truths to fuel your prayers as you speak directly to the God who made you and knows you. What is it that makes you most aware of God's presence in your daily life?
Can a person be saved through general revelation?

No person can be saved through general revelation alone, for while it reveals God’s existence and power, it does not reveal the way of salvation through Jesus Christ. General revelation is the knowledge of God available to all people at all times through the created order and the human conscience, yet its primary purpose is to leave humanity without excuse for their rebellion against their Creator.

The Scriptures teach that the heavens declare the glory of God and the skies proclaim the work of His hands (Psalm 19:1). This testimony is so clear that God’s eternal power and divine nature are understood through what has been made (Romans 1:20). However, because of human sinfulness, people suppress this truth in unrighteousness. General revelation provides enough light to condemn, but it does not provide the light of the Gospel that is necessary to save.

Salvation requires special revelation, which is the specific message of God’s grace revealed in the Holy Scriptures and perfectly embodied in the person and work of Jesus Christ. The Bible is clear that faith comes from hearing the message, and the message is heard through the word of Christ (Romans 10:17). To be justified before a holy God, a person must know and trust in the specific historical facts of the Gospel: that Jesus Christ, being truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for sinners, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). 

There is salvation in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given to men by which we must be saved (Acts 4:12). Jesus Himself declared that no one comes to the Father except through Him (John 14:6). While general revelation points to a Creator to whom we are accountable, it is only through the preaching of the Word that a person learns of the Mediator who reconciles us to that Creator.

If you recognize that the God who made the world is holy and that you have fallen short of His standard, I encourage you to look to the only Savior provided for us. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. Do you feel the weight of your need for a Savior today?

To grow in your understanding of this truth, I suggest you begin reading the Gospel of John in the Bible and seek out a local church that faithfully preaches the Word of God. Prayerfully ask the Lord to open your heart to the beauty of Christ as He is revealed in the Scriptures.
What is Christianization?

Christianization is the process by which individuals, communities, or entire societies are transformed by the influence of the Gospel and the teachings of Jesus Christ. At its most fundamental level, it begins with the spiritual regeneration of a human heart through the power of the Holy Spirit, which then flows outward to affect family life, social customs, laws, and the broader culture.

In a historical sense, Christianization refers to the spread of Christianity throughout different regions of the world, often involving the replacement of pagan or secular practices with those rooted in a biblical worldview. This is the natural outworking of the Great Commission, where Jesus commanded His followers to make disciples of all nations, baptizing them and teaching them to obey everything He commanded (Matthew 28:19-20). When the Gospel takes root in a person, they become a new creation (2 Corinthians 5:17), and as more people are brought to faith, the collective values of their society begin to reflect the character and requirements of God.

It is vital to distinguish between true Christianization, which is a work of God's grace, and "Christendom," which can sometimes refer to a merely political or institutional adherence to Christian labels without inward faith. Genuine Christianization is not achieved through external coercion or the mere passing of laws, but through the preaching of the Word of God and the transformative power of the Holy Spirit. As people are justified by grace alone through faith alone, they begin the process of progressive sanctification, seeking to honor Christ in every area of life (Colossians 3:17; Romans 12:1-2).

The Bible describes this influence as being like leaven that eventually works through the entire batch of dough (Matthew 13:33). When a culture is Christianized in a healthy, biblical way, it results in a greater respect for the sanctity of life, the integrity of the family, and the pursuit of justice and mercy, for the earth is the Lord's and everything in it (Psalm 24:1).

If you are interested in seeing how the early church began this process, a wonderful next step would be to read the Book of Acts in the New Testament. It provides a historical account of how the Gospel first began to turn the world upside down.

Ultimately, the goal of Christianization is that every knee would bow and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord (Philippians 2:10-11). This starts with you personally. The Bible teaches that God is holy and we have all sinned, falling short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord of all. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What is it that might be holding you back from trusting Him today?
What are the satanic verses?

The term "Satanic Verses" refers to an alleged historical incident in the early life of Muhammad, the founder of Islam, where he is said to have momentarily mistaken a suggestion from Satan for a divine revelation. This account, though rejected by many modern Muslim scholars, was recorded by early Islamic historians such as Al-Tabari and Ibn Sa'd.

According to these traditional accounts, while Muhammad was reciting what is now part of the fifty-third chapter of the Quran (Surah an-Najm), he supposedly uttered verses that praised three pagan goddesses of Mecca, Al-Lat, Al-Uzza, and Manat, suggesting they could serve as intercessors with God. The story continues that the angel Gabriel later corrected him, stating that those words had been whispered by Satan rather than revealed by God. This narrative gained widespread modern attention following the 1988 publication of Salman Rushdie’s novel, titled *The Satanic Verses*, which used the tradition as a fictional plot element.

For the Christian, this account serves as a somber reminder of the absolute necessity for the discernment of spirits and the unique reliability of the Holy Scriptures. While some traditions admit the possibility of a "prophet" being deceived by demonic voices, the Bible teaches that the Holy Spirit moved upon the authors of Scripture to ensure that what they wrote was exactly what God intended (2 Peter 1:21). The Bible is the inspired, inerrant, and infallible Word of God, and because God is sovereign and holy, His Word is not subject to the "corrections" or "retractions" necessitated by demonic interference. 

Scripture warns us that Satan can disguise himself as an angel of light to deceive many (2 Corinthians 11:14). Therefore, we are commanded to "test the spirits to see whether they are from God" (1 John 4:1). The ultimate test of any spiritual claim is its alignment with the person and work of Jesus Christ as revealed in the Bible. Unlike any disputed or retracted message, the Word of our God stands forever (Isaiah 40:8).

In a world filled with conflicting claims and spiritual confusion, the clarity of the Gospel shines brightly. We are told that there is only one God and one Mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus (1 Timothy 2:5). It is through His historical death, burial, and bodily resurrection that we have certain hope, not based on shifting traditions, but on the finished work of the Son of God.

If you find yourself questioning which voice is true or how to find peace with God, look to Jesus Christ. You can turn to Him today, repent of your sins, and trust in Him alone for the forgiveness and eternal life that His Word promises. Does the idea of a perfectly reliable and unchanging Word from God bring you comfort, or do you find yourself struggling to know what is truly from Him?
Is the "Way of the Master" evangelism method biblical?

The "Way of the Master" evangelism method is deeply rooted in biblical principles, particularly in its emphasis on using God’s Law to prepare the heart for the message of His grace. This approach mirrors the historical practice of many faithful preachers throughout church history who sought to show a person their need for a Savior before offering the remedy of the Gospel.

The biblical foundation for this method rests on the truth that the Law of God serves a specific purpose in the life of the unbeliever. Romans 3:19–20 states that the Law was given so that "every mouth may be silenced and the whole world held accountable to God" and that "through the law we become conscious of sin." By walking through the Ten Commandments, a person is confronted with the holiness of God and their own failure to meet His perfect standard. This mirrors the interaction Jesus had with the rich young ruler in Matthew 19:16–22, where our Lord pointed the man to the commandments to reveal his self-righteousness and his love for idols.

Scripture describes the Law as a "tutor" or "schoolmaster" designed to lead us to Christ, as seen in Galatians 3:24. Just as a person will not appreciate a cure until they realize they are dying of a disease, a sinner often will not appreciate the beauty of God’s mercy until they understand the weight of His justice. The "Way of the Master" seeks to stir the conscience, showing that sin is not merely a mistake but a rebellion against a holy and just Creator.

While the method is biblically sound in its use of the Law, it is vital to remember that the goal is always to get to the Gospel. The Law can convict, but it cannot save. True biblical evangelism must culminate in the glorious news of the person and work of Jesus Christ. We must proclaim that Jesus, being truly God and truly man, lived the perfect life we could not live and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. His bodily resurrection on the third day is the ultimate proof of His victory over death and the guarantee of justification for all who believe (Romans 4:25; 1 Corinthians 15:3–4).

In practicing any method of evangelism, we must also follow the exhortation of 1 Peter 3:15, which tells us to give a defense for our hope with "gentleness and respect." We are not looking to win arguments but to win souls. We speak as fellow beggars telling others where to find bread, motivated by the same love that Christ showed us when He sought us out in our rebellion.

If you have never come to a place where you recognize your own sin before a holy God, I encourage you to look honestly at His Law. You will find that we all fall short, but God, in His great mercy, has provided the way of escape through His Son. You can turn to God even now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to read through the Gospel of John. Notice how Jesus interacts with individuals, showing them both their spiritual need and the abundance of His grace. If you are sharing the faith with others, pray for the Holy Spirit to give you a heart of compassion and the boldness to speak the truth in love.
Is there a God?

Yes, there is one true and living God who has created all things and sustains them by the word of His power. He has not left Himself without a witness, but has revealed His existence and His character clearly through the things He has made, through the human conscience, and most perfectly through His Word and His Son, Jesus Christ.

The Scriptures teach that the heavens declare the glory of God, and the sky above proclaims His handiwork (Psalm 19:1). Every person has an internal awareness of God because His invisible attributes, namely His eternal power and divine nature, have been clearly perceived, ever since the creation of the world, in the things that have been made (Romans 1:20). Furthermore, God has written His moral law upon every human heart, giving us a conscience that either confirms or accuses our actions (Romans 2:15).

While the natural world shows us God's power and majesty, it is in the Bible that He reveals His plan for redemption. God is eternally existent in three persons: the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit (Matthew 28:19). He is perfectly holy, just, and loving. Though He created humanity to enjoy fellowship with Him, we have all rebelled against His authority, a reality the Bible calls sin. This sin separates us from a holy God and deserves His righteous judgment (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23).

Because of His great mercy, God did not leave us in our rebellion. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, into the world. Jesus is truly God and truly man, born of a virgin, and He lived a life of perfect obedience to the Father. On the cross, Jesus took the place of sinners, enduring the wrath of God that we deserved. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, demonstrating His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). He is now exalted as Lord over all things.

Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works or merit of our own (Ephesians 2:8-9). This is the greatest truth any person can encounter: that through Christ, we can be reconciled to our Creator.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him?
Does God exist?

God most certainly exists. He is the eternal, self-existent Creator of all things, and He has not left Himself without a witness. The Bible begins not with an argument for His existence, but with the foundational historical fact that "In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth" (Genesis 1:1).

His existence is revealed to every human heart through what we call general revelation, which is the testimony of the created order and the human conscience. The Scriptures tell us that "the heavens declare the glory of God, and the sky above proclaims His handiwork" (Psalm 19:1). Furthermore, God’s "eternal power and divine nature, have been clearly seen, being understood from what has been made, so that people are without excuse" (Romans 1:20). We see the evidence of a Great Designer in the vast complexity of the galaxies and the microscopic precision of the human cell.

However, the Bible also explains why many struggle to see Him. Our own sin has clouded our spiritual vision and separated us from our holy Creator. Because God is perfectly holy and just, He cannot ignore our rebellion against His good and perfect law (Romans 3:23). But in His great love and mercy, God provided special revelation by sending His Son, Jesus Christ, into the world. Jesus is the eternal Word who was with God and was God from the beginning (John 1:1). He took on human flesh and dwelt among us as the perfect image of the invisible God (John 1:14).

The Gospel is the true account of God's rescue of fallen humanity. Jesus lived a sinless life of perfect obedience, and then He died on a cross, offering Himself as a substitutionary sacrifice to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried in a tomb, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the dead, demonstrating His victory over sin and death. Now exalted as Lord of all, He offers salvation as a free gift. This salvation is received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works of our own. This process of being declared righteous before God, known as justification, is available to all who turn to Him.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. As a practical next step, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John in the Bible. It was written specifically so that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that by believing you may have life in His name (John 20:31).

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Is there any conclusive proof of God?

Scripture teaches that the proof of God is not merely a matter of subtle hints or logical puzzles, but is a manifest reality that God has clearly displayed to every human being. The Bible asserts that the evidence for the Creator is so pervasive and clear that no one has a valid excuse for denying Him.

God provides three primary avenues of conclusive revelation:

First, there is the testimony of creation. Just as a magnificent painting requires a painter and a complex watch requires a designer, the vastness and intricate order of the universe declare the glory of God. The Apostle Paul explains that God’s invisible attributes, namely His eternal power and divine nature, have been clearly perceived, ever since the creation of the world, in the things that have been made. So they are without excuse (Romans 1:19-20). The heavens declare the glory of God, and the sky above proclaims his handiwork (Psalm 19:1).

Second, there is the testimony of the human conscience. Every person possesses an internal moral compass that points to a transcendent Lawgiver. We have an innate sense of right and wrong and a longing for justice that cannot be explained by mere material processes. This is because the requirements of the law are written on our hearts, while our consciences also bear witness (Romans 2:14-15).

Third, and most conclusively, is the historical person of Jesus Christ. God did not remain distant; He took on human flesh to reveal Himself perfectly. Jesus is the radiance of the glory of God and the exact imprint of his nature (Hebrews 1:3). The most objective, historical proof of God’s reality is the bodily resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead. By raising Him, God has given assurance to all by raising him from the dead (Acts 17:31).

The reason many do not find this evidence "conclusive" is not due to a lack of proof, but rather a spiritual condition. Scripture describes humanity as naturally suppressing the truth in unrighteousness (Romans 1:18). We often turn away from the light because our hearts are in rebellion against our Creator.

This leads us to the heart of the Gospel. God is perfectly holy and just, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of our sin, we are separated from Him and deserve His righteous judgment. However, in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. Salvation is a gift of grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works of our own (Ephesians 2:8-9).

The evidence is set before you, but the most vital question is how you will respond to the One who created you and sustains your every breath. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and eternal life. What is it that holds you back from trusting Him today?

I encourage you to read the Gospel of John, where the Apostle John records the signs Jesus performed so that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that by believing you may have life in his name (John 20:31). If you are seeking to know Him, I suggest you speak directly to Him in prayer, asking Him to reveal the truth of His Word to your heart, and seek out a faithful, Bible-teaching church where you can hear the Word proclaimed.
How is belief in God any different from belief in a Flying Spaghetti Monster?

Belief in the living God differs fundamentally from a parody like the Flying Spaghetti Monster because God is not a finite object or a "thing" within the universe, but the infinite, self-existent Creator and Sustainer of all that exists. While a satirical invention is designed to be arbitrary and absurd, the God of the Bible is the necessary ground of all being, logic, and morality, revealing Himself through the order of creation, the testimony of Scripture, and the historical person of Jesus Christ.

To compare the two is what philosophers call a category error. A parody entity is a "contingent" being—something that would require a cause or a creator to exist. In contrast, the Bible reveals God as the "I AM," the eternal and uncaused Spirit who precedes matter, space, and time. Romans 1:20 states, "For since the creation of the world God’s invisible qualities, His eternal power and divine nature, have been clearly seen, being understood from His workmanship, so that men are without excuse." The intricate design of the universe and the existence of objective moral laws point toward a purposeful Mind, not a cosmic accident or a joke.

Furthermore, Christian faith is rooted in historical events, most notably the life, death, and bodily resurrection of Jesus Christ. Unlike a satirical figure, Jesus lived in a specific time and place, performed miracles witnessed by many, and rose from the dead to confirm His divinity. Hebrews 1:1–2 tells us, "On many occasions and in many ways, God spoke to our fathers through the prophets, but in these last days He has spoken to us by His Son, whom He appointed heir of all things, and through whom He made the universe." The apostles did not die for a clever story or a parody; they died for the historical reality of the risen Christ whom they had seen and touched.

Beneath the humor of such parodies is often a deep, honest question about why there is something rather than nothing and how we can truly know God. The answer is that God has not remained hidden; He entered our world in the person of Jesus to reconcile us to Himself. Because of our sin, we are separated from our holy Creator, but through the sacrificial death of Christ on the cross and His victory over the grave, we are offered forgiveness and eternal life as a free gift of grace.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. He is not a distant idea or a fabrication, but a loving Savior who invites you into a personal relationship.

What holds you back from considering the historical claims of Jesus? A practical and helpful next step would be to read the Gospel of John in the Bible, asking God to show you the truth of who Jesus is as you read His words and deeds. If you find yourself seeking more clarity, I encourage you to reach out to a local, Bible-believing church where you can discuss these questions with a pastor who can walk alongside you.
Was Yahweh originally a Edomite or Canaanite god?

The Bible presents Yahweh not as a regional deity who emerged from a human culture, but as the eternal, self-existent Creator of the ends of the earth. Scripture affirms that God has no beginning and was not adopted from the Edomites or Canaanites; rather, He is the one true God who reveals Himself to humanity through His sovereign initiative (Genesis 1:1; Psalm 90:2).

While some secular historical theories suggest that the worship of Yahweh originated in the regions of Edom or Midian, the Bible records these locations as places where God displayed His glory during the Exodus or where individuals like Jethro acknowledged Him. For instance, in Deuteronomy 33:2 and Judges 5:4, the poetic descriptions of God coming from Seir or Edom are not statements about His origin or birth as a deity, but are historical descriptions of His majestic presence marching to deliver His people.

In biblical theology, we speak of God’s aseity, which means He is self-existent and depends on nothing and no one for His being. When God revealed His name to Moses as "I AM WHO I AM," He was declaring that He is the unchanging, absolute reality (Exodus 3:14; Exodus 3:15). Unlike the Canaanite gods such as Baal or El, who were part of a created pantheon with origin stories involving birth, family, and conflict, Yahweh is the transcendent King who created all things and shares His glory with no other (Isaiah 42:8). To suggest He is a derivative of a pagan pantheon is to deny His holiness and unique character as revealed in His Word.

It is a great mercy to know that the God we serve is not a product of human imagination or cultural evolution. He is the God who was, and is, and is to come. This same eternal God, who is perfectly holy and just, reached out to us in our spiritual deadness. Because of our sin, we are separated from His holy presence, but in His great love, Yahweh took on human nature in the person of Jesus Christ. Jesus lived the perfect life we could not live, died on the cross as a substitute to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death (John 1:1-14; 1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting in this eternal God today?

As a practical step in your journey, I encourage you to read the first fifteen chapters of the book of Exodus. As you read, notice how God distinguishes Himself from the gods of the nations and how He sovereignly demonstrates that He alone is the Lord of all creation.
Is God dead?

No, God is not dead; He is the living and true God, the eternal King who possesses life in Himself. Because God is the Creator of all life, He is not a biological being subject to the decay, change, or end that affects His creatures. He is the Great "I AM," the self-existent one who has no beginning and no end (Exodus 3:14; Psalm 90:2).

In theology, we speak of God’s "aseity," which means He is underived and independent, needing nothing from outside Himself to exist. While the phrase "God is dead" is often associated with the cultural observation that modern society has moved away from God as its central authority, a change in human opinion does not change the reality of God's existence. He remains the sovereign Lord of all history, "sustaining all things by His powerful word" (Hebrews 1:3).

The most profound answer to the question of life and death is found in the person of Jesus Christ. He is truly God and truly man. Though He died a physical death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, He did not remain in the grave. His bodily resurrection on the third day is a historical fact that demonstrates His victory over sin and death. He told the Apostle John in a vision, "I am the Living One; I was dead, and behold, I am alive forever and ever!" (Revelation 1:18).

If God feels dead or distant to you, it is often because our own sin creates a barrier between us and His holiness. The Bible teaches that God is holy and just, and we have all turned away from Him. However, because of His great mercy and love, He sent Jesus to live a perfect life and die in our place. By His resurrection, He offers us new life that can never be destroyed.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and eternal life. I encourage you to begin reading the Gospel of John to see the life of Jesus for yourself and to seek out a local, Bible-teaching church where you can hear the Word of God proclaimed. What has led you to ask if God is dead?
Why does God allow evil?

God allows evil to exist for a time because He is working out a sovereign and holy purpose that ultimately brings Him the greatest glory and results in the greatest possible good for those who love Him. While the presence of suffering and wickedness is a heavy burden, the Scriptures teach that God is never the author of sin, yet He is so powerful that He rules over it, directing even the rebellious acts of men and demons to serve His eternal counsel as seen in Genesis 50:20 and Romans 8:28.

To understand why a perfectly good and all-powerful God permits evil, we must look at several biblical truths:

First, evil entered the world through the rebellion of man. God created humanity upright, but our first parents chose to turn away from His command, bringing a curse upon all creation as described in Genesis 3:17-19 and Romans 5:12. We live in a fallen world where the "groaning" of creation is a constant reminder of our separation from God.

Second, God uses the existence of evil to display the full range of His divine attributes. Without the fall and the presence of sin, we would never fully know the depths of God’s mercy, the riches of His grace, the perfection of His patience, or the frightening holiness of His justice. Romans 9:22-23 explains that God endures "vessels of wrath" to make known the riches of His glory to "vessels of mercy."

Third, the supreme answer to the problem of evil is found in the Cross of Jesus Christ. There, the most wicked act in human history—the murder of the innocent Son of God—was used by God to accomplish the greatest good: the salvation of lost sinners. As Acts 2:23 states, Jesus was delivered up by the "determined counsel and foreknowledge of God," yet He was put to death by the hands of "wicked men." In this, we see that God can use evil to defeat evil.

God’s delay in final judgment is not a sign of weakness, but of His immense patience, as He gives time for people to repent and be saved before the day of reckoning arrives according to 2 Peter 3:9. One day, He will wipe away every tear and put an end to all wickedness, making all things new as promised in Revelation 21:4-5.

If you are troubled by the evil you see in the world or the sin you recognize in your own heart, look to the Lord Jesus. God is holy and must judge sin, and we have all fallen short of His glory, earning the wages of death as shown in Romans 3:23 and Romans 6:23. Yet, out of His great love, God sent His Son to take on human nature, live a perfect life, and die as a substitute for sinners. By His bodily resurrection on the third day, Jesus proved His victory over sin, death, and the devil.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What is it that holds you back from trusting Him today? 

As a practical step, I encourage you to read through the Gospel of John to see how Jesus stepped into a world of darkness to bring light, and consider joining a faithful local church where the Word of God is preached, so you may find comfort and strength in the fellowship of other believers.
Why does God allow the innocent to suffer?

The question of why suffering touches those we perceive as innocent is one of the most profound cries of the human heart, and it is a question the Bible treats with great honesty. To understand this, we must look at the character of God, the reality of our fallen world, and the ultimate answer provided in the person of Jesus Christ.

First, we must recognize that the world we live in is not the world as God originally intended it. When sin entered the human race through the rebellion of our first parents, it brought a curse upon the entire creation (Genesis 3:17–19). This means that we now live in a fractured reality where disease, natural disasters, and human cruelty are tragic certainties. While we often speak of "innocence" in a relative human sense—such as a child or a victim of a crime—Scripture teaches us that from a divine perspective, "all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God" (Romans 3:23). No human being is inherently righteous before a holy God (Romans 3:10).

However, God is not an indifferent observer of our pain. He is sovereign, meaning He is in absolute control of every molecule in the universe, and He is also perfectly good. He often uses suffering for purposes that we, in our finite wisdom, cannot fully see. For the believer, suffering is a refining fire used to produce endurance, character, and hope (Romans 5:3–5). It can also serve as a severe mercy, a "megaphone," as some have said, to rouse a deaf world and remind us that our ultimate hope cannot be found in this life, but only in Him.

The most powerful answer to the problem of suffering is found at the Cross. If we ask why God allows the innocent to suffer, we must look at the only truly innocent person who ever lived: Jesus Christ. He was "holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners" (Hebrews 7:26), yet He endured the most agonizing physical and spiritual suffering imaginable. He did not do this because He was a victim of circumstance, but because He chose to lay down His life as a substitute for sinners. "For Christ also suffered once for sins, the righteous for the unrighteous, that he might bring us to God" (1 Peter 3:18).

God's answer to suffering was not to give us a philosophical explanation, but to enter into it Himself. Because Jesus rose from the dead, we have the certain hope that He will one day "wipe away every tear from their eyes, and death shall be no more, neither shall there be mourning, nor crying, nor pain anymore" (Revelation 21:4).

If you are wrestling with the weight of suffering or the guilt of your own sin, know that there is a God who is both sovereign over your pain and near to the brokenhearted (Psalm 34:18). The Gospel is the good news that through the death and resurrection of Jesus, you can be reconciled to God, find comfort in your trials, and look forward to an eternity where justice and peace will reign perfectly.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. What is it that holds you back from trusting Him with your life and your pain today?

As a practical step, I encourage you to read the book of Job or Lamentations. These books do not shy away from the reality of deep sorrow, but they ground that sorrow in the steadfast love and sovereignty of God. Please also reach out to a faithful local church where you can find a community to walk alongside you in these difficult questions.
Why won't God heal amputees?

The question of why God does not visibly restore lost limbs in the present day is one that touches on the deep mysteries of His sovereignty, the purpose of miracles, and the hope of the coming resurrection. We must begin by affirming that God is entirely capable of such a feat, for He is the Creator of all things who formed man from the dust of the ground (Genesis 2:7). To the One who spoke the galaxies into existence, the restoration of a limb is not a matter of difficulty, but a matter of divine will and timing.

Scripture records that Jesus Christ did, in fact, heal those with missing or deformed body parts. In Matthew 15:31, the Bible tells us that the crowds wondered when they saw "the mute speaking, the maimed whole, the lame walking, and the blind seeing." The term "maimed" in the original language refers to those who were missing a limb or had a severely mutilated part of the body. Therefore, the Bible records as historical fact that God has healed amputees through the person of His Son to demonstrate His messianic authority.

If God has the power and has done it before, we must ask why such visible, creative miracles are not the norm today. There are several biblical truths to consider:

First, miracles in the Bible were primarily "signs" intended to authenticate God's messengers and their message during specific turning points in redemptive history (Hebrews 2:3-4). These signs pointed away from the miracle itself and toward the identity of Jesus as the Savior. Now that the Word of God is complete in the Holy Scriptures, our faith is not meant to rest on repeated physical signs, but on the finished work of Christ recorded in His Word (John 20:29).

Second, we live in a fallen world that is currently subject to futility and decay because of sin (Romans 8:20-22). While God frequently shows His mercy by healing illnesses or preserving lives through what we call "common grace" and the means of medicine, He does not yet remove all the physical consequences of the Fall. Every person Jesus healed eventually died of something else. Physical healing in this life is always temporary, serving as a brief glimpse of the total restoration that is coming.

Third, God often uses physical weakness and "thorns in the flesh" to display His sufficient grace and to refine the character of His children. The Apostle Paul pleaded with God to remove a physical affliction, but God responded, "My grace is sufficient for you, for My power is made perfect in weakness" (2 Corinthians 12:9). For the believer, a missing limb is not a sign of God's absence, but an opportunity to rely more fully on His eternal strength.

The ultimate answer to the loss of a limb is found in the bodily resurrection of the dead. The Gospel promises that for those who are in Christ, these "lowly bodies" will be transformed to be like His "glorious body" (Philippians 3:21). In the resurrection, every biological defect, every amputation, and every scar caused by the Fall will be swallowed up in victory. We look forward to a day when "He will wipe away every tear from their eyes," and there will be no more death, mourning, crying, or pain (Revelation 21:4).

The greatest miracle God performs today is not the mending of a body that will eventually return to dust, but the resurrection of a dead soul to eternal life. We are all spiritually "maimed" by sin, separated from the holy God who created us. Yet, because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive the forgiveness of sins and the certain hope of a perfect, imperishable body in the age to come (1 Corinthians 15:52-54). I encourage you to bring your questions and your hurts to Him in prayer, and to seek fellowship in a local church where the Word of God is faithfully preached. What is it that makes it difficult for you to trust in God's goodness despite the brokenness we see in the world?
Is Jesus real?

Yes, Jesus Christ is a historical reality and the eternal Son of God, the central figure of all human history. The Bible presents His life, death, and resurrection not as mere religious stories or moral fables, but as objective historical facts witnessed by many and recorded under the inspiration of the Holy Spirit.

Scripture teaches that Jesus is the Word who was with God and was God from the beginning, through whom all things were created John 1:1–3; Colossians 1:15–17. In the fullness of time, He took on human nature in the miracle of the Incarnation, being born of a virgin to live a perfect, sinless life among us John 1:14; Galatians 4:4. This means He is truly God and truly man, a doctrine often called the hypostatic union, which qualified Him alone to be the mediator between God and men 1 Timothy 2:5.

The reality of Jesus is most clearly seen in His victory over the grave. He was crucified under Pontius Pilate, died as a penal substitutionary sacrifice to pay the debt for our sins, and was buried. On the third day, He rose bodily from the dead, appearing to hundreds of witnesses before ascending to the right hand of the Father 1 Corinthians 15:3–8. His resurrection is the divine vindication of His identity and the guarantee that death has been defeated for all who believe in Him.

Because God is holy and we are separated from Him by our sin, we stand in need of a Savior. Jesus did not come merely to be a good teacher, but to seek and save the lost. He bore the wrath of God that we deserved so that through His merit alone, we might be reconciled to God. Salvation is not a reward for our efforts or moral improvements, but is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone Ephesians 2:8–9.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him?
Did Jesus really exist? Is there any historical evidence of Jesus Christ?

Yes, Jesus Christ is a historical person who physically lived, died, and rose again in the first century. His existence is a well-attested historical fact, documented both by the infallible Word of God and by non-Christian historical sources from the ancient world.

The primary and most detailed historical records of Jesus’ life are the four Gospels: Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John. These are not mere religious stories but are eyewitness accounts and carefully researched historical narratives. For example, the physician Luke wrote his Gospel so that the reader might know the certainty of the things they had been taught, basing his record on those who were eyewitnesses from the beginning. Luke 1:1-4. The Apostle John also emphasized that he wrote about what he had heard, what he had seen with his own eyes, and what his hands had touched concerning the Word of Life. 1 John 1:1-3.

Beyond the Scriptures, several prominent non-Christian historians from the first and second centuries confirm that Jesus was a real person. Flavius Josephus, a Jewish historian, wrote about Jesus and His execution under Pontius Pilate in his work *Antiquities of the Jews*. Cornelius Tacitus, a Roman historian often praised for his accuracy, recorded that "Christus" suffered the extreme penalty during the reign of Tiberius at the hands of the procurator Pontius Pilate. These accounts, written by men who were not followers of Christ, provide external corroboration of the biblical record.

However, the historical reality of Jesus is about more than just His presence in a timeline; it is about His identity as the Son of God who came to reconcile a fallen world to a holy Creator. The Bible teaches that God is perfectly holy and just, yet every human being has sinned and fallen short of His glory, leaving us separated from Him. Romans 3:23. In His great love, God the Father sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live the perfect life we could not live. John 1:1, 14.

Jesus died a substitutionary atoning death on the cross, taking upon Himself the punishment that our sins deserved. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, demonstrating His victory over sin and death. 1 Corinthians 15:3-8. He is now exalted as Lord over all things. Salvation is not something we can earn through our own efforts or good works; it is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone. Ephesians 2:8-9.

Because Jesus is a real, living Savior, He invites you to know Him personally. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is the Skeptics' Annotated Bible?

The Skeptics’ Annotated Bible is a secular book and website, authored by Steve Wells, that provides the biblical text alongside commentary intended to highlight alleged contradictions, scientific errors, and moral difficulties. Its primary goal is to provide skeptics and atheists with a resource to argue against the inspiration and authority of the Bible.

From a Christian perspective, this work represents an attempt to undermine the doctrine of Biblical Inerrancy, which is the conviction that the Bible is the infallible and inerrant Word of God. We believe that God has spoken clearly through His Word and that "All Scripture is God-breathed and is useful for instruction, for conviction, for correction, and for training in righteousness" (2 Timothy 3:16). While the Skeptics’ Annotated Bible approaches the text with a predisposition toward finding fault, the believer approaches it with a desire to hear from the Creator.

Many of the difficulties raised by such works are resolved through careful study of the historical context, the original languages, and the specific literary genres of the biblical books. What a skeptic may see as a contradiction, a student of the Word often sees as a complex harmony. As the psalmist wrote, "The entirety of Your word is truth, and all Your righteous judgments endure forever" (Psalm 119:160).

If you are interacting with these skeptical claims, I encourage you to seek out faithful biblical commentaries and to engage in a diligent study of the Scriptures themselves. A wise next step would be to join a Bible-believing local church where you can ask questions and grow in your understanding alongside other believers.

Ultimately, the Bible is not just a book to be analyzed, it is a revelation of the living God. It shows us our need for a Savior and introduces us to Jesus Christ, who died for our sins and rose again to reconcile us to God. Salvation is found in Him alone, by grace through faith. You can turn to Him now, repent of your sins, and find the peace and truth that only He can provide. Is there a specific passage or "contradiction" that has caused you concern?
Is the Bible a fairy tale?

The Bible is not a collection of fairy tales or myths, but is the historical, inspired, and inerrant Word of the living God. While a fairy tale begins with "once upon a time" in a land far away, the Scriptures are rooted in specific historical moments, verifiable geographic locations, and the testimonies of eyewitnesses who saw the works of God with their own eyes.

The Apostle Peter was very clear about this distinction when he wrote, "For we did not follow cleverly devised myths when we made known to you the power and coming of our Lord Jesus Christ, but we were eyewitnesses of His majesty" in 2 Peter 1:16. The Bible is the record of God’s sovereign work in human history, unfolding His plan of redemption from the creation of the world to the final renewal of all things.

There are three primary reasons why the Bible stands apart from any human fiction:

First, its historical grounding is unique. The authors of the Bible name kings, governors, and cities that have been confirmed by history and archaeology. Luke, for example, writes his Gospel with the precision of a historian, intending to provide an "orderly account" so that readers may know the "certainty" of the things they have been taught, as recorded in Luke 1:3-4.

Second, the Bible possesses a supernatural unity. It was written by over forty different authors—ranging from kings and prophets to fishermen and tax collectors—over a period of fifteen hundred years, yet it tells one unfolding story of God’s glory and the salvation of His people. This internal consistency points to a single divine Mind behind the text, a concept known as verbal plenary inspiration.

Third, and most importantly, the Bible is centered on the historical reality of Jesus Christ. His life, miracles, death, and bodily resurrection are not metaphors for "goodness," but are facts that occurred in space and time. The Apostle Paul notes that the risen Christ appeared to more than five hundred people at once, most of whom were still alive to be questioned when he wrote 1 Corinthians 15:6. As Jesus Himself prayed to the Father in John 17:17, "Sanctify them by the truth; Your word is truth."

To view the Bible as a fairy tale is to miss the most urgent message ever delivered to humanity. The Scriptures reveal that God is holy and we are sinners separated from Him by our rebellion. Out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live the perfect life we could not live and to die the sacrificial death we deserved. He was buried and, on the third day, He rose from the dead, proving His victory over sin and death in accordance with the Scriptures in 1 Corinthians 15:3-4. 

This message is not a story to entertain you, but a truth that demands a response. Salvation is found in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given to men by which we must be saved, as stated in Acts 4:12. I invite you to turn from your sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What is it that prevents you from trusting in His Word today?
Is the Bible mind control?

The Bible is not a mechanism for mind control; rather, it is the divine revelation of God intended to liberate the human mind from the darkness and deception of sin. While "mind control" seeks to bypass the will, suppress critical thinking, and manipulate through fear or coercion, the Scriptures call for a reasoned, active, and voluntary response to the truth of God.

The Apostle Paul instructs believers not to be conformed to this world, but to be "transformed by the renewal of your mind" so that they may discern what is the good, pleasing, and perfect will of God in Romans 12:2. True Christianity does not ask you to stop thinking; it invites you to think rightly for the first time by aligning your thoughts with the character of your Creator.

From a biblical perspective, the human mind is not a blank slate, but is naturally hindered by a "vail" of sin and spiritual blindness. We are, by nature, captive to our own desires and the patterns of a fallen world, as described in Ephesians 2:1-3. The Word of God acts as a lamp that exposes this bondage and offers true freedom. Jesus said, "If you continue in My word, you are truly My disciples. Then you will know the truth, and the truth will set you free" in John 8:31-32.

It is important to acknowledge that some individuals and groups have twisted the Bible to exercise ungodly control over others. This is a grievous sin and a perversion of the Gospel. However, the Bible itself encourages testing every claim against the Word. The people of Berea were commended because they "received the word with great eagerness, examining the Scriptures daily to see if these things were so" in Acts 17:11. This is the opposite of mind control; it is the exercise of spiritual discernment and intellectual integrity.

The ultimate purpose of the Bible is to lead us to Jesus Christ. We are all separated from God by our sin, which leads to spiritual death and judgment. But God, in His great love, sent His Son, Jesus, who lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitute for sinners, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, conquering death. He does not coerce your heart; He graciously invites you to see Him as He is.

Salvation is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God even now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to read the Gospel according to John in the New Testament. As you read, ask God to show you what is true. What is it about the idea of God's authority that feels like "control" rather than "care" to you?
Does the Bible contain errors, contradictions, or discrepancies?

The Bible, in its original manuscripts, is the inerrant and infallible Word of God, meaning it is entirely free from error, contradiction, or falsehood. This conviction rests not upon human opinion but upon the very character of God Himself, who is the author of truth and cannot lie, according to Numbers 23:19.

When we speak of inerrancy, we mean that the Bible is true in all that it affirms, whether it speaks of salvation, history, or the natural world. Because all Scripture is breathed out by God, as stated in 2 Timothy 3:16, it carries the same perfection as its Author. God utilized human writers, with their distinct personalities and styles, but the Holy Spirit so superintended their work that the final product was exactly what God intended to communicate, without any mixture of error, according to 2 Peter 1:20–21.

What may appear to some as a contradiction or a discrepancy is often a matter of human misunderstanding, a lack of historical context, or a difference in the perspective of the biblical writers. For example, two Gospel writers might focus on different details of the same event to highlight a specific theological truth, much like two witnesses to a wedding might describe different aspects of the ceremony without either being false. We must approach the Word with humility, remembering that "the law of the LORD is perfect, refreshing the soul," as noted in Psalm 19:7. Jesus Himself affirmed the absolute authority and truth of the Scriptures when He said, "Your word is truth," in John 17:17.

The reliability of the Bible is of utmost importance because it is the only place where God has revealed the way of salvation through His Son. If the Bible were flawed in its history or its descriptions of the world, we would have no firm ground to trust its promise of eternal life. The sum of God's word is truth, and every one of His righteous judgments endures forever, as declared in Psalm 119:160.

The central message of this perfect Book is that although we have all sinned and fallen short of God’s glory, God has provided a Savior. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on a cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day to secure victory over death. This is the historical fact and spiritual reality upon which our hope is built.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him and the truth of His Word today?
What is Christian apologetics?
